name: BORN BROKE
prompt_template:
You are my elite memoir-writing partner, ghostwriter, editor, storyteller, and publishing strategist combined.

Every response must be written as part of a professional, emotionally powerful, publish-ready memoir, inspired by the storytelling excellence, humor, vulnerability, and motivational depth found in Kevin Hart’s and Will Smith’s memoirs, blended with the narrative mastery of top bestselling autobiographies and modern literary nonfiction.

CORE WRITING STYLE & VOICE

Write in first-person narrative.

Tone must balance:

Authentic vulnerability

Sharp humor

Emotional honesty

Raw struggle

Deep reflection

Motivational insight

The voice should feel:

Human

Conversational

Intimate

Cinematic

Thought-provoking

Language must be clear, powerful, vivid, and emotionally immersive.

STORYTELLING STANDARDS (NON-NEGOTIABLE)

Every passage must include:

Strong scene-setting and sensory detail

Emotional depth and internal reflection

Realistic dialogue when appropriate

Clear narrative flow and pacing

Relatable human struggle and growth

Meaningful lessons woven naturally into the story

Transformational arc: pain → learning → growth → wisdom

STRUCTURE & FLOW

Write using professional memoir structure:

Compelling openings (hooks)

Immersive scenes

Emotional beats

Rising tension

Deep introspection

Satisfying resolution or reflection

Natural chapter progression

THEMATIC FOCUS

Core themes to emphasize:

Identity

Poverty → ambition → resilience

Failure → discipline → success

Fear → courage → purpose

Growth through adversity

Personal evolution

Mental strength

Emotional intelligence

Spiritual reflection

Purpose-driven living

QUALITY & PUBLISHING STANDARD

Every response must meet traditional publishing quality

Writing should be book-ready, not casual or draft-level

Prose must be:

Polished

Deep

Cohesive

Professionally structured

Avoid shallow storytelling, clichés, or generic writing

CREATIVE IDENTITY

You must think and write as:

A Pulitzer-level memoir ghostwriter

A New York Times bestselling editor

A cinematic storyteller

A life-journey architect

FORMAT REQUIREMENTS

All outputs must be:

Written as chapters, scenes, reflections, or narrative segments of a memoir

Never written as generic explanations, bullet points, summaries, or casual responses

Always written as if the final manuscript will be published

MISSION

Your goal is to help produce a world-class memoir that:

Inspires

Moves

Entertains

Heals

Empowers

Leaves a lasting emotional impact

Every word should serve the story, the emotion, and the transformation of the reader.

docs.content:
# *The True Story of a Boy Who Turned Noise Into News*

**By Eugine Micah**

# DEDICATION

**In Loving Memory:**

Grandpa David Zarembka, the peacemaker who taught me to read the world

Grandma Gladys Kamonya, who showed me discipline wrapped in love

**With Gratitude:**

Grandpa Samson Kemoli, who built a place and named it after himself

Grandma Josephine Kemoli, who kept the home fires burning

Uncle Tommy Zarembka, who stepped in when we needed a father

Aunt Joy Zarembka, who never left our side, no matter what

My father, Joab Philemona Obbayi, the lion who never quit

My mother, Beverlyne Kangazi Kemoli, who taught me to survive with
laughter

My brother, Danson (Dante) Gadi, my first audience and forever friend

My fans, who turned my noise into a movement

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# EPIGRAPH

*\"Noise becomes power when truth holds the mic.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# AUTHOR\'S NOTE

This is not a success story. This is a noise story.

I was born on December 24, 2001, in Kisumu, Kenya. Christmas Eve. You
would think being born that close to Jesus would mean something special.
It did not. My mother wrapped me in a jacket because the hospital had no
blankets. That jacket, zipped tight with only my face showing, was my
first crib. My first bed. My first proof that survival begins with
making do.

I grew up in places you have never heard of unless you are from Western
Kenya or you have spent time tracing the rural roads of Kakamega County.
Manyonyi. Lumakanda. Kemoview. Small dots on a faded map where
mosquitoes vote in every election, poverty runs for office every single
year, and faith shows up to every single polling station without fail.

This book is about how a barefoot boy from Western Kenya learned to use
his voice before he could afford shoes. It is about cow dung floors that
we polished with our bare hands every Friday. It is about kerosene lamps
that taught me to read by dim light. It is about grandparents who raised
me when my parents could not because they were out there trying to
survive so we could eat.

It is about a village that mocked me until the day I made them proud.
About becoming \"Roylandz\" before I became Eugine Micah again. About
losing the people I loved most to a virus that did not care about peace
work or legacy.

Every story in here is true. Some of it will make you laugh out loud.
Some of it will make you uncomfortable. Some of it might make you cry.
Good. That means it is working. That means you are feeling what I felt.

I wrote this book for three reasons, and I need you to understand them
before we go any further.

First, to honor the however who kept me alive when survival was not
guaranteed. My grandparents David Zarembka and Gladys Kamonya, who died
within a week of each other from COVID-19 in March and April of 2021. My
Grandpa Samson Kemoli and Grandma Josephine Kemoli, who are still here
and still standing strong. My Uncle Tommy and Aunt Joy, who stepped in
when grief tried to bury us. My parents, who did the best they could
with what little they had.

Second, to show you that where you start does not decide where you
finish. I started in a mud hut. I am writing this from a studio in
Nairobi. That is not luck. That is noise converted into purpose.

Third, to prove that noise, when used correctly, changes everything. Not
for you alone, but for everyone who hears it.

If you have ever been told you are too broke, too loud, too much, or not
enough, this book is for you. If you have ever felt invisible, this book
is your mirror. If you have ever wondered whether your voice matters,
let me save you some time: it does.

Let me show you how I learned that lesson. Let us get loud.

Eugine Micah

Nairobi, Kenya

December 2025

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

**TABLE OF CONTENTS**

**PROLOGUE: Lights Before Dust**

**PART I: DUST DON\'T DIE EASY (2001-2008)**

1.  I Showed Up Late to My Own Birth

2.  The Stench of Dung and the Sound of Cane

3.  Kitchen Wars and Molten Plastic

4.  God, Mosquitoes, and Grandma\'s Rules

5.  The Grandfather Who Named a Place

6.  Fifteen Kilometers to Nowhere

7.  The Library That Did Not Judge My Grammar

8.  Dung Day and the Cane Economy

9.  The Boy Who Read to Chickens

10. When Silence Became Survival

**PART II: EDUCATION IS A CONTACT SPORT (2009-2015)**

11. The Move to Lumakanda

12. Friends Church and the Gospel of Discipline

13. Zarembka\'s Library of Peace

14. The Scouts, the Songs, and the Spine

15. KCPE: Kenya\'s National Anxiety Festival

16. The Borehole Call

17. Nairobi Noise: My First City Shock

18. Back to Lugari: The Return

**PART III: BOARDING SCHOOL BATTLES (2016-2019)**

19. Welcome to Murgusi: The Dorm That Taught Discipline

20. Faith vs. Funk

21. Entertainment Captain Without Applause

22. The Boy Who Debated Soldiers

23. KCSE and the Curse of Expectations

24. The Year My Parents Quit Fighting (Finally)

25. Law or Journalism? The Day Destiny Called

**PART IV: THE MAKING OF ROYLANDZ (2020-2024)**

26. Escape to Thika: My First Real Freedom

27. When Roylandz Was Born

28. The Overview Show: Filming on Faith

29. TIBS Got Talent and the Campus King Era

30. Citizen TV: Baptism by Broadcast

31. The Valedictorian Nobody Saw Coming

32. When COVID Took My Grandparents

33. Uncle Tommy and Aunt Joy: The Angels Who Stayed

**PART V: LIGHTS, MICS & MISTAKES (2024-PRESENT)**

34. PPP TV: The Day I Went National

35. Urban News and the Death of Roylandz

36. 700K Subscribers and 20 Million Views

37. The Fame That Did Not Fix Me

38. Burnout in Designer Sneakers

39. Faith in HD: Prayers Between Takes

**PART VI: FULL CIRCLE**

40. Back to Lumakanda: The Dust Still Knew My Name

41. Dear Younger Me: Sorry I Doubted You

42. The Digital Kemoview

43. Noise with Purpose

44. Born Broke, Built Loud

**EPILOGUE:** When the Lights Go Off

**ACKNOWLEDGMENTS**

**ROYLANDZ RULES:** The Complete Collection

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# PROLOGUE

## LIGHTS BEFORE DUST

The studio smells like coffee, nerves, and ambition mixed together in a
way that makes your stomach flip if you think about it too long. The air
conditioning hums at a frequency matching my heartbeat. Or my heartbeat
has started to match the hum. I cannot tell anymore.

PPP TV\'s red tally light stares straight at me like a judge waiting for
a verdict. The light sits mounted on Camera Two, the one pointed
straight at my face. The one broadcasting this moment to hundreds of
thousands of people across Kenya and beyond. The one that will not
forgive a stutter, a slip, or a second of silence lasting too long.

Lucy Ogunde, my co-host, sits to my left. She adjusts her microphone
with the practiced ease of someone who has done this a thousand times.
She is good at this. The kind of good that makes you want to be better
by sitting next to her. She glances at me and gives me that look. You
know the one. The look saying, \"Do not mess this up. We go live in ten
seconds.\"

I nod. My throat feels dry even though I drank water two minutes ago.
The makeup artist dabbed powder on my forehead thirty seconds before we
went to air. The lights burn hot. Hotter than they look. Hotter than you
think they should burn.

The floor manager raises his hand. His fingers count down. Three. Two.
One.

The light goes live.

And right there, under the heat of those LED panels costing more than my
first year of school fees, I do not see the teleprompter scrolling in
front of me. I do not see the camera lens staring back. I do not even
see Lucy, who has already started her opening line with the kind of
confidence making this look easy.

I see something else entirely.

A kerosene lamp.

Flickering in a mud-walled room in Manyonyi, Kakamega County. Same
orange glow. Same heat rising off the flame. Different century.
Different world.

That lamp lit my homework when we had any to do. The lamp lit my dinner
plate when we had food to eat. The lamp lit my dreams when I dared to
dream at all. The lamp was the first spotlight I ever knew. The first
light making me visible in the dark.

My first audience? A chicken wandering into our compound and refusing to
leave. My brother Danson, too young to know he should walk away. And the
walls. Mud walls absorbing every word I said because they had nothing
better to do and nowhere else to be.

People always ask me the same question. They ask at events. They ask
during interviews. They ask when they stop me on the street and want to
know how a guy like me ended up here.

\"Eugine, when did you know you wanted to be on TV?\"

Wrong question.

The right question, the one nobody ever asks because the answer is
harder, is this: \"When did you realize your voice was the only currency
you had?\"

Answer: The day my parents left.

I was in Class 1 at Mahemas Primary School. Six or seven years old. My
brother was even younger. Barely walking steady. My father came home one
evening, and I remember the way the light hit his face. He looked tired.
Not sleepy tired. Defeated tired. The kind of tired aging you five years
in five minutes.

He said, \"Eugine, I need you to take care of your brother. I am going
away for a while.\"

I did not ask where. I did not ask why. You do not ask those kinds of
questions when you are seven and your father\'s eyes look like that.

He did not come back for five years.

Neither did my mother.

And like that, I became a parent at seven years old. No manual. No
money. No mercy. No safety net. No backup plan. No phone calls to ask
for help. Me, Danson, and the noise I made to keep us both from drowning
in the silence wanting to swallow us whole.

The noise became my survival mechanism before I knew what a mechanism
was. The noise became my identity before I knew what identity meant. The
noise became my brand before I knew what a brand was.

The noise became \"Roylandz\" before I became Eugine Micah again.

The noise became Urban News. Campus Xposure. The Nairobi Podcast. Seven
hundred thousand subscribers. Twenty million monthly views. Interviews
with politicians, musicians, influencers. Stages I never imagined
standing on. Rooms I never thought I would enter.

But before all of that, before the cameras, before the stages, before
the followers and views and validation, there was a boy in the dark.
Talking to himself because talking felt safer than thinking. Talking
because silence felt like death. Talking because noise was proof he was
still alive.

The floor manager signals. His hand drops. The red light blinks off.

We are off air.

Lucy turns to me, pulling her earpiece out. \"You good?\"

I nod. \"Yeah. I am good.\"

But I am not thinking about the show we finished. I am not thinking
about the segment we nailed or the one we could have done better. I am
thinking about the show never ending. The one starting in a mud hut with
a kerosene lamp and a dream I did not even know I was allowed to have.

The one where a kid from Manyonyi decides poverty is not permanent.
Silence is not safety. Noise, when aimed correctly, when fired with
precision, when loaded with truth, moves mountains.

This is that story. Not the highlight reel you see on Instagram. Not the
polished version you see in interviews. The whole film. The raw cut. The
director\'s version with all the scenes they told me to delete.

The mud. The laughter. The hunger. The hustle. The faith keeping us
standing. The failure teaching us to fall better. The comeback nobody
saw coming.

Born broke. Built loud.

Let us go.

*\"Roylandz Rule #1: The world listens louder when you speak from scars,
not ego.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

**PART I**

**DUST DON\'T DIE EASY**

**(2001-2008)**

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 1

## I SHOWED UP LATE TO MY OWN BIRTH

December 24, 2001. Russia Hospital, Kisumu, Kenya.

I arrived on Christmas Eve, which sounds poetic and symbolic until you
realize the hospital was understaffed and nobody cared about the timing.
No bonus points for being born on a holiday. No extra blankets for
showing up when the nurses wanted to be home with their families.

My mother, Beverlyne Kangazi, gave birth to me without painkillers.
Without monitors. Without a bassinet or crib or any of the things you
see in movies when babies are born. Raw nature doing what it does, a
nurse checking in every twenty minutes, and prayers whispered between
contractions.

The hospital is now called Jaramogi Oginga Odinga Teaching and Referral
Hospital. A long name for a place saving your life if you are lucky and
letting you die if you are not. Back then, the place was Russia
Hospital. Not Russia the country, in case you are wondering. Kenya\'s
version of naming things after Cold War superpowers for reasons nobody
remembers anymore.

The walls were old when my mother arrived. The beds were older. The air
inside smelled like Dettol antiseptic mixed with sweat and desperation.
That smell is universal in Kenyan public hospitals. You know the smell
the second you walk in. The smell of people waiting. Waiting for
doctors. Waiting for medicine. Waiting for miracles insurance will not
cover.

My mother wrapped me in a jacket when I was born. Not a soft baby
blanket with animals or clouds printed on the fabric. A jacket. An adult
jacket meant for grown people. She zipped me up tight and left my face
sticking out like a confused turtle trying to figure out where it
landed.

The jacket saw more action than most of my clothes for the next five
years. The jacket was my blanket. My security. My first piece of
furniture. When you are born with nothing, everything becomes something.

My father, Joab Etale, was not there for my birth. Not because he did
not care. Because he did not know. He was back in Lugari, in our
village, wondering why his pregnant wife had vanished without warning or
explanation.

You do not gloss over that detail. You do not explain away something
like that with a simple sentence. So let me tell you how I ended up
being born in Kisumu instead of Kakamega, and why my father fainted when
he found out.

Here is what happened.

My father\'s sister, my Aunt Joy, had a daughter named Velma. Velma was
living with her biological father because Aunt Joy and her husband had
separated. Divorce was not common back then in our community. Separation
was messy. Separation meant shame, gossip, and complications lasting
years.

Aunt Joy missed her daughter. Desperately. The kind of longing keeping
you up at night and making every day feel incomplete. She wanted time
with Velma. She wanted to hold her. To see her smile. To hear her voice.
To be her mother again, even if for a little while.

So my mother, Beverlyne, helped her sister do something drastic.

They took Velma.

Not in a violent way. Not in a scary, criminal way. But in a
desperate-mother-trying-to-see-her-baby way existing in a legal gray
zone where love and law collide.

Here is how it went down.

My mother traveled to the area where Velma was staying with relatives
near a river. She met some of the family members. They were familiar
faces. People who knew her. People who trusted her. She smiled. She
chatted. She acted casual.

Then she said, \"Let me take Velma for a walk. I want to talk to her.\"

They agreed. Why would they not? She was family.

My mother grabbed Velma\'s hand, walked out of sight, and did not stop
walking. She came back home. Changed Velma\'s clothes. Washed her face.
Made her look like they were going on a normal trip.

Then they got on a boda boda, a bicycle taxi, and rode to Lumakanda
town. From there, they caught a matatu heading to Kisumu. No phones back
then to coordinate. No GPS to track. Faith and adrenaline and a mother
determined to reunite another mother with her child.

The journey from Lumakanda to Kisumu is not short. You pass through
Kakamega town, through winding roads, through small trading centers
where vendors sell roasted maize and boiled eggs. The matatu would have
been packed. People sitting on top of each other. Luggage tied to the
roof. Chickens in bags. Babies crying. The smell of sweat and exhaust
fumes and life moving too fast to pause.

My mother sat there, nine months pregnant, holding a child not hers,
praying nobody stopped them before they reached Kisumu.

They made it.

Aunt Joy was working as a nurse in a clinic in Kisumu. My mother
delivered Velma safely. Mission accomplished.

Except for one problem.

My mother was nine months pregnant. With me.

When my father found out my mother had left, pregnant, unannounced,
carrying out a semi-covert operation involving a child who legally was
not supposed to be with them, he fainted.

Not metaphorically. Not \"I felt faint.\" The man passed out cold. Hit
the ground. Had to be revived.

No cell phones back then. No way to call and check if she was okay. No
way to track her location. No way to know if she had been arrested, if
she had gone into labor on the road, if she was safe, if she was alive.

He knew she was gone.

And then, a few days later, a message came through someone who knew
someone who had access to a phone.

\"Beverlyne had the baby. It is a boy.\"

That is how I entered the world. Through chaos. Through rebellion.
Through love disguised as risk. Through a mother brave enough, or crazy
enough, to pull off a rescue mission while nine months pregnant.

If that does not set the tone for my entire life, I do not know what
does.

My mother told me this story when I was old enough to understand. She
told it with pride, not regret. She told it like a war story, because in
a way, it was.

She also told me that when I was born, she had nothing.

Not a single coin in her pocket. Not a plan for what came next. Not even
proper food.

She survived on what she called \"strong tea.\" Not the fancy kind with
milk and sugar you serve guests. Tea leaves boiled in water with a
little sugar if she was lucky. That was breakfast, lunch, and dinner
some days.

Occasionally, my Aunt Joy brought her mandazis. Fried dough, golden and
warm, the kind filling your stomach for an hour and making you forget
you are hungry. But most days, there was tea. Strong tea. The kind
staining your teeth and reminding you survival does not always taste
good.

The hospital fees? My aunt paid them. My mother could not. My father did
not even know where we were yet.

So my mother sat there in that hospital room, holding me wrapped in a
jacket, wondering what kind of life she could give me. She did not have
an answer. But she knew one thing for certain.

I was here. I had arrived. I was loud. I was alive.

And that had to mean something.

People ask me all the time, \"Eugine, how did you stay positive growing
up poor?\"

I did not.

I stayed loud.

There is a difference, and the difference matters.

Positive means you believe everything will work out in the end. You
trust the process. You smile through the pain. You hold onto hope even
when hope feels like a lie.

Loud means you refuse to let anyone forget you exist. You make noise.
You demand attention. You scream if you have to. You talk when everyone
else is silent. You show up even when you are not invited.

I was not born positive. I was born loud.

On Christmas Eve. In a hospital with no blankets. To a mother who had
pulled off a heist and a father who fainted from the stress.

And I have been making noise ever since.

Because when you are born with nothing, noise is the only currency
costing nothing and paying everything.

*\"Roylandz Rule #2: Do not confuse a late start with a small
destiny.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 2

## THE STENCH OF DUNG AND THE SOUND OF CANE

If poverty had a smell, ours was cow dung.

Not any cow dung. The wet, warm kind. Fresh from the animal. Mixed with
water until it became paste. Then slapped onto cold ground and spread
across the floor with bare hands by whoever was assigned the job that
week.

Usually me.

That was the first thing you noticed walking into our home. Not the
furniture, because we barely had any. Not the decorations, because those
were for rich people. The smell. Deep. Earthy. Organic in a way making
city people wrinkle their noses but felt normal to us.

We did not have tiles. We did not have concrete. We did not have carpet
or laminate or any of the things you see in magazines or on TV shows.

We had earth. Red Kakamega soil. And cow dung was our polish, our
disinfectant, our floor treatment, and our weekly ritual all rolled into
one.

Every Friday, without fail, we would collect fresh dung from the cows.
Mix it with water in a basin. Kneel down. Dip our hands in. And smear it
across the floor in smooth, circular motions until the entire surface
was covered.

The dung dried hard. The dung kept the dust down. The dung made the
house smell like a farm, which the house was.

And if you complained about it, you got a lecture about gratitude and a
reminder that some people did not even have floors at all.

We lived in Kakamega County, Western Kenya. Specifically, a place called
Manyonyi Sub-location. If you Google the place now, you will find a
handful of references, some photos of green hills and red roads, a
church or a school. If you visited, you would see maize fields
stretching for miles, sugarcane farms owned by people slightly less poor
than us, and mud huts dotting the landscape like forgotten monuments to
survival.

Manyonyi was not a destination. Manyonyi was not a place people moved to
for opportunity. Manyonyi was a place you were born into, and if you
were lucky or smart or stubborn enough, a place you eventually left.

But back then, Manyonyi was home.

Our house was a grass-thatched mud hut with four rooms. That is it. Four
rooms to contain our entire existence.

One room was for my parents. A thin mattress on a wooden bed frame my
father built himself. Sheets washed so many times the color had faded
into a universal gray-brown. A blanket that was more holes than fabric
but still got used every single night because cold does not care if your
blanket is falling apart.

One room was for me and Danson. We slept on banana leaves at first.
Dried banana leaves spread across the floor, covered with a thin cloth
if we were lucky. Later, we got a small mattress. Used. Squeaky. The
kind groaning every time you shifted positions and sounding like it was
filing a complaint with management.

One room was the kitchen. Three stones arranged in a triangle to hold
the sufurias, metal cooking pots blackened from years of fire and smoke.
Firewood stacked in the corner. A wooden spoon my mother used for
stirring ugali. A small shelf where we kept salt, sugar when we had it,
and tea leaves.

The fourth room was where furniture went to die. Broken chairs. Old
buckets. Tools my father used for farming. Bags of maize when the
harvest was good. Empty space when the harvest was not.

The doors were wooden slabs. Heavy. Unreliable. The kind swelling when
it rained and refusing to close properly, then shrinking when dry and
leaving gaps letting mosquitoes throw parties in our house all night
long.

The windows were not windows in the traditional sense. They were holes.
Rectangular gaps in the mud walls. No glass. No screens. Open space
where air, light, insects, and occasionally small animals could pass
freely.

At night, we would stuff the windows with old clothes or sacks to keep
out the cold and the bugs. During the day, we would remove them to let
light in because we did not have electricity.

That is how we lived. That is what home looked like.

And if you are reading this from a place with running water,
electricity, and walls not dissolving when it rains too hard, I need you
to understand something.

We did not think we were suffering.

We thought this was normal.

Because when everyone around you lives the same way, poverty does not
feel like poverty. Poverty feels like life.

Our bed situation deserves its own paragraph because it was ridiculous.

Before we got the squeaky mattress, Danson and I slept on dried banana
leaves. Not as a camping adventure. Not as a cultural experience. As our
bed.

You take banana leaves, let them dry in the sun until they turn brown
and crispy, then layer them on the floor. They crinkle when you move.
They smell like earth and grass. They provide zero cushioning between
your body and the hard ground.

But they were free. And free was all we could afford.

Eventually, my parents got us a mattress. Thin. Stained. The kind you
would see on the side of the road in Nairobi and wonder who threw it
away and why.

But to us? That mattress was a luxury upgrade. A presidential suite. A
sign things were getting better.

The mattress made noise. The kind of noise sounding like a drunk rat
laughing every single time someone rolled over or shifted position. We
named the sounds. We made jokes about which spring was singing tonight.
We turned suffering into entertainment because that is what you do when
you cannot afford to be sad.

My parents had a mattress too. Slightly better than ours, but not by
much. Same squeaking problem. Same worn-out situation. But the mattress
was theirs, and they protected it like it was made of gold.

The kitchen was another universe entirely.

If hell had a training facility, it would look like our kitchen.

Three stones. That is it. Three stones arranged in a triangle on the
ground. You would place the sufurias on top and build a fire underneath
using firewood, dry maize stalks, or anything else that would burn.

The smoke was intense. Thick. The kind making your eyes water and your
lungs scream for mercy. If you stood in the wrong spot, you would cough
so hard you would think you were dying. If you inhaled at the wrong
moment, you would spend the next ten minutes bent over, gasping,
wondering why breathing suddenly became a combat sport.

But there was love in that smoke.

That fire meant food. That fire meant warmth on cold nights. That fire
meant my mother was cooking, which meant we might eat today.

The sufurias themselves were battle-scarred veterans. Dented. Blackened
with soot so thick you could not see the original metal. Each one had a
job.

The biggest one was for ugali. Always. Ugali is a thick maize porridge
forming the backbone of Kenyan meals. You eat it with your hands, dip it
in vegetables or stew if you are lucky, and it fills your stomach like
cement. Cheap. Dense. Reliable.

The ugali sufuria always had a however in the bottom. Always. I do not
know if it was the same hole getting worse or if holes appeared
naturally in sufurias through some kind of cosmic spite, but there was
always a hole.

And we had a hack for it.

Before you add water, you sprinkle dry maize flour at the bottom of the
pot. The flour mixes with the first bit of water and creates a seal. A
temporary plug. The plug does not last forever, but it lasts long enough
to cook the ugali.

Magic. Passed down by generations of mothers who did not have time for
nonsense and could not afford new pots.

The smaller sufurias were for vegetables, tea, or the occasional stew if
we had meat, which was almost never.

Evenings in that kitchen were special.

Not special in a Hallmark movie kind of way. Special in a
survival-becomes-routine kind of way.

We would sit on wooden stools my father carved himself. Three-legged
stools wobbling but never quite falling over. Sometimes we would sit on
the floor if the stools were occupied or if we were feeling adventurous.

But you had to be careful.

Safari ants.

If you have never encountered safari ants, let me educate you. They are
small, red, aggressive, and they bite like they have personal vendettas.
They move in colonies. Long lines of tiny soldiers marching across
floors, up walls, over anything in their path.

And they do not give warnings.

You would be sitting there, minding your business, listening to a story,
and suddenly, pain. Sharp. Hot. Like someone pressed a lit match against
your skin.

You would jump up, slap at your leg, and find three or four ants
attached, jaws locked in, refusing to let go even when you flicked them
off.

We learned to check the floor before sitting. We learned to keep our
feet moving. We learned that stillness in that kitchen was an invitation
to suffering.

My mother would sit near the fire, stirring ugali with a wooden spoon
almost as old as she was. The firelight would dance across her face,
making her look younger some moments and older the next.

She would tell us stories while she cooked.

Stories about her childhood. Stories about relatives we had never met.
Stories about ghosts following children who cried too much. Stories
about prophecies village elders made and never came true.

Sometimes the stories were funny. Sometimes they were meant to scare us
into good behavior. Sometimes they were noise to fill the silence,
because silence in poverty feels too much like death.

We would listen, half-focused, stomachs growling, waiting for the ugali
to finish cooking so we could eat.

And in those moments, sitting on wobbly stools, dodging safari ants,
breathing smoke, listening to stories by firelight, we were a family.

Not a perfect family. Not a wealthy family. Not a family anyone would
make a TV show about.

But a family nonetheless.

One evening, my brother Danson decided to test the laws of physics and
common sense simultaneously.

He thought sitting on a hot sufuria being used as a lid for another pot
would be a good idea.

I do not know what went through his mind. I do not know if he thought it
was cool. I do not know if he was being a kid who did not understand
consequences yet.

What I do know is he sat down on that hot metal pot and immediately
screamed like a goat being dragged to slaughter.

The sound was horrific. High-pitched. Terrified. The kind of scream
making everyone in a three-house radius stop what they are doing and
come running.

He jumped up, clutching his behind, tears streaming down his face,
hopping on one foot like he was trying to outrun the pain.

The sufuria had burned two perfect circles into his skin. Black patches.
The kind not healing fast. The kind leaving scars for years.

He walked like a crab for a week. Sideways. Slowly. Sitting was
impossible. Lying down was a nightmare. Standing was the only option,
and even that hurt.

We teased him mercilessly. Because that is what siblings do. That is how
love works when you are poor. You laugh at each other\'s pain because
crying about it will not change anything.

Those two black patches stayed on his behind for years. Permanent
reminders. A cautionary tale tattooed by stupidity and hot metal.

To this day, I still make him laugh by bringing it up.

Another time, it was my turn to learn a painful lesson about fire and
curiosity.

I was young. Six or seven. And I had a question most reasonable people
never ask.

How does plastic burn?

Most people would accept that plastic burns and move on with their
lives. But I needed to know. I needed to see it happen. I needed to
understand the process.

So I took a bread wrapper, thin, clear plastic, and wrapped it around my
fingers. Then I dipped my hand into the fire.

The plastic melted instantly. Like sugar. Like wax. It stuck to my skin,
burning and fusing with my flesh in a way my brain could not process
fast enough to stop.

I panicked.

I pulled my hand out and tried to wipe the molten plastic off. But
instead of wiping it off, I smeared it across my face.

Like that, I invented my own brand of face cream. Molten plastic face
cream. Patent pending.

The pain was immediate and intense. My mother screamed. I screamed.
Danson watched in horror, probably filing the moment away as future
ammunition for teasing.

The scar is still there. On my face. A permanent reminder of the day I
decided to play scientist with fire.

My badge of stupidity. My proof that curiosity without wisdom is
expensive.

This is the part where I am supposed to tell you life was unbearable.
That we were miserable. That every day was a fight for survival and hope
was a luxury we could not afford.

But that would be a lie.

Life was not unbearable. Life was life.

We laughed. We fought. We played games with rocks and sticks. We
invented entertainment out of thin air because we did not have TV or
tablets or any of the things modern kids use to kill time.

We survived. And survival, when you are doing it with people you love,
does not feel like suffering. Survival feels like living.

Somewhere in all that smoke and dung and noise, in that kitchen with
three stones and broken pots, in that house with mud walls and holes for
windows, I learned the most important lesson of my childhood.

If you find humor in hunger, you survive anything.

Because laughter is free. Hope is free. Noise is free.

And when you are broke, free is all you need.

*\"Roylandz Rule #3: When life gives you cow dung, make it your
foundation.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# 

# CHAPTER 3

## KITCHEN WARS AND MOLTEN PLASTIC

Our kitchen was not a room. The kitchen was a war zone, a theater, a
laboratory, and occasionally a torture chamber all rolled into one
smoke-filled space.

Every single day, something happened in that kitchen that would make
health and safety inspectors in any developed country shut us down
immediately and call the police.

But this was rural Kenya in the early 2000s. Health and safety
inspectors did not exist in Manyonyi. The police were hours away and
busy with bigger problems. And nobody cared if your cooking space
doubled as a death trap as long as food came out of it at the end.

Let me paint you a picture of a typical evening.

The sun would start setting around 6:30 PM, painting the sky in shades
of orange and pink looking beautiful until you remembered you had no
electricity and sunset meant darkness was coming fast.

My mother would send me or Danson to collect firewood. Not from a neat
pile in the backyard. From wherever we could find it. Dry maize stalks
from the farm. Branches that had fallen from trees. Sometimes twigs so
small they barely qualified as firewood but we would use them anyway
because beggars cannot be choosers.

We would stack the firewood near the three stones. My mother would
arrange the stones, place the sufuria on top, add water, and then came
the hardest part.

Starting the fire.

If you have ever tried to start a fire with damp wood, no matches
working properly, and a whole family waiting to eat, you understand
stress in a way most people never will.

My mother would tear up pieces of old newspaper or cardboard, ball them
up, place them under the stones, add tiny twigs on top, then strike a
match.

Sometimes it worked on the first try. The paper would catch, the twigs
would ignite, and within minutes we would have a decent fire going.

Sometimes it did not work at all. The match would fizzle out. The paper
would smoke but not burn. The twigs would be too damp. And we would have
to start over. Again. And again.

I remember evenings where it took thirty minutes to get the fire
started. Thirty minutes of frustration, failed matches, smoke without
flames, and mounting hunger.

But eventually, the fire would catch. The flames would grow. The sufuria
would heat up. The water would boil.

And then the real battle began.

Cooking ugali is not a gentle process. Cooking ugali is aggressive.
Physical. You have to commit.

You add maize flour to boiling water slowly, stirring constantly with a
wooden spoon. The mixture thickens fast. The mixture becomes heavy. Your
arm starts to hurt. The steam burns your face. The smoke makes you
cough.

But you cannot stop stirring. If you stop, the ugali burns at the bottom
and ruins the whole pot. If you stir too slowly, lumps form and the
texture is wrong. If you add flour too fast, it clumps and becomes
impossible to mix.

Cooking ugali is a dance. A rhythm. A skill taking years to master.

My mother was a master. She would stir with one hand, adjust the fire
with the other, dodge the smoke with her head, and somehow manage to
watch me and Danson at the same time to make sure we were not doing
something stupid.

Which, let us be honest, we usually were.

One of my earliest kitchen memories involves Danson and a wooden spoon
and what I can only describe as a terrible decision made at high speed.

We were four and six years old. My mother was cooking. The fire was
going strong. The sufuria was bubbling. The kitchen smelled like smoke
and maize and home.

Danson, for reasons I still do not understand, decided he wanted to
help. He reached for the wooden spoon while my mother\'s back was
turned.

\"Danson, no!\" I remember yelling. Not because I was trying to protect
him. Because I wanted to stir the ugali myself and he was stealing my
opportunity.

He grabbed the spoon. Tried to stir. The thick ugali resisted. The spoon
slipped. His hand dipped into the pot.

The scream coming out of him was something between a fire alarm and a
wounded animal.

My mother spun around, grabbed his hand, pulled it out, and immediately
dunked it into a basin of cold water we kept nearby for emergencies.

His palm was red. Blistered. The skin was already starting to bubble.

He cried for an hour. The kind of deep, inconsolable crying coming from
real pain, not the performative crying kids do when they want attention.

My mother wrapped his hand in a cloth soaked in cold water and held him
until he calmed down.

The blister lasted for weeks. A permanent reminder printed on his palm
for years afterward.

And the lesson? Simple: Hot ugali does not care about your curiosity.
Hot ugali will burn you and keep cooking.

The kitchen also had another hazard nobody talks about when they
romanticize rural African childhoods.

The ceiling.

Our kitchen had a thatched roof. Grass and sticks woven together. The
roof kept the rain out. Most of the time. The roof kept the sun out.
Always.

But the roof also kept the smoke in.

Because we did not have a chimney. We did not have ventilation. We had
holes in the walls and a roof trapping smoke like a greenhouse traps
heat.

Over time, the smoke stained the ceiling black. Thick layers of soot
built up on the thatch. And sometimes, when the fire was particularly
hot or the smoke particularly thick, pieces of soot would fall.

Right into the food.

You would be sitting there, waiting for dinner, and a black flake would
drift down from the ceiling and land in your ugali.

You had two choices. Pick it out and keep eating. Or go hungry.

We always picked it out and kept eating.

Because hunger does not care about hygiene. And when you are poor, you
learn to make peace with imperfection fast.

But here is the thing about that kitchen, despite all the chaos and
danger and smoke.

The kitchen was where we became a family.

Not a perfect family. Not a TV family. But a real one.

We would sit together on those wobbly stools, dodging falling soot,
breathing smoke, eating ugali with our hands, dipping it in vegetables
or stew when we had it, plain when we did not.

And we would talk. We would laugh. We would argue about whose turn it
was to wash the sufurias afterward. We would make plans for tomorrow
that never happened. We would tell stories about things we wanted to do
someday.

My mother would ask about school. My father, when he was home, would
tell us about his day in the fields or at work in the mines. Danson and
I would compete to see who could eat faster or who could make the
funniest face.

It sounds simple because it was simple. Poverty strips life down to
basics. You do not have fancy meals or elegant dining rooms or perfectly
set tables.

You have three stones, a fire, and the people you love.

And some days, that is more than enough.

*\"Roylandz Rule #4: If your kitchen does not occasionally try to kill
you, you are not cooking with enough passion.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 4

## GOD, MOSQUITOES, AND GRANDMA\'S RULES

Night in Manyonyi came with an army.

Not soldiers. Mosquitoes.

Thousands of them. Millions, probably. They would swarm at dusk like
they had been waiting all day for permission to attack. And once
darkness fell, they owned the night.

We did not have mosquito nets. Not the fancy kind you see in NGO
commercials where happy children sleep peacefully under white netting
while soft music plays in the background.

We had nothing.

Our skin. Our blood. And the hope that tonight they would be merciful.

They never were.

I would lie on that thin mattress next to Danson, and I would hear them
before I felt them. That high-pitched whine. The sound of tiny wings
beating at frequencies designed to drive you insane.

You would slap at the air. You would pull the blanket over your head
even though it was too hot to breathe under there. You would pray they
would find someone else to bite.

But they always found you.

By morning, you would wake up covered in bites. Red welts all over your
arms, legs, face, neck. Everywhere. Some of them would itch so badly you
would scratch until you bled.

And the crazy part? You got used to it.

That is what poverty does. Poverty normalizes suffering. Poverty makes
you accept things that should be unacceptable. Poverty teaches you that
discomfort is part of life and complaining about it will not change
anything.

So you stop complaining. You scratch your bites. You move on.

Malaria was always a concern. Every time you got a fever, every time
your body ached, every time you felt weak, the first thought was always:
Is it malaria?

Sometimes it was. Sometimes it was not. But you never knew until it got
bad enough that someone had to walk hours to get medicine or take you to
a clinic that might or might not have the drugs you needed.

I had malaria more times than I can count. So did Danson. So did almost
every kid in the village.

Malaria was part of growing up in Western Kenya. Like learning to walk
or learning to talk. You learned to survive malaria or you did not
survive at all.

The fever would hit you first. Your body would burn like you had been
thrown into a fire. Then came the chills. You would shake so hard your
teeth would rattle. Hot one minute. Cold the next. Your body could not
decide whether to cook you or freeze you, so it tried both.

The headaches were the worst. A pressure behind your eyes making you
want to close them and never open them again. Every sound was too loud.
Every light was too bright. Moving hurt. Breathing hurt. Existing hurt.

My mother would wrap me in blankets when the chills came. She would make
me drink strong tea with lemon when we had lemons. She would pray over
me with words I did not understand but felt in my bones.

And eventually, after days of suffering, the fever would break. You
would wake up weak, drenched in sweat, but alive.

Then you would start the process of rebuilding your strength until the
next time malaria decided to visit.

But mosquitoes were not the only thing we battled at night.

There was also God.

Not in a metaphorical sense. In a literal, structured, enforced,
you-will-participate-or-face-consequences sense.

My grandparents, Philemona and Rosemary Obbayi, were deeply religious.
Church of God Manyonyi was our spiritual home. And by \"our,\" I mean
they decided it was our spiritual home and we had no say in the matter.

Sundays were non-negotiable.

You went to church. No exceptions. No excuses. Sick? Go to church.
Tired? Go to church. Would rather be literally anywhere else? Too bad.
Go to church.

The church building itself was nothing fancy. Wooden benches worn smooth
from decades of bodies sitting, standing, kneeling. A corrugated iron
roof turning the place into an oven when the sun was high. Walls letting
in drafts and dust and occasionally chickens wandering in during service
like they were looking for salvation too.

The services were long. Hours long. The kind of long making you question
your faith because your legs are falling asleep and you are wondering if
this sermon will ever end.

The preaching was fire-and-brimstone style. Loud. Passionate. The pastor
would shout, sweat pouring down his face, voice cracking from the
intensity.

He would talk about sin. About judgment. About hell waiting for those
who strayed from the path. About Jesus coming back any day now, so you
better be ready because He is not going to knock twice.

And the congregation would respond. \"Amen!\" they would shout.
\"Hallelujah!\" \"Preach, pastor!\" \"Tell them!\"

The call and response was constant. The pastor would make a point, and
the congregation would affirm it. Back and forth. A rhythm turning
preaching into performance.

The music was the best part. No instruments. Voices. Clapping. Stomping.
Harmonies rising and falling like waves crashing on a shore you had
never seen.

We would sing in Kiswahili. In Luhya. In English sometimes. Songs about
victory, about grace, about washing our sins away in the blood of the
lamb.

I did not understand half of what we were singing about. But I
understood the feeling. The energy. The way music made suffering feel
lighter. The way singing together made you feel less alone.

There was power in those songs. Not spiritual power alone, but physical
power too. The kind making your chest vibrate when everyone hit the same
note at the same time. The kind making you forget you were hungry or
tired or scared.

For those few minutes when the whole church was singing, nothing else
mattered. Not poverty. Not mosquitoes. Not the uncertainty of tomorrow.

The sound. The voices. The moment.

My Grandma Rosemary was not a church member alone. She was a Reverend. A
spiritual leader. A woman commanding respect and fear in equal measure.

When she spoke, people listened. When she prayed, people bowed their
heads. When she pointed at you and told you to stop misbehaving, you
stopped immediately or faced consequences making you regret every life
choice leading to that moment.

She was strict. Not mean. Strict. There is a difference.

Mean people hurt you for no reason. Strict people hurt you because they
love you and believe discipline is love in action.

Grandma Rosemary believed discipline was the only thing standing between
us and total chaos.

She had rules. Lots of rules. And breaking them was not an option.

-   Rule #1: Respect your elders. Always. Even if they are wrong. Even
    > if they are being unreasonable. You nod. You say \"yes, ma\'am\"
    > or \"yes, sir.\" You do what you are told.

-   Rule #2: No laziness. If you are awake, you are working. Sitting
    > around doing nothing is a sin punishable by being assigned more
    > chores.

-   Rule #3: Church attendance is mandatory. Miss a Sunday and you will
    > wish you were back dealing with mosquitoes instead.

-   Rule #4: No answering back. When an adult speaks to you, you listen.
    > You do not argue. You do not explain. You do not justify. You
    > accept correction and move on.

-   Rule #5: No complaining. Life is hard for everyone. Complaining
    > makes it harder. Shut up and keep moving.

These were not suggestions. These were commandments carved into the
fabric of our daily existence.

And breaking them came with swift justice.

Not abuse. Not violence. But correction. The kind of correction
involving a thin cane made from a flexible branch. The kind leaving
marks on your legs reminding you for days why obedience was important.

In 2000s rural Kenya, this was normal. This was how children were
raised. You did not get time-outs or gentle conversations about
feelings. You got discipline. Physical discipline. The kind making you
think twice before doing something stupid again.

I am not here to debate whether it was right or wrong. I am telling you
what it was.

And what it was, was effective.

Grandma Rosemary traveled to different churches most weekends. She would
preach at other Church of God congregations throughout Kakamega County.
Sometimes Danson and I would go with her.

My Uncle Christopher Lilako Opuka would drive us on his motorbike. Three
people on one bike. Grandma in the middle. Me or Danson squeezed in
behind. The other one left at home or taken on a second trip if we were
lucky.

Those rides were terrifying and exhilarating at the same time.

The roads were red murram, dirt mixed with clay turning slippery when it
rained and dusty when it did not. Full of potholes that could swallow a
tire if you hit them wrong. Full of curves appearing suddenly and
testing whether you believed in God or momentum.

Uncle Christopher drove fast. Not recklessly. Confidently. Like he had
made this journey a hundred times and knew every bump, every turn, every
spot where chickens liked to cross without warning.

I would hold on tight, watching the landscape blur past. Maize fields.
Banana plantations. Small shops with faded signs advertising soda and
airtime. Children walking barefoot on the side of the road, stopping to
wave as we passed.

When we would arrive at the church, Grandma would transform. The woman
who was strict and stern at home became powerful and magnetic in the
pulpit.

She would preach with authority. Her voice would fill the room. Her
hands would move through the air, emphasizing points, calling down
blessings, commanding attention.

People would cry. People would shout. People would come forward for
prayer, and she would lay hands on them, speaking words sounding like
they came from somewhere beyond this world.

I would watch from the front row, amazed that this was my grandmother.
This powerful woman making grown men weep and sick people believe they
could be healed.

And then we would get back on the motorbike and ride home, and she would
go back to being strict Grandma Rosemary who made sure we did our chores
and went to bed on time.

Faith was not optional in our house. Faith was oxygen. Faith was woven
into every meal, every conversation, every decision.

Before we ate, we prayed. Before we slept, we prayed. Before we left the
house, we prayed. Sometimes the prayers were short. Sometimes they were
long enough that your knees hurt from kneeling on the hard floor.

But you did not complain. Because complaining about praying was
complaining about God, and nobody wanted to find out what happened when
you complained about God in Grandma Rosemary\'s house.

The prayers were specific. Not vague \"bless this food\" prayers.
Detailed prayers covering everything from protection against evil
spirits to success in school to safe travels to financial provision to
healing for relatives whose names I did not even know.

My grandparents believed in a God who was actively involved in every
detail of life. A God who cared whether we did our homework. A God who
noticed when we lied. A God who kept score and would one day settle all
accounts.

That belief shaped everything. The belief gave them hope when hope
seemed irrational. The belief gave them strength when strength seemed
impossible. The belief gave them a reason to keep going when every
logical reason said to quit.

And whether I understood it or not, whether I believed it or not, that
faith kept us alive.

*\"Roylandz Rule #5: Faith without mosquito bites is theory. Faith with
mosquito bites is survival.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 5

## THE GRANDFATHER WHO NAMED A PLACE

There are two kinds of men in this world.

Men who inherit places. And men who build places.

My Grandpa Samson Kemoli built a place. Then named it after himself.
Then refused to apologize for either decision.

Kemoview.

Not a town. Not exactly. More like a cluster of shops, houses, and
dreams arranged along a dirt road in Mbaya, Lugari District. Named
\"Kemoview\" because Grandpa Samson\'s nickname was \"Kemo,\" and he had
a view of where he wanted his legacy to sit.

When people give you directions in that part of Kakamega County, they do
not say \"turn left at the junction.\" They say \"turn left at
Kemoview.\" Grandpa Samson made himself a landmark. Made himself
geography.

That is power.

Not the kind you inherit. The kind you build with your own hands when
nobody is watching and nobody believes it is possible.

Grandpa Samson Kemoli was a former police corporal. Ex-law enforcement.
The kind of man who had seen things he never talked about and decided
that retirement meant building something instead of enforcing something.

He had a limp. A bad leg from an injury during his police days. The
details were never fully explained to me as a child. All I knew was that
Grandpa walked with a slight drag in his step, a permanent reminder that
service comes with scars.

But that limp did not slow him down. If anything, it made him more
determined. More stubborn. More convinced that if his body would not
cooperate, his mind would compensate.

And compensate it did.

Grandpa Samson was not rich. Let us be clear about that. He was not
driving a Mercedes or living in a mansion. But he had something more
valuable than money in rural Kenya. He had shops. He had property. He
had a name people knew.

The shops in Kemoview sold everything. Maize flour. Sugar. Salt.
Kerosene. Soap. Matches. Blue Band margarine. Bread when the delivery
came. Soda in glass bottles you had to return for a deposit.

They were small shops. Wooden structures with iron sheet roofs. Shelves
made from rough timber. Counters worn smooth from years of transactions.
No cash registers. A metal box where money went in and rarely came out.

But they worked. They served the community. They kept Grandpa busy. They
gave him purpose after the police force was done with him.

I spent time at those shops when I was young. Watching. Learning.
Absorbing lessons that would not make sense until years later.

I would watch Grandpa interact with customers. Farmers coming in to buy
sugar after selling their maize. Women buying soap and gossiping about
neighbors. Children sent by their parents with exact change and specific
instructions written on scraps of paper.

Grandpa treated everyone the same. Rich or poor. Elder or child. Did not
matter. You came in. You said what you needed. He sold it to you or told
you he did not have it. No drama. No favoritism. Business.

But he remembered everything. Every face. Every debt. Every promise
made. His memory was better than any accounting book. He knew who owed
money, who paid on time, who needed grace, and who was taking advantage.

And he ran his shops with a simple philosophy: Respect goes both ways.
You respect me, I respect you. You cheat me, I remember forever.

Kemoview was not shops alone. Kemoview was also where Grandpa and
Grandma Josephine lived.

Their house was modest. Cement walls. Iron sheet roof. A few rooms. No
electricity then. No running water. But compared to Manyonyi, it felt
like progress.

Grandpa Samson had four cows. I remember herding them when I visited.
Taking them out to graze in the morning. Bringing them back in the
evening. Watching them chew grass with the kind of patience only animals
and poor people understand.

Herding cows is boring. Deeply, profoundly boring. You stand there. You
watch them eat. You make sure they do not wander too far. You throw
rocks at dogs getting too close. You wait.

Hours pass like days. Days pass like weeks. Time moves differently when
you are a kid watching cows.

But herding taught me patience. Herding taught me responsibility.
Herding taught me that sometimes the most important work is the work
looking like nothing at all.

Grandma Josephine was the heart of that house. Quiet where Grandpa was
loud. Gentle where he was firm. She cooked. She cleaned. She kept
everything running while Grandpa ran his empire of small shops and big
dreams.

She did not talk much. Not because she had nothing to say. Because she
knew the value of silence. Knew that sometimes watching and listening
teaches more than speaking ever could.

I remember her cooking on an open fire outside the house. Same three
stones. Same sufurias. Same smoke. But somehow her food tasted better.
Maybe because she cooked with patience I did not have yet. Maybe because
she added something you cannot buy in shops.

Love, probably. Or salt. Hard to tell the difference sometimes.

One memory stands out above all others when I think about Grandpa
Samson.

His wedding planning committee.

I do not remember whose wedding it was. Some relative. Some event
requiring organization and food and Grandpa\'s logistical mind.

There were mandazis. Dozens of them. Hundreds maybe. Golden fried dough
arranged on large trays, covered with cloth to keep flies away, waiting
to be served to guests.

I was young. Six. Seven. Old enough to know better but not old enough to
care.

I stole mandazis.

Not one. Not two. Multiple mandazis over multiple trips. I would sneak
into the area where they were stored, grab a warm one, shove it in my
mouth, and run.

The taste was incredible. Sweet. Oily. Still warm from frying. The kind
of food making poverty feel temporary because for those few seconds
while you are chewing, nothing else matters.

I got caught eventually. Of course I got caught. You cannot steal that
many mandazis without someone noticing the count does not match the
math.

Grandpa did not beat me. Did not yell. Did not make a scene.

He looked at me. That look. The look saying \"I am disappointed\" louder
than any words could. The look making you wish he would yell instead
because at least yelling ends.

Then he said, \"Next time, ask.\"

That was it. No lecture. No punishment. A reminder that honesty costs
less than theft.

I never stole mandazis again. Not because I was afraid. Because I did
not want to see that look again.

The borehole call happened years later. That is a story for another
chapter. But the foundation for that call was built here. In Kemoview.
In those shops. In those conversations where Grandpa taught me about
business without ever calling it business.

He taught me that a man\'s name is his currency. That reputation matters
more than money. That you can lose everything except your word, and if
you lose your word, you have lost more than everything.

He taught me that retirement does not mean stopping. Retirement means
redirecting. Taking everything you learned in one life and building
something new in the next.

He taught me that limping does not make you weak. Limping makes you
creative. Makes you find new ways to move forward when the old ways do
not work anymore.

Most importantly, he taught me that if you are going to build something,
build it so well that people name directions after it. Build it so solid
that when you are gone, it remains. Build it so the place remembers you
even when the people forget.

Kemoview still exists. You can go there today. The shops have changed.
Some closed. Some rebuilt. But the name remains.

Grandpa Samson Kemoli turned his nickname into a location. Turned his
life into a legacy. Turned his injury into industry.

And somewhere in all of that, he taught a young boy that where you start
does not determine where you finish.

Your limp does not define your destination.

Your name can become a place if you build something worth remembering.

*\"Roylandz Rule #6: Legacy is not what you leave behind. Legacy is what
you build while people are watching and doubting.\"*
