# CHAPTER 6

## FIFTEEN KILOMETERS TO NOWHERE

The walk to Mahemas Primary School was either five kilometers or fifteen
kilometers, depending on which adult you asked and how tired they were
of hearing you complain.

The truth was somewhere in the middle. Closer to ten kilometers on
average. But when you are walking barefoot on red murram roads at six in
the morning, distance becomes subjective. Distance is not about
kilometers. Distance is about how many times you step on thorns, how
many snakes you see, and whether it rains before you arrive.

We walked barefoot. Not by choice. Not as some character-building
exercise designed by parents wanting us to appreciate hardship. We
walked barefoot because shoes were expensive and feet were free.

My feet were tough. Calloused. The soles hardened from years of walking
on rough ground, hot stones, sharp grass. They looked like they belonged
to someone three times my age. Cracked. Dusty. Strong.

But tough does not mean invincible.

Thorns still hurt. Stones still cut. And when you stepped on something
sharp at full walking speed, the pain was instant and unforgettable.

The worst part was not the initial injury. The worst part was stopping
to remove whatever had stabbed you, knowing you were now behind
schedule, knowing you would have to walk faster to make up time, knowing
faster meant more chances to step on something else.

The roads were red. Not red like a tomato or a rose. Red like dried
blood mixed with clay. Red like the earth had been wounded and never
quite healed.

When it was dry, the dust would rise in clouds with every step. You
would breathe it in. Taste it. Feel it coat the inside of your throat.
By the time you reached school, you would cough red.

When it rained, the roads became something worse than mud. They became
slippery, sticky traps grabbing your feet and refusing to let go. You
would pull your foot out with a sucking sound, take another step, sink
again.

Walking in the rain meant arriving at school soaked. Uniform clinging to
your body. Shivering even though it was not cold. Sitting in class wet
for hours because there was nowhere to dry off and no spare clothes to
change into.

The journey started in darkness most mornings. We would leave the house
around 6 AM when the sky was still gray, the sun beginning to think
about rising.

Sometimes I walked alone. Sometimes with Danson. Sometimes with other
kids from nearby compounds whose parents also believed education was
worth ten kilometers of suffering.

We would walk in silence mostly. Conserving energy. Watching the ground
for hazards. Listening for sounds that did not belong. Dogs that might
attack, snakes that might strike, vehicles that might offer a ride if
the driver was generous.

The landscape was beautiful if you had time to notice. Rolling hills
covered in maize and sugarcane. Banana plantations. Small farms with
cows grazing. Birds singing songs sounding cheerful until you remembered
they had wings and you had feet.

But beauty does not make you less tired. Beauty does not make the
distance shorter or the destination closer.

We would pass other children walking to different schools. Primary
school uniforms in various states of repair. Some kids had shoes. Some
did not. Some carried bags. Some carried nothing but hope that today\'s
lessons would be worth the journey.

Nobody complained. Complaining was pointless. Everyone was walking.
Everyone was tired. Everyone had the same choice: keep moving or stay
home and face the consequences.

The consequences of staying home were worse than walking. Missing school
meant falling behind. Falling behind meant failing exams. Failing exams
meant disappointing parents, teachers, yourself. Meant wasting the
sacrifice your family made to pay school fees.

So we walked.

Snakes were a constant concern. Kenya has many snakes. Some harmless.
Some deadly. None interested in getting to know you better.

The most dangerous time was early morning when snakes were still warming
up from the cold night. They would lie across the path, soaking up
whatever heat the ground offered. You would be walking, focused on not
stepping on thorns, and suddenly, snake.

The key was to see it before you stepped on it. If you saw it first, you
could stop, back up slowly, find a stick to throw or take a wide detour.
If you stepped on it first, well, that is how some kids did not make it
to school.

I saw snakes dozens of times over the years. Green mambas in trees. Puff
adders sunbathing on rocks. Black mambas watching you with eyes saying
\"try me.\"

We learned which snakes to fear and which to avoid. Learned the
difference between a stick and a snake faster than any primary school
science class could teach. Learned that running from a snake was
sometimes smarter than standing still, even though adults said to stand
still.

Adults were not walking barefoot through snake territory every morning.
We were. And we trusted our fear more than their advice.

Mahemas Primary School appeared like a mirage after that walk. Brown
buildings with iron sheet roofs. A flagpole where we would gather for
morning assembly. A playground that was dirt and dreams.

The school had three hundred students. Eight classes. Class One through
Class Eight. One teacher per class if we were lucky. Two classes sharing
one teacher if we were not.

The classrooms were basic. Wooden desks. Wooden benches. A blackboard
that was black from years of chalk dust ground into the wood. Windows
with no glass, iron bars alone to keep kids from climbing out during
lessons.

We shared desks. Three to four students per desk designed for two. You
would squeeze in, hip to hip, fighting for space to write. Your exercise
book half on the desk, half on your lap. Elbows bumping. Someone always
complaining they did not have enough room.

But we made it work. Because complaining meant getting sent outside to
stand in the sun, which was worse than sharing a cramped desk.

The teachers were strict. Not cruel. Not abusive. Strict in the way
teachers had to be in rural Kenyan schools where discipline was measured
in fear and respect came from consequences.

Mr. Kipande was the headmaster. A tall man with a voice that could stop
a running child mid-stride. When he walked through the compound,
students scattered. Not because he was mean. Because he had standards
and no patience for children who wasted his time or their parents\'
money.

He carried a cane. Not as decoration. As a tool. A teaching aid. A
reminder that choices have consequences and some consequences come with
stripes on your legs.

Deputy Zipporah was his enforcer. She had a gift for spotting lies. You
could tell her your grandmother died and you needed to miss school, and
she would look at you with eyes seeing through excuses like they were
made of glass.

\"Your grandmother died last term too,\" she would say. \"How many
grandmothers do you have?\"

No answer to that. You would stand there, caught, knowing the cane was
coming.

Mr. Mujivane was the teacher I remember most. Not because he was kind.
Not because he was inspiring. Because he had the longest pinky
fingernail I had ever seen.

I am talking centimeters long. Curved. Yellow. It looked like a weapon.
And he used it as one.

When you did something wrong, Mr. Mujivane did not yell. He would walk
over calmly. Place that long pinky nail against your head. Press hard
enough that you could feel it digging into your scalp but not breaking
skin.

Then he would ask, \"Are you going to do that again?\"

You would shake your head carefully, trying not to move too much because
that nail was still pressed against your skull.

\"Good,\" he would say. \"Now sit down and do not make me use this
again.\"

Nobody knew why he grew that nail so long. Some kids said it was for
witchcraft. Some said it was for picking his nose when students were not
looking. Some said it was because he could.

Whatever the reason, it worked. Nobody messed with Mr. Mujivane. His
nail had a reputation preceding him into every classroom.

Despite everything, the walk, the cane, the cramped desks, the teachers
who ruled through fear, I loved school.

Not in a romantic way. Not like kids in movies who skip to class singing
songs about learning. I loved school in a practical way. I loved school
because school was my way out.

Out of poverty. Out of Manyonyi. Out of a future looking exactly like
the past.

I was good at school. Top of my class most terms. Not because I was
smarter than other kids. Because I understood something they did not
yet: education was currency. Education was investment. Education was the
only thing I had that nobody could take away.

You can lose money. You can lose property. You can lose everything you
own in a single bad season.

But knowledge? Knowledge stays. Knowledge compounds. Knowledge opens
doors staying closed to people who only have hands and feet.

So I studied. I paid attention. I memorized. I answered questions. I
stayed late when teachers offered extra lessons. I volunteered to read
aloud even though some kids laughed at my pronunciation.

I made education my job before I knew what a job was.

And slowly, education started paying dividends.

*\"Roylandz Rule #7: The longest journey teaches you more than the
shortest shortcut ever could.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 7

## THE LIBRARY THAT DID NOT JUDGE MY GRAMMAR

The first time I saw someone reading for pleasure, I thought they were
crazy.

Her name was Naomi. My cousin. She was sitting under a tree outside
their house, holding a thin book with a colorful cover, completely
absorbed in whatever story was happening on those pages.

I walked up to her. Curious. Confused.

\"What are you doing?\" I asked.

She looked up, smiled. \"Reading.\"

\"For school?\"

\"No. For fun.\"

Fun. Reading. Two words that did not belong together in my young mind.
Reading was work. Reading was school. Reading was something teachers
made you do, not something you chose to do.

\"Can I see?\" I asked.

She handed me the book. The cover had a picture of a goat. The title
said something about Goat Matata. I did not understand what \"matata\"
meant yet. Did not know it was Swahili for \"trouble.\"

I opened the book. The words were simple. The sentences short. The story
immediate.

And for the first time in my life, I disappeared into something that was
not real but felt more real than anything around me.

That book changed everything.

Not immediately. Not in some dramatic revelation where I suddenly became
a reader. But the book planted a seed. A possibility. A question: What
if reading was not for school alone? What if stories existed beyond
textbooks? What if there were whole worlds hidden in pages I had not yet
seen?

I started borrowing books from anyone who had them. Naomi. Other
cousins. Friends at school whose parents had money for things like
storybooks.

The selection was limited. Rural Kenyan kids in the early 2000s did not
have libraries or bookstores or Amazon Prime. We had whatever books made
it to our corner of the world through missions, donations, or rare
purchases.

But the few books we had, we read until the pages fell apart.

\"The Greedy Hyena.\" \"Otoyo and the Magic Drum.\" Stories about
animals that talked. About children who found magic. About problems
getting solved through cleverness instead of strength.

These stories were not sophisticated. They were not literature with a
capital L. But they were portals. Escapes. Proof that life could be
different than the one I was living.

I read them over and over. Memorized passages. Acted out scenes with
Danson when we had nothing else to do. Created voices for characters.
Made the stories bigger than they were on the page.

Reading became my superpower. Not because reading made me smarter.
Because reading made me believe that different was possible.

If a goat could solve problems, maybe I could too. If Otoyo could find a
magic drum, maybe I could find something equally transformative. If
stories existed about worlds beyond Manyonyi, maybe Manyonyi was not the
end of the story.

Maybe Manyonyi was the beginning.

But the real library, the one changing my life, came later. When I moved
to Lumakanda. When I met Grandpa David Zarembka.

That is a story for the next chapter. For now, know this: Books saved me
before I knew I needed saving. Stories gave me vocabulary before I had
language for what I was feeling.

And somewhere between Goat Matata and The Greedy Hyena, I learned that
noise did not always have to be loud to be powerful.

Sometimes the most powerful noise is the quiet sound of pages turning in
the dark.

*\"Roylandz Rule #8: The right story at the right time rewrites your
entire life.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 8

## DUNG DAY AND THE CANE ECONOMY

Every Friday at Mahemas Primary School was Dung Day.

Not officially. Nobody called it that in the school calendar or morning
announcements. But every student knew. Every parent knew. Every cow in
Kakamega County probably knew by the smell Friday mornings carried.

Friday was the day you brought cow dung to school.

Fresh cow dung. Collected from your family\'s cows, wrapped in banana
leaves or old plastic bags, carried carefully during that ten-kilometer
walk so it did not spill on your uniform.

This was not punishment. This was not hazing. This was maintenance. This
was how we kept our classrooms clean in a school with no budget for
cleaning supplies or janitors.

The dung served the same purpose it served at home. Mixed with water,
spread across the classroom floors, left to dry. Natural polish. Natural
disinfectant. Natural solution to a problem with no money attached to
it.

Each student was expected to bring a portion. Size depended on your
class. Younger students brought smaller amounts. Older students brought
more. If your family did not have cows, you borrowed from neighbors or
found another family willing to share their supply.

Missing Dung Day was not an option. Come to school empty-handed on
Friday and you would face consequences making the smell of cow dung seem
pleasant by comparison.

I remember walking to school on Friday mornings carrying dung wrapped in
polythene sacks. The smell was inescapable. The smell seeped through the
wrapping. The smell clung to your hands. The smell announced your
presence before you arrived.

You would walk past adults on the road, and they would nod knowingly.
Friday. School day. The children carrying their contribution to
education.

Nobody judged. Nobody mocked. Because everyone had done it. Everyone
understood this was part of the deal. You want your children educated?
You provide the materials. And sometimes the materials come from a
cow\'s rear end.

When we arrived at school, we would line up by class. A teacher would
inspect what each student brought. Too little, and you would be sent
home to get more. Too dry, and you would be told it was useless. Too
wet, and you would slop it everywhere and make a mess.

There was an art to bringing the right cow dung. You wanted fresh but
not liquid. Solid but not dried. The kind mixing well with water and
spreading smoothly without falling apart or clumping.

Yes, I wrote three paragraphs about the quality standards of cow dung
for educational purposes. That was my life. That was normal.

Once the dung was approved, we would get to work. Buckets of water were
brought from the borehole. The dung was dumped in. Mixed with hands,
bare hands, until it reached the right consistency. Then we would enter
the classrooms.

On your hands and knees, you would spread the mixture across the floor.
Starting from the back. Working forward. Covering every inch. Making
sure no spots were missed because uneven coverage meant uneven drying
meant rough patches that would hurt when you knelt or sat on the floor.

It took about an hour for a whole class to finish one classroom. By the
time you were done, your hands smelled like farm. Your uniform had
splatters. Your knees were wet. Your dignity was somewhere between the
cow and the classroom.

But the floor looked good. Smooth. Clean. Ready for another week of
students sitting, kneeling, playing during breaks.

And that is what mattered. Not how it smelled. Not how we looked. What
mattered was the job got done and we could get back to learning.

The cane economy at Mahemas Primary School operated on simple
principles: break a rule, face the consequence. The consequence was
usually measured in strokes.

Not all rules carried equal weight. The punishment fit the crime. Or at
least, the punishment fit what teachers believed the crime deserved.

Late to school? Two strokes.

Forgot your homework? Three strokes.

Talking during class? Four strokes.

Fighting with another student? Six strokes.

Disrespecting a teacher? Eight strokes and a personal lecture from Mr.
Kipande or even suspension.

The cane itself was thin. Flexible. Made from a type of wood bending but
not breaking. It made a whistling sound when it cut through the air.
That sound alone was enough to make most students reconsider their
choices.

The punishment was administered in front of the class or during
assembly, depending on the severity. You would be called forward. Asked
to explain yourself. Given one chance to confess or apologize. Then told
to bend over or hold out your hands.

The pain was sharp. Immediate. The kind making your eyes water even if
you were determined not to cry. The welts would appear instantly. Red
lines across your palms or legs stinging for hours afterward.

But you did not cry. Not in front of other students. Crying meant
weakness. Weakness meant more teasing. So you took the strokes, bit your
lip, walked back to your seat with your head up.

Later, when you were alone or with close friends, you would compare
welts. See whose were worse. Joke about teachers who swung hardest. Turn
pain into competition because competition was better than self-pity.

I got caned more than I would like to admit. Not because I was a bad
student. Because I was a loud student. Because I had opinions. Because I
asked questions teachers did not want to answer. Because I talked when
silence was expected.

One time, I got six strokes for laughing during a serious assembly. Mr.
Kipande was speaking about discipline. About respect. About how students
were becoming too casual, too comfortable, too disrespectful.

And someone next to me whispered a joke. I do not even remember what the
joke was. But I laughed. Out loud. During a lecture about not being
casual.

The irony was not lost on Mr. Kipande.

\"You,\" he said, pointing at me. \"Come here.\"

The assembly went silent. Three hundred students watching. Waiting.
Grateful it was not them.

I walked forward. Heart pounding. Knowing what was coming.

\"You think this is funny?\" Mr. Kipande asked.

\"No, sir.\"

\"But you laughed.\"

\"Yes, sir.\"

\"Why?\"

\"Someone said something funny, sir.\"

Wrong answer. The correct answer was \"I do not know, sir.\" Or \"I am
sorry, sir.\" Not honesty. Never honesty.

\"Six strokes,\" he said. \"Maybe that will teach you when to laugh and
when to listen.\"

He called me forward. I bent over. He swung. Six times. Each stroke
worse than the last because your skin gets sensitive after the first few
hits.

I walked back to my place in line. Eyes dry. Head up. Legs burning.

And I learned something valuable that day: Honesty is overrated when you
are dealing with authority not wanting truth. Authority wants
compliance.

Despite the canes, despite Dung Day, despite the long walks and shared
desks and teachers who ruled through fear, I thrived at Mahemas.

I was top of my class most terms. Not always first. Sometimes second or
third. But always in the top five. Always on the list read during
assembly. Always among the names making parents proud.

Being smart in a poor school is complicated. On one hand, teachers
notice you. They give you opportunities. They call on you to read aloud.
They recommend you for competitions or prizes when they exist.

On the other hand, students notice you too. And not always in good ways.
Being smart when everyone else is struggling means standing out.
Standing out means becoming a target.

Some kids were supportive. They would ask for help with homework. Share
lunch when they had extra. Celebrate when you won awards.

Other kids resented it. Called you names. Said you were showing off.
Said you thought you were better than them. Said you would fail
eventually and then you would see what it felt like to be normal.

I did not think I was better. I knew I was different. And different was
my only way out.

So I kept studying. Kept raising my hand. Kept answering questions. Kept
getting top marks even when it made me unpopular.

Because popularity does not pay school fees. Popularity does not get you
to secondary school. Popularity does not change your life.

Education does. Knowledge does. Being the kid who refuses to accept that
poverty is permanent does.

Years later, I would think about Mahemas Primary School and realize
something important: The walk was not the hardest part. The dung was not
the hardest part. The canes were not the hardest part.

The hardest part was believing that all of it mattered. That walking ten
kilometers was worth it. That carrying cow dung was worth it. That
getting caned for laughing was worth it.

The hardest part was faith. Faith that education would pay off. Faith
that this suffering had purpose. Faith that somewhere beyond those red
murram roads, there was a life waiting looking nothing like the one I
was living.

I did not know if that faith was justified. I knew it was all I had.

So I walked. I carried dung. I took the cane. I studied.

And I waited for the future to prove me right.

*\"Roylandz Rule #9: Dignity is not about avoiding dirty work. Dignity
is about doing dirty work without losing yourself.\"*

# CHAPTER 9

## THE BOY WHO READ TO CHICKENS

I had my first audience before I had my first friend.

The audience was a chicken.

Not a pet chicken. Not a special chicken with a name or personality. A
regular village chicken wandering into our compound one afternoon and
deciding our home was interesting enough to stay.

Chickens in rural Kenya do not belong to anyone specific until someone
decides to cook them. They roam freely. Lay eggs wherever they want.
Answer to no one. This particular chicken seemed to think our compound
was safe. Or at least safer than wherever it came from.

So it stayed. Pecking at the ground. Looking for insects. Making that
low clucking sound chickens make when they are content.

And I talked to it.

Not because I was lonely. Though I probably was. Not because I thought
it would understand. But because I needed an audience. Needed to
practice the words piling up in my head with nowhere to go.

I would sit on a rock or a log outside our house, and I would read
aloud. Homework assignments. Stories from the few books I could borrow.
Sometimes made-up stories about things I had seen or wanted to see.

The chicken would listen. Or at least it would stay close enough that I
could pretend it was listening.

It never judged. Never interrupted. Never told me my pronunciation was
wrong or my grammar needed work. It pecked and clucked and existed while
I practiced being someone who had something to say.

Danson was my second audience. Younger. Easier to impress. More captive
than the chicken because he lived with me and could not escape.

We would tell each other stories at night when we were lying on that
squeaky mattress, waiting for sleep. Stories about heroes who were not
from rich families. About boys who started with nothing and ended with
everything. About adventures beginning with someone saying \"I can do
more than this.\"

I did not know I was preparing him for something. Did not know I was
preparing myself. I knew that words felt powerful when you spoke them
out loud. That stories had weight when someone was listening.

Danson would fall asleep halfway through most stories. His breathing
would slow. His body would relax. And I would keep talking anyway,
finishing the story for myself more than for him.

Because the story was not entertainment alone. The story was practice.
The story was rehearsal for a future I could not see yet but could feel
coming.

At school, I volunteered to read aloud during lessons. Not because I was
brave. Because I was strategic.

Reading aloud meant being noticed. Being noticed meant being remembered.
Being remembered meant opportunities when opportunities were scarce.

But being noticed also meant being criticized.

Some teachers would stop me mid-sentence. \"No, no, no. That is not how
you pronounce that word. Again.\"

I would repeat it. Get it wrong again. Feel the heat rise in my face as
other students giggled.

\"Again.\"

Wrong again. More giggling. Some students openly laughing now.

\"Someone else read,\" the teacher would say, dismissing me back to my
seat with a wave feeling like failure.

I would sit down, burning with embarrassment. Swearing I would never
volunteer again. Swearing reading aloud was overrated anyway.

Then next week, I would raise my hand again. Because embarrassment was
temporary. Being invisible was permanent.

And I refused to be invisible.

One assembly stands out above all others. I do not remember what I was
supposed to present. Some assignment. Some report. Something requiring
standing in front of the entire school and speaking.

I prepared for days. Memorized what I wanted to say. Practiced in front
of the chicken. In front of Danson. In front of the mud walls of our
house.

When the day came, I walked to the front during assembly. Three hundred
students staring. Teachers watching. Mr. Kipande waiting to see if I
would waste his time.

I opened my mouth. Started speaking.

And halfway through, someone laughed.

Not a polite chuckle. A loud, mocking laugh. The kind saying \"this is
hilarious and I do not care who knows it.\"

Other students joined. Within seconds, a wave of laughter was rolling
through the assembly. Not everyone. But enough. More than enough.

I stopped speaking. Stood there. Frozen. Humiliated.

Mr. Kipande raised his hand. \"Silence!\"

The laughter stopped. But the damage was done. I could feel it. The
shame. The heat in my face. The desire to disappear into the ground and
never come back.

\"Finish,\" Mr. Kipande said to me. Not unkindly. Matter-of-fact.

I finished. Rushed through the rest. Walked back to my place in line
with my head down.

After assembly, a teacher pulled me aside. I expected more criticism.
More correction. More proof I should not have tried.

Instead, she said, \"You did well. Do not let them discourage you.\"

\"They laughed,\" I said.

\"They always laugh at what they do not understand. Keep speaking.\"

I did not keep speaking right away. For weeks, I stayed quiet. Stopped
volunteering. Stopped reading aloud. Became one of the silent majority
who survived school by being unremarkable.

But something about that silence felt worse than the laughter. Felt like
giving up. Felt like letting the people who laughed win.

So eventually, I raised my hand again. Started reading again. Started
speaking again.

And they laughed again. But this time, I did not stop. I kept reading.
Kept speaking. Kept going until the laughter faded into background
noise.

Because I learned something crucial in that moment: You cannot control
whether people laugh. You can only control whether you stop.

And I refused to stop.

The chicken eventually left. Wandered off to some other compound. Some
other life. Never knew it had been my first audience. Never knew it had
played a role in turning a quiet boy into someone who would not shut up.

Danson grew older. Started having his own thoughts. His own stories.
Stopped being a passive audience and became an active participant.
Started challenging my stories. Making them better through his
questions.

And somewhere in all of that, between the chicken and Danson and the
students who laughed, I learned the most important lesson about voice:

Your first audience does not have to be big. Your first audience does
not have to be sophisticated. Your first audience does not even have to
be human.

Your first audience has to be there. Present. Giving you a reason to
speak. Giving you a reason to believe what you have to say matters.

Because once you believe that, really believe it, no amount of laughter
can make you silent again.

*\"Roylandz Rule #10: Practice your voice before you need it, so when
opportunity comes, you are already loud.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 10

## WHEN SILENCE BECAME SURVIVAL

The day my parents left is the day silence moved into our house like an
uninvited guest refusing to leave.

I was in Class 1. Six, maybe seven years old. Danson was younger. Still
in that phase where the world is magic and adults know everything and
tomorrow always comes.

My father came home one evening. I remember the way the light looked.
Orange. Fading. The kind of light making everything look temporary.

He sat me down. Looked me in the eyes. And said words I did not fully
understand but felt in my stomach.

\"I have to go away for a while. Take care of your brother.\"

Not \"I might go.\" Not \"I am thinking about leaving.\" \"I have to
go.\"

I nodded. What else do you do when you are six and your father tells you
something sounding like forever but feeling like goodbye?

\"Where are you going?\" I asked.

\"Nairobi.\"

\"When are you coming back?\"

\"Soon.\"

Soon turned into months. Months turned into years. Years turned into
five.

Five years. That is how long my parents were gone. Both of them. My
mother first. My father shortly after. Different reasons. Same result.
Two children left behind with grandparents already tired from raising
their own kids and now having to raise someone else\'s.

I do not blame my parents for leaving. I need you to understand that
before we go further. I do not blame them.

My father left because he had to. There was a situation involving cattle
theft accusations. His friends were killed. The police were looking.
Staying meant danger. Danger to him. Danger to us.

So he fled to Nairobi. Disappeared into the chaos of a city where nobody
asks questions and everyone is running from something.

My mother left because staying meant violence. Domestic violence. The
kind not making headlines in rural Kenya because it is so common it is
not considered news.

So she also fled to Nairobi. Found work as a househelp. Made 7,000
shillings a month cleaning other people\'s houses while her own children
lived ten hours away without her.

They did what they had to do to survive. To keep us safe. To give us a
chance at a future not including whatever they were running from.

I understand that now. I did not understand it then.

Then, all I understood was silence. The kind of silence filling a house
when the people who are supposed to fill it are gone.

Taking care of Danson became my full-time job. School was secondary.
Homework was optional. Survival was mandatory.

I had to make sure he ate. Make sure he was clean. Make sure he did not
wander off or hurt himself or do any of the thousand dangerous things
children do when nobody is watching.

I was seven. Acting like seventeen. Feeling like seventy.

I would wake him up in the morning. We would dress. Make sure he had
something to eat, even if it was tea and stale bread alone. Walk him
part of the way to wherever he needed to go before I headed to school.

After school, I would come home and make sure he was okay. That he had
eaten. That he had not gotten into trouble. That he was not crying for
parents who were not there.

At night, I would lie next to him on that squeaky mattress and tell him
stories. Not the adventure stories anymore. Different stories. Stories
about how Mom and Dad would come back soon. About how everything would
be okay. About how we had to wait a little longer.

Lies. All of it. I had no idea if they would come back. No idea if
anything would be okay. No idea how much longer \"a little longer\"
meant.

But I told him those stories anyway. Because he needed them. Because
hope, even false hope, is better than despair. Because at seven years
old, I understood something adults forget: Sometimes lying is love.

The silence in our house during those years was different from normal
silence. It was not peaceful. It was heavy. Dense. The kind of silence
pressing on your chest and making breathing feel like work.

Danson and I learned to fill it with noise. Not meaningful noise. Sound.
Anything to prove we were still alive. Still there. Still worth
remembering.

We would talk about nothing. Ask each other questions we already knew
the answers to. Make up games with no rules. Laugh at things that were
not funny.

Because the alternative was sitting in that heavy silence and letting it
crush us.

Our grandparents did their best. Philemona and Rosemary Obbayi took us
in without hesitation. Fed us when they had food. Disciplined us when we
needed it. Kept us alive.

But they were old. Tired. Their bodies hurt from decades of farm work.
Their patience was worn thin from raising one generation and now having
to raise another.

They could not give us what we needed most. Not because they did not
want to. Because what we needed most was our parents, and they could not
provide that.

So we existed in that space between family and orphan. Cared for but not
parented. Alive but not thriving. Waiting for a return feeling less
likely with each passing month.

I learned to lie during those years. Not small lies. Big ones. Strategic
ones. Survival lies.

At school, when teachers asked why I did not have lunch, I would say I
ate at home. When they asked why my uniform was torn, I would say I fell
playing. When they asked where my parents were, I would say they were
working in Nairobi and would visit soon.

All lies. But lies keeping people from asking more questions. Lies
keeping me from being the kid everyone pitied. Lies maintaining the
illusion of normalcy.

I lied to Danson too. Told him Mom and Dad called and said they would
visit next month. Told him they sent money even when they did not. Told
him everything was going to work out even when I had no evidence to
support that claim.

And slowly, I started lying to myself. Started believing my own stories.
Started thinking maybe if I said it enough times, it would become true.

That is what trauma does to children. Trauma turns them into liars. Not
because they are bad. Because lying is easier than facing a truth too
big to hold.

The silence broke in 2013. Five years after it started. Five years after
my father said \"I have to go\" and my mother followed shortly after.

I was in Class 4 by then. A few years old. Old enough to use a phone.
Old enough to understand what it meant when Grandma Rosemary handed me
her mobile and said, \"Call your mother.\"

My hands shook as I dialed the number she gave me. A number I had never
seen. A number connecting to a person I barely remembered.

The phone rang. Once. Twice. Three times.

Then a voice. \"Hello?\"

My mother\'s voice. Older than I remembered. Tired. But unmistakably
hers.

I started crying before I said a word. Not quiet crying. Deep,
gut-wrenching sobs coming from five years of silence breaking all at
once.

\"Eugine?\" she said. And then she started crying too.

We talked for five minutes. Both of us crying so hard we could barely
speak. Making promises about visiting. About reuniting. About making up
for lost time.

When I hung up, I looked at Danson. His eyes were wide. Hopeful. Scared.

\"Was that Mom?\" he asked.

\"Yeah.\"

\"Is she coming back?\"

I did not lie this time. \"I do not know.\"

The silence never fully left. Even after my parents came back,
sporadically, briefly, never for long, the silence stayed. It lived in
the spaces between words. In the things we did not talk about. In the
five years nobody wanted to explain or examine.

But something shifted in me after that phone call. Something hardened.
Something clarified.

I realized that silence was the enemy. Not poverty. Not distance. Not
even absence. Silence was what killed hope. Silence was what made
suffering worse. Silence was what turned pain into trauma and trauma
into permanent damage.

And I decided right then that I would never be silent again. Not
voluntarily. Not unless someone physically stopped me from speaking.

I would be loud. Deliberately loud. Permanently loud. The kind of loud
annoying people but also making sure nobody could ignore me.

Because being ignored for five years taught me something crucial: If
people cannot hear you, they cannot help you. And if they cannot help
you, you have to help yourself.

And the first step in helping yourself is making sure people know you
exist.

So I opened my mouth. And I have not closed it since.

*\"Roylandz Rule #11: Silence is survival only until you realize noise
is power.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

**END OF HOWEVER I: DUST DON\'T DIE EASY (2001-2008)**
