\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

**PART II**

**EDUCATION IS A CONTACT SPORT**

**(2009-2015)**

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

\\

# CHAPTER 11

## THE MOVE TO LUMAKANDA

Lumakanda was not far from Manyonyi. Twenty kilometers. Thirty at most.
But it felt like moving to another country.

The decision was not mine. Children do not get to decide where they
live. They get told. Packed. Moved. Unpacked. And expected to adjust
without complaining because complaining changes nothing.

I do not remember the exact conversation. Do not remember who decided it
or why. I remember one day being in Manyonyi with Grandparents Philemona
and Rosemary, and the next day being told I would be moving to Lumakanda
to live with different grandparents.

Grandpa David Zarembka and Grandma Gladys Kamonya.

The American Quaker and his Kenyan wife.

The peacemaker and the disciplinarian.

The man with more books than furniture and the woman who believed
television was the devil\'s primary recruitment tool.

I was eight or nine years old. Old enough to feel nervous. Young enough
to have no say in the matter.

Danson came with me. That was the only comfort. Whatever was waiting in
Lumakanda, at least we would face it together.

The day we moved, our belongings fit into two plastic bags. That is not
an exaggeration. Two bags. Everything we owned. Clothes that barely fit.
A few school exercise books. Nothing else worth carrying.

Someone drove us. I do not remember who. An uncle maybe. Someone with
access to a vehicle willing to transport two children and two bags
across rutted roads testing the suspension of even the strongest cars.

I watched out the window as we left Manyonyi. Watched the compound
disappear. The church. The paths I had walked barefoot for years. The
chicken that used to listen to my stories was long gone, but I looked
for it anyway.

Everything familiar was shrinking in the distance. Everything unknown
was growing larger ahead.

Danson sat next to me, quiet. He was too young to fully understand what
was happening. Too young to feel the weight of change. But old enough to
sense that something significant was shifting.

\"Where are we going?\" he asked.

\"Lumakanda.\"

\"Why?\"

\"To live with Grandpa David and Grandma Gladys.\"

\"Why?\"

\"I do not know.\"

That was the truth. I did not know why. Did not know what we had done to
deserve relocation or what we were moving toward. I knew that adults had
decided and children had to follow.

Lumakanda was different immediately. Not richer. Not more modern.
Different in the way two villages can be thirty kilometers apart but
feel like different worlds.

The roads were slightly better. The houses slightly more permanent. More
iron sheet roofs, fewer grass thatch. More tin walls, fewer mud walls.
More evidence that money occasionally made it this far from Kakamega
town.

Grandpa David and Grandma Gladys lived in a compound feeling organized
compared to what I was used to. Their house had rooms with doors. Walls
that did not let in mosquitoes without permission. Windows with frames.
A kitchen separate from the main house.

And electricity.

Not all the time. Not reliable electricity. But sometimes, when Kenya
Power decided to send current through the lines, there would be light.
Real light. Not from kerosene lamps or fire. From bulbs hanging from the
ceiling and turning night into artificial day with the flip of a switch.

I stood in that house the first evening, staring at the light bulb like
it was magic. Which it was. The kind of magic you take for granted if
you grew up with it. The kind of magic changing your life if you did
not.

\"Do not waste electricity,\" Grandma Gladys said. \"Turn it off when
you leave the room.\"

I nodded. Filed that rule away with all the other rules I would learn in
the coming weeks. Rules that would shape how I lived. How I thought. How
I became someone different from the boy who arrived.

Grandpa David Zarembka was unlike any grandfather I had ever
encountered. Not because he was American. Not because he was white.
Because he was curious.

Most adults in my world had stopped being curious. They knew what they
knew. Believed what they believed. Asked questions only when they needed
specific answers to specific problems.

Grandpa David asked questions constantly. Not to test you. Not to catch
you lying. Because he genuinely wanted to know.

\"What did you learn today?\"

\"What do you think about that?\"

\"Why do you think that happened?\"

\"How would you solve that problem?\"

Questions with no single right answer. Questions making you think
instead of memorize alone. Questions treating children like people whose
thoughts mattered.

It was unsettling at first. I kept waiting for the trick. The moment
when my answer would be wrong and punishment would follow. But the trick
never came. He listened. Nodded. Asked follow-up questions. Treated my
thoughts like they were valuable even when they were clearly the
thoughts of a child who knew almost nothing.

He was tall. Thin. Had glasses making his eyes look bigger. Spoke
English and Kiswahili with equal fluency. Moved slowly, deliberately,
like someone who had learned that rushing through life meant missing
important details.

He dressed simply. Khaki pants. Button-up shirts. Sandals. Nothing
fancy. Nothing screaming \"I am different from you.\" Practical clothes
for a practical man doing impractical work like trying to bring peace to
Central Africa.

Grandma Gladys was the opposite in many ways. Not in a bad way. In a
complementary way. Where David was gentle, Gladys was firm. Where David
asked questions, Gladys gave answers. Where David believed in
discussion, Gladys believed in discipline.

She was Kenyan. Luhya. Understood the culture David studied from
textbooks. Understood that gentleness without structure was another word
for chaos.

She had rules. Clear rules. Non-negotiable rules.

Wake up at 6 AM. No exceptions.

Make your bed before doing anything else.

Wash your face and hands before breakfast.

No playing until chores are done.

No leaving the compound without permission.

No lying. Ever. For any reason.

Church on Sunday. Bible study during the week.

And the big one: Limited television.

TV existed in their house. A small box receiving a few channels when the
signal was good. But watching it was a privilege. A rare treat. Not a
right. Not something you did because you were bored.

Grandma Gladys believed television rotted minds. Made children lazy.
Filled their heads with nonsense they did not need. She would rather we
read or played outside or did literally anything else.

So we did. Not because we agreed. Because she said so. And in Grandma
Gladys\'s house, \"because I said so\" was the final word.

The adjustment period was rough. Everything was different. The food. The
schedule. The expectations. The way adults talked to us.

In Manyonyi, affection was implied. You were family, therefore you were
loved. Nobody said it out loud. Nobody hugged or praised or gave
explicit encouragement. You knew. Or you assumed. Or you hoped.

In Lumakanda, with David and Gladys, things were different. More verbal.
More explicit. More American in that way where feelings get stated
instead of implied.

\"I am proud of you,\" David would say when I did well in school.

\"Good job,\" Gladys would say when I finished chores properly.

\"I love you,\" they would both say at random times. Not prompted by
anything special. Stated as fact.

It was uncomfortable. Not because it was unwanted. Because it was
unfamiliar. Like learning a new language where the words sound right but
feel strange coming out of your mouth.

Danson adjusted faster than I did. He was younger. More flexible. Less
attached to the old ways. Within weeks, he was calling them Grandpa and
Grandma like he had always known them.

I took longer. Watched. Waited. Tested boundaries. Looked for the moment
when kindness would turn to cruelty. The moment when the rules would
become weapons. The moment when I would realize this was another place
where children survived instead of thrived.

But that moment never came.

Slowly, carefully, I started to believe that maybe this place was
different. Maybe these grandparents were different. Maybe Lumakanda was
the beginning of something instead of another chapter in survival.

*\"Roylandz Rule #12: New places do not erase old scars, but they give
you room to heal differently.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 12

## FRIENDS CHURCH AND THE GOSPEL OF DISCIPLINE

Sunday mornings in Lumakanda started with a routine allowing no
negotiation.

6 AM: Wake up.

6:15 AM: Wash. Cold water from a basin. No excuses about being cold.
Everyone is cold. Wash anyway.

6:30 AM: Breakfast. Usually tea and bread. Sometimes porridge if we were
lucky.

7 AM: Get dressed. Church clothes. The good uniform, not the school
uniform. Clean. Pressed. Presentable.

7:30 AM: Walk to Friends Church Lumakanda.

The church was two kilometers away. Close enough to walk. Far enough
that you could not be late without someone noticing.

Friends Church, Quakers to the rest of the world, was different from
Church of God Manyonyi in almost every way. Quieter. Calmer. Less
fire-and-brimstone, more thoughtful-reflection.

The building itself was simple. Wooden benches arranged in rows. A
pulpit at the front but no elevated stage. Windows letting in natural
light and fresh air. Everything designed for simplicity. For focus. For
removing distractions so you could hear whatever voice needed hearing.

Quaker worship was strange at first. I was used to loud preaching. Call
and response. Music making the walls shake. Pastors who worked
themselves into sweaty performances feeling more like theater than
church.

Friends Church was different. Silent worship. Periods where everyone sat
in silence, waiting for someone to feel moved to speak. No planned
program. No set order. People sitting together in shared quiet until the
spirit moved someone to break it.

The first time I experienced it, I thought something was wrong. We had
arrived, sat down, and then, nothing. Silence. People sitting with their
eyes closed or staring at nothing in particular. Minutes passing. Five.
Ten. Fifteen.

I looked at Danson. He looked at me. Both of us confused. Waiting for
the service to start. Not realizing the service had already started.
That the silence was the service.

Finally, an elder stood up. Spoke briefly about peace. About finding God
in the quiet spaces. About listening more than talking. Then sat down.
More silence.

Another person stood. Shared a short testimony. Sat down. Silence again.

This went on for an hour. Brief interruptions in an ocean of quiet.

I hated it at first. The silence felt oppressive. Made me anxious. Made
me want to fill it with noise, with movement, with anything other than
sitting still while my thoughts circled like vultures.

But slowly, over weeks and months, the silence started feeling
different. Less oppressive. More spacious. Like it was making room for
something I did not know I needed.

Grandpa David was deeply involved in Friends Church Lumakanda. Not as a
pastor. Quakers do not have pastors in the traditional sense. As a
member. A participant. Someone who believed in the Quaker principles of
peace, simplicity, integrity, community, and equality.

He would speak during worship sometimes. Brief messages about peace work
in Rwanda or Burundi. About reconciliation programs. About bringing
former enemies together to talk instead of kill.

I did not understand most of it. Did not understand why anyone would
want to forgive people who had hurt them. Did not understand why talking
was supposed to fix problems that violence had created.

But I listened. And I watched the way people responded to David\'s
words. The respect. The attention. The way a white American could speak
in a Kenyan church and people would listen not because of his color but
because of his character.

That taught me something important. Respect is not given. Respect is
earned. And respect is earned through consistency, through integrity,
through showing up and doing the work even when nobody is watching.

Sunday school was mandatory. Not the silent worship kind of church. The
children-sit-in-a-separate-room-and-learn-Bible-stories kind of church.

The teacher was a woman named Joyce. Patient. Kind. Had a gift for
making ancient stories feel relevant to children living in rural Kenya.

She would tell us about David and Goliath, but frame it as courage
overcoming impossible odds. She would tell us about Moses leading people
out of slavery, but frame it as leadership requiring faith when you
cannot see the destination.

She did not teach us to memorize verses alone. She taught us to think
about what they meant. To ask questions. To connect old stories to
current lives.

One Sunday, she asked us what we wanted to be when we grew up.

Most kids said teacher. Or doctor. Or pilot. Safe answers. Impressive
answers. Answers making adults smile and nod approvingly.

When she got to me, I said, \"I want to be on TV.\"

The room went quiet. Some kids giggled. Joyce\'s smile did not change,
but I could see the question in her eyes. TV? What kind of dream is that
for a village boy with no electricity most days?

\"Why?\" she asked.

\"Because I want people to listen,\" I said.

\"People listen to teachers and doctors too,\" she pointed out.

\"But not the same way. TV makes people listen even when they do not
want to.\"

She paused. Looked at me for a long moment. Then nodded.

\"Then you should work hard in school. Because TV people have to be
smart.\"

\"I am smart.\"

\"I know you are. Now prove it.\"

That conversation stayed with me. Not because she encouraged the dream.
Because she did not dismiss it. Did not laugh or tell me to be realistic
or suggest I aim for something more achievable.

She told me to prove it.

So I decided I would.

The Gospel of Discipline, capital G, capital D, was Grandma Gladys\'s
specialty. She believed discipline was love made visible. That structure
was freedom. That rules were protection against chaos.

Her discipline was not punishment-focused. It was habit-focused.
Building routines becoming automatic. Behaviors becoming character.

Morning routine: Same every day. Wake up. Make bed. Wash. Breakfast.
Chores. School prep. No variation. No exceptions.

Homework routine: Come home. Change clothes. Eat something if food was
available. Homework immediately. No playing until homework was done and
checked.

Evening routine: Dinner. Family time. Bible reading. Bed by 8 PM.
Earlier if you had misbehaved.

The routine was suffocating at first. Felt like prison. Felt like
someone had taken freedom and replaced it with schedules and
expectations.

But over time, something shifted. The routine stopped feeling like
restriction and started feeling like security. Like knowing what came
next. Like having a map when everything else felt lost.

Punishment, when it came, was swift and clear. Grandma Gladys did not
yell. Did not threaten. Did not make speeches about disappointment. She
acted.

Broke a rule? Consequence followed immediately. No debate. No
negotiation. You knew the rule. You broke it. You face the consequence.
Move on.

The consequence usually involved extra chores or early bedtime or
temporary loss of privileges. Physical discipline existed but was rare.
Reserved for serious offenses. And even then, measured. Controlled.
Never angry. Never vindictive.

\"I am doing this because I love you,\" she would say before correction.
\"Because discipline now prevents problems later.\"

I did not believe her at first. Thought it was something adults said to
justify control.

But years later, when I watched other kids from undisciplined homes
struggle with basic time management, struggle with commitment, struggle
with following through, I understood.

Grandma Gladys was not teaching me to follow rules. She was teaching me
to make decisions. To understand that choices have consequences. To
build habits that would serve me when nobody was watching.

The Gospel of Discipline was not about control. The Gospel of Discipline
was about character.

And character, I would learn much later, is the only thing you take with
you when everything else gets stripped away.

*\"Roylandz Rule #13: Discipline feels like chains until you realize it
is building wings.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 13

## ZAREMBKA\'S LIBRARY OF PEACE

The room smelled like old paper, wood polish, and possibility.

Grandpa David\'s library was not a library in the public sense. No
checkout desk. No catalog system. No librarian stamping due dates. A
room in his house filled with books. Hundreds of them. Maybe more.
Stacked on shelves he had built himself. Organized in a system making
sense to him and nobody else.

The first time I entered that room, I stopped breathing.

I had seen books before. School textbooks. The few storybooks
circulating through Mahemas Primary. Religious books at church. But I
had never seen books like this. Books about countries I had never heard
of. Books about ideas I did not have words for. Books not trying to
teach me math or make me memorize Bible verses.

Books existing to make you think.

\"You can read anything in here,\" Grandpa David said. \"Put it back
when you are done.\"

I stared at the shelves. Overwhelmed. Where do you even start when every
book looks both interesting and impossible?

He pulled one down. Handed it to me. \"Start with this.\"

The cover showed a building. The title said something about Africa and
peace. The author was David Zarembka.

\"You wrote this?\" I asked.

\"I did.\"

\"About what?\"

\"About my work. About trying to help people who have hurt each other
learn to live together again.\"

I opened it. The words were adult words. Complicated words. Words I
would have to read slowly and maybe ask about later. But they were his
words. My grandfather\'s words. Printed. Published. Permanent.

If he could write a book, maybe writing was not impossible. Maybe
thoughts could become more than noise in your head. Maybe stories could
outlive the person who told them.

I took the book to my room. Started reading. Understood maybe thirty
percent. But that thirty percent changed something fundamental in how I
saw the world.

Grandpa David\'s library became my escape. My education. My window into
worlds not including cow dung or kerosene lamps or walks to school
leaving your feet bleeding.

I would go in there after finishing homework. After chores. After dinner
when Danson was already asleep and the house was quiet except for
crickets and distant dogs.

I would pull books off shelves at random. Read titles. Open to random
pages. Try to understand what adults were talking about when they used
words like \"reconciliation\" and \"post-genocide\" and \"trauma
healing.\"

Most of it was over my head. But some of it stuck. And the parts
sticking started building something. A framework. An understanding that
the world was bigger and more complicated than I had imagined.

There were books about Rwanda. About the 1994 genocide where neighbors
killed neighbors. Where churches became slaughterhouses. Where ethnic
division turned into systematic extermination.

There were books about Burundi. About cyclical violence going on so long
nobody remembered who started it or why. People knew that Hutu and Tutsi
were supposed to be enemies and killing was what enemies did.

There were books about peacemaking. About bringing survivors and
perpetrators together in the same room. About creating space for people
to tell their stories without interruption. About healing coming not
from forgetting but from acknowledging.

And there were books about America. About civil rights. About people who
looked different from each other fighting for the right to be treated
the same. About Martin Luther King Jr. and nonviolent resistance and the
idea that you could fight oppression without becoming oppressive.

But the book changing my life, really changing it, was sitting on a
shelf I almost did not reach.

\"Half of a Yellow Sun\" by Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie.

I do not remember why I pulled it down. Maybe the cover. Maybe the
thickness. Maybe luck. But once I started reading, I could not stop.

It was about Nigeria. About the Biafran War. About ordinary people
caught in political conflict they did not create and could not control.
About love and loss and survival and the way war destroys things you
cannot measure in body counts.

But more than that, it was about Africa. Written by an African. For
Africans. In a voice not apologizing or explaining or translating for
Western audiences.

Adichie wrote like she expected African readers to understand. Like our
experiences mattered. Like our stories were worth telling without
filtering them through European perspectives first.

I read that book three times. Then a fourth. Each time finding new
layers. New meanings. New proof that African stories were valuable. That
African voices deserved platforms.

If Chimamanda Adichie could write stories that powerful, maybe I could
too. Not now. Not yet. But someday. If I learned. If I practiced. If I
refused to believe that storytelling was someone else\'s job.

Grandpa David noticed my reading habit. Started leaving books out for
me. Books he thought I would like. Books he thought I needed. Books
challenging or comforting or complicating how I saw the world.

\"Born a Crime\" by Trevor Noah appeared on my bed one day. I picked it
up, read the subtitle: \"Stories from a South African Childhood.\"

I finished it in two days. Read it again immediately.

Trevor Noah\'s story was different from mine but similar in ways that
mattered. Mixed-race kid in a place where mixing was not allowed. Poor
but funny. Surviving through wit and words when other resources were not
available.

He had turned his voice into currency. Turned his story into comedy.
Turned his childhood into a career. Made the world listen by refusing to
be quiet about where he came from and what he had survived.

If Trevor Noah could do it, maybe I could too.

Different country. Different context. Different challenges. But same
principle: Your voice is valuable. Your story matters. Being loud is not
a character flaw. Being loud is a survival strategy.

And when survival strategies get refined enough, they become careers.

Grandpa David also gave us assignments. Not school assignments. Life
assignments. Questions without single answers. Essays about topics we
had never considered.

\"Write about what peace means to you.\"

\"Explain why people fight.\"

\"Describe a time when you forgave someone.\"

\"Tell me about a leader you admire and why.\"

These were not graded. Were not checked for grammar or spelling. He
wanted to see what we thought. How we processed. Whether we could take
abstract concepts and turn them into concrete words.

I hated these assignments at first. They were hard. Required thinking
beyond memorization. Required me to have opinions about things I had
never thought about.

But slowly, I started enjoying them. Started using them to figure out
what I believed. About peace. About justice. About leadership. About
what kind of person I wanted to become.

Grandpa David would read my essays. Nod. Ask questions. Never told me I
was wrong. Asked why I thought that. What evidence I had. Whether I had
considered alternative perspectives.

He was training me to think critically. Training me to question.
Training me to understand that intelligence is not about having all the
answers. Intelligence is about knowing which questions to ask.

The library taught me something school never could. That learning
happens everywhere. That education is not confined to classrooms or
textbooks or formal instruction.

Sometimes education is sitting in a room full of books you do not
understand yet, picking one at random, reading until your eyes hurt,
putting it back, and knowing you have changed even if you cannot
articulate how.

Sometimes education is reading about genocide in Rwanda and realizing
that violence is a choice, not an inevitability. That people who grow up
together can become enemies, but they can also become neighbors again.
That forgiveness is possible even when it seems impossible.

Sometimes education is reading about Trevor Noah\'s mother throwing him
out of a moving car to save his life from his stepfather, and
understanding that love sometimes looks like abandonment. That survival
sometimes requires impossible choices. That judging people without
knowing their full story is arrogance disguised as morality.

Grandpa David\'s library did not teach me facts alone. The library
taught me empathy. Nuance. The ability to hold multiple truths
simultaneously without feeling like one negates the other.

And most importantly, the library taught me that words were weapons. Not
violent weapons. Transformative weapons. The kind changing minds,
hearts, systems.

The kind I wanted to learn to use.

*\"Roylandz Rule #14: Libraries are dangerous places for people who want
to keep you small.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 14

## THE SCOUTS, THE SONGS, AND THE SPINE

I joined the Boy Scouts in Class 1 at Mahemas Primary School. Seven
years old. Barely tall enough to see over the desk. Already wearing a
uniform I would not fully understand for years.

Most kids joined scouts in Class 4 or 5. When they were old enough to
understand the principles. Old enough to follow complex instructions.
Old enough to tie knots and read maps and survive in the wild for more
than fifteen minutes without crying for their mothers.

I joined at seven because someone told me scouts went on adventures. And
adventures sounded better than sitting at home watching cows.

The uniform was the first challenge. Khaki shirt. Khaki shorts. A scarf
in colors I cannot remember now. A belt with a buckle I spent weeks
learning to fasten properly. Badges you earned by doing things I did not
yet know how to do.

We did not have money for a new uniform. So my mother found a used one.
Too big. The shirt hung on me like a dress. The shorts reached my knees
when they were supposed to stop mid-thigh. The scarf was frayed at the
edges.

But I wore it with pride. Because wearing a uniform meant belonging to
something bigger than yourself. Meant being part of a group with rules
and rituals and a history stretching back further than my lifetime.

Scout meetings happened every Saturday. Eight AM sharp. No excuses. You
showed up or you explained yourself the following week, and the
explanation better be good.

We would gather in formation. Stand at attention. Salute the flag.
Recite the scout promise and law. Then break into activities teaching us
skills we would need for life.

Knot tying. Map reading. First aid. Fire starting. Tent pitching.
Survival skills for the wilderness and metaphors for life.

I was terrible at most of it at first. My knots fell apart. My maps made
no sense. My first aid consisted of panicking and yelling for help. My
fires produced smoke but no flames.

But I kept showing up. Kept trying. Kept failing until failing turned
into learning and learning turned into knowing.

The scout law became my second set of commandments after Grandma
Rosemary\'s rules. A scout is trustworthy. Loyal. Helpful. Friendly.
Courteous. Kind. Obedient. Cheerful. Thrifty. Brave. Clean. Reverent.

Twelve words shaping how I saw the world and my place in it.

Music festivals were another matter entirely. Different from scouts but
equally important. A chance to represent your school, showcase your
talent, and compete against other schools in singing, dancing, and
performance.

I joined the school choir in Class 3. Not because I was a good singer.
Because I was loud. And loud, when properly directed, sounds like
confidence.

We practiced every week. After school. In the same classroom where we
learned math and English. The desks pushed aside. The teacher at the
front, clapping rhythm, correcting pitch, demanding excellence from
children who had never been told they were excellent at anything.

The songs were in Kiswahili. In Luhya. In English. Songs about Kenya.
About independence. About unity. About the future being bright if we
worked hard and stayed together.

I loved the harmonies. The way voices layered on top of each other
creating something bigger than any single voice could create alone. The
way singing together required you to listen, to blend, to support the
people around you even when you wanted to stand out.

Music festivals took us to places we had never been. Kakamega town.
Eldoret. Kisumu. Cities that felt like different planets compared to
Manyonyi.

We would travel in buses borrowed or rented for the occasion. Packed
tight. Sweating. Singing the whole way because we were too excited to
sit quietly.

The competition was fierce. Schools with better resources. Schools with
trained music teachers. Schools with students who had been singing since
they could talk.

And then there was us. Village kids in wrinkled uniforms. Barefoot or in
worn shoes. Carrying lunch in plastic bags because we could not afford
the food sold at the venues.

But when we sang, none of that mattered. The judges did not care where
we came from. They cared about harmony. About pitch. About performance.
About whether we moved them.

And sometimes we did.

We did not always win. Most times we did not. But we competed. We
represented our school. We proved that poverty was not a
disqualification from excellence.

And somewhere in all that singing and competing and traveling to places
that smelled different and sounded different, I learned something
crucial.

Your voice is powerful not because it is perfect. Your voice is powerful
because it is yours.

Scouts taught me discipline. Structure. The importance of showing up
even when you do not feel like it. The value of learning skills you
might never use but knowing you can use them if you need to.

Music taught me harmony. Collaboration. The beauty of blending with
others while still being heard. The courage required to open your mouth
and let sound come out knowing people are listening and judging.

Together, they taught me spine. Not the biological kind. The
metaphorical kind. The kind keeping you upright when life wants to bend
you. The kind making you stand when sitting would be easier. The kind
reminding you that your presence matters even when you feel small.

By the time I left Mahemas Primary School for Lumakanda Township
Primary, I had been a scout for years. I had competed in music
festivals. I had learned to tie knots and sing harmonies and stand at
attention without fidgeting.

I had developed a spine.

And that spine would carry me through everything coming next.

*\"Roylandz Rule #15: Discipline shapes you. Music fills you. Together
they build a spine strong enough to carry your dreams.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 15

## KCPE: KENYA\'S NATIONAL ANXIETY FESTIVAL

KCPE. Kenya Certificate of Primary Education.

Four letters that turned every Class 8 student into a nervous wreck for
an entire year.

KCPE was not an exam. KCPE was a judgment. A verdict. A single set of
tests determining your entire future. Whether you would go to a good
secondary school or a bad one. Whether your parents would be proud or
disappointed. Whether your years of primary education had been worth it.

The exam covered five subjects: English, Kiswahili, Mathematics,
Science, and Social Studies. Five papers. Five days. Maximum score: 500
marks.

Anything above 400 was considered excellent. Get you into a national
school. Make your parents cry tears of joy. Turn you into a village
celebrity.

Anything above 350 was good. Get you into a provincial school. Make your
parents satisfied. Give you options.

Anything above 300 was acceptable. Get you into a district school. Keep
your parents calm. Keep your future open.

Below 300? You were in trouble. Limited options. Disappointment.
Whispers about how you had wasted your primary education.

I took KCPE in 2015. Class 8 at Lumakanda Township Primary School.
Thirteen years old. Terrified. Determined.

The preparation started a year earlier. Class 7. Teachers began
mentioning KCPE like it was a monster living under the bed. Waiting.
Watching. Ready to devour students who did not study hard enough.

By Class 8, KCPE was all anyone talked about. Teachers pushed us harder.
Homework increased. Practice exams every week. Mock tests every month.
Constant reminders that this exam would determine our futures.

The pressure was intense. Not the supportive kind. The crushing kind.
The kind making you wake up at 4 AM to study by lamplight because you
were terrified of failing. The kind making you skip meals because your
stomach was too knotted with anxiety to eat.

Parents felt the pressure too. My grandparents, David and Gladys, tried
to stay calm. But I could see the concern in their eyes. The way they
would ask about my studies. The way they would encourage me to revise
more. The way they prayed specifically for my success during family
prayers.

The stakes were clear. Do well, and your future opens. Do poorly, and
your options narrow fast.

I studied like my life depended on it. Because in a way, it did.

Mornings: Wake up at 5 AM. Study until 7 AM. Breakfast. School.

Afternoons: School until 4 PM. Home. Change. Study until 8 PM.

Evenings: Dinner. More studying. Bed by 10 PM.

Weekends: Study. Study. Study.

I memorized everything. English grammar rules. Kiswahili vocabulary.
Math formulas. Science concepts. Social studies facts about Kenyan
geography, history, government.

My exercise books were filled with notes. Color-coded. Organized.
Reviewed repeatedly until I could recite them in my sleep.

The mock exams were brutal. Designed to be harder than the real exam.
Designed to prepare us for the worst. Designed to separate students who
were ready from students who were fooling themselves.

I scored well on mocks. Not perfect. But well enough to give me
confidence. Well enough to make my teachers believe I had a shot at a
good score.

But confidence and anxiety lived together in my chest. Every day. Every
hour. A constant tension between believing I could do it and fearing I
would fail.

The exam period arrived in November 2015. Five days. Five papers. Five
chances to prove I had learned something in eight years of primary
school.

The government takes KCPE seriously. Exam papers are printed and sealed.
Transported under armed guard. Opened in front of students by
invigilators who treat the process like a military operation.

You cannot cheat. Phones are banned. Calculators are banned. Anything
except pens, pencils, rulers, and your brain is banned.

If you are caught cheating, your exam is cancelled. Your school is
penalized. Your future is destroyed.

So nobody cheated. At least not obviously.

The first paper was English. I walked into the exam room feeling like I
was walking into battle. Stomach churning. Hands shaking. Praying I
remembered everything I had studied.

The invigilator opened the envelope. Distributed the papers. Set the
timer.

\"You have two hours. Begin.\"

I opened the paper. Read the instructions. Started answering.

And something strange happened.

The anxiety faded. The fear quieted. Muscle memory took over. Eight
years of education flowing through my pen onto the paper.

I knew these answers. I had prepared for these questions. I was ready.

By the time I finished the first paper, I felt different. Calmer. More
confident. Like I had crossed a threshold and could not go back.

The remaining four papers followed the same pattern. Fear before. Focus
during. Relief after.

Five days passed. The exams ended. The papers were collected and sealed.
Shipped to Nairobi for marking. And then we waited.

Waiting for KCPE results is torture. A month of not knowing. A month of
replaying every question. A month of wondering if you did enough.

The results came in January 2016. Released nationally. Announced on
radio. Posted at schools.

I scored 287 marks out of 500.

Not excellent. Not terrible. Solidly average. Good enough for a district
school. Good enough to keep my future open. Not good enough to make me a
village celebrity.

My grandparents were proud. They hugged me. Told me I had done well.
Reminded me that marks do not define worth.

But I was disappointed. I had wanted 300. Wanted to break that barrier.
Wanted to prove I was more than average.

I missed it by 13 marks. Thirteen marks that felt like a canyon between
where I was and where I wanted to be.

But KCPE taught me something important. Something I would carry with me
forever.

Success is not always about hitting your target. Sometimes success is
about showing up. About doing your best. About finishing what you
started even when the outcome is uncertain.

I had shown up. I had done my best. I had finished.

And that, in its own way, was enough.

*\"Roylandz Rule #16: Exams measure what you know. Life measures what
you do with what you know.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 16

## THE BOREHOLE CALL

The call came on a Tuesday afternoon. I was at the borehole fetching
water. Carrying two jerricans. Twenty liters total when full. Heavy
enough to make your arms shake and your legs wobble on the walk home.

The borehole was a community water source. A hand-pump system bringing
groundwater to the surface. Free for anyone to use. Always crowded.
Always a line of people waiting with containers.

You would pump the handle up and down. Up and down. Up and down. Water
would sputter out. Slow at first. Then faster. Filling your container
drop by drop.

Filling twenty liters took time. Your arms would burn. Your back would
ache. But you kept pumping because water was life and complaining would
not make it flow faster.

I was mid-pump when I heard my name.

\"Eugine!\"

I turned. Saw my grandmother Rosemary Obbayi walking toward me. Fast.
Urgent. Holding a mobile phone.

\"Your grandfather wants to talk to you,\" she said.

Grandpa Samson Kemoli. The man who built Kemoview. The man who never
called unless something important needed saying.

I took the phone. \"Hello?\"

\"Eugine,\" his voice came through. Calm. Direct. \"You are finished
with primary school now. You have your KCPE results.\"

\"Yes, Grandpa.\"

\"What do you want to study?\"

The question caught me off guard. What did I want to study? Nobody had
asked me that before. Education was not about what you wanted. Education
was about what you could afford and what schools would accept you.

\"I want to go to a good secondary school,\" I said. \"Study well. Maybe
become a journalist.\"

There was a pause. A long one. I could hear him thinking through the
phone.

\"There is a school,\" he said. \"Murgusi Seventh-day Adventist High
School. It is a good school. I can pay your fees. But you need to apply.
Get accepted. Show me you are serious.\"

I stood there at the borehole. Water overflowing from my jerrican. Phone
pressed against my ear. Brain trying to process what I was hearing.

My grandfather was offering to pay for secondary school. Four years.
Fees, uniform, books, everything. The kind of opportunity most village
kids never got. The kind of opportunity that could change everything.

\"Are you serious?\" I asked.

\"Am I ever not serious?\"

Fair point. Grandpa Samson did not joke about money or education.

\"What do I need to do?\" I asked.

\"Get the admission letter. Bring me the fee structure. We will handle
the rest.\"

\"Thank you, Grandpa.\"

\"Do not thank me. Prove me right.\"

The call ended. I handed the phone back to Grandma Rosemary. Finished
pumping water in a daze. Lifted the jerricans. Started walking home.

But I was not thinking about the weight. I was thinking about the
future. About Murgusi SDA High School. About the fact that someone
believed in me enough to invest in my education.

Grandpa Samson was not rich. He ran small shops in Kemoview. Made enough
to survive but not enough to waste. Every shilling mattered. Every
expense was calculated.

And he was choosing to spend his money on me. On my education. On my
future.

That call at the borehole changed everything. Not because it guaranteed
success. Because it removed the biggest obstacle standing between me and
my dreams.

Money.

I had scored 287 marks in KCPE. Good enough for a district school but
not good enough for a scholarship. My family could not afford school
fees. Could barely afford food some months.

Without Grandpa Samson, my options would have been limited. A cheap day
school. A school with overcrowded classrooms and underpaid teachers. A
school where education was something that happened to you instead of
something you pursued.

But with Grandpa Samson\'s support, I had options. I could go to a
boarding school. A school with structure and discipline and teachers who
cared. A school where education was taken seriously.

I did not have a phone then. Could not call him back to say thank you
again. Could not tell him how much that call meant.

But I promised myself something that day at the borehole. I would not
waste this opportunity. I would study harder than I had ever studied. I
would make him proud. I would prove that his investment was worth it.

That call was not just about school fees. That call was about belief.
About someone seeing potential in you when you barely see it in
yourself. About someone investing in your future when the return is
uncertain.

Grandpa Samson did not ask for proof of my grades. Did not demand
guarantees. Did not attach conditions beyond \"show me you are
serious.\"

He believed in me. And that belief became fuel. Became motivation.
Became the foundation for everything that came next.

Years later, I would understand the full weight of what he did. How rare
it is to find someone willing to invest in you without expecting
immediate returns. How precious it is to have family members who see
beyond your current circumstances to your future potential.

But in that moment at the borehole, I understood one thing clearly.

I had been given a chance. A real chance. The kind that does not come
twice.

And I was not going to waste it.

*\"Roylandz Rule #17: Belief is the most valuable investment anyone can
make in you. Honor it by becoming who they saw you could be.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 17

## NAIROBI NOISE: MY FIRST CITY SHOCK

I visited Nairobi for the first time in 2014. Class 7. Twelve years old.
On a school trip costing 3,000 shillings I had saved for months to
afford.

Three thousand shillings was a fortune. More than some families earned
in a month. More than I had ever held in my hand at once.

I raised it through odd jobs. Washing clothes for neighbors. Herding
cows for relatives. Running errands for anyone willing to pay.
Collecting coins in a tin under my bed until the tin was full and the
dream was real.

The trip was organized by the school. A reward for top-performing
students. A chance to see the capital city. To visit museums and
monuments and places we had only read about in textbooks.

We traveled by bus. Left Lumakanda at 5 AM. Drove through towns and
villages. Passed matatu stages and roadside markets. Watched the
landscape change from rural to semi-urban to fully urban as we
approached Nairobi.

The city announced itself long before we arrived. Traffic. Noise.
Buildings taller than anything I had ever seen. Cars everywhere. People
everywhere. Movement and chaos and energy pulsing like a living thing.

I pressed my face against the bus window. Eyes wide. Brain trying to
process the scale of what I was seeing.

This was not Lumakanda. This was not even Kakamega town. This was
something else entirely. Something bigger. Louder. Faster.

We arrived in Nairobi mid-morning. The bus parked. We disembarked. And I
stepped into the city for the first time.

The noise hit me first. Not bird songs or cow sounds or wind through
trees. Car horns. Matatu conductors shouting for passengers. Street
vendors advertising wares. Music blasting from shops. People talking
loudly into phones. A symphony of urban chaos.

The smells came next. Exhaust fumes. Roasted maize. Grilled meat.
Perfume. Sweat. Garbage. All of it mixing together in a way making my
nose confused about whether to enjoy or reject.

The sights were overwhelming. Buildings stretching toward the sky. Glass
windows reflecting sunlight. Billboards advertising products I had never
heard of. People dressed in clothes I had only seen on TV.

And the people. So many people. Moving fast. Walking with purpose.
Nobody making eye contact. Nobody stopping to chat. Everyone rushing
somewhere as if late for something important.

We spent the day touring. Visited the Nairobi National Museum. Saw
artifacts and exhibits about Kenyan history. Learned about tribes and
traditions and the path to independence.

We visited Uhuru Park. A massive green space in the middle of the city.
Walked around. Sat on the grass. Ate packed lunches while watching
people jog and families picnic and couples take photos.

But the real education happened between the official stops. Watching how
city people moved. How they talked. How they carried themselves with a
confidence I did not yet have.

I noticed everything. The way they dressed. The way they walked. The way
they ordered food at restaurants without looking at prices. The way they
seemed comfortable in spaces that made me feel like an intruder.

We slept that night in Kangemi slums. Not a hotel. A church. Provided
free accommodation for school groups. Mattresses on the floor. Shared
bathrooms. No hot water.

Kangemi was poverty in a different accent. Not village poverty. Urban
poverty. Packed together. Living on top of each other. Iron sheet walls.
Narrow paths between houses. Noise and smoke and humanity compressed
into impossible density.

It was poorer than Manyonyi in some ways. But richer in others. Because
being poor in the city meant being close to opportunities. Close to
jobs. Close to schools. Close to possibilities.

Being poor in the village meant being far from everything. Isolated.
Forgotten.

The next morning, we went to Uhuru Park again. Budget reading day. The
finance minister was presenting the national budget. Security was tight.
Police everywhere. GSU officers in full gear. Barriers and checkpoints.

We were walking around when I saw them. GSU officers harassing my
friend. Asking for his ID. Treating him like a criminal for no reason
except being young and Black and in the wrong place at their wrong time.

I walked over. \"What is the problem?\"

The officer turned to me. \"Who are you?\"

\"His friend. Why are you harassing him?\"

\"We are not harassing anyone. We are doing our job.\"

\"Your job is to protect citizens, not intimidate them.\"

The officer looked at me differently then. Not with anger. With
curiosity. Like he was trying to figure out if I was brave or stupid.

Another officer joined. Older. More stripes on his uniform. \"What is
happening here?\"

I explained. Calmly. Respectfully. Cited the constitution. Talked about
rights and responsibilities. Used words I had learned from Grandpa
David\'s books.

The senior officer listened. Looked at my friend. Looked at the younger
officer. \"Let him go.\"

Then he turned to me. \"What do you want to be?\"

\"I want to be a journalist.\"

He smiled. \"You should consider law first. If that does not work, then
journalism. You argue well.\"

That conversation stayed with me. Not because it was dramatic. Because
it was a moment of recognition. A moment where an adult authority figure
saw something in me beyond my age or my village background or my
wrinkled school uniform.

He saw potential. And he named it.

Nairobi terrified me and fascinated me in equal measure. Terrified me
because it was so big and fast and indifferent. Fascinated me because it
was full of possibilities I could not even name yet.

I returned to Lumakanda changed. Not dramatically. But subtly. I had
seen what was possible. Had tasted a life different from the one I was
living. Had glimpsed a future I wanted to build.

That trip cost 3,000 shillings. But the education I got was priceless.

*\"Roylandz Rule #18: Sometimes you need to see where you are going
before you know how to get there.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 18

## BACK TO LUGARI: THE RETURN

After four years in Lumakanda, I moved back to Lugari. Not to Manyonyi.
Not back to the mud hut and cow dung floors and kerosene lamps.

To Kemoview. With Grandpa Samson and Grandma Josephine.

The move happened in late 2015. After KCPE. After the borehole call.
After Grandpa Samson had committed to paying my secondary school fees.

It made practical sense. Murgusi SDA High School was closer to Kemoview
than to Lumakanda. Being near Grandpa Samson meant easier coordination
for fees and supplies. Meant being close to the man investing in my
future.

But it also meant leaving Grandpa David and Grandma Gladys. Leaving the
library. Leaving the routine and structure and discipline that had
shaped me for four years.

Leaving was harder than arriving had been. Because leaving meant saying
goodbye to people who had become family. People who had taken me in when
I needed shelter and given me more than a roof. Given me books and
discipline and belief.

Grandpa David hugged me goodbye. \"Keep reading. Keep thinking. Keep
asking questions.\"

Grandma Gladys was more practical. \"Remember what you learned here.
Discipline. Structure. Do not waste what we gave you.\"

I promised I would remember. Promised I would not waste it. Promised I
would make them proud.

And then I left. Back to Lugari. Back to the place I had come from but
was no longer the same person who had left.

Kemoview felt different this time. Or maybe I felt different. Either
way, something had changed.

The house had electricity now. Not reliable electricity. But more
reliable than before. Lights that worked most evenings. A TV that
received a few channels when the signal was strong.

Grandpa Samson was older. Walked slower. The limp more pronounced. But
his mind was sharp. His memory perfect. His expectations clear.

\"You are here to prepare for secondary school,\" he said. \"I am paying
your fees. You will study hard. You will make it worth it.\"

Not a question. A statement. An expectation.

Grandma Josephine welcomed me back quietly. Cooked my favorite foods.
Made sure my clothes were clean. Asked about school without pushing.
Provided stability without drama.

The months between KCPE results and secondary school admission were
strange. Liminal. I was done with primary school but not yet started
with secondary school. Between chapters. Waiting for the next one to
begin.

I helped at Grandpa\'s shops. Served customers. Counted money. Learned
business by watching and doing. Saw how he treated people. How he
remembered debts. How he balanced kindness with firmness.

I also helped around the compound. Herded the four cows. Fetched water
from the borehole. Did chores without being asked because I understood
what was at stake.

Grandpa Samson was investing in me. The least I could do was prove I was
worth the investment.

During those months, I thought a lot about the journey so far. About
Manyonyi and Lumakanda and everywhere in between. About the people who
had raised me when my parents could not. About the lessons learned in
kitchens and classrooms and libraries.

I thought about the boy who had read to chickens. Who had walked
barefoot to school. Who had carried cow dung on Fridays. Who had been
mocked for pronouncing words wrong. Who had cried when his parents left
and learned to be loud when silence hurt too much.

That boy was still me. But I was also becoming someone else. Someone
with more education. More exposure. More possibilities.

The boy from Manyonyi was meeting the boy who wanted to be on TV. And
somewhere between those two versions, the person I would become was
taking shape.

Secondary school was coming. Murgusi SDA High School. Boarding school.
Four years away from home. Four years to prove myself. Four years to
build on everything I had learned and become something more.

I was ready. As ready as a thirteen-year-old can be for a future he
cannot fully see but can feel approaching like a storm on the horizon.

I was ready to leave Lugari again. But this time, I was leaving
differently. Not as a child being moved. As someone choosing to move
toward something.

The difference mattered.

\"Roylandz Rule #19: Going back is not always going backwards. Sometimes
you return to a place to launch forward.\"
