**PART III**

**BOARDING SCHOOL BATTLES**

**(2016-2019)**

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# 

# CHAPTER 19

## WELCOME TO MURGUSI: THE DORM THAT TAUGHT DISCIPLINE

January 2016. Murgusi Secondary School. Lugari Sub-County, Kakamega
County.

Boarding school is prison with homework.

Let me be clear about that upfront. You are locked in. You cannot leave.
You follow rules you did not write. You eat what they serve. You sleep
when they say. You wake when they command. And if you complain, there
are consequences.

The only difference between boarding school and actual prison is that
your parents pay for you to be there. And somehow that makes it
educational.

Murgusi Secondary School sat in the heart of Lugari, a mixed day and
boarding school known for discipline, academics, and producing students
who either loved or hated authority depending on which year you asked
them.

The school was not fancy. Not one of those national schools with
swimming pools and computer labs and manicured lawns. Murgusi was a
district school. Working class. The kind of school serving students
whose parents had dreams bigger than their bank accounts.

But what Murgusi lacked in resources, it made up for in structure.
Rules. Discipline. The kind of environment turning village kids into
college students through sheer force of routine.

I arrived on a Saturday. Reporting day. The compound was chaos. Parents
dropping off students. Matrons shouting instructions. Prefects directing
traffic. Form Ones looking terrified. Form Fours looking bored. Everyone
else looking like they wished they were somewhere else.

My metal box contained everything I owned for the next four months.
Uniforms. Bedding. Toiletries. Books. A few personal items. All packed
tight because space in a metal box is limited and choices must be made.

The dormitories were long buildings with iron sheet roofs. Rows of bunk
beds. Forty students per dorm. Eighty if you counted both levels.
Lockers made from wood. Windows with no glass. Concrete floors that
stayed cold year-round.

I was assigned a lower bunk. Bed number 23. The mattress was thin. The
sheets were scratchy. The pillow was a folded blanket because we had not
been told to bring pillows and nobody had money for extras.

The student above me introduced himself. \"Evans. Form Two. Do not touch
my stuff and we will be fine.\"

Great. Rooming with someone whose opening line was a threat. This was
going to be fun.

The rules were explained during assembly that evening. And there were
many rules. So many rules that I wondered if the school administration
sat around inventing new ones for entertainment.

Wake up: 5 AM. No exceptions.

Morning preps: 5:30 AM to 6:30 AM. Study in silence or face
consequences.

Breakfast: 6:45 AM. Eat fast. The line is long.

Classes: 7:30 AM to 4 PM. Pay attention. Take notes. Do not sleep.

Games: 4 PM to 5:30 PM. Mandatory sports. Sitting is not a sport.

Evening preps: 6 PM to 9 PM. More studying. Still in silence.

Lights out: 9:30 PM. Sleep immediately. Talking is forbidden.

Murgusi ran on the Seventh-day Adventist calendar. Sabbath was Friday
sunset to Saturday sunset. No work. No sports. No entertainment. Pure
rest and worship.

Which sounds peaceful until you realize that teenage boys locked in a
dorm with nothing to do for twenty-four hours will find ways to
entertain themselves. And those ways usually involved getting into
trouble.

The food was prison food with religious justification. No pork.
Seventh-day Adventists do not eat pork. No meat most days. Vegetables.
Beans. Ugali. Rice when the budget allowed. Milk that tasted like
sadness mixed with water.

Breakfast was uji, a thin porridge that was more water than grain. You
drank it fast because it was not good and lingering made it worse.

Lunch was beans and ugali. Beans and rice. Beans and chapati if you were
lucky. Beans and more beans if you were not.

Dinner was leftovers from lunch with a new name. Yesterday\'s beans
became today\'s stew. Last week\'s cabbage became this week\'s soup.

I lost weight in the first month. Not because I was exercising. Because
I was hungry. The portions were small. The quality was questionable. And
complaining got you lectured about gratitude and starving children in
Somalia.

But here is what boarding school teaches you that day school cannot.

Independence. You learn to wash your own clothes. Make your bed. Manage
your time. Solve your own problems without running to parents.

Resilience. You learn to survive on less. Sleep less. Eat less. Complain
less. Because complaining changes nothing and action changes everything.

Community. You learn to live with people you did not choose. People from
different tribes. Different religions. Different backgrounds. You learn
that getting along is a skill worth developing.

And discipline. You learn that structure is freedom. That rules create
space for growth. That doing hard things when you do not want to builds
character you cannot buy.

Murgusi was tough. The first week, I cried at night when nobody was
looking. Missed home. Missed Grandpa Samson and Grandma Josephine.
Missed sleeping past 5 AM. Missed food that tasted like food.

But by the second week, I adjusted. By the third week, I found my
rhythm. By the first month, I stopped counting days until mid-term break
and started focusing on making the most of where I was.

Because boarding school was not punishment. Boarding school was
preparation. Preparation for university. For work. For life in a world
that does not care if you are ready or not.

And Murgusi, with all its rules and terrible food and 5 AM wake-up
calls, was preparing me whether I liked it or not.

*\"Roylandz Rule #20: Boarding school is where comfort goes to die and
character comes to life.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 20

## FAITH VS. FUNK

Murgusi Secondary School had a split personality. On one hand,
Seventh-day Adventist values. On the other hand, teenage energy. The two
were constantly at war.

The school took faith seriously. We had worship every morning. Chapel
every Wednesday. Sabbath services every Saturday. Bible study groups.
Christian Union meetings. Prayer sessions before exams.

You could not escape God at Murgusi. God was in the curriculum. In the
rules. In the conversations. In the expectations. God was watching, and
the administration made sure you knew it.

I joined Christian Union in Form One. Not because I was exceptionally
holy. Because CU students got privileges. Extra food at events.
Permission to skip certain manual labor. Respect from teachers who
assumed CU members were well-behaved.

Also, CU meetings had good music. Not the slow hymns from my childhood.
Contemporary gospel. Songs you could actually move to. Songs making
worship feel less like duty and more like celebration.

By Form Two, I was CU leader. Leading prayers. Organizing events. Giving
testimonies about faith and God\'s goodness while secretly wondering if
God noticed I was faking enthusiasm sometimes.

Here is the thing about being a Christian Union leader in a boarding
school full of teenagers. You become a walking contradiction. You are
supposed to be holy. Set an example. Lead by living right.

But you are also a teenager. With hormones. With curiosity. With desires
that do not align with the Ten Commandments or school rules.

So you live in this weird space between who you are supposed to be and
who you are. Between faith and funk. Between prayer and temptation.
Between leading worship on Saturday and sneaking contraband on Monday.

The contraband at Murgusi was not drugs or alcohol. The contraband was
sugar. Specifically, glucose sweets. Chocolate when someone\'s parents
visited. Soda smuggled in metal boxes. Anything with flavor beyond beans
and ugali.

Teachers called it \"junk food.\" Students called it \"survival
supplies.\" We called it different things, but we all wanted the same
result. Something making life taste better.

I never got caught with contraband. Not because I was innocent. Because
I was strategic. Learned early that being CU leader gave me cover.
Teachers assumed I followed rules. Prefects gave me benefit of doubt. I
used that reputation like a shield while quietly breaking minor rules
behind it.

The music group was my other identity. I joined in Form One. Made leader
by Form Two. We performed at assemblies. School events. Music festivals.
Represented Murgusi in competitions across Kakamega County.

I was not the best singer. My voice was average. But I was loud.
Confident. Could hold a tune well enough. And I understood something
important about performance: Confidence sells better than perfection.

Our music group traveled to Bungoma, Kisumu, Nakuru, Masinde Muliro
University for competitions. We would pack into buses. Sing the whole
way there. Compete against schools with better resources. Sometimes win.
Sometimes lose. Always learn.

Music taught me stage presence. How to command attention. How to use
your voice not for volume alone but for effect. How to connect with an
audience through sound and emotion.

Those skills would serve me later. Much later. When stages got bigger
and audiences became cameras and performance became career.

But at Murgusi, music was fun. An escape from academics. A chance to be
creative in a system designed for conformity.

The tension between faith and funk showed up everywhere. In
conversations. In choices. In the constant negotiation between what you
were supposed to do and what you wanted to do.

Take relationships. Dating was forbidden at Murgusi. Completely
forbidden. Get caught with a girlfriend and you faced suspension. Get
caught writing love letters and you got publicly shamed during assembly.

But teenagers are teenagers. Hormones do not respect school rules. So
relationships happened. Secretly. Carefully. Through coded language and
stolen glances and notes passed during class changes.

I had my first girlfriend at Murgusi. Multiple relationships over four
years. Nothing serious. Nothing lasting. Teenage romance that felt
important at the time and silly looking back.

My first kiss happened in Form Three. Her name was Millicent. She wore a
red T-shirt and gray skirt. We were behind the dining hall. Quick.
Awkward. The kind of kiss you remember not because it was good but
because it was first.

I felt guilty immediately after. Not because kissing was wrong. Because
I was CU leader and CU leaders were not supposed to be sneaking behind
dining halls kissing girls in red T-shirts.

But I also felt alive. Because breaking rules, even small ones, reminded
me I was human. Not perfect. Not holy. Not the person everyone thought I
was. A teenager trying to figure out who he was while wearing masks for
different audiences.

Faith and funk coexisted at Murgusi. You could be CU leader on Saturday
and sneak sweets on Monday. You could lead worship and have a secret
girlfriend. You could pray before exams and cheat during them if
desperation hit hard enough.

The question was not whether you would balance faith and funk. The
question was how. How would you navigate the gap between who you were
supposed to be and who you were? How would you reconcile expectations
with reality? How would you grow up without losing yourself in the
process?

I did not have good answers then. Still working on them now. But Murgusi
taught me that perfection is a lie. That everyone is performing to some
degree. That the goal is not to be perfect but to be honest about your
imperfections.

Faith without funk is rigid. Funk without faith is chaotic. Somewhere in
the middle is where real people live.

And I was learning to be a real person. Messy. Complicated. Trying my
best. Failing often. Growing slowly.

One contradiction at a time.

*\"Roylandz Rule #21: You can be holy and human at the same time. Anyone
telling you otherwise is lying or delusional.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 21

## ENTERTAINMENT CAPTAIN WITHOUT APPLAUSE

Form Three. Entertainment Captain. A title sounding more impressive than
it was.

Being Entertainment Captain at Murgusi meant organizing events nobody
wanted to attend. Planning entertainment for students who would rather
sleep. Convincing teenagers that participating in school activities was
better than sitting in the dorm complaining about being bored.

It was a thankless job. But someone had to do it. And the administration
decided that someone was me.

My responsibilities included organizing talent shows, coordinating drama
performances, managing music events, and keeping students entertained
without breaking school rules or the budget.

The budget was the bigger problem. Murgusi did not have money for
entertainment. The school barely had money for food. Expecting funds for
fun was like expecting rain in the desert. Possible, but do not hold
your breath.

So I learned to create something from nothing. Organize talent shows
using student talent alone. No hired performers. No expensive equipment.
Students singing, dancing, acting, telling jokes. Free entertainment
performed by people who would be there anyway.

The first talent show I organized was a disaster. Three people showed
up. Three. Out of four hundred students. Three brave souls willing to
sit in the assembly hall on a Saturday afternoon watching their
classmates embarrass themselves.

I stood on stage looking at an almost empty hall and realized something
important. If you build it, they will not necessarily come. Especially
if \"it\" is a talent show during Sabbath when everyone would rather be
sleeping.

But I learned. Adjusted. Tried again. The second show had better
marketing. Posters. Announcements. Peer pressure. Threats that
attendance was mandatory disguised as \"highly encouraged.\"

Twenty people showed up. Better than three. Still terrible. But
progress.

By Form Four, I had figured it out. The secret to getting students to
attend events was simple. Make it worth their time. Give them something
they could not get elsewhere. Music they wanted to hear. Comedy making
them laugh. Drama reflecting their lives.

And food. Always have food. Students will attend anything if there is
food involved. That is a universal law of boarding school.

My biggest success was TIBS Got Talent. Wait, wrong school. That was
later. At Murgusi, my biggest success was the Form Four farewell party.
An event celebrating graduating students. Music. Drama. Dance. Speeches.
Tears. Laughter. The whole emotional package.

I organized everything. Selected performers. Coordinated timing. Made
sure teachers were happy and students were entertained. Balanced respect
for authority with giving students what they wanted.

The event was a success. Students laughed. Teachers smiled. The
principal complimented my organizational skills. I felt proud.

But here is what nobody tells you about being Entertainment Captain. You
work hardest on events you barely enjoy. You organize shows you cannot
fully attend because you are managing logistics. You create fun for
others while sacrificing your own.

That was my introduction to leadership. Not the glamorous kind. The
behind-the-scenes kind. The kind where your value is measured by other
people\'s happiness. Where success means nobody notices you because
everything ran smoothly.

I also led the Scout group. Same pattern. Organize camping trips.
Coordinate badge ceremonies. Teach skills. Manage chaos. Make it look
easy when it was anything but.

And I was First Aid leader. Trained students in basic first aid.
Organized health events. Kept the first aid kit stocked. Responded to
emergencies ranging from serious injuries to students faking illness to
skip class.

You learn to spot fakers fast. They always complain about stomach pain.
Always during math class. Always recover miraculously once class is
over.

Being a leader at Murgusi taught me skills I did not appreciate at the
time. Organization. Communication. Problem-solving under pressure.
Managing egos and emotions. Balancing multiple responsibilities without
losing your mind.

These were not skills you learned from textbooks. These were skills you
learned by doing. By failing. By trying again. By figuring out what
worked through trial and error.

Years later, when I became Entertainment Captain at Thika Institute,
when I organized TIBS Got Talent, when I started managing shows and
events professionally, I would trace those skills back to Murgusi. Back
to organizing talent shows nobody attended. Back to leading groups
nobody wanted to join. Back to creating something from nothing because
nothing was all we had.

Entertainment Captain was not glamorous. But it was training.
Preparation. Boot camp for a future I could not see yet but was being
shaped for one failed talent show at a time.

*\"Roylandz Rule #22: Leadership is not about the applause you receive.
Leadership is about the work you do when nobody is clapping.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 22

## THE BOY WHO DEBATED SOLDIERS

March 2019. Uhuru Park, Nairobi. Budget Day.

We were on a school trip. Final year. Form Four. The kind of trip
schools organize to reward students who survived four years of boarding
school without getting expelled or going insane.

The trip cost 3,000 shillings. Again. Same price as my Class 7 trip.
Inflation had not reached school trips yet, or the schools were too
cheap to raise prices. Either way, 3,000 shillings bought you a bus ride
to Nairobi, accommodation in Kangemi slums at a church, and a day
touring the city.

We left Murgusi early morning. Packed into a bus designed for forty
people but carrying sixty because schools believe physics is optional.
Sang the whole way. Laughed. Told jokes. Enjoyed freedom from routine
even if the freedom was temporary and chaperoned.

We spent the first night in Kangemi. Same church I had stayed at during
my Class 7 trip. Mattresses on the floor. Shared bathrooms. The smell of
urban poverty mixing with teenage sweat and cheap soap.

The next morning, we went to Uhuru Park. Budget reading day. The Cabinet
Secretary for Treasury was presenting the national budget. Security was
everywhere. Police. GSU. Barriers. Checkpoints. Tension thick enough to
taste.

We were walking around, taking photos, enjoying being in Nairobi, when I
saw them.

GSU officers. General Service Unit. The paramilitary wing of Kenya
Police. Known for crowd control. Known for not taking nonsense from
anyone.

They were harassing my friend. Asking for his ID. Treating him like a
criminal. Rough. Aggressive. The kind of treatment that assumes guilt
and demands proof of innocence.

I walked over. \"What is the problem?\"

The officer turned. Young. Maybe mid-twenties. Uniform pressed. Boots
shined. Face showing he was used to people backing down when he spoke.

\"Who are you?\"

\"His friend. Why are you harassing him?\"

\"We are not harassing anyone. We are doing our job.\"

\"Your job is protecting citizens, not intimidating them.\"

The officer looked at me differently. Trying to figure out if I was
brave or stupid or both.

\"You know the constitution?\" he asked.

\"I do. Article 29 guarantees freedom and security of the person.
Article 50 guarantees fair hearing. Article 51 protects rights of
arrested persons. You want me to continue?\"

I did not know if I was quoting correctly. I had read Grandpa David\'s
books about human rights. Paid attention during Civics class. But
standing in front of a GSU officer testing whether my classroom
knowledge worked in real life was different from reading it in a book.

The officer paused. Not backing down. Recalculating.

Another officer approached. Older. More stripes on his uniform. Senior.
\"What is happening here?\"

I explained. Calmly. Respectfully. No attitude. No aggression. Facts.
Rights. Constitution. The law as I understood it.

The senior officer listened. Looked at my friend. Looked at the younger
officer. \"Let him go.\"

Then he turned to me. Really looked at me. Like he was seeing something
beyond a school uniform and teenage confidence.

\"What do you want to be?\" he asked.

\"I want to be a journalist.\"

He smiled. Not mocking. Genuine. \"You should take law. If that fails,
journalism. You argue well.\"

That moment changed everything.

Not because I suddenly decided to become a lawyer. Because someone in
authority, someone who did not have to, recognized something in me. Saw
potential. Named it. Validated it.

That GSU commander gave me permission to believe in myself. Permission
to think my voice mattered. Permission to imagine a future where
standing up and speaking out was not just allowed but necessary.

I went back to my classmates buzzing. Told my girlfriend at the time
what happened. She looked at me like I was crazy.

\"You argued with GSU? Are you trying to get shot?\"

Fair point. GSU was not known for patience with civilians who talked
back. But something about that moment felt right. Felt like I was doing
what I was supposed to do. Using my voice. Standing up. Speaking out.

That trip to Uhuru Park was supposed to be about tourism. About seeing
monuments and learning history and taking photos for memories.

But what I remember most is not the monuments. Not the photos. Not even
the terrible church accommodation.

I remember standing in front of armed officers. Heart pounding. Mouth
moving faster than my brain. Voice steady even though I was terrified.
Doing what needed doing because someone had to and nobody else was
stepping up.

I remember the GSU commander\'s words. \"Take law. If that fails,
journalism.\"

And I remember deciding right then that journalism was not the backup
plan. Journalism was the goal. Law was interesting, but journalism was
calling.

Because journalism was about using your voice to tell stories. To hold
power accountable. To give voice to people who could not speak for
themselves. To stand between authority and abuse and say \"not today.\"

That was the career I wanted. The life I wanted. The person I wanted to
become.

A journalist. A storyteller. Someone who used words as weapons against
injustice and noise as power against silence.

The path was becoming clearer. Not easier. Clearer.

*\"Roylandz Rule #23: Your voice is your weapon. Learn to use it before
life forces you to.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 23

## KCSE AND THE CURSE OF EXPECTATIONS

Form Four. 2019. Kenya Certificate of Secondary Education.

The exam determining your entire future. Again. Because apparently KCPE
was not stressful enough, so Kenya decided to traumatize students twice.

KCSE was worse than KCPE. More subjects. Harder questions. Higher
stakes. The difference between university and unemployment. Between
making your parents proud and hearing \"I told you so\" for the rest of
your life.

The pressure started in Form Three. Teachers mentioning KCSE like it was
Judgment Day. Warning us that everything we had learned for four years
would be tested in one month. Reminding us that failure was not an
option because too many people had invested too much.

By Form Four, KCSE was all anyone talked about. Teachers pushed harder.
Homework multiplied. Mock exams every month. Practice papers every week.
Revision until your brain felt like overcooked ugali.

The mocks were brutal. Designed to be harder than the real exam.
Designed to break you down so the actual KCSE felt easier by comparison.
Designed to separate students who were ready from students who were
fooling themselves.

I performed well on mocks. Not excellent. Not terrible. Solid B range.
Good enough to aim for university. Good enough to make my grandparents\'
investment worth it.

But good enough was not what I wanted. I wanted excellent. Wanted A\'s.
Wanted to prove I was more than average. Wanted to make everyone who
believed in me look smart for believing.

The studying was intense. Wake up at 4 AM. Study until breakfast.
Classes all day. Study during breaks. Study after games. Study until
lights out. Study some more with a torch under the blanket after lights
out because sleep was for people without exams.

My brain was full. Overloaded. Crammed with information I would forget
the moment exams ended but needed desperately right now.

English literature. Kiswahili grammar. Mathematics formulas. Chemistry
equations. Physics laws. Biology systems. History dates. Geography
patterns. Religious knowledge.

Everything. All at once. Fighting for space in a brain already tired
from four years of nonstop academic combat.

Then, in the middle of KCSE prep, my parents separated.

Again.

Not the first time. They had separated before. Come back together.
Separated again. The cycle repeating like a bad song nobody wanted to
hear but kept playing anyway.

But this time felt different. Final. Like they were done trying. Done
pretending. Done forcing something that was broken.

The timing was terrible. One month before KCSE. When I needed stability.
When I needed focus. When I needed to believe everything would be okay.

Instead, I got chaos. Parents fighting. Family drama. Phone calls asking
me to pick sides. Pressure to be the adult when I was still trying to
figure out how to be a functioning teenager.

I pushed it down. Buried it. Told myself I would deal with family issues
after exams. Right now, exams were priority. Everything else could wait.

But emotions do not wait. They sit in your chest. They distract you
during study sessions. They show up in the middle of the night when you
are supposed to be sleeping. They make concentration feel impossible
even when you are desperate to focus.

KCSE started in November. Four weeks. Multiple papers per subject.
Morning papers. Afternoon papers. Papers that felt easy. Papers that
felt impossible. Papers where you knew every answer. Papers where you
guessed and prayed.

I walked out of some exams confident. Walked out of others defeated. The
inconsistency was frustrating. But there was no time to dwell. Next
paper tomorrow. Next subject the day after. Keep moving. Keep answering.
Keep hoping you did enough.

The exams ended in late November. Papers collected. Sealed. Shipped to
Nairobi for marking. And then we waited.

Waiting for KCSE results is torture. Two months of not knowing. Two
months of replaying questions. Two months of wondering if you passed or
failed or fell somewhere in the disappointing middle.

Results came in January 2020. Released nationally. Announced on radio.
Posted at schools.

I scored a C.

C. Not C+. Not B-. C.

Average. Middle of the pack. Good enough to get into a college
somewhere. Not good enough for the university I wanted. Not good enough
for the career I imagined.

Not good enough to make Grandpa Samson\'s investment feel worth it.

My father\'s reaction was worse than the grade. He was ashamed. Angry.
Disappointed. Said I had wasted four years. Said I had embarrassed the
family. Said I should have done better.

His words hurt more than the grade. Because the grade was a number. His
words were judgment. And judgment from parents cuts deeper than any exam
score.

I wanted to defend myself. Explain about the family drama. About the
separation happening during prep. About trying my best while everything
was falling apart.

But what was the point? Excuses do not change grades. Explanations do
not erase disappointment. I had scored a C. That was the reality.
Everything else was noise.

KCSE taught me a hard lesson. Your best is not always good enough.
Sometimes you work hard and still fall short. Sometimes life throws
chaos at you during critical moments and you have to perform anyway.
Sometimes expectations crush you even when you are trying to meet them.

But here is what I learned later, what I wish I knew then: Grades do not
define you. KCSE does not determine your worth. One exam does not
predict your future.

I scored a C in KCSE. And I still made it. Still became a journalist.
Still got on TV. Still built a career. Still proved that where you start
does not determine where you finish.

That C was not the end of my story. That C was a comma, not a period.

And anyone telling you otherwise is selling something you do not need to
buy.

*\"Roylandz Rule #24: Bad grades sting. But they do not define you
unless you let them.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 24

## THE YEAR MY PARENTS QUIT FIGHTING (FINALLY)

2018\. Form 4. The year my father stopped drinking.

Seventeen years. That is how long my father drank. Seventeen years of
alcohol being part of family life. Part of the chaos. Part of the reason
things kept breaking and never quite healing.

I do not remember when he started. I was too young. But I remember the
effects. The arguments. The tension. The way alcohol turned
conversations into fights and fights into silence and silence into
resentment that lasted for days.

My father was not a violent drunk. He did not hit. Did not rage. But
alcohol changed him. Made him distant. Made him unpredictable. Made it
hard to know which version of him would show up on any given day.

My mother dealt with it the only way she knew how. Left. Came back. Left
again. A cycle repeating until everyone was tired but nobody knew how to
stop.

Then in 2018, something shifted. My father quit. Cold turkey. No rehab.
No intervention. No dramatic moment where someone sat him down and
begged him to stop.

He just decided. And he stopped.

I was at school when I found out. Phone call from home. My mother\'s
voice on the other end sounding lighter than I had heard in years.

\"Your father stopped drinking.\"

I did not believe her at first. I had heard promises before. Had seen
attempts. Had watched him quit for a week, a month, then slide back into
old patterns.

\"For how long?\" I asked.

\"Three months now. He is serious this time.\"

Three months. Long enough to be real. Long enough to believe it might
last.

When I came home for mid-term break, I saw the difference. My father
looked healthier. Clearer. Present in a way he had not been for years.

We talked. Not about deep things. About school. About farming. About
plans for the future. Normal conversations that felt strange because
they were normal.

The house felt different too. Calmer. Less tension hanging in the air.
Less walking on eggshells wondering when the next argument would
explode.

My parents were not suddenly happy. Seventeen years of damage does not
heal in three months. But they were civil. Respectful. Trying.

And trying, after years of not trying, felt like progress.

My father quitting alcohol did not fix everything. Did not erase the
past. Did not undo the separations or the arguments or the years of
chaos.

But it changed the trajectory. Opened possibilities that had been
closed. Created space for healing that had not existed before.

I wish I could say my parents stayed together after that. That they
lived happily ever after. That my father staying sober meant everything
was fine.

But life is not that clean. They separated again in 2019. During my KCSE
prep. The final separation. The one that stuck.

But here is what matters. My father stayed sober. Even through the
separation. Even through the stress. Even through losing the family he
had fought so hard and so badly to keep.

He stayed sober. And that was its own kind of victory.

Addiction is a monster. It destroys families. Ruins lives. Steals
futures. And breaking free from it, even when everything else is falling
apart, is heroic.

My father was not perfect. Made mistakes. Hurt people. Failed at things
that mattered.

But he fought. And he won. At least in that one battle. And that win
mattered more than any loss that came after.

*\"Roylandz Rule #25: Quitting your demons is victory even when
everything else is falling apart.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 25

## LAW OR JOURNALISM? THE DAY DESTINY CALLED

The GSU commander\'s words stayed with me. \"Take law. If that fails,
journalism.\"

He meant well. Saw my argumentative skills and thought lawyer. Logical
conclusion. Good advice from someone who did not know me beyond one
conversation.

But I knew something he did not. I did not want to be a lawyer.

Lawyers deal with law. Journalists deal with truth. And truth, messy and
complicated and often uncomfortable, was what called to me.

I wanted to tell stories. Wanted to ask questions power did not want to
answer. Wanted to hold people accountable. Wanted to give voice to
people nobody was listening to.

Law was about winning arguments. Journalism was about finding truth.
Close, but not the same.

The decision became clear during Form 4. Not because of one moment.
Because of accumulation. Years of reading. Years of watching news. Years
of practicing voice. Years of realizing what made me come alive.

Journalism made me come alive. Standing in front of people. Asking
questions. Telling stories. Using words to move people. That was the
work I wanted to do.

My father wanted me to study law. Said it was prestigious. Said lawyers
made money. Said journalism was risky.

He was right. Journalism was risky. Still is. Low pay. High stress.
Constant deadlines. Job insecurity. The kind of career your parents warn
you against.

But risk and calling are not opposites. Sometimes the thing you are
meant to do is the thing everyone tells you not to do.

I applied to Thika Institute of Business Studies for a diploma in
journalism. Not university. Not a degree. A diploma. Two years. Cheaper.
Faster. Good enough to start.

Grandpa Samson made another call. Different from the borehole call. This
one was shorter.

\"You got into TIBS. What do you need?\"

\"Fees.\"

\"I will handle it. Do not waste my money.\"

That was it. No lecture. No conditions. No speech about making him
proud. Just support. Simple. Direct. The way Grandpa Samson did
everything.

I was going to study journalism. The decision was made. The path was
set. Everything I had learned, everything I had survived, everything I
had become was leading to this.

Murgusi Secondary School taught me discipline. Taught me structure.
Taught me how to lead. How to perform. How to survive on less. How to
keep going when quitting looked easier.

Four years of boarding school. Four years of 5 AM wake-ups. Four years
of terrible food and strict rules and KCSE stress and teenage drama.

Four years preparing me for what came next.

I left Murgusi with a C in KCSE. Not the grade I wanted. But with skills
grades could not measure. Confidence. Resilience. Voice. The knowledge
that I could survive hard things and come out stronger.

I left knowing who I was. What I wanted. Where I was going.

I left ready. As ready as anyone ever is for a future they cannot fully
see but are walking toward anyway.

Journalism school was next. Then Citizen TV internship. Then PPP TV.
Then Urban News. Then seven hundred thousand subscribers and twenty
million monthly views and stages I never imagined standing on.

But that is getting ahead of the story. Right now, in 2019, I was
eighteen years old. Fresh out of high school. Heading to Thika. Ready to
learn how to turn noise into news.

The GSU commander was right about one thing. I argued well. But arguing
in court was not my destiny.

My destiny was holding the mic. Asking questions. Telling stories. Being
loud with purpose.

Born broke. Built loud. The story was just beginning.

*\"Roylandz Rule #26: Destiny calls softly at first. Then louder. Then
so loud you cannot ignore it even if you try.\"*

**PART IV**

**THE MAKING OF ROYLANDZ**

**(2020-2024)**

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 26

## ESCAPE TO THIKA: MY FIRST REAL FREEDOM

January 2020. Thika Institute of Business Studies. TIBS.

Freedom tastes different when you have never had it before. Sweet.
Terrifying. Intoxicating.

I arrived at TIBS with one metal box, 5,000 shillings, and zero idea
what I was doing. The campus was bigger than Murgusi. More students.
More buildings. More everything.

But the biggest difference was not the size. The difference was freedom.
Real freedom. Not boarding school freedom where you could choose between
beans and more beans. College freedom. Adult freedom. The kind where
nobody checks if you made your bed or went to class or ate breakfast.

The kind where you succeed or fail based entirely on your own choices.
And nobody is there to catch you if you fall.

TIBS was a private college. Not university. Not a place with massive
budgets or fancy facilities. But solid. Respected. Known for producing
graduates who got jobs.

I was studying Diploma in Journalism and Mass Communication. Two years.
Four semesters. The fast track to a career telling stories.

My first roommate was Luckyboy Maingi. We met during orientation. Both
journalism students. Both ambitious. Both broke. Perfect match.

Lucky and I became inseparable. Spent hours talking about media. About
careers. About how we were going to change Kenyan journalism through
sheer force of will and student loans.

We created \"The Overview Show\" in our first semester. A student-run
news show covering campus events. No budget. No equipment. Just two kids
with smartphones and delusions of grandeur.

The name came from our philosophy. We wanted to give people the
overview. The big picture. The context. Not sensational headlines alone.
Not clickbait. Real journalism.

Looking back, the show was terrible. Bad lighting. Worse sound. Shaky
camera work that made viewers seasick. But we were learning. Trying.
Failing. Getting better slowly.

We filmed episodes in dorm rooms. In classrooms after hours. Anywhere we
could set up without getting kicked out. We interviewed students.
Covered campus politics. Reported on events nobody else was reporting
on.

Nobody watched at first. Maybe ten views per episode. Nine of those were
us checking if the upload worked. But we kept going. Because the point
was not views. The point was practice. Building skills. Creating a body
of work. Proving we could do this.

By second semester, people started noticing. The Overview Show was
becoming known on campus. Students would stop us. \"Hey, you are the
journalists!\" Said with respect. With recognition.

That recognition felt good. Felt like validation. Felt like we were on
the right path even if the path was unclear and mostly uphill.

I also joined every club that would have me. Drama club. Debate club.
Christian Union again because old habits die hard. Anything giving me
stage time. Mic time. Practice time.

I was collecting skills like Pokémon cards. Public speaking.
Interviewing. Video editing. Scriptwriting. All the things you need to
be a journalist but do not realize until you try and fail spectacularly.

The freedom at TIBS was amazing and dangerous in equal measure. Amazing
because I could explore. Try things. Fail without parents knowing
immediately. Learn who I was without constant supervision.

Dangerous because freedom without discipline is chaos. And I was twenty
years old with more freedom than sense.

I stayed out late. Skipped classes occasionally. Spent money on things I
did not need. Made choices my high school self would have judged
harshly.

But I also grew. Learned. Figured out what worked and what did not.
Developed work ethic. Built relationships. Created opportunities.

TIBS gave me space to become someone. Not who my grandparents wanted.
Not who teachers expected. Who I wanted. Who I was meant to be.

The boy from Manyonyi was still in there. The kid who walked barefoot to
school. The teenager who led Christian Union while secretly breaking
rules. The Form Four student who argued with GSU.

But now he was becoming something more. A journalist. A storyteller.
Someone learning to use his voice professionally. Someone building a
career one terrible student news show at a time.

Thika was freedom. And freedom, when used right, is the best education
you can get.

*\"Roylandz Rule #27: Freedom without discipline is chaos. Discipline
without freedom is prison. Balance is the goal.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 27

## WHEN ROYLANDZ WAS BORN

Class 8. 2015. Lumakanda Township Primary School. That is when Roylandz
was born.

Not the person. The name. The brand. The identity that would carry me
through college and beyond.

I needed a stage name. Something memorable. Something different.
Something making people pay attention.

\"Eugine Micah\" was fine. Safe. Normal. But normal does not get
noticed. Normal blends in. And I was tired of blending in.

So I created Roylandz. With a Z. Because adding a Z to anything makes it
cooler. That is science.

The name evolved over time. Started as Eugine Roylandz. Simple. One
addition to my real name. Then became Micahansel Eugine Roylandz.
Longer. More dramatic. More mysterious because what is a Micahansel?

By high school, it was Royal Royale Micahansel Eugine Roylandz. A
mouthful. A whole paragraph of name. The kind of name you see on a
concert poster and think \"who is this person and why do they need four
names?\"

I used that name everywhere. Signed school assignments with it.
Introduced myself with it. Made people call me Roylandz instead of
Eugine.

Teachers hated it. \"What kind of name is Roylandz? Use your real
name.\" But I insisted. This was my identity. My brand. My way of
standing out in a world trying to make everyone the same.

At TIBS, Roylandz became official. All my media work was under that
name. The Overview Show credits: \"Hosted by Roylandz.\" Campus events:
\"Featuring Roylandz.\" Social media: \"@roylandz\_\" everything.

The name worked. People remembered it. Could not forget it even if they
tried. \"You mean the guy with the weird name?\" Yes. That guy. Me.

But Roylandz was more than a name. Roylandz was an attitude. A persona.
The version of me who was confident even when scared. Who was loud even
when uncertain. Who performed even when I felt like hiding.

Eugine Micah was the kid from the village. The one who struggled. The
one who doubted. The one who sometimes felt like an impostor pretending
to belong.

Roylandz was the version who belonged. Who owned the room. Who commanded
attention. Who made things happen.

I needed both. Eugine Micah kept me grounded. Reminded me where I came
from. Kept me humble. Roylandz gave me wings. Let me fly. Gave me
permission to be bigger than my circumstances.

The name became a brand. Roylandz Media. My production company. Founded
while still in college. Registered? Not yet. But the dream was real even
if the paperwork was not.

I organized events under that name. TIBS Got Talent. Campus concerts.
Drama shows. Anything needing organization and someone willing to risk
failure.

TIBS Got Talent was my masterpiece. A campus-wide talent competition.
Singers. Dancers. Comedians. Spoken word artists. Everyone competing for
prizes we barely had budget for.

I promoted it everywhere. Posters. Social media. Word of mouth. Annoying
people until they agreed to come just to make me stop talking.

The event was packed. Standing room only. Students performing their
hearts out. Judges judging. Audience cheering. Energy electric.

And me? Backstage. Coordinating. Troubleshooting. Making sure everything
ran smoothly. Not performing. Managing. That was the shift. From being
talent to creating platforms for talent.

Roylandz Media was not a real company yet. But it was real in my head.
Real in the work I was doing. Real in the reputation I was building.

By 2024, I would stop using Roylandz publicly. When Urban News launched.
When I got on national TV. When being \"Eugine Micah, journalist\"
mattered more than being \"Roylandz, campus personality.\"

But I would never forget what that name taught me. That sometimes you
need to become someone else to find yourself. That confidence is a
costume you wear until it becomes your skin. That branding yourself is
not vanity. Branding yourself is survival in a world that forgets people
who do not make themselves memorable.

Roylandz was not a stage name. Roylandz was a survival strategy. And it
worked.

*\"Roylandz Rule #28: Sometimes you need to create a version of yourself
brave enough to do what you are too scared to attempt.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 28

## THE OVERVIEW SHOW: FILMING ON FAITH

The Overview Show started with a question: What if we made our own news?

Lucky and I were sitting in his dorm room. Second semester. Tired of
watching other people tell stories. Wondering why we were not telling
our own.

\"We should start a show,\" Lucky said.

\"With what budget?\" I asked.

\"Who needs a budget? We have phones.\"

He was right. We had smartphones. We had voices. We had audacity.
Everything else was optional.

The first episode was a disaster. We filmed it on my phone. No script.
No plan. No idea what we were doing. We talked about campus news for ten
minutes. Uploaded it to YouTube. Waited for the views to roll in.

Three views. One was me. One was Lucky. One was probably a bot.

But we kept going. Episode two. Episode three. Each one slightly better
than the last. We learned lighting matters. Sound matters. Having
something worth saying matters most.

By episode ten, we had a format. Opening segment. Campus news.
Interviews. Closing thoughts. Thirty minutes of content we filmed,
edited, and uploaded ourselves.

Students started watching. Not millions. Not thousands. But enough.
Enough to feel like we were building something. Enough to keep going.

The show taught me everything textbooks could not. How to interview
people. How to ask follow-up questions. How to edit footage. How to
handle criticism when people said we were terrible. How to ignore
criticism when people said we were terrible.

Because people definitely said we were terrible. And they were not
wrong. We were terrible. But we were learning. And learning requires
being terrible at first.

*\"Roylandz Rule #29: Every expert started as someone who had no idea
what they were doing. The difference is they kept doing it.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 29

## TIBS GOT TALENT AND THE CAMPUS KING ERA

TIBS Got Talent was my Super Bowl. My Olympics. My moment to prove I
could organize something bigger than a dorm room news show.

I pitched the idea to the student council. \"We should have a talent
show. Showcase student talent. Make campus culture better.\"

They loved it. Gave me a tiny budget. Told me not to embarrass the
school. Sent me on my way.

I promoted that event like my life depended on it. Posters everywhere.
Social media blasts. Classroom announcements. Bribing people with free
entry. Whatever worked.

Auditions were packed. Singers. Dancers. Comedians. Poets. Rappers.
Everyone wanted to perform. Everyone wanted to win.

We selected the best fifteen acts. Booked the hall. Set up lights and
sound. Created a stage from nothing. Made it look professional even
though we were faking it.

The night of the event, the hall was full. Standing room only. Students
sitting on the floor. Teachers watching from the back. Energy buzzing.

I hosted. Introduced acts. Kept the show moving. Made jokes. Engaged the
crowd. Did everything I had been practicing for years.

And it worked. The crowd loved it. The acts performed well. The winners
deserved their prizes. The school administration was impressed.

TIBS Got Talent became annual. Other students took over after I
graduated. But that first one was mine. My proof that I could create
something from nothing. That I could lead. That I could make things
happen.

That event turned me into a campus personality. People knew Roylandz.
Stopped me for photos. Asked me to host their events. Treated me like I
was somebody.

And for the first time in my life, I believed them. I was somebody. Not
because of family name or money or connections. Because I had built
something. Created value. Made people\'s lives better even if just for
one night.

*\"Roylandz Rule #30: Your reputation is built one event at a time. Make
each one count.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 30

## CITIZEN TV: BAPTISM BY BROADCAST

2022\. Second year at TIBS. Citizen TV internship. The big leagues.

Citizen TV is one of Kenya\'s biggest television stations. National
reach. Millions of viewers. Real journalists with real credentials doing
real work.

And somehow, they accepted me as an intern. A kid from Lugari with a
diploma in progress and a YouTube show nobody watched.

The first day was terrifying. The newsroom was massive. People rushing
everywhere. Phones ringing. Monitors showing live feeds. Energy at
levels I had never experienced.

I was assigned to the field reporting team. My job: Shadow experienced
journalists. Learn by watching. Eventually report my own stories if I
did not mess up too badly.

My first live report was a mess. I stumbled over words. Forgot my lines.
Looked at the wrong camera. The kind of performance making you want to
change your name and move to a different country.

But the senior journalists were patient. \"Everyone\'s first live shot
is terrible,\" they said. \"You will get better.\"

And I did. Slowly. Each report slightly less terrible than the previous.
Each appearance on camera building confidence.

I covered everything. Political rallies. Accidents. Human interest
stories. Crime scenes. Anything needing a reporter willing to show up
and work.

The internship taught me television is different from YouTube. Higher
standards. Faster pace. More pressure. Mistakes broadcast to millions.
Success shared with teams.

I learned to write for broadcast. Short sentences. Active voice. Clear
language. No jargon. Assume your audience is smart but knows nothing
about the story.

I learned to handle rejection. Pitch ten stories. Get one approved.
Accept it. Move on. Pitch ten more.

I learned that talent matters less than reliability. Show up on time. Do
the work. Do not complain. Those things get you hired more than being
brilliant.

The internship ended after six months. They did not offer me a job.
Budget constraints. Limited positions. The usual reasons.

But I left with something more valuable than a job. Experience.
Credibility. Proof that I could work at a national level. Proof that the
kid from Manyonyi belonged in professional media.

That internship validated everything. The long walk to school. The cow
dung Fridays. The sleepless nights studying. The KCSE disappointment.
The Roylandz persona. All of it led here. To this moment. To this proof.

I was a journalist. Not training to be one. Not hoping to be one. I was
one. And I had the clips to prove it.

*\"Roylandz Rule #31: Internships are not about getting hired.
Internships are about getting proof you belong.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 31

## THE VALEDICTORIAN NOBODY SAW COMING

2024\. TIBS graduation. I was named valedictorian. Top student. Best
performer. The one giving the speech.

Nobody was more surprised than me. I was a good student. Not perfect.
Not the person everyone expected to finish first.

But I had worked. Shown up. Turned in assignments. Asked questions.
Built relationships with lecturers. Done the work when others were
partying.

And apparently, that was enough.

Writing the valedictorian speech was terrifying. You are supposed to
inspire your classmates. Give them hope. Send them into the world ready
to succeed.

No pressure.

I watched Jeff Koinange\'s speeches for inspiration. Studied how he
structured his thoughts. How he balanced humor and wisdom. How he made
people feel something.

Then I wrote my own. Not copying him. Using what I learned. Finding my
voice. Saying what needed saying.

Graduation day arrived. The hall was packed. Parents. Students. Faculty.
Everyone dressed up. Everyone ready to celebrate.

When they called my name for the speech, I walked to the podium. Heart
pounding. Hands shaking. Mouth dry.

And then I started talking.

I talked about the journey. About starting with nothing. About building
something. About failing and trying again. About the classmates who
became family. About the future waiting for all of us.

I talked about doubt. About the voices saying we were not good enough.
About proving them wrong not through words but through work.

I talked about gratitude. About the people who invested in us. About
Grandpa Samson who paid my fees. About teachers who believed in us when
we did not believe in ourselves.

And I talked about responsibility. About using our education to serve.
About remembering where we came from. About lifting others as we
climbed.

The speech was not perfect. I stumbled in places. Lost my place once.
Had to pause and find my spot.

But people listened. People clapped. People cried. People came up
afterward and said, \"Thank you. I needed to hear that.\"

That was the moment I realized something important. My voice mattered.
Not because I was the smartest or most talented. Because I was willing
to speak. Willing to be vulnerable. Willing to share truth even when
truth was uncomfortable.

The valedictorian speech was not about me. The speech was about everyone
in that room. Everyone who had struggled. Everyone who had doubted.
Everyone who had kept going anyway.

I was speaking for all of us. And that responsibility, that honor, meant
more than any grade ever could.

*\"Roylandz Rule #32: Your voice gains power when you speak for others,
not yourself.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 32

## WHEN COVID TOOK MY GRANDPARENTS

March 2021. COVID-19. The virus took Grandma Gladys first.

March 25, 2021. She died from COVID complications. The woman who taught
me discipline. Who limited my TV time. Who made me make my bed. Who
showed me that love looks like structure.

Gone.

One week later, April 1, 2021. Grandpa David followed.

April 1, 2021. The man who gave me books. Who asked questions. Who
treated my thoughts like they mattered. Who introduced me to Half of a
Yellow Sun and Born a Crime. Who taught me that peace was possible even
after genocide.

Gone.

Two deaths. Seven days apart. The couple who took me in when my parents
could not. Who raised me for four years. Who shaped me in ways I am
still understanding.

Both gone. Taken by a virus that did not care about peace work or legacy
or the grandchildren who still needed them.

I was at TIBS when I got the news. Phone call. Crying. Confusion. The
kind of grief that makes the world stop making sense.

I could not process it. Could not believe it. Could not accept that they
were both gone. That I would never see them again. That I never got to
say proper goodbye.

The funeral was April 7, 2021. They were buried together in Lumakanda.
Under the mango tree they planted. Side by side. The way they lived.

I stood at the grave watching the coffins lowered. Thinking about the
library. The books. The questions. The discipline. The love disguised as
rules. The way Grandpa David asked \"What did you learn today?\" The way
Grandma Gladys insisted on morning devotions.

All of it was gone. The people were gone. But the lessons remained. The
impact remained. The person I became because of them remained.

COVID took many people in 2021. The news reported numbers. Statistics.
Death tolls rising. But numbers do not capture loss. Numbers do not show
the hole left when someone who shaped your life disappears.

I lost two grandparents in one week. Lost the people who believed in me
when believing seemed irrational. Lost the home that taught me education
was possible even when poverty said otherwise.

That loss broke something in me. But it also crystallized something.
Life is short. Time is limited. The people who matter can disappear
without warning.

So you honor them by living well. By using what they taught you. By
becoming the person they believed you could be.

Grandpa David and Grandma Gladys did not live to see me on national TV.
Did not see Urban News launch. Did not see me hit 700,000 subscribers.

But they saw the foundation. They helped build it. And every success
after is partly theirs. Every time I read a book, I think of Grandpa
David. Every time I show up on time, I think of Grandma Gladys.

They are gone. But they are not gone. Not while I carry what they taught
me. Not while their lessons live in my choices.

Death took their bodies. But death could not take their impact. And
that, in the end, is what matters.

*\"Roylandz Rule #33: The people who shape you never truly die. They
live in every choice you make because of them.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 33

## UNCLE TOMMY AND AUNT JOY: THE ANGELS WHO STAYED

When Grandpa David and Grandma Gladys died, Uncle Tommy and Aunt Joy
stepped in.

Tommy Zarembka, David\'s son. Joy Zarembka, David\'s daughter. My uncle
and aunt by relation. My heroes by choice.

They did not have to step in. Did not have to take responsibility for
grandchildren who were not technically their responsibility. Did not
have to deal with the mess of grief and logistics and figuring out what
happens next.

But they did. Without hesitation. Without complaining. Without making me
and Danson feel like burdens.

Uncle Tommy handled the practical things. Paperwork. Bills. Making sure
we had what we needed. Checking in. Being present in the quiet,
consistent way that stability requires.

Aunt Joy handled the emotional things. Listening. Comforting. Reminding
us we were loved. Being the person you could call when everything felt
like too much.

Together, they made sure we did not fall apart. Made sure we finished
school. Made sure we knew we still had family. Made sure loss did not
become the end of the story.

I do not talk about them enough. Do not thank them enough. Because what
they did was not loud. Was not dramatic. Was the kind of love that shows
up in actions instead of words.

They never left our side. No matter what. And that phrase, \"no matter
what,\" carries weight. Because there were moments when leaving would
have been easier. When stepping back would have been understandable.
When doing less would have been forgivable.

But they stayed. And staying, when leaving is easier, is heroism.

This book is dedicated to them. To Uncle Tommy who picked up the phone
every time. To Aunt Joy who showed up every time. To the two people who
proved that family is not about blood alone. Family is about who shows
up when everyone else disappears.

Thank you feels too small. But thank you anyway. For everything. For
showing up. For staying. For being the angels we needed when heaven took
the ones we loved.

*\"Roylandz Rule #34: Real family shows up when everyone else has
reasons to leave.\"*

# CHAPTER 34

## PPP TV: THE DAY I WENT NATIONAL

2024\. PPP TV. Urban News. National television. The dream I dreamed in
Sunday school when I said \"I want to be on TV\" and adults gave me that
look.

That look saying \"sure, kid, and I want to be an astronaut.\" That look
saying dreams are cute until reality arrives.

Well, reality arrived. And it looked like a TV studio with cameras and
lights and a red tally light staring at me like judgment.

PPP TV found me through my work at TIBS. The clips from Citizen TV
internship. The campus shows. The social media presence. The body of
work I had been building when nobody was watching.

They called. Offered me a position. Head of Digital. Plus hosting duties
for Urban News, a show covering city news, youth culture, trends, the
pulse of Nairobi.

I said yes before they finished explaining. Said yes before asking about
salary. Said yes because this was the shot. The opportunity. The moment
everything had been building toward.

The first day was surreal. Walking into a professional studio. Meeting
Lucy Ogunde, my co-host. Meeting the team. Realizing this was real. This
was my job. This was my life now.

Lucy was experienced. Confident. Professional. The kind of person who
made hosting look easy because she had done it a thousand times. I was
nervous. Excited. Trying not to show how terrified I was.

Our first show together was live. No practice run. No second takes. Live
television broadcast to hundreds of thousands of people across Kenya.

The red light came on. Lucy started talking. Then it was my turn.

And something clicked. The fear disappeared. Training took over. Years
of practice. Years of failure. Years of learning. All of it flowing
through me in that moment.

I was not perfect. Made mistakes. Stumbled over words. But I was there.
On national TV. Doing the thing I said I would do when nobody believed
it was possible.

That first show ended. The light went off. Lucy looked at me and smiled.
\"You did well. You will be fine.\"

And I believed her. Because I had done it. The hardest part was over.
Now it was just work. Show up. Prepare. Perform. Repeat.

PPP TV was not the finish line. PPP TV was the starting line. The
beginning of a career. The proof that the noise I had been making for
years was worth something.

Born broke. Built loud. Now broadcasting to the nation.

*\"Roylandz Rule #35: The moment you have been preparing for does not
feel like you imagined. It feels like work. And that is perfect.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 35

## URBAN NEWS AND THE DEATH OF ROYLANDZ

Urban News launched. Eugine Micah emerged. Roylandz died.

Not dramatically. Not with a funeral. Names do not die with ceremonies.
They fade. Stop being useful. Get replaced by something more
appropriate.

Roylandz served its purpose. Got me noticed. Built confidence. Created
opportunities. But Roylandz was a college name. A campus persona. And I
was not a campus personality anymore.

I was Eugine Micah. Journalist. TV host. Professional. Operating at a
level where stage names felt unnecessary. Where real names carried
weight.

The decision to drop Roylandz was not easy. That name represented years.
Represented growth. Represented the version of me who was brave when
being brave felt impossible.

But holding onto it felt wrong. Felt like clinging to an identity I had
outgrown. Like wearing clothes from five years ago that do not fit
anymore but you keep anyway because of memories.

So I let it go. Stopped using it publicly. Stopped introducing myself as
Roylandz. Started being Eugine Micah everywhere. Consistent.
Professional. Real.

Urban News needed me to be real. The show covered real issues. Real
people. Real stories. Hiding behind a stage name felt dishonest. Felt
like putting distance between me and the work.

So Eugine Micah showed up. Every episode. Every interview. Every story.
The kid from Manyonyi who walked barefoot to school was now on national
television using his real name. Full circle.

The audience responded well. They did not care about Roylandz. They
cared about the work. The stories. The authenticity.

And authenticity requires showing up as yourself. Not as a character.
Not as a persona. Yourself.

Roylandz taught me to be confident. Eugine Micah taught me confidence
does not require costume. Real power comes from being yourself loud
enough that people listen anyway.

*\"Roylandz Rule #36: Outgrowing your old self is not betrayal. Growth
is the goal.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 36

## 700K SUBSCRIBERS AND 20 MILLION VIEWS

The numbers happened fast. Faster than I expected. Faster than I was
ready for.

Urban News grew. YouTube channel exploded. TikTok clips went viral.
Instagram followers multiplied. Suddenly, I was not a journalist with a
small audience. I was a journalist with a platform.

700,000 subscribers. 20 million monthly views. Numbers I used to see on
other people\'s channels and think \"that will never be me.\"

But it was me. The analytics did not lie. The comments sections proved
it. The recognition when walking through Nairobi confirmed it.

People knew my face. Stopped me for photos. Asked for autographs. Wanted
to talk about episodes. Wanted advice. Wanted access. Wanted pieces of
me I did not know how to give.

Fame is weird. You want it. Then you get it. Then you realize it is not
what you imagined.

Fame is not freedom. Fame is responsibility. Every word matters. Every
mistake gets magnified. Every opinion gets dissected. Every relationship
gets complicated.

The positive was incredible. Messages from people saying the show helped
them. Young journalists saying I inspired them. Parents saying I made
them proud. Teachers from Mahemas and Lumakanda and Murgusi saying \"we
knew you would make it.\"

That validation felt good. Felt like all the struggle meant something.
All the walking. All the cow dung. All the sleepless nights. All the
doubt. All of it led here. To impact. To influence. To mattering.

But the negative was exhausting. Critics saying I was not good enough.
Trolls attacking everything. Jealous people spreading rumors. Pressure
to be perfect when perfect is impossible.

700,000 subscribers meant 700,000 opinions about who I should be. 20
million views meant 20 million expectations I could not meet.

And somewhere in all those numbers, I started losing myself. Started
performing even when cameras were off. Started measuring worth by
metrics. Started believing validation from strangers mattered more than
peace with myself.

The numbers were real. The success was real. But so was the exhaustion.
The pressure. The slow realization that getting what you want sometimes
means losing what you need.

*\"Roylandz Rule #37: Big numbers feel good until you realize they come
with big expectations you never agreed to carry.\"*

# CHAPTER 37

## THE FAME THAT DID NOT FIX ME

Fame did not fix my childhood. Did not heal the abandonment. Did not
erase the poverty. Did not make the pain disappear.

I thought it would. Thought success would make everything make sense.
Thought being on TV would prove I was worth something. Thought
subscribers would fill the holes left by absent parents and dead
grandparents.

But trauma does not care about subscribers. Pain does not care about
views. Hurt does not heal because people recognize you.

I was famous and still broken. Successful and still sad. On national
television and still struggling with the same demons I fought in
boarding school.

The difference was now I had to hide it. Had to smile for cameras. Had
to be inspirational. Had to be the success story when some days I felt
like the failure story wearing a mask.

I started therapy in 2024. Should have started years earlier. But
therapy felt like admission of weakness. Like saying \"I cannot handle
this alone\" which felt like giving up.

But therapy is not giving up. Therapy is showing up for yourself the way
you show up for work. Doing the hard work of healing instead of
pretending you are already healed.

Therapy taught me fame does not fix you. Fame exposes what needs fixing.
Puts a spotlight on cracks you were ignoring. Forces you to deal with
what you were avoiding.

I learned my loud voice was trauma response. Learned I talked so much
because silence felt like death. Learned I needed external validation
because internal validation was broken.

I learned my drive was not just ambition. Drive was survival. Proving I
deserved to exist. Proving I was worth the investment. Proving
abandonment was not because I was unlovable.

These realizations hurt. Hurt more than poverty. More than hunger. More
than any physical pain. Because this was soul pain. The kind you cannot
see but feel in everything.

But pain acknowledged is pain that can heal. Wounds hidden stay
infected. Wounds exposed can be treated.

Fame gave me a platform. Therapy gave me a mirror. Both were necessary.
Neither was sufficient alone.

*\"Roylandz Rule #38: Success shows you who you became. Therapy shows
you why. You need both.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 38

## BURNOUT IN DESIGNER SNEAKERS

Burnout looks good on camera. You smile. You perform. You look
successful. And you are dying inside.

2024 was my year. On paper. PPP TV. Urban News. Campus Xposure. The
Nairobi Podcast. Subscribers growing. Views increasing. Opportunities
multiplying.

On paper, I was winning. In reality, I was exhausted.

I was working seven days a week. Filming. Editing. Posting. Responding
to messages. Attending events. Networking. Hustling. Never stopping.
Never resting. Because rest felt like losing momentum.

And in media, losing momentum means losing relevance. Miss one week and
someone else takes your spot. Skip one trend and you are forgotten. The
game never stops. So I could not stop.

Except I was stopping. My body was stopping. My mind was stopping. The
machine was breaking down and I was pretending it was fine.

I stopped sleeping well. Four hours a night. Five if I was lucky. Coffee
in the morning. Energy drinks in the afternoon. Adrenaline carrying me
through evening shows. Crashing immediately after.

I stopped eating properly. Fast food between shoots. Skipping meals.
Forgetting to eat until someone reminded me. Losing weight I could not
afford to lose.

I stopped enjoying work. The thing I loved became the thing I dreaded.
Shows felt like chores. Interviews felt exhausting. Even success felt
empty.

I was burning out. In designer sneakers. With followers and fame and
success visible to everyone while the fire inside was dying and nobody
could see.

I took a break. Two weeks. First real break in a year. Turned off
notifications. Ignored work emails. Disappeared.

People asked where I went. Some thought I quit. Some thought I was sick.
Some made up stories. I did not care. I needed space. Needed silence.
Needed to remember why I started.

The break helped. Not fixed. Helped. I came back rested but still tired.
Healed but still hurt. Better but not whole.

Burnout teaches you something important. You cannot pour from an empty
cup. You cannot inspire others while destroying yourself. You cannot
build a career on a foundation of exhaustion.

Rest is not laziness. Rest is maintenance. The kind of maintenance
keeping machines running. Keeping humans human.

*\"Roylandz Rule #39: Burnout in designer sneakers is still burnout.
Success does not protect you from breaking down.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 39

## FAITH IN HD: PRAYERS BETWEEN TAKES

I still pray before shows. Every time. Without fail. Even when I do not
feel it. Especially when I do not feel it.

Quick prayer between makeup and camera. \"God, help me do this well.
Help me serve. Help me honor the people watching.\"

Simple. Honest. The same prayer Grandma Rosemary taught me years ago in
Manyonyi church. The same prayer I prayed before walking to school.
Before KCPE. Before KCSE. Before everything that mattered.

Faith looks different now than it did at Murgusi. Less performance. More
private. Less about proving holiness. More about finding peace.

I do not attend church regularly anymore. Schedule does not allow it.
Sunday is work day. Early morning shoots. Late night edits. The grind
does not respect Sabbath.

But faith is not location. Faith is not attendance. Faith is the
conversation you have with God when nobody is watching. The prayers
between takes. The gratitude in quiet moments. The seeking when you are
lost.

My faith survived boarding school. Survived KCSE stress. Survived my
grandparents\' deaths. Survived fame and failure and everything between.

Not because my faith is strong. Because God is patient. Patient with
questions. With doubt. With anger. With distance. With the mess of being
human while trying to be faithful.

I do not have all the answers. Do not claim to. Still figuring it out.
Still learning. Still wrestling. Still showing up to the conversation
even when I do not know what to say.

But I pray before shows. Thank God after success. Cry to Him during
failure. Keep the line open. Keep the conversation going.

Because at the end of the day, when lights go off and cameras stop
rolling and crowds disperse, it is me and God. The same way it was in
Manyonyi when I had nothing. The same way it will be when everything
else fades.

Faith in HD looks a lot like faith in poverty. Less polished. More
honest. Not performing for audience. Just talking to the One who knows
you fully and loves you anyway.

*\"Roylandz Rule #40: Faith is not how often you attend church. Faith is
how often you remember you are not doing this alone.\"*
