**PART VI**

**FULL CIRCLE**

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 40

## BACK TO LUMAKANDA: THE DUST STILL KNEW MY NAME

I went back to Lumakanda in 2025. First time since the funeral. First
time since losing Grandpa David and Grandma Gladys.

The compound looked the same. Different. Same structures. Different
energy. The people who made it home were gone. What remained were
memories and ghosts.

The library was still there. Books on shelves. Collecting dust. Waiting.
Uncle Tommy maintained it. Kept it as it was. A memorial. A museum. A
reminder.

I walked in and cried. Could not help it. The smell of old books brought
everything back. Grandpa David asking \"What did you learn today?\"
Grandma Gladys insisting on homework before play. The discipline. The
structure. The love disguised as rules.

The mango tree where they were buried stood strong. Bigger than I
remembered. Branches spreading. Shade providing. A living monument.

I sat under it for hours. Talking to them. Updating them. Telling them
about PPP TV and Urban News and the subscribers and the life I was
living. The life they helped build.

I wondered if they would be proud. If they would recognize the person I
became. If they would approve of the choices I made.

I think they would. Not because I am perfect. Because I kept going.
Because I used what they gave me. Because their investment was not
wasted.

Lumakanda taught me that going back is not always about returning.
Sometimes going back is about remembering. About honoring. About
connecting who you were with who you became.

The dust still knew my name. The roads remembered my feet. The compound
recognized my voice. And that recognition, that homecoming, healed
something I did not know was broken.

You can go home again. But home is not the place. Home is the people.
And when the people are gone, home becomes memory. And memory, when
honored, becomes strength.

*\"Roylandz Rule #41: Going back shows you how far you have come. And
sometimes that is the reminder you need most.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 41

## DEAR YOUNGER ME: SORRY I DOUBTED YOU

Dear Eugine at seven years old walking to Mahemas barefoot,

I am sorry I doubted you. Sorry I thought you would not make it. Sorry I
believed the people who said you were too poor, too loud, too much, not
enough.

You made it. Despite everything. Despite the odds. Despite the poverty
and abandonment and hunger and all the reasons giving up made sense.

You made it to places you did not know existed. To stages you could not
imagine. To a life you were not supposed to have access to.

I wish I could tell you it gets easier. It does not. Different problems
replace old problems. Success brings new stress. Fame creates new
struggles.

But it gets better. Worth it better. Meaningful better. The kind of
better making the struggle make sense.

You were right to be loud. Right to refuse silence. Right to make noise
when everyone wanted you quiet. Noise saved you. Noise became your
career. Noise changed your life.

I am proud of you. Proud of the boy who read to chickens. Who carried
cow dung. Who walked barefoot. Who survived when survival was not
guaranteed.

You became someone. Not by forgetting where you came from. By
remembering. By using it. By turning pain into power and poverty into
purpose.

The scars remained. The plastic burn on your face. The memories of
hunger. The abandonment wounds. All still there. But scars are proof.
Proof you survived. Proof you fought. Proof you won.

Keep going, younger me. It is worth it. You are worth it. And everyone
who doubted you will see. They will all see.

Love,

Eugine at twenty-three on national television

*\"Roylandz Rule #42: Your younger self deserves your gratitude, not
your pity. They survived so you could thrive.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 42

## THE DIGITAL KEMOVIEW

Grandpa Samson built Kemoview with shops. I am building my version with
media.

Roylandz Media. Not registered yet. Still a dream. But dreams with work
become plans. Plans with consistency become reality.

The vision is simple. Create content. Tell stories. Build platform. Help
others do the same. Turn noise into value. Turn voice into career.

I want to build something leaving my name on the map. Not literally. Not
a physical location. Digital location. A space where people come for
content. For stories. For truth told loud.

Roylandz Barbershop & Spa exists. Physical business. Haircuts and
services. Revenue stream. Proof I am not one-trick pony. Proof I can
build multiple things.

But media is the core. Media is the calling. Media is where I want my
legacy.

I look at Grandpa Samson\'s Kemoview and see blueprint. He built from
nothing. Created something lasting. Made his name geography.

I want to do the same. Different industry. Same principle. Build
something so solid people cannot ignore it. Build something outliving
you. Build something making your name immortal.

The Digital Kemoview is not there yet. But it is coming. Subscribers are
foundation. Content is structure. Consistency is the work. Impact is the
goal.

Grandpa Samson limped but built anyway. I have scars but building
anyway. That is the family tradition. Build despite. Build anyway. Build
until your name means something.

*\"Roylandz Rule #43: Legacy is not inherited. Legacy is built. Start
building before you are ready.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 43

## NOISE WITH PURPOSE

I have been loud my whole life. But loud without purpose is just
annoying. Loud with purpose changes things.

I learned that distinction slowly. Painfully. Through mistakes and
failures and feedback I did not want to hear.

Being loud got attention. Being loud with purpose kept attention. Being
loud with purpose built careers. Being loud with purpose changed lives.

Purpose came from pain. From remembering where I came from. From seeing
kids like younger me who needed someone showing them different was
possible.

Purpose came from stories. From telling them honestly. From refusing to
sanitize. From showing the struggle and the success. From being real
instead of perfect.

Purpose came from platform. From using visibility for more than fame.
From asking questions power did not want to answer. From giving voice to
people media ignored.

Urban News is loud with purpose. Campus Xposure is loud with purpose.
The Nairobi Podcast is loud with purpose. Everything I build now has
intent behind the volume.

I am not loud because I need attention anymore. I am loud because
attention is power. And power, used right, serves people.

That is the evolution. Noise for survival became noise for career became
noise for purpose. Same voice. Different mission. Better impact.

Born broke taught me to be loud. Building loud taught me to be
purposeful. Being loud with purpose is teaching me to be useful.

And useful is the goal. Not famous. Not rich. Not celebrated. Useful.
The kind of useful making people\'s lives better. The kind of useful
leaving the world different than you found it.

That is noise with purpose. That is the legacy. That is the point.

*\"Roylandz Rule #44: Noise without purpose is pollution. Noise with
purpose is power. Choose your volume wisely.\"*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# CHAPTER 44

## BORN BROKE, BUILT LOUD

This is not the end of the story. This is the middle. The part where you
take stock. Look back. Look forward. Decide what comes next.

I was born broke on Christmas Eve in a hospital with no blankets. Born
to a mother who had just pulled off a semi-kidnapping while nine months
pregnant. Born loud in a family that could not afford quiet.

Poverty was my first teacher. Taught me hunger builds character. Taught
me walking barefoot builds thick skin. Taught me nothing is guaranteed.
Taught me noise is free and sometimes free is all you have.

Boarding school was my second teacher. Taught me discipline. Structure.
The value of showing up. The cost of excellence. The reward of
persistence.

Grandparents were my third teachers. David and Gladys taught me
education. Samson and Josephine taught me investment. All four taught me
love looks different than you expect. Sometimes love looks like rules.
Sometimes love looks like paying school fees. Sometimes love looks like
asking questions.

Media was my fourth teacher. Taught me voice has value. Platform is
responsibility. Fame is complicated. Success is not finish line. Work
never stops.

Loss was my fifth teacher. Taught me time is limited. People disappear.
Hold them while you have them. Tell them while they can hear. Do not
wait for perfect moment because perfect never comes.

I am twenty-three years old. On national television. Seven hundred
thousand subscribers. Twenty million monthly views. Living a life I was
not supposed to access.

But I am still learning. Still growing. Still figuring out who I am
beyond the performance. Beyond the platform. Beyond the noise.

This book is not victory lap. This book is progress report. This is me
saying \"look how far I came. Look what is possible. Look what happens
when you refuse to let circumstances define destiny.\"

If you are reading this from poverty, know this: Your start does not
determine your finish. Your circumstances are real but not final. Your
voice matters even when nobody is listening yet.

If you are reading this from doubt, know this: Doubt is normal. Feeling
like impostor is normal. Questioning yourself is normal. Do the work
anyway. Show up anyway. Build anyway.

If you are reading this from success, know this: Success does not mean
you are done. Success means you have platform. Use it for more than
validation. Use it for impact. Use it to pull others up.

Born broke was not my choice. Building loud was. And that choice changed
everything.

You are born into circumstances you cannot control. But you build
yourself into the person you choose to become. That is power. That is
freedom. That is the point.

I was born broke. I built loud. The story is still being written. More
chapters coming. More failures. More successes. More lessons. More
growth.

But this is the foundation. This is where it started. This is proof that
miracles are real. Not the supernatural kind. The kind where a kid from
Manyonyi ends up on national television telling stories.

That kind of miracle is available to anyone willing to work. Willing to
fail. Willing to try again. Willing to be loud even when everyone says
be quiet.

Born broke. Built loud. Still building.

The story continues.

*\"Roylandz Rule #45: Your story is not about where you started. Your
story is about what you built from that starting point. Build something
worth telling.\"*

**END OF PART VI: FULL CIRCLE**

# EPILOGUE

## WHEN THE LIGHTS GO OFF

The studio empties fast after a show.

One minute, full of energy. Cameras rolling. Lights blazing. People
everywhere.

Next minute, empty. Quiet. Dark. Like it was all a dream.

I sit in the empty studio sometimes after everyone leaves. In the
silence. In the dark. Processing. Remembering.

This is when I think about the kerosene lamp. The mud walls. The
barefoot walks. The cow dung Fridays. The hungry nights. The absent
parents. The grandparents who stepped in. The teachers who believed. The
moments of doubt. The moments of hope.

This is when I remember I am not supposed to be here. Not according to
statistics. Not according to circumstances. Not according to everyone
who said village kids stay in villages.

But here I am. And the story is not over.

Lights go off. But the work continues. Stories still need telling.
People still need hearing. Voice still needs using.

The lights will come back on tomorrow. New show. New stories. New
opportunities to serve. To inform. To inspire. To be loud with purpose.

Born broke taught me resilience. Built loud taught me voice. The
combination changed my life.

And if it changed mine, it can change yours.

Find your voice. Use it loud. Build something. Serve someone. Tell your
story.

The world is waiting to hear you. Stop waiting for permission. Stop
waiting for perfect conditions. Stop waiting for someone else to believe
in you.

Believe in yourself. Work hard. Fail forward. Try again.

Born broke is not the end. Born broke is the beginning of every good
comeback story.

Including yours.

The lights go off.

But you keep going.

# ROYLANDZ RULES: THE COMPLETE COLLECTION

1.  The world listens louder when you speak from scars, not ego.

2.  Do not confuse a late start with a small destiny.

3.  When life gives you cow dung, make it your foundation.

4.  If your kitchen does not occasionally try to kill you, you are not
    > cooking with enough passion.

5.  Faith without mosquito bites is theory. Faith with mosquito bites is
    > survival.

6.  Legacy is not what you leave behind. Legacy is what you build while
    > people are watching and doubting.

7.  The longest journey teaches you more than the shortest shortcut ever
    > could.

8.  The right story at the right time rewrites your entire life.

9.  Dignity is not about avoiding dirty work. Dignity is about doing
    > dirty work without losing yourself.

10. Practice your voice before you need it, so when opportunity comes,
    > you are already loud.

11. Silence is survival only until you realize noise is power.

12. New places do not erase old scars, but they give you room to heal
    > differently.

13. Discipline feels like chains until you realize it is building wings.

14. Libraries are dangerous places for people who want to keep you
    > small.

15. Discipline shapes you. Music fills you. Together they build a spine
    > strong enough to carry your dreams.

16. Exams measure what you know. Life measures what you do with what you
    > know.

17. Belief is the most valuable investment anyone can make in you. Honor
    > it by becoming who they saw you could be.

18. Sometimes you need to see where you are going before you know how to
    > get there.

19. Going back is not always going backwards. Sometimes you return to a
    > place to launch forward.

20. Boarding school is where comfort goes to die and character comes to
    > life.

21. You can be holy and human at the same time. Anyone telling you
    > otherwise is lying or delusional.

22. Leadership is not about the applause you receive. Leadership is
    > about the work you do when nobody is clapping.

23. Your voice is your weapon. Learn to use it before life forces you
    > to.

24. Bad grades sting. But they do not define you unless you let them.

25. Quitting your demons is victory even when everything else is falling
    > apart.

26. Destiny calls softly at first. Then louder. Then so loud you cannot
    > ignore it even if you try.

27. Freedom without discipline is chaos. Discipline without freedom is
    > prison. Balance is the goal.

28. Sometimes you need to create a version of yourself brave enough to
    > do what you are too scared to attempt.

29. Every expert started as someone who had no idea what they were
    > doing. The difference is they kept doing it.

30. Your reputation is built one event at a time. Make each one count.

31. Internships are not about getting hired. Internships are about
    > getting proof you belong.

32. Your voice gains power when you speak for others, not yourself.

33. The people who shape you never truly die. They live in every choice
    > you make because of them.

34. Real family shows up when everyone else has reasons to leave.

35. The moment you have been preparing for does not feel like you
    > imagined. It feels like work. And that is perfect.

36. Outgrowing your old self is not betrayal. Growth is the goal.

37. Big numbers feel good until you realize they come with big
    > expectations you never agreed to carry.

38. Success shows you who you became. Therapy shows you why. You need
    > both.

39. Burnout in designer sneakers is still burnout. Success does not
    > protect you from breaking down.

40. Faith is not how often you attend church. Faith is how often you
    > remember you are not doing this alone.

41. Going back shows you how far you have come. And sometimes that is
    > the reminder you need most.

42. Your younger self deserves your gratitude, not your pity. They
    > survived so you could thrive.

43. Legacy is not inherited. Legacy is built. Start building before you
    > are ready.

44. Noise without purpose is pollution. Noise with purpose is power.
    > Choose your volume wisely.

45. Your story is not about where you started. Your story is about what
    > you built from that starting point. Build something worth telling.

**DEDICATION**

For Grandpa David Zarembka and Grandma Gladys Kamonya, who taught me that education is the only inheritance that appreciates. You built a library out of belief. I am still living inside it.

For Grandpa Samson Kemoli, who made the call. Every call. Without a single lecture. This book is the receipt.

For Grandma Josephine Kemoli, who kept the fires lit when the nights were long.

For Uncle Tommy Zarembka and Aunt Joy Zarembka, the two who stayed when heaven took the others.

For my father, Joab Philemona Etale Obbayi, the lion who never learned how to quit.

For my mother, Beverlyne Kangazi Kemoli, who taught me to survive with laughter and never to die quiet.

For my brother, Danson, my first audience and forever crew.

And for every barefoot kid reading this by candlelight or by phone screen: your starting point is not your ceiling. Keep walking. The road remembers your feet.

**EPIGRAPH**

“Until the lions have their own historians, the history of the hunt will always glorify the hunter.”

A proverb, carried into the world by Chinua Achebe.

This is me, the lion, picking up the pen.

**AUTHOR’S NOTE**

This is a book made of memory, and memory is a liar with good intentions. It keeps the smell of rain on red soil but loses the year the roof leaked worst. It saves the exact words my grandmother prayed before supper and misplaces the date she prayed them. So I went and checked the things that could be checked. My birth certificate says I arrived on the twenty-fourth of December, 2001, which makes me a Christmas Eve baby and explains why I have spent my whole life trying to be the present nobody asked for. The hospital was real. The grandparents were real. The deaths were real, and I have the dates now, even though for a long time I refused to.

Where my memory and somebody else’s memory disagreed, I went with whatever felt most honest rather than whatever felt most comfortable. Some names I changed to protect people who never signed up to appear in a book written by a loud boy from Lugari. Some conversations I rebuilt from the inside, the way you rebuild a house from a photograph, because I did not walk through my childhood carrying a recorder. Nobody does.

Everything important in here is true. Some of it is so true it sounds invented. That is the thing about being born broke. Your life writes itself like a script, and all you have to do is survive long enough to read it back.

Eugine Micah

Nairobi, 2025

# PROLOGUE: LIGHTS BEFORE DUST

The studio at PPP TV smells like cold coffee, hot electronics, and the particular sweat of people who are pretending not to be nervous. The air conditioner hums at a pitch that has, over the months, become indistinguishable from my own pulse. Camera Two wears a small red light the way a judge wears an expression. It has already decided something about you. It is only giving you the courtesy of a final statement.

Lucy Ogunde sits to my left, adjusting her mic with the calm of a woman who has done this a thousand nights and will do it a thousand more. She gives me the look. Every co-host in the world knows the look. It says, we go live in nine seconds, and I will not be rescuing you. I nod like a man who has water in his throat, which I do not, because I drank some two minutes ago and my body decided to evaporate it on principle.

The floor manager lifts his hand. Three. Two. One.

The red light catches.

And here is the thing nobody warns you about live television. In the half-second before you speak to a few hundred thousand strangers, your brain does not show you the script. It shows you home. So instead of the teleprompter I see a kerosene lamp. Orange, unsteady, sitting on a three-legged stool in a mud-walled room in Manyonyi village, Lugari, Kakamega County. The wick needs trimming. Nobody is going to trim it, because paraffin costs money and a lower flame burns longer, and a longer flame means more nights you can read, and more nights you can read means the wall between where you are and where you might end up gets a little thinner.

I am seven in that lamplight. I am sitting on a floor I smeared with cow dung that very morning, because that is what Friday means in our house. The dung has dried smooth and clean and the smell has settled into something earthy and mine. I am holding a schoolbook and reading out loud to an audience of six chickens who have wandered in through the open door and arranged themselves in a half-circle, heads tilted, the way creatures look when they do not understand the words but respect the commitment.

The chickens, I want you to know, were better listeners than most adults. They never interrupted. They never told me to lower my voice. They never asked the question village adults love to ask a dreaming child, the one that arrives dressed as concern: who told you that you could?

The floor manager’s hand drops. Kenya is watching. I open my mouth and the words come, and they do not come only from the prompter, even though a professional learns to read scrolling text while looking like he is making it up out of his own genius. They come from the floor. From the lamp. From the chickens. From the barefoot walk to school and the third day of beans and the silence that poverty teaches you to fill with noise, because in a house where the adults are fighting or absent or doing arithmetic on tomorrow’s food, silence is the loudest and most dangerous sound there is.

This is the story of how a boy born in a hospital named after a country that no longer exists, raised on dung and beans and the stubborn refusal to be quiet, ended up under these lights saying these words to people who have no idea that their journalist used to rehearse on poultry.

The lights are on. The camera is rolling. And the boy from Manyonyi has something to say.

# PART I: DUST DON’T DIE EASY

*(2001 to 2008)*

## Chapter 1: Born in a Hospital Named After a Dead Country

I was born on Christmas Eve, 2001, in a hospital named after a country that was already dead.

Let that sit. Russia Hospital, Kisumu. Officially the Jaramogi Oginga Odinga Teaching and Referral Hospital now, but nobody calls it that, the same way nobody calls your uncle by his Christian name. Everybody calls it Russia, because the Soviet Union built it in the nineteen-sixties, back when the Cold War turned Africa into a chessboard and every clinic was a handshake and every road was a sentence that meant choose us. Jaramogi Oginga Odinga flew to Moscow, shook hands with men in heavy coats, and came home with money for a hospital. The Soviets sent engineers and a design so distinctly theirs that you could feel the snow in the architecture even under the equator sun. President Kenyatta opened it in 1969 and the celebration turned into a massacre on the same afternoon, but that is a different book and a different grief.

By the time I showed up, the country on the signboard had collapsed. The USSR was gone. The hospital remained, painted that exact shade of institutional green that hospitals in poor countries love, not because it soothes anyone but because green paint is cheap and forgiving toward stains. If you wanted a metaphor for the life ahead of me, you could not have engineered a better one. A child of a broke family, born in an underfunded hospital, named after a superpower that went broke and disappeared. The universe was not subtle with me. It never has been.

My arrival required a small act of war. My mother, Beverlyne Kangazi Kemoli, was nine months heavy and living among my father’s people in Bunyore, in Vihiga. Tradition had a plan for me. I was to be born close to the paternal homestead, welcomed into the Abatongoi clan with the proper prayers and the proper aunts present, on soil that belonged to my father’s line. My mother, who had been raised by people who handed her books and opinions in equal measure, looked at this plan, weighed it, and quietly declined to participate. Before the roosters had even clocked in for their morning shift, she packed a small bag, walked to the road, flagged down a matatu, and removed herself from the situation with the efficiency of a general who has already calculated how much head start she needs.

The family noticed by mid-morning. By then she was already checked in, timing her contractions and ignoring her phone, which in 2001 was a luxurious thing to be able to ignore. I came that day. The nurse, my mother swears, held me upside down, smacked my back once, waited for the scream, got it at full volume, nodded, and pronounced a single word that would turn out to be a prophecy.

“Loud one.”

My father was not there. This was normal in 2001, in our part of the world, where a man’s presence at a birth ranked somewhere between optional and inconvenient. Joab Philemona Etale Obbayi, a name that carries four generations of weight, arrived two days later, held me, studied my furious wrinkled face, and said, “Good.” In the emotional vocabulary of Luhya men of his generation, “good” was a standing ovation with tears in its eyes.

Kenya in 2001 was a country holding its breath. Daniel arap Moi was limping through the final stretch of a twenty-four-year presidency that had turned patience from a virtue into a survival skill. The shillings were tired. Corruption was not a glitch in the machine, it was the machine, running exactly as built. HIV was moving through western Kenya with the indifference of bad weather, and the sugar industry that fed our region was already coughing the cough that would eventually kill it. Being born broke where I was born was not a tragic distinction. It was a demographic fact I shared with millions, the most ordinary thing about me.

Three days later my mother took me, not to Bunyore where the offended relatives waited, and not yet to Lumakanda where the grandparents kept their famous order, but to a single rented room in town with a bed, a chair, a paraffin stove, and the ghost of the last tenant’s cooking oil in the walls. She set me down. She sat beside me. And there it was, the real beginning, not the cinematic one that memoirists prefer but the true one: a young woman, a brand-new baby, a rented room, and the very quiet understanding that nobody was coming to help.

She was wrong about that, as it turned out. But she had no way of knowing it yet.

*Roylandz Rule #1: Where you are born does not get a vote in who you become. It only gets to write the first line. Make sure it is a good one.*

## Chapter 2: Manyonyi, Where the Dust Knew My Name

Manyonyi was not on most maps, which never stopped it from being the center of the world.

It sat in Lugari, in the western highlands, where the soil is so red it looks like the land is keeping a secret about blood, and so fertile that if you dropped a coin it would probably sprout. The mornings smelled of woodsmoke and wet grass and somebody’s tea boiling over. You woke to roosters who took their jobs far too seriously, then to the swish-swish of women sweeping bare earth yards with brooms made of twigs, a sound so constant it became silence, the way you stop hearing your own heartbeat.

Our house was mud and wattle, with a roof that argued with the rain and usually lost. The walls were the color of the earth because they were the earth, plastered and smoothed and waiting for Friday. The floor was packed dung and soil, which sounds like a hardship until you have felt how cool and clean it stays, how it holds the day’s heat and gives it back at night like a faithful friend. We had a paraffin lamp, a few stools, a suf, a row of cooking pots that doubled as the family’s net worth, and six chickens who believed, with the confidence of the truly mediocre, that they ran the place.

I learned the geography of poverty before I learned the geography of Kenya. I knew which neighbor lent salt without making a speech about it. I knew the path to the borehole and exactly how heavy a jerrican gets across the final hundred meters, when your arms have filed a formal complaint and your spine has joined them. I knew that the rich kids, the ones whose fathers had government jobs in town, wore shoes to school on ordinary days, while the rest of us saved our one pair, if we had a pair, for church and examinations.

But here is what the brochures about African childhood poverty always leave out. We were happy. Not the brave, captioned happiness of a charity photo. Real happiness, the loud, ordinary kind. We made footballs out of plastic bags and twine and played until the ball disintegrated and then we made another one and kept the score from the old game. We told stories at night that were so frightening I would not look at the doorway, then so funny I forgot to be frightened. The poverty was real and it took real things from us, but it never managed to take the noise, and the noise, it would turn out, was the whole inheritance.

Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie warns about the single story, the danger of telling only one true thing about a place until that one true thing becomes the only thing. The single story of Manyonyi would be the hunger. And the hunger was true. But it was not the only true thing, and a man who tells you only the hunger has told you a fact and hidden a life.

I was a child who talked. Constantly. To adults, to chickens, to myself, to the wall. My grandmother said I came out of the womb mid-sentence and had simply continued. Other parents praised quiet children. Mine were handed a boy with a permanent broadcast license and no off switch, in a region where they say a closed mouth catches no flies, and I caught everything but flies.

The dust of Manyonyi got into everything. Your feet, your food, your lungs, your laughter. People say dust dies when the rains come. It does not. It only goes quiet and waits, the way certain things in you go quiet and wait, and then one dry season later it rises again and remembers your name.

*Roylandz Rule #2: A poor childhood is not a sad story. It is a loud one. Learn the difference and you will never be a victim of your own beginning.*

## Chapter 3: My Father’s Silence and My Mother’s War

My parents were a paraffin lamp and a thunderstorm trying to share one room.

My father, Joab, was a quiet man, and not the peaceful kind of quiet. His was the dense, pressurized quiet of a man carrying things he had no language for and no permission to set down. Luhya men of his generation were issued exactly two acceptable emotions at birth, anger and silence, and were expected to ration even those. He loved us. I know this now the way you know the sun is behind the clouds, by the warmth and not the sight. But as a boy I could not read his love because it had been translated into a language with no vowels. He provided what he could and disappeared into work and worry, and his absences were so consistent they felt like a kind of presence, a father-shaped space at the table.

My mother was the thunderstorm, and thank God for her. Beverlyne did not believe in suffering quietly. She believed in suffering at full volume, with commentary, and somehow this made the suffering smaller. She had been raised by Gladys and David in Lumakanda, raised with books and structure and the idea that a woman gets a vote in her own life, and she carried that idea into a marriage and a village that had not requested it. She and my father fought the way drought and flood fight, two disasters with opposite methods, neither one wrong about the danger and both of them wrong about the cure.

I learned to read a room before I could read a book. I could tell from the angle of my mother’s back at the cooking fire what kind of evening we were going to have. I could tell from the length of my father’s silence whether to be invisible or to be funny, and being funny became my trade because invisibility never suited me. When the air in the house got heavy, I would say something ridiculous, and if I got the timing right, the laugh would crack the pressure and we would all get to breathe. That was my first job. Not comedian. Pressure valve. The comedy came later, but the instinct that built it was forged in that small mud room, watching two people I loved fail to love each other gently.

People assume comedians come from happy homes. The opposite is closer to the truth. You do not learn timing in a house where everyone is calm. You learn it in a house where a joke, deployed at the exact right second, can change the weather. I was a meteorologist of my own family before I was tall enough to reach the lamp.

Kevin Hart wrote that he refused to let his circumstances become his excuse. I did not have his book then. I had the lesson before I had the words for it, the way you have a fever before you have a thermometer. My father’s silence and my mother’s war did not break me. They trained me. They made me a boy who could feel the temperature of a room from the doorway and decide, in one heartbeat, whether tonight needed quiet or needed noise.

I usually chose noise. I almost always choose noise.

*Roylandz Rule #3: Some children inherit money. I inherited timing. One of these things you cannot lose in a bad year.*

## Chapter 4: Dung Day Economics

Every Friday I smeared cow dung on the floor and walls of our house, and I want to explain this carefully, because I have seen the face people make.

The face says, oh, you poor thing. Put the face away. You are embarrassing both of us.

Smearing the floor with a mixture of fresh cow dung and water and a little fine soil is not a symptom of misery. It is a technology. It seals the packed-earth floor against dust, hardens it into a smooth surface, keeps jiggers and other small enemies out, and leaves the room smelling, once it dries, of clean earth and not of anything you are imagining. Across rural Kenya, across a good part of the continent, women have maintained homes this way for longer than your favorite skyscraper has existed. It is unpaid, invisible, brilliant engineering, and the world does not call it engineering because the world does not look closely at what poor women do.

My job, from about age five, was to help. You collect the dung while it is fresh, because old dung is a different and more aggressive proposition. You mix it to the right consistency, which you learn by hand and never by instruction. You spread it in smooth arcs with your palm, working from the far corner toward the door so you do not paint yourself into a stinking corner like a fool, which I did exactly once and never again. Then you wait for it to dry, and the whole house breathes a little easier.

I called it, much later and only in my head, dung day economics. Because that is what it was. In a cash economy you buy flooring. In our economy you converted free inputs and your own labor into a maintained home, and the only currency that changed hands was time and effort and the willingness to put your hands in something the squeamish refuse to touch. I have since sat in boardrooms where men in good suits used the phrase value creation as if they had invented it. Brother, I was creating value with a cow’s leftovers before I could spell value. We just did not have the slide deck.

There is a dignity in this that took me years to claim, because the world spent those same years trying to teach me shame. Town kids who learned I smeared floors would make the face, or worse, the joke. And for a while the joke worked on me, because a child believes the world’s opinion of him before he has built his own. Then one day Grandpa David, who had grown up middle-class in Missouri and chosen to live in a Kenyan village on purpose, watched me finish a floor, looked at it the way other men look at a painting, and said, “That is good work. Be proud of good work.” And the shame cracked, just a little, the way the pressure used to crack when I made my mother laugh.

Be proud of good work. He gave me that sentence over a dung floor and I have spent twenty years spending it.

*Roylandz Rule #4: The world will try to shame you for the honest work that kept you alive. Do not accept the loan. The interest on borrowed shame will bankrupt you.*

## Chapter 5: Reading to Chickens

I had no audience, so I built one out of poultry, and they were the best crowd I have ever worked.

It started with necessity. I loved to read aloud, and reading aloud requires a listener, and the listeners in my life were either busy surviving or too tired to perform attention for a small boy with a schoolbook. So I went to the only beings who had time. Our six chickens. I would sit on the dung floor in the lamplight with whatever I could find, a tattered reader, an old newspaper that had wrapped fish, a torn page of a book, and I would perform it. Full voice. Full feeling. Different characters, different accents, the works.

The chickens, to their credit, gathered. I do not claim they understood. I claim they showed up, which is more than you can say for most people who say they will support you. They tilted their heads. They blinked. One of them, a brown hen I privately considered the intellectual of the group, would settle nearest and stay longest, and I directed most of my best material at her. She never gave notes. She never told me the dream was foolish. She never asked who told me I could. She just listened, and a child who is listened to becomes a different animal than a child who is not.

I understand now what was happening, though I did not then. I was practicing. Every news anchor, every comedian, every preacher and politician who ever held a room got their start somewhere small and ridiculous, talking to a mirror or a sibling or, in my case, a livestock focus group. The skill of speaking to an audience that cannot answer, of pacing yourself for ears that are not yet there, of believing your voice matters before anyone has confirmed it, that is the rarest skill in broadcasting and I learned it from chickens for free.

J. K. Rowling wrote her first stories about a rabbit when she was six and read them aloud to her little sister, who was a captive audience much like my hen. The detail comforts me. Somewhere a future writer is reading nonsense to a sibling who would rather be doing anything else, and somewhere a future anchor is reading the back of a cereal box to a goat, and the world has no idea what it is incubating.

The crack in the wall that Grandpa David’s library would later widen, the chickens opened first. They taught me the thing every kid from the margins has to learn the hard way or never. That your voice does not become valuable when an important person finally validates it. It is valuable from the first word, in the lamplight, to an audience of hens, and if you wait for permission to believe that, you will wait until the lamp runs out of paraffin and the night swallows the dream whole.

I did not wait. I performed for the chickens like they were Kenya, and years later, when Kenya was finally watching, I performed for the nation like it was still just six chickens and a clever brown hen. That is the secret, if you want it. The room never changes. Only the size of the coop.

*Roylandz Rule #5: Do not wait for a worthy audience to find your voice worth using. Start with chickens. They show up, which is more than most people do.*

## Chapter 6: Grandma and the God Frequency

My grandmother prayed the way other people breathe, constantly and without announcing it, and she ran her house on a frequency tuned somewhere between heaven and a military barracks.

In the Kemoli and Kamonya households, faith was not a Sunday costume you put on and took off. It was the operating system. We were Quaker on one side, Friends Church people, the spiritual descendants of the missionaries who came to western Kenya in 1902 and stuck. Friends worship has a stillness to it, a belief in the inner light, the conviction that there is something of God in every single person, even the difficult ones, even the loud boy who will not stop talking. And there was the other current too, the Pentecostal heat that ran through the region, the all-night prayers, the songs that could lift a tin roof off its nails.

Grandma believed that a child raised without discipline was a child you were quietly handing to the world to ruin. So she disciplined. The slipper was a theological instrument in her hand. So was the look, the one that could stop a charging bull and definitely stopped a charging grandson. Her love did not arrive as softness. It arrived as structure, as rules, as the relentless expectation that you would do the right thing whether or not anyone was watching, because God was always the audience and God did not blink.

I resented it the way every child resents the wall before they understand it is a wall and not a cage. You smear that floor properly. You greet your elders or you do not eat. You finish what is on your plate because somewhere a child has nothing on theirs, a sentence that made no logistical sense to me as a boy and perfect moral sense to me as a man. You pray before you eat, before you sleep, before a journey, and you mean it, because the day you start praying without meaning it is the day you start lying to the only one who is never fooled.

The discipline felt like punishment. It revealed itself as love only the way bitter medicine reveals itself, long after you have stopped complaining about the taste, when you notice you survived the thing it was protecting you from. Every deadline I have ever met, every promise I have kept, every time I showed up when showing up was inconvenient, I was spending my grandmother’s discipline. She was depositing it in me with a slipper and a stare, and I have been withdrawing it my whole adult life and the account has never run dry.

Nelson Mandela wrote that the brave man is not the one who does not feel afraid but the one who conquers that fear. My grandmother would have nodded and added that bravery, like a clean floor, is mostly daily maintenance, done whether you feel like it or not. She did not read Mandela. She did not need to. She was running the same software in a smaller house.

When she died, years later and far too close to when David died, a structure went out of the world that I am still trying to rebuild inside myself, beam by beam. But that grief belongs to a later chapter, and I am keeping her alive in this one, slipper in hand, eyes like a searchlight, tuned to the God frequency, raising a loud boy into a man who would one day be loud on purpose.

*Roylandz Rule #6: Discipline is love wearing work clothes. You will not recognize it until much later, when it is the only thing still holding your roof up.*

## Chapter 7: When Kenya Burned

I was six years old when my country tried to set itself on fire, and I remember the smell before I understood the politics.

The election came at the end of 2007. Mwai Kibaki against Raila Odinga, the whole nation split along lines that the politicians swore were about leadership and that everybody on the ground knew were about tribe, because that is the poison the colonizers brewed and the independence generation never poured out. The results were announced in a hurry, in a way that smelled wrong even to people who could not yet name the wrongness, and then the country came apart at exactly the seams the colonial mapmakers had drawn.

In our part of western Kenya, the violence was close. Not on the television. On the road. Smoke rose in the wrong directions. Neighbors who had borrowed each other’s salt now eyed each other across a line that had been invisible the week before and was suddenly the most real thing in the world. People who had been simply people became, overnight, their tribe, the way water becomes ice, the same substance turned hard and dangerous by a drop in temperature. More than a thousand Kenyans died in those weeks. Hundreds of thousands were driven from their homes and became internally displaced people, a phrase so clean it hides the dirty truth, which is that families slept in tents in church compounds and stadium grounds while their houses burned behind them.

Grandpa David and Grandma Gladys were in the middle of it, and not as victims hiding but as peacemakers working. This is when I first understood what David’s whole life had been about. He was a Quaker, a man who had built his entire existence on the radical, almost foolish belief that violence is never the answer and that enemies can be turned into neighbors through patient, stubborn, unglamorous talking. When the country burned, the Friends Church Peace Teams went into the displacement camps to run reconciliation seminars, and the work my grandfather had spent decades building suddenly stood between people and their worst instincts. Thirty-two pastors once asked the peace teams to come and even sent the women to preach, and the women preached so well the pastors confessed they had not known women could do that. David told that story with delight for years.

I was too young for the seminars. But I was not too young for the lesson, which arrived in my body before it arrived in my mind. I learned that the loud men with machetes were not strong. They were terrified, and terror had simply turned them inside out. I learned that real strength was the quiet American grandfather walking into a camp full of grief to say, sit down, let us talk, you are still neighbors. I learned that a tribe is a story people tell about each other, and that stories can be rewritten, and that the rewriting is the most dangerous and necessary work there is.

Achebe wrote that when the center cannot hold, things fall apart. In 2008 our center did not hold, and things fell apart, and then a few stubborn people with no weapons and a great deal of faith spent years putting the pieces back, one conversation at a time. I have built a career on talking. The first time I understood that talking could be a weapon for peace and not just for show, I was six, smelling smoke, watching my grandfather refuse to be afraid.

*Roylandz Rule #7: The loudest man in the room is usually the most frightened. Real power is the quiet one walking toward the fire with both hands open.*

## Chapter 8: The Silence That Taught Me Sound

For all my talking, the deepest lessons of my childhood arrived in silence, and I have spent my life trying to fill the kinds of silence that should never be left empty.

There is the silence of a hungry house, where nobody mentions that the pot is light tonight because mentioning it would make it real, so everyone eats slowly and says the food is good and the silence does the lying for them. There is the silence after a fight, when my parents would orbit each other in the small room saying nothing, and the nothing was so loud it pressed on your ears. There is the silence of being poor in a place where some people are not, the silence you keep when the town kids ask what you ate or where you bought your shirt, and you smile and change the subject because the truth would cost you something you cannot afford to spend.

I came to understand silence as a thing with a texture and a weight, almost a sound of its own. And somewhere in there I made the decision that has organized my entire life, though I made it without words because I was a child and children decide things in the body. I decided that I would be loud. Not rude. Not constant. Loud where loudness was needed, in the places where silence was doing harm. I would be the boy who said the thing nobody was saying. I would crack the pressure with a joke. I would name the hunger by laughing at it, which is a way of refusing to let it have the dignity of being unspeakable.

Ken Walibora, the great Swahili writer, gave us the line kufa kuzikana, to die and be buried by one another, an idea about the bonds that survive even death, the obligations we carry for each other in a community. The silence in my childhood was the silence of people who carried those bonds but had run out of the words to say so. Love that cannot find its language goes quiet, and the quiet curdles, and a child grows up inside the curdling and either becomes silent too or becomes the opposite. I became the opposite. I became the noise that the silence needed and never produced.

This is the engine under everything I have built. People see a journalist, a presenter, a loud and confident young man on a national screen, and they assume the confidence is the cause. It is not. The confidence is the symptom. The cause is a small boy in a quiet, heavy room deciding that he refused to let silence win, refused to let the unspoken thing keep its power, refused to grow into one more western Kenyan man with only two emotions and a slipper’s worth of tenderness rationed out across a lifetime.

I would speak. I would say the food was light when the food was light. I would say I was scared when I was scared. And I would do it loudly, on purpose, because the silence had taught me, in its long heavy lessons, exactly what it costs a family to leave the true thing unsaid.

*Roylandz Rule #8: Silence is not peace. Sometimes silence is just fear that learned good manners. Learn to tell which kind you are sitting in, then break the dangerous kind.*

## Chapter 9: Mahemas Primary, My First Stage

Mahemas Primary School was a cluster of buildings, a flagpole, a field of stubborn grass, and the first place that ever handed me a microphone, except the microphone was just a moment of silence and the whole school looking at me.

I walked there barefoot, like most of us, saving the shoes that some of us did not even have. The classrooms had more children than desks and more dreams than either, packed onto wooden benches, sharing torn textbooks three to a copy, reciting in the call-and-response chant that anyone schooled in Kenya can still hear in their sleep. Free Primary Education had just come in, Kibaki’s big promise from 2003, and suddenly the rooms were fuller than the budget, because when a poor country says school is free, every family with a child and a hope sends that child walking down the red road in the morning. The teachers were overwhelmed and underpaid and, the good ones, somehow magnificent anyway.

I was not the best student. I want to be honest, because the inspirational version of this book would lie to you here and tell you I topped every class. I did not. My mind wandered. I talked when I should have listened. But there was one arena where I was undefeated, and it was the front of the room. The recitation. The poem at the parents’ day. The little play. The moment a teacher needed somebody to stand up and speak, and the whole room went shy, and I, who had been rehearsing on chickens for years, rose like it was what I had been born to do, because it was.

The first time I made a whole room laugh on purpose, something happened in my chest that I have been chasing ever since, and I am not ashamed to say it was close to a religious experience. I was small and poor and barefoot and forgettable, and then I said a thing the right way at the right moment and thirty children and one tired teacher laughed, and for that one second I was not small. I was the size of the laugh. I was the size of the room. And I understood, the way you understand your own name, that this was the thing. Not the only thing. But the thing.

Trevor Noah grew up performing survival as comedy, turning the absurd cruelty of his world into something he could hold and hand to other people so they could hold it too. I did not know his story then. I was just a boy at Mahemas finding out that the front of the room was the one place poverty could not follow me. Up there I was not the kid with the light supper and the borrowed textbook. Up there I was the voice, and the voice owed nothing to anyone, and the voice was already, even then, looking past the cluster of buildings and the stubborn field toward something it could not name but could absolutely feel, the way you feel weather coming before you see the cloud.

Mahemas gave me my first stage. It was a dirt-floored classroom with a leaking roof and a flag that had seen better governments. It was perfect. Every important room I have stood in since, every studio, every stage, every camera with its little red judge of a light, has secretly been that classroom, and I have secretly been that barefoot boy, rising while the others go shy, opening his mouth because somebody has to and it might as well be the loud one the nurse warned everybody about.

*Roylandz Rule #9: Your first stage will be small and unimpressive and probably free. Stand on it like it is national television. The habit is the whole point.*

# PART II: BLOODLINES AND LIBRARIES

*(2008 to 2015)*

## Chapter 10: The Abatongoi Lineage

Before I was Eugine, before I was Roylandz, before I was anybody’s anchor, I was Abatongoi, and that word carries more of me than my passport does.

The Abatongoi are a clan of the Bunyore people, who are part of the great Luhya nation of western Kenya, who are part of the Bantu migration that walked this continent for centuries before a single European drew a single border across it. My father’s line runs back through that clan to an ancestor, Anyole, whose name the Bunyore carry the way a river carries the name of its source. When you understand that your name is a road, and that the road was being walked by your people while Europe was still arguing about the shape of the world, it does something to your spine. You stop apologizing for taking up space, because your line was taking up space here first.

I learned the lineage the way every Luhya child learns it, in fragments, at funerals and weddings and the long evenings when an old man decides the young ones have grown soft and need reminding. The names came in lists, generation after generation, and I could not hold them all, but I held the feeling, which is the thing the lists are really transmitting. The feeling that you are not the beginning. That you are a continuation. That somewhere behind you stands a crowd of people who survived things that would unmake a modern man before breakfast, and that their survival is the only reason you are here being dramatic about Wi-Fi.

There is a tension in the lineage too, and an honest book has to say so. The clan that claimed me at birth was the same clan my mother fled in a matatu before dawn so she could give birth on her own terms. The Abatongoi welcomed children with prayers and ceremonies and the firm hand of tradition, and tradition, for all its beauty, was often a hand that pressed hardest on women. My mother loved the people and refused the cage, and I grew up in the space between, claimed by a clan and raised by people the clan had not chosen. I am Abatongoi by blood and something else by upbringing, and that doubleness is not a wound. It is a vantage point. From it you can see two worlds at once, and a man who can see two worlds at once was practically born to stand between a story and a camera.

Adichie writes that to understand a person you must understand the stories that made them, and that a single story is always a lie of omission. My single story, the one strangers tell, is the loud TV boy from a poor village. The fuller story starts with Anyole and walks forward through centuries of people whose names I am still learning, who farmed and fought and migrated and married and buried and were buried, kufa kuzikana, until the road reached a mud house in Manyonyi and produced a child who would not stop talking.

I carry them when I walk into a studio. The crowd behind me. I have never once felt alone under those lights, and now you know why.

*Roylandz Rule #10: You are not the beginning of your story. You are the part that gets to talk. Honor the silent generations who bought you the microphone.*

## Chapter 11: David Zarembka and the Library That Saved Me

I found out today, writing this book, that my grandfather founded a school. A whole school. And the moment I read it, my entire life clicked into a shape I had felt my whole life but never seen.

David Zarembka was an American, born in 1943 in Clayton, Missouri, a middle-class white boy who went to Harvard, which is to say a young man with every available exit from hardship, and who looked at all those exits and walked, instead, straight into the work. He joined the Peace Corps. He came to East Africa. And in 1968, before I or my mother or even the politics that shaped me existed, David Zarembka became the founding head of Mua Hills Harambee Secondary School in Machakos. A school. Harambee, the Kenyan word that means let us all pull together. He built a place for children to learn and he built it by pulling together, and then he spent the rest of his life building places and pulling people together, an alternative high school in America, food cooperatives, an orphans program, a technical school in Uganda, and finally the African Great Lakes Initiative, which carried peace into the bleeding heart of Rwanda and Burundi and Congo.

I did not know any of that as a boy. I only knew the library.

It sat in his house in Lumakanda, where he lived with my grandmother Gladys, in a village in Lugari that he had chosen on purpose over every comfortable life available to him. Not a grand library with marble and donors. A home library, shelves from floor to ceiling, hundreds of books arranged by a man who considered disorder a small disrespect toward knowledge. Fiction and biography and science and poetry and encyclopedias and children’s stories, in English and in Swahili, shipped from America and bought in Nairobi and donated by passing Quakers, accumulated across decades with the patient greed of a man who believed a library was not a luxury but a survival tool, the way other men believed it about cattle.

Here is the thing he did that rewired me. He let me touch the books. Most adults, faced with a small village boy near valuable objects, perform the protective flinch, the quick interception, the careful, those are not toys. David did the reverse. He pulled books down and put them in my hands. He read passages aloud. He pointed at pictures and named the world. He treated a barefoot six-year-old from a dung-floored house as a person capable of having a thought, which was, looking back, the single most radical act anyone performed on me in my entire childhood.

And he asked questions. Never lectures, always questions. What do you see in this picture. Why do you think the man did that. What would you have done. Questions that assumed I had answers worth hearing. It is the oldest Quaker idea, that there is that of God in every person, an inner light that does not need installing, only drawing out. He did not try to fill me up. He tried to draw me out. He treated my mind as full and waiting rather than empty and lacking, and the distance between the boy reading to chickens in Manyonyi and the boy reading in a library in Lumakanda turned out not to be a distance of intelligence at all. It was a distance of access. He closed it with his own two hands and a shelf of books.

So when I read, today, that he founded a school in 1968, I understood my own life backward all at once. My thing with schools. The way I have always walked into a classroom and felt at home, the way I became valedictorian, the way I cannot pass a school without wanting to go in and talk to the children, the way I keep building little academies of attention everywhere I go. That is not random. That is inheritance. The man who raised my mind founded a school the way other men found dynasties, and he poured the same founding instinct into me across that library floor, one question at a time. I am, in a way I only just understood, his last school. Enrollment of one. Open all hours. Still in session.

*Roylandz Rule #11: One person who hands you a book and believes you can read it is worth a thousand who tell you to be realistic. Find that person. Then become that person for somebody else. That is how the debt gets paid.*

## Chapter 12: Grandma Gladys, Love Disguised as Discipline

If David ran the library, Grandma Gladys ran the law, and the law was love with the softness sanded off so it would last longer.

Gladys Kamonya was a Quaker woman of formidable spiritual seriousness, the kind of grandmother whose prayer could go on long enough that you opened one eye to check whether the food was still warm and met her gaze, already open, already disappointed in your faith. She had married the American and stayed Kenyan to the bone, and her household ran on a discipline so total it felt, to a loud boy, like a slightly holy prison. You greeted elders correctly or the day did not proceed. You did your chores fully and not theatrically. You told the truth, because she could smell a lie the way you can smell rain, and the punishment for the lie was always worse than the punishment for the thing you lied about, a piece of moral mathematics I resisted for years and now teach to anyone who will sit still.

Her love did not announce itself. It did not arrive as cuddles and indulgence and the sugary affirmation that the modern world has confused for love. It arrived as structure. As the slipper, yes, and the searchlight stare, but mostly as the relentless, unsexy, daily expectation that I would become someone, and that becoming someone was not a feeling you waited for but a thing you built with your hands every single morning whether or not you felt like it.

I resented her exactly as much as a child is supposed to resent the person trying hardest to save him. And I understood her, fully, only after she was gone, which is the cruelest design flaw in the entire human arrangement, that we decode our grandmothers a few years too late to thank them properly.

Mandela wrote that the brave man conquers fear rather than failing to feel it. Gladys would have shortened that. She would have said courage is just doing your duty when nobody is clapping, and then she would have asked whether I had finished my homework, because to her these were the same question. She built me the way she built everything, slowly, without applause, expecting the structure to outlast her.

It has. Every time I keep a promise I do not feel like keeping, that is Gladys, holding up a beam inside me. She disguised the love as discipline so well that I almost missed it. Almost. I caught it in the end, Grandma. A little late. But I caught it.

*Roylandz Rule #12: If your grandmother’s love felt like rules, you were not unloved. You were being built. Go back and read the architecture. It will move you.*

## Chapter 13: Lumakanda Township Primary

When I moved to Lumakanda for stretches of my schooling, I went from the village edge of things to the township center, and the geography of my ambition changed with the geography of my address.

Lumakanda was a town with a market and a tarmac road that mostly behaved and shops with actual signboards, and to a Manyonyi boy that was nearly a city. Lumakanda Township Primary was bigger and busier than Mahemas, with more children who wore shoes on ordinary days and more teachers and the same fundamental Kenyan classroom energy, which is the energy of too many bright children and not enough resources, all of it held together by chalk, repetition, and the cane.

I should talk about the cane, because to skip it would be to lie by omission, and Achebe taught me that omission is the favorite lie of people who want to look good. Corporal punishment was the weather of Kenyan schooling in those years. You were caned for lateness, for wrong answers, for noise, for the crime of being a child in a system too overwhelmed to manage children any other way. Some of it built discipline. Much of it just built fear and a generation of adults who flinch at sudden movements and call it nothing. I am not going to pretend the cane made me. I am going to tell you that I survived it, that I learned to read a teacher’s mood the way I had learned to read my father’s, and that the room reading I had practiced at home became, in school, a survival instrument that doubled as a performance skill.

Because here is what I did with the township school. I made it my arena. The bigger crowd did not frighten me, it fed me. More children meant more laughter when I got the joke right, more silence to break, more eyes to win. I discovered debate, the formal kind, where you are handed a side and told to argue it whether or not you believe it, and I was instantly, dangerously good at it, because I had been arguing both sides of my parents’ marriage in my own head since I was five. I discovered that a boy who could speak could become somebody in a place where most children were taught to keep quiet and copy notes.

Rowling built a whole world out of a school, because a school is where a child first discovers that there are systems larger than the family, with their own rules and their own hierarchies and their own chances to be either crushed or chosen. Lumakanda Township Primary was my first such system. I learned to work it. Not to cheat it, to work it, to find the cracks where a loud poor boy could earn a place the official channels would never have offered him. The crack was always the same crack. The microphone. The front of the room. The moment they needed a voice and I already had mine warmed up from years on the dung floor with the chickens.

*Roylandz Rule #13: A system that was not built for you can still be worked by you. Find the crack where your gift fits, and walk through it before anyone tells you it was not a door.*

## Chapter 14: The Avamaseero Thread

My mother gave me a second lineage to stand on, the Avamaseero thread, woven through the green hills of Maragoli in Vihiga, and it is the thread of the people who actually raised me.

The Luhya are not one people so much as a federation of related peoples, and the Maragoli are among them, famous across Kenya for two things, an almost supernatural respect for education and a population density so high the joke is that there is no more room in Maragoli, only more Maragoli. They are a people who decided, generations ago and against every economic logic, that the way out of a small crowded land was through the head, through school, through the relentless conversion of poverty into qualifications. A Maragoli grandmother will sell the last goat for school fees and consider it the best trade she ever made.

This is the soil my mother grew in, and through Gladys and through the Kemoli connection, the soil I was transplanted into. The Avamaseero thread is why education was never optional in my world, never one path among several. It was the path, the only sanctioned escape, the family religion underneath the family religion. You could fail at many things in my upbringing and be forgiven. Failing to take school seriously was the one heresy with no path back to grace.

For a long time I thought this was just my family being intense. Then I grew up and traveled and read and understood that the Maragoli educational fever is one of the great quiet stories of modern Kenya, a whole people lifting themselves by deciding, collectively, that the mind was the one piece of property the colonizer and the drought and the corrupt government could not seize. You cannot repossess a degree. You cannot burn down what someone knows. The Avamaseero understood this in their bones, and they planted it in me before I could spell it.

When I became valedictorian years later, when I stood up to give the speech, I was not just a kid who had done well. I was a return on a multigenerational investment, the dividend of a thread of people who had bet everything on the head and finally, in me, in that moment, watched the bet pay. My mother was in that audience. The Avamaseero were in that audience, all of them, the living and the kufa kuzikana dead, the whole green hill of Maragoli leaning in to see whether the family religion had been true.

It was true. It is still true. I am the proof, and I do not say that to boast. I say it to testify, the way you testify in a Friends meeting when the inner light moves you, plainly, because the thing happened and somebody has to say so out loud.

*Roylandz Rule #14: Some families pass down land. Mine passed down the conviction that no one can repossess what you know. It is the only inheritance that survives a bad harvest.*

## Chapter 15: Samson Kemoli and the Borehole Call

Grandpa Samson Kemoli built a place and named it after himself, which sounds like vanity until you understand that the man simply refused to wait for the world to leave him a mark, so he left his own.

He called it Kemoview. A place with his name on it, his stake in the earth, his statement that a Kemoli had stood here and built something and would be remembered for it. As a boy I thought it was just a name. As a man I understood it was a philosophy. Do not wait to be commemorated. Commemorate yourself by building. Put your name on good work and let the work argue for you after you are gone. I built a media brand called Roylandz out of nothing but a nickname and a refusal to be small, and I did not realize until recently that I had simply done, in the digital world, what Grandpa Samson did with land and a signboard. Kemoview was the first Roylandz. He just got there first, with a borehole instead of a YouTube channel.

The borehole. That is the other thing about Samson Kemoli, the practical, life-giving, unglamorous thing. Water. In a region where the borehole is the difference between a day spent living and a day spent walking with a jerrican, Samson invested in water, and water in western Kenya is not infrastructure, it is mercy made physical. He invested in school fees too, and here is the part I carry hardest. He never lectured. Other relatives gave you a little help wrapped in a long sermon about gratitude and responsibility and how things were harder in their day. Samson gave the help clean. No interest. No lecture. A phone call at the exact moment a fee was due, a quiet arrangement made, a problem dissolved before it could become a crisis, and not one word about it afterward.

The call always came at the right time. I do not know how he knew. Maybe my grandmother told him. Maybe he simply paid attention, the rarest gift, the one David also had. But across the years there were these interventions, small and surgical, a fee here, a fare there, a word to the right person, each one arriving like a quiet engineer adjusting one valve in a complicated system so that the whole thing kept running. He understood, the way good engineers understand, that you do not need to fix everything. You need to fix the one thing, at the one moment, and the rest holds.

This book is, among other things, the receipt I promised him. Grandpa, the fees were used well. The water you bought ran through a boy who grew into a man with a microphone, and he is using it to say your name out loud, the way you said your own name into the earth at Kemoview, refusing to be forgotten, building your monument while you were still alive to enjoy the view.

*Roylandz Rule #15: The best help arrives without a lecture attached. If you are ever in a position to give, give clean. The interest you do not charge is the interest that compounds in someone’s whole life.*

## Chapter 16: Nairobi Noise, My First City Shock

The first time I went to Nairobi, the city tried to swallow me, and I, being who I am, decided to make friends with the throat.

Nothing prepares a village boy for Nairobi. Not stories, not television, not the most detailed warning from the most traveled cousin. The matatus alone are a civilization, painted like tropical fish, blasting music loud enough to rearrange your organs, conductors hanging out the door doing complex arithmetic and complex insults at the same time, the whole thing a controlled riot on wheels that somehow gets you where you are going. The crowds at the bus station moved like a single creature with a hundred thousand legs. The buildings were taller than anything I had a category for. The noise was constant, layered, total, a city that never once stopped talking, and I, the boy whose nurse named him Loud One, finally met something louder than myself and felt, for the first time in my life, almost quiet.

Almost. I am still me.

The city showed me the gap. In Manyonyi and Lumakanda, poor was the water you swam in, so common it was invisible, and you could go whole stretches forgetting you lacked anything because everyone around you lacked it too. Nairobi removed that mercy. In Nairobi I saw, for the first time, what money looked like up close, the cars, the malls, the children my age in clean uniforms being dropped at school by drivers, and I saw what no money looked like too, the kids my age living in the street, sniffing glue to kill the hunger, invisible to the very people the city had made rich. The gap was not a statistic in Nairobi. It was a thing you could see by turning your head, wealth on this corner and ruin on that one, and the corner you stood on decided your whole life.

I felt the shame come up, the old town-kid shame, the smear-the-floor shame. And then I did the thing my grandfather’s library and my grandmother’s discipline had quietly trained me to do. I refused the loan. I looked at the gap and I did not decide I was small. I decided the gap was a problem to be crossed, not a sentence to be served. I noticed, the way David had taught me to notice, that the people on the rich corner were not smarter than me. They had access. And access, I already knew, can be closed in by a man with a voice and a refusal to quit, the way David had closed the access gap with a shelf of books.

So Nairobi did not break me. It briefed me. It showed me the whole board, the wealth and the ruin and the long road between them, and it lit, somewhere behind my ribs, the specific ambition that would drag me back to this city years later, not as a frightened visitor but as a voice it would have to reckon with. The city tried to swallow me. I made a note of the way out, which was always going to be straight up the throat and into the light.

*Roylandz Rule #16: The first time you see how big the gap is, you get to choose. It is either a sentence or a distance. One you serve. The other you cross. Choose the verb that keeps you moving.*

## Chapter 17: Back to Lugari, the Return

After the city, the village received me back, and a boy who has seen Nairobi can never again be fully a village boy, which is both a gift and a small grief.

The return is its own genre of experience, the going-back, and every Kenyan who has ever left a place and come home knows the strange double vision it produces. Lugari was exactly as I had left it, the red road, the green fields, the woodsmoke mornings, the borehole, the church, the relatives who greeted me with the warmth that the city had taught me to recognize as priceless. And it was completely different, because I was different, because I had stood in Nairobi and seen the size of the world, and you cannot unsee the size of the world. The village had not shrunk. My idea of the possible had grown, and the gap between the two became a quiet ache I had no name for at thirteen.

This is the moment a certain kind of young person makes a decision that defines the rest of their life, and they usually make it without knowing they are making it. You can resent the village for being small. You can grow ashamed of it, deny it, build a future that pretends it never happened, become one more western Kenyan in Nairobi who answers questions about home with a vague wave and a change of subject. Or you can do the harder, better thing. You can carry it. You can decide that the village is not the thing you escaped but the thing you represent, that your whole future is going to be a long argument on behalf of the red road and the borehole and the children still reading to chickens in the lamplight.

I chose to carry it. I will not pretend the choice was clean or instant or that I never felt the shame. I felt it. But David had founded a school. Samson had named a place. Gladys had built me with discipline. The Avamaseero had bet the goat on my head. I was not a self-made anything. I was a community-made boy, built by people who had poured themselves into me on purpose, and to be ashamed of where they built me would have been to spit on the building.

So I returned, and I let the return do its work, which was to sharpen the ambition into a mission. Not just to get out. Anyone can want to get out. To get out and turn around and pull, harambee, the way David’s school was built, the way the whole Maragoli people had lifted themselves, each one reaching back for the next. The village was not my embarrassment. It was my assignment. And a boy with an assignment walks differently than a boy with an escape plan. He walks like he is going somewhere and coming back.

I was. I did. But first there was boarding school, and boarding school, my friends, was a war.

*Roylandz Rule #17: When you go back home after seeing the world, do not be ashamed of the smallness. Be assigned by it. The place that built you is not your past. It is your purpose, wearing work clothes again.*

# PART III: BOARDING SCHOOL BATTLES

*(2016 to 2019)*

## Chapter 18: Welcome to Murgusi, the Dorm That Taught Discipline

The day my mother left me at the gate of Murgusi, a Seventh-day Adventist boarding school, I understood for the first time that discipline at home and discipline at school were two different animals, and the school animal had bigger teeth.

Kenyan boarding school is a national rite of passage that nobody fully describes to you beforehand, the way nobody describes childbirth honestly to a pregnant woman, out of a kind of mercy and a kind of cowardice. You arrive with a metal box of provisions and a list of regulations longer than the Bible the school will be making you read. You are assigned a bed in a dormitory of forty boys, a cube of mattress and a strip of floor that constitute your entire private kingdom. And then the system begins to do to you what the system does, which is to take a soft, mothered village boy and apply pressure until he becomes either a stronger version of himself or a broken one, with very little room in between.

The first weeks are designed to humble you. The seniors had a thousand small tyrannies, the errands, the mockery, the unwritten hierarchy that every institution of confined young men reinvents from scratch as if it were the first to think of cruelty. The food was an education in scarcity all over again, githeri that you learned to be grateful for, the occasional miracle of meat that the whole dorm discussed for days. The cold mornings, the cold water, the bell that did not care about your dreams, ringing you into a day that belonged to the timetable and not to you.

And I want to tell you the truth, which is that I both hated it and needed it.
