I had been built at home by Gladys with the slipper and the stare, but home discipline has love woven visibly through it. Murgusi discipline was love-free, structure without affection, and that turned out to be its own kind of necessary education, because the world I was walking toward does not love you either. The world has a bell. The world does not care about your dreams in the morning. The world will hand you a list of regulations and a strip of floor and dare you to build something anyway. Murgusi was the world in miniature, with a tin roof, and it taught me to build anyway.

Mandela described prison as a place that either destroys you or forges you, and I am not comparing a boarding school to Robben Island, I am not a fool. But the principle scales down. Confinement under harsh rules will find your weakest point and press on it, and you will discover, under that pressure, whether you are made of something that hardens or something that crumbles. I hardened. I had been pre-tempered at home, and Murgusi finished the job. By the end I could take the bell, the cold, the scarcity, the hierarchy, and still find the front of the room, still find the joke, still find the voice. They could regulate everything except the noise. The noise was mine, and I kept it.

*Roylandz Rule #18: A hard place will either break you or finish building you, and the difference is mostly whether someone built a foundation in you first. Thank whoever poured your foundation. Then go let the hard place finish the walls.*

## Chapter 19: Faith Versus Funk

I was raised Quaker, sent to an Adventist school, and spent four years caught in a holy traffic jam between Saturday and Saturday night.

The Seventh-day Adventists keep the Sabbath from Friday sunset to Saturday sunset, and they keep it seriously, the whole school folding into worship and rest and the particular hush that descends when an institution genuinely believes God is watching the clock. I had grown up with the Friends, with the inner light and the long silent waiting and the conviction that worship is a daily and inward thing. Now I was in a tradition that worshipped on a different day with a different intensity, and the theological whiplash was real for a teenage boy already trying to figure out what he actually believed underneath what he had been handed.

But the funk. Ah, the funk. Because here is the thing about a school full of teenagers under heavy religious discipline. The energy does not disappear. It goes underground and gets creative. Saturday night, after the Sabbath had set, was when the place came alive, and I was its electrician. Entertainment. Music. The dorm performances, the talent moments, the smuggled phone playing a song that forty boys knew every word to, the comedy I had been refining since the dung floor now finding its largest and most appreciative audience yet, a captive crowd of teenage boys desperate to laugh, the easiest and the most ruthless audience on earth, because they will love you if you are funny and destroy you if you are not.

The tension between faith and funk was not a problem to me. It was the whole point. I learned that the sacred and the hilarious are not enemies, that you can fear God on Saturday and make the dorm cry laughing on Saturday night and that both are forms of the same fundamental human reaching, the reaching for something bigger than the cold and the bell and the strip of floor. The preacher and the comedian are doing the same job from different angles. They are both trying to lift the room. I have been both, sometimes in the same hour, and I learned the trick at Murgusi, in the gap between the Sabbath silence and the Saturday-night roar.

Trevor Noah survived an absurd and dangerous world by finding the funny in it, by refusing to let cruelty be the only voice. The Adventist rigor and the underground funk taught me the same survival mathematics. You honor the serious thing. And then you find the joke, because the joke is how the human animal keeps its soul through a hard timetable, and a school that has too much faith and not enough laughter will produce graduates who are correct and brittle and quietly miserable. I refused to graduate brittle. I kept the funk alive, on purpose, as a kind of ministry. The dorm needed a laugh more than it needed another rule. So I supplied the laugh, and I have been supplying it ever since.

*Roylandz Rule #19: The sacred and the hilarious are not enemies. They are two hands lifting the same heavy room. Learn to use both and you will never leave a room lower than you found it.*

## Chapter 20: Entertainment Captain Without Applause

They made me Entertainment Captain, which sounds like a position of glory and was, in practice, a position of unpaid emotional labor with a fancy title, and it taught me everything about leadership that the leadership books leave out.

The job was to organize the school’s entertainment, the events, the talent shows, the performances, the morale of a few hundred confined and homesick young people. And here is what nobody tells you about being the one responsible for everyone else’s joy. It is lonely. While everyone else gets to relax and be entertained, you are working, watching the room, managing the act that did not show, soothing the performer with stage fright, reading the crowd’s mood and adjusting in real time, carrying the weight of whether the night succeeds or dies. The audience laughs and goes to bed happy. The Entertainment Captain lies awake replaying the two minutes that went flat.

I learned that leadership is mostly invisible. The visible part, the captain standing up front, getting the applause, that is maybe five percent of it. The other ninety-five percent is the unseen labor, the planning, the cleaning up, the absorbing of other people’s anxiety so the show can go on, the showing up early and leaving late, the thousand small choices nobody will ever know you made. I learned that you do not lead for the applause, because the applause goes to the act and you are not the act, you are the one who made the act possible, and if you need the applause you will quit, and good leaders cannot afford to quit when the applause goes elsewhere.

This was a more important lesson than any I got in class, and I got it for free, lying awake in a dorm at sixteen feeling the strange loneliness of being responsible for the happiness of people who had no idea what it cost me. Years later, running digital for a national television station, leading teams, carrying shows, I would feel that exact loneliness again, the captain’s loneliness, and I would recognize it like an old roommate. The applause goes to the screen. The cost stays with the one who built the screen’s content at two in the morning. I knew that cost at sixteen. Murgusi taught it to me with a title and no salary, which is, when you think about it, the most realistic leadership training money cannot buy.

There is a Mandela line I have always loved, that it is better to lead from behind and let others believe they are in front, that you take the front line only in danger. The Entertainment Captain leads from behind by definition. You put the performers in front. You take the front line only when an act collapses and someone has to save the night, and then you step up, save it, and step back into the shadow where the work actually lives. I did not read that line until I was older. By then I had already lived it, in a tin-roofed dorm, captain of a joy I rarely got to feel.

*Roylandz Rule #20: Leadership is ninety-five percent invisible labor and five percent applause that goes to someone else. If you cannot do the ninety-five for the love of it, do not take the title. The title is the lightest part.*

## Chapter 21: The Boy Who Debated Soldiers

The single moment that pointed me at my whole future arrived dressed in a paramilitary uniform, and I almost missed it because I was too busy arguing.

The General Service Unit, the GSU, is Kenya’s paramilitary force, the hard edge of the state, the men you do not want to meet on a bad day for the government. One of them came to our school, I will not relitigate exactly why, some event, some talk, some official visit, and there was a discussion, and I, being the boy I had always been, did what I always did. I engaged. I did not stay quiet and small in the presence of authority and a uniform and a gun. I debated him. Respectfully, but fully, the way I had been arguing both sides since I was five, the way Lumakanda’s debate club had sharpened, the way the dorm had encouraged. I made my points. I held my ground. I was sixteen or seventeen and I went toe to toe, in words, with a trained agent of state power, and I did not flinch.

And the officer, instead of crushing the loud boy, did something that rerouted my life. He looked at me, after, and he told me, in essence, that I had a gift, that the way I spoke, the way I thought on my feet, the way I held a position and pressed it, was not normal, that I should do something with it. He saw it. He named it. A hard man in a hard uniform, who had every reason to dislike a teenager who would not back down, instead pointed at the very thing that made me difficult and called it a gift.

I have thought about that man many times. He probably does not remember me. He met a loud schoolboy on an ordinary day and said an encouraging sentence and walked back into his demanding life. He has no idea that the sentence landed in a boy who collected such sentences like Grandpa Samson’s borehole collected rain, that it joined David’s you can read this and the GSU officer’s you have a gift and the nurse’s loud one into a small private constellation that a poor boy navigates by when the night is dark and the dream is large.

This is the thing about the people who change your life. They almost never know they did it. They drop a sentence and walk away, and the sentence grows in you for years, and they go to their graves with no idea that they founded a school in a boy’s chest the way Grandpa David founded one in Machakos. The GSU officer who debated a schoolboy and lost gracefully and then said the schoolboy had a gift, wherever you are, sir, this paragraph is your monument. You pointed me at the microphone before I knew the microphone was waiting. A hard man can be a kind man. You were both, for one sentence, and one sentence was enough.

*Roylandz Rule #21: The person who changes your life will probably never know they did it. So go drop encouraging sentences everywhere, recklessly, like seeds. You will never see the forest. Plant it anyway.*

## Chapter 22: Pacifica and the Bucket of Water

Pacifica came into my life the way weather comes in, with no warning and no permission, and the first thing she ever gave me was a bucket of water in the face in front of three schools.

She was at a neighboring girls’ school, and I met her the way boarding school teenagers met anyone, at the carefully chaperoned inter-school events, the drama festivals, the music competitions, the social functions that the schools organized with the doomed intention of letting hormonal teenagers socialize without socializing. Pacifica was loud the way I was loud, confident the way I was confident, the kind of person who had learned that personality is currency and had decided to be wealthy. We did not start as a couple. We started as rivals. We argued about everything, performances and politics and who was funnier, the kind of sparring that two show-offs do when they are attracted to each other and do not yet have the vocabulary to admit it.

Then, at a social, I said something. A joke. At her expense, almost certainly, delivered with the careless confidence of a boy who had been rewarded for his mouth so often that he had stopped checking whether everyone enjoyed it. I genuinely do not remember the words, and that is not convenient amnesia, it is evidence, evidence of how little the comment cost me to make and how much it cost her to receive. Because Pacifica did not respond with words, which I could have handled. She responded with a full bucket of water, thrown directly at my face, in public, in front of roughly a hundred students from three schools, all of whom stopped breathing and then started laughing in the same instant.

I stood there, soaked, the loudest boy in Murgusi dripping in stunned silence while a girl held an empty bucket and the satisfied expression of a person who had located the single most efficient method of communicating an opinion. The laughter was immediate and merciless and universal. Boys I had entertained. Boys I had led as captain. All of them delighted, because the boy who was always in control was standing in a puddle of his own arrogance and the reversal was too perfect to waste.

That bucket of water was the most useful thing anyone did for me in those years. It taught me, in cold and public form, that confidence without awareness is just arrogance, that a mouth without a brake is a danger to its owner, that being funny does not give you the right to be cruel, and that the universe keeps a bucket ready for boys who forget this. I deserved it. I knew I deserved it even while I was wiping my eyes. And something in Pacifica respected that I did not retaliate, did not sulk, did not turn it ugly, just took the correction.

So we became friends, and then, in time, we became more, and Pacifica was my first real relationship, the first girl I was actually with, the first time I learned that loving a person is a different and harder skill than entertaining a room. We argued as a couple the way we had argued as rivals, two storms sharing one sky. And eventually, the way these things go, the storms wore each other out, and we broke up, and the breakup hurt in a way the bucket never had, because the bucket only got my face wet and the breakup got somewhere deeper. But she had already given me the lesson that mattered most, the cold and public one. Check what you said first. The correction might be exactly what you needed. I have checked what I said, ever since, and Pacifica, wherever you are, you can take the credit. You aimed the bucket. It landed. The boy grew up a little. Thank you, I think. Mostly.

*Roylandz Rule #22: If someone throws water at your face, check what you said before you check who threw it. The most important corrections in your life will arrive cold, in public, and entirely deserved.*

## Chapter 23: Millicent and the First Kiss

A few months after Pacifica and I ended, Millicent arrived, and where Pacifica had been weather, Millicent was a quiet room you did not want to leave, and she gave me my first kiss.

I want to be careful telling this, because it is tender, and tenderness is the hardest thing to write without ruining it. Millicent was at another neighboring school, and she was smart in a way that did not perform. Pacifica’s intelligence announced itself and dared you to keep up. Millicent’s intelligence sat quietly and waited for you to notice, and when you noticed, you realized she had understood the whole conversation three layers deeper than you and had simply been too kind to say so. She listened. Actually listened, the rare kind, where the other person responds to what you meant instead of what you said, and a boy who has spent his whole life filling silences finds, suddenly, a silence he does not need to fill, because she is in it with him, and the silence is good.

I was still bruised from Pacifica, still learning that a heart can work and break in the same season. And into that bruised season came this calm, intelligent, attentive girl, and I fell, slower this time, more carefully, having recently been taught humility by a bucket. We exchanged letters, because it was the late twenty-tens and phones were banned in school and a letter on blue-lined exercise book paper was still the technology of teenage love in Kenya. The same paper I did homework on. The same paper Gladys had once inspected for neatness, now carrying the most terrifying assignment of all, the truth about how I felt.

I wrote her the inside-out letter eventually, the one where a boy turns himself over completely and mails the result and then cannot eat until the reply comes. And the reply, when it came, contained two words at the top that rewired me, written above a half page of other things I barely read because the two words ate all my attention. I know. Not I love you too. Not rejection. Something braver than both, an acknowledgment that she saw me, that the inside-out version had arrived intact and been received by someone honest enough not to pretend, and serious enough to say so. I know. She knew. I carried that letter until the paper went soft.

And then, on an afternoon I will not cheapen by over-describing, Millicent gave me my first kiss. I had performed for crowds, debated soldiers, led a school’s entire entertainment, made hundreds of people laugh on command, and none of it, not one second of all that noise, had prepared me for the silence of that. The boy who always had something to say had, for once, absolutely nothing, and the nothing was the most complete sentence of my young life. First love does not teach you whether it will last. It teaches you that your heart works, that the machinery is real, that you are capable of the thing the poets keep insisting on. Millicent taught me my heart worked. That was her gift, and it was enormous, and you do not forget the person who proves to you that you are not made of stone.

We did not last forever, the way first loves rarely do, pulled apart by distance and schedules and the relentless gravity of two young lives heading toward separate futures. But she is in me permanently, the calm one, the listener, the two-word letter, the first kiss that turned the loudest boy in three schools silent for the only time anyone can remember. Achebe wrote that a man who pays respect to the great paves the way for his own greatness. I pay respect to Millicent here, in print, where it lasts. She was great to me, in the precise and quiet way that the loud do not expect greatness to come, and she paved a way she will never see.

*Roylandz Rule #23: First love does not promise to last. Its only job is to prove your heart works. Let it do its one job, thank it, and carry the proof into every love that comes after.*

## Chapter 24: KCSE and the Curse of Expectations

The Kenya Certificate of Secondary Education exam is the closest thing my country has to a national judgment day, and I walked into it carrying the expectations of every person who had ever invested in me, which is a heavy bag to sit an exam with.

If you did not grow up in Kenya, it is hard to convey the total psychic weight of KCSE. For weeks at the end of secondary school, an entire nation of teenagers funnels toward a set of examinations that will, the system insists, determine the rest of their lives, which university they enter, which course, which career, which version of the future becomes available and which doors quietly lock. The results are read out on national television by the education minister, school by school, grade by grade, a public reckoning conducted in front of the whole country. Families plan around it. Communities pray over it. And every child sitting it knows that they are not just sitting it for themselves. They are sitting it for the people who sold the goat.

I felt the Avamaseero behind me, the whole education-mad lineage, the family religion demanding its return. I felt Gladys’s discipline and David’s library and Samson’s quiet fees, all of it converging on a desk and a paper and a clock. The curse of expectations is that the very people who lift you also, without meaning to, load you, and you cannot tell them you are buckling because they have given you no permission to buckle, because in their day things were harder, because a boy who has been given so much has no right to be afraid of an exam. So you carry it silently, which is exactly the silence my whole childhood had taught me to distrust, and I sat in that exam room filling that silence with the only thing I had, the will to not let the people behind me down.

I will not turn this into a triumphant test-score chapter, because the truth is more interesting than triumph. I did well enough. Not the perfect, mythological result of inspirational books, the result that opens every door at once. A real result, a good result, the result of a clever boy who talked too much and studied unevenly and still, when it mattered, when the whole lineage was watching, summoned enough to be proud of. Good enough to walk forward with my head up. Good enough to honor the goat.

But the deeper thing KCSE taught me was about expectations themselves, that they are a gift wrapped in a weight, and that you have to learn to carry the gift without being crushed by the weight, and that this is one of the central problems of being a person who other people believe in. I would face it again, larger, when a national audience started believing in me, when seven hundred thousand people were watching and the expectations stopped being a lineage and became a public. The skill is the same at every scale. You hold the belief of others as fuel, never as a verdict. The day you let their expectations become the judge instead of the engine, you have handed strangers the gavel over your own soul, and they will not use it kindly, because a crowd never does.

*Roylandz Rule #24: The people who believe in you are also, accidentally, loading you. Carry their belief as fuel, never as a verdict. The day you let it become the judge, you have lost the case before the bell.*

## Chapter 25: Law or Journalism, the Day Destiny Called

At the crossroads after secondary school, the respectable world pointed me toward law, and a quieter voice pointed me toward journalism, and choosing between them was choosing between the life people wanted for me and the life that was actually mine.

Law was the dream destination for a clever, argumentative African boy with a gift for words, and everyone could see it. Look at how he debates, they said. Look how he holds a position, how he thinks on his feet, how he made the GSU officer take him seriously. He should be an advocate. He should be in the courtroom, in the silk, in the prestige and the income and the respectability that a poor family understandably craves for its brightest son. Law was the safe loud, the sanctioned use of a big mouth, the version of my gift that came with a title the village would recognize and a salary the village would celebrate.

And it was wrong for me, and I knew it the way you know your own name, even though I could not yet defend the knowing.

Because I did not actually want to win arguments. I wanted to tell stories. I did not want to represent one client against another in a system most of my people could not afford to enter. I wanted to hold a microphone and turn the silence of the unheard into sound, the way David had turned my childhood silence into questions, the way I had turned my family’s heavy quiet into noise. The courtroom argues over the past. The studio shapes the present and reaches for the future. I am, at my core, a forward-facing creature, a man who would rather narrate the country than litigate it, and journalism was the only profession that fit the actual shape of my soul instead of the impressive shape of my résumé.

This is the hardest choice a young person from a poor family ever makes, choosing the right life over the respectable one, because respectability is not vanity to a poor family, it is security, it is the difference between a son who can be bragged about and a son who has to be explained. To choose journalism over law was to ask the people who sold the goat to trust a path they could not see the end of, in an industry they did not understand, for reasons that sounded, in the telling, dangerously like feelings. I had to look at the people who built me and tell them that the gift they had sharpened for the courtroom belonged, actually, in front of a camera, and that they would have to believe me without proof, one more time, the way they had been believing me without proof my whole life.

They believed me. Of course they did. The library, the school David founded, the borehole, the discipline, the whole apparatus of love that had built me had been building, all along, a person capable of trusting his own voice over the world’s expectations, and the first big use of that capability was to choose the right loud over the safe loud. I chose journalism. I chose the studio over the courtroom, the story over the argument, the future over the past. Destiny did not call on a phone. It called the way David always called to me, with a question. What do you actually want to do with the voice. And for the first time, I answered out loud, to everyone, in my own name.

*Roylandz Rule #25: The respectable life and the right life are sometimes the same and sometimes opposites, and telling them apart is the first real adult decision you make. Choose the life shaped like your soul, not the one shaped like your résumé.*

# PART IV: THE MAKING OF ROYLANDZ

*(2020 to 2024)*

## Chapter 26: Escape to Thika, My First Real Freedom

When I left for college in Thika, I tasted freedom for the first time, and freedom, it turns out, is delicious and dangerous in exactly equal measure.

Thika is a town northeast of Nairobi, an industrial place with a famous highway and a famous road in a famous old book, and to me it was, above all, the first place where no one who had raised me could see what I was doing. No Gladys with the searchlight stare. No Murgusi bell. No clan, no village, no grandmother who could smell a lie like rain. For the first time in my life I was accountable to no one but myself, and a young man newly accountable to no one but himself is a chemistry experiment that can go several ways, and I am grateful I got to do mine without anyone hurt.

I enrolled to study journalism, the right loud, the choice I had fought for. And I discovered the strange double life of the broke college student in Kenya, the life where you are pursuing a future that feels enormous while surviving a present that is counted in single coins. There were days the budget was a serious mathematical problem, the kind where you decide between transport and lunch and choose to walk so you can eat, or eat so you can walk, depending on which class could not be missed. The financial childhood never fully leaves you, I told you that already, and in Thika it came back in adult clothes, the same arithmetic of scarcity I had learned at the borehole, now applied to data bundles and bus fare and the eternal question of whether there was enough.

But the freedom. The freedom was real and it was the soil my whole future grew in. Because freedom is not the absence of struggle, I had plenty of struggle. Freedom is the presence of choice, the ability to point yourself, to spend your scarce hours on the thing you actually believe in instead of the thing you were assigned. I pointed myself at media with the focus of a boy who had waited his whole life to be allowed to chase the right thing. I read everything. I watched everything. I studied the great broadcasters the way a young boxer studies tape, Jeff Koinange and his bench and his theatrical command of an interview, the international anchors, the comedians who were really just very disciplined storytellers, all of them, anyone who had figured out how to hold a room through a screen.

Will Smith wrote that the first step to a dream is the decision that you are willing to do whatever it takes, and that the willingness has to come before the way, because the way only reveals itself to people already moving. I did not have the way in Thika. I had no connections, no money, no platform, nothing but a diploma in progress and a voice trained on chickens. But I had the decision, fully made, no exit clause, and freedom gave me the room to act on it. So I started moving before I could see the road, which is the only way anyone has ever found a road, and the movement, not the map, is what eventually put me on a national screen.

*Roylandz Rule #26: Freedom is not the end of struggle. It is the beginning of choice. The day you can finally point your scarce hours at the right thing, point them hard, because that aim is the whole prize.*

## Chapter 27: When Roylandz Was Born

Roylandz was a nickname before it was a brand, a costume before it was a man, and I built him on purpose because the boy I was did not yet feel big enough to carry the dream the boy I was had.

Every public person has a version of this, the construction of the name you walk out under, the persona that is both you and a slightly braver, louder, more finished you. Roylandz started as something friends called me, the way nicknames start, and then I noticed something. When I was Eugine, I carried Eugine’s whole history, the broke village boy, the dung floor, the bucket of water, the every doubt anyone had ever planted. When I was Roylandz, I could carry only the dream. Roylandz had no childhood to apologize for. Roylandz was confident before I was, on screen before I deserved it, building before the foundation was finished. I put on the name like the entertainment captain putting on a title he had not yet grown into, and then I grew into it, because that is how titles work, you wear them ahead of yourself and let your character catch up.

There is a version of this that is fake and hollow, and the internet is full of it, young people performing a success they have not earned, all costume and no man underneath. I want to be clear that Roylandz was never that, because underneath the name was a person doing the unglamorous work, the studying, the failing, the showing up, the ninety-five percent invisible labor I had learned at Murgusi. The costume was real because the man was real. The name was a tool, a focusing device, a way of separating the dream from the doubt long enough to act on the dream. And it was, I realize now, exactly what Grandpa Samson did when he carved Kemoview into the earth, and exactly what David did when he named the schools and the organizations he founded. You give the thing a name, and the name makes it real, and the realness pulls the work out of you.

Achebe understood the weight of a name, that in many African traditions a name is a sentence, a prayer, a destiny spoken over a person at birth. Roylandz was a name I spoke over myself, a destiny I declared before I had any evidence for it, and the declaring was not arrogance, it was strategy, the same strategy as a boy reading to chickens like they were the nation. You behave as if the big thing is already true, not to deceive others but to instruct yourself, and slowly the instruction becomes the fact. I was Roylandz before Kenya had ever heard of Roylandz, alone in Thika with a borrowed dream and a self-given name, behaving like a brand until, one improbable day, I became one.

*Roylandz Rule #27: Give your dream a name and wear it before you have earned it. Not to fool the world. To instruct yourself. The name goes first, the work follows, and one day the man catches up to the costume.*

## Chapter 28: The Overview Show, Filming on Faith

The Overview Show was my first real production, and we filmed it on faith, by which I mean we had no money, no equipment worth the name, no permission from anyone, and a conviction that bordered on the spiritual that it would work anyway.

This is the chapter every self-made creator recognizes, the chapter of beginning with nothing. My partner in the early madness was Luckyboy Maingi, and the two of us decided to make a show before either of us had any business making a show. We had phones. We had a borrowed sense of how television worked, assembled from years of watching it hungrily. We had locations that cost nothing because we did not ask anyone whether we could use them. And we had the one resource that actually mattered, which is the willingness to look foolish in pursuit of a thing nobody had asked us to make.

You do not understand how much courage it takes to make bad content in public until you have done it. The first episodes were rough. Of course they were rough. The first anything is rough, the first floor I smeared was lopsided, the first letter I wrote Millicent was too long, the first time I made a room laugh I did it by accident. But most people will not release the rough first thing, because the rough first thing exposes you, it announces to the world that you are trying, and trying is the most vulnerable public act there is, more vulnerable than failing, because failing at least implies you stopped. We released the rough thing. We filmed on faith and posted on hope, and we got better in public, episode by episode, the way I had gotten better at the front of the room, by doing it in front of people before I was ready, because there is no other way to get ready.

The internet had, by then, changed the entire physics of my dream. When I was a boy reading to chickens, the path to a national audience ran through gatekeepers, through the established stations and their decisions about who deserved a microphone, and a poor boy from Lugari was not high on anyone’s list. By the time I was in Thika, the gatekeepers had been quietly overthrown. A phone and a connection meant a barefoot boy from Manyonyi could broadcast to the nation without anyone’s permission, could build an audience one person at a time, could become a station of one and dare the old stations to notice. This is the great democratization of our age, and most people my age in Kenya understood it as a way to be entertained. I understood it as a way to be heard, which is a different and more dangerous understanding, and it changed everything.

The Overview Show was not a hit in the way later things would be hits. It was something more important. It was proof of concept, proof that I could conceive a thing and birth it and improve it and that an audience, however small at first, would gather, the way the chickens gathered, the way the dorm gathered for the Saturday-night funk. The audience gathered, and I performed, and somewhere in the rough early episodes the man caught up a little more to the name, and Roylandz, filming on faith with Luckyboy and a phone, took his first real steps toward the red light that was waiting, patient as destiny, in a studio he had not yet seen.

*Roylandz Rule #28: Release the rough first thing. Trying is more vulnerable than failing, which is why most people never start. Get better in public. There is no private road to a public gift.*

## Chapter 29: TIBS Got Talent and the Campus King Era

At TIBS College, I stopped being a student who performed and became a force that organized, and the difference between those two things is the difference between an act and a leader.

TIBS Got Talent was the kind of campus event that, on paper, is a small thing, a college talent show, hardly the stuff of memoirs. But I was not content to enter it. I helped build it, run it, host it, shape it into something the campus actually cared about, and in doing so I rediscovered the lesson I had learned as Entertainment Captain at Murgusi and had been too young to fully bank, that leadership hides inside service, that the person who creates the stage for everyone else’s talent ends up more powerful than any single performer who stands on it. The performers got the applause for one night. I got the thing that lasts, which is the reputation of the one who makes things happen, the one the institution turns to, the one whose name becomes a kind of guarantee.

They called it, half joking and half not, the campus king era, and I want to be honest about how much I enjoyed it and how little it ultimately meant, both of which are true at once. I enjoyed it. After a childhood of being the poor loud boy, after the bucket of water, after the lonely Entertainment Captain labor, to be visibly, openly the most influential young person on a campus was a balm to an old wound, and I will not pretend otherwise to look humble. I drank it. But I also knew, even while drinking it, that campus fame is a small pond’s worth of celebrity, that the king of a college is still a college student, and that the applause of one institution is a training audience, not the destination. The chickens, the dorm, the campus, each a larger coop, none of them yet the nation.

The deeper work of those years was the steady accumulation of the skills that fame would later require and most famous people never bother to build. Hosting, which is harder than it looks, the management of energy and time and a live room. Producing, the invisible architecture. Networking, the human work of becoming known to the people who could open doors. Branding, the relentless tending of the Roylandz name. I was running, in effect, a small media company with myself as the only product, and I was learning by doing, failing, adjusting, in the same get-better-in-public method I had used since the dung floor.

Will Smith talks about how he wanted to be the biggest movie star in the world and he studied the patterns, broke down what the biggest films had in common, treated his ambition like a problem to be solved rather than a wish to be made. I did a smaller version of that on a Thika campus. I studied what made a campus event work, what made a host land, what made an audience return, and I treated my own rise as an engineering problem with a solution, not a lottery with a winner. The campus king era was my laboratory. I was not playing king. I was conducting experiments, on a small and safe population, in the science of holding an audience, and every result went into the notebook I have been keeping in my head since David first taught me to notice the world and write down what I noticed.

*Roylandz Rule #29: Enjoy the small pond, but never confuse it with the ocean. The king of a small pond is still a small fish in training. Drink the applause, then get back in the lab.*

## Chapter 30: Citizen TV, Baptism by Broadcast

My industrial attachment at Royal Media Services, at Citizen TV, was the first time I stood inside a real, national, professional newsroom, and it humbled me and confirmed me in the same breath.

Citizen TV is one of Kenya’s biggest stations, a giant, the place where the country gets its news, and to a young man from Lugari who had been building a one-man media operation out of phones and faith, walking into that building was like a parish singer being handed a seat in a cathedral choir. Everything was bigger. The equipment that cost more than my entire upbringing. The professionals who did, casually and correctly, things I had been straining to imitate with no resources. The pace, the standards, the unforgiving precision of an operation that puts polished broadcast in front of millions every single day without the luxury of getting better in public, because at that level the public expects you to already be good.

I came in as an attaché, the lowest creature in the newsroom ecosystem, the student, the one who fetches and observes and is grateful to be in the room. And I did the thing I have always done, which is to refuse to be merely small in a big place. I observed like David had taught me, attention as a skill, seeing as different from looking. I noticed how the professionals worked, the discipline beneath the apparent ease, the preparation behind the spontaneity, the way a great anchor reads scrolling text while seeming to invent it, the exact illusion I had first practiced for chickens. I made myself useful. I asked questions, the David way, questions that assumed I could understand the answers. I treated the attachment not as a box to tick on a diploma but as the most important short course of my life, taught by the best in the country, for free, and I was determined to extract every drop.

The humbling was necessary and good. On my campus I was the king. At Citizen I was nobody, and being nobody in a great institution is one of the most useful experiences a young person with rising confidence can have, because it recalibrates you, it shows you the actual ceiling of the room you thought you were tall in, it replaces the campus pond with the ocean and lets you feel how much water there is. I needed that. The campus king era had inflated me a little, in the healthy way and also in the dangerous way, and Citizen TV let the dangerous air out without bursting the healthy thing, which is a delicate operation that the best institutions perform on the young without even meaning to.

And the confirming. Because here is the other half of the truth. Standing in that great newsroom, watching the professionals, feeling small, I also felt, underneath the smallness, a thing I can only call recognition. This is the world I belong to. Not as a visitor. As a future citizen. The smallness was temporary, an attaché’s smallness, the smallness of the not-yet, and beneath it ran the same certainty that had let a boy read to chickens like they were Kenya, the certainty that I would be back in a building like this one, not fetching but anchoring, not observing but broadcasting, not nobody but a name. Citizen TV humbled me about the distance and confirmed me about the direction, and a young person who knows both how far they have to go and exactly which way to walk is the most dangerous kind of young person there is. I left the attachment more sure than I arrived, which is the opposite of what the smallness was supposed to do, and exactly what David’s library had built me to do with smallness.

*Roylandz Rule #30: Be nobody in a great place at least once. It will show you the real size of the ocean and, if you are built right, it will make you more sure of your direction, not less. Smallness is information, not a sentence.*

## Chapter 31: The Valedictorian Nobody Saw Coming

When they named me valedictorian, the broke loud boy who talked too much and studied unevenly, the lineage behind me exhaled all at once, and I stood up to give the speech as the dividend on a bet generations old.

I have already told you about the Avamaseero, the education-mad people who sold the goat for school fees and considered it the best trade they ever made. I have told you about David who founded a school, Gladys who built me with discipline, Samson whose quiet fees arrived at the exact right moment, and the whole apparatus of community love that had constructed me on purpose. The valedictorian stage was the moment all of that converged into a single visible point, a poor boy from Lugari standing before his graduating class, chosen to speak for all of them, the highest honor the institution had to give, handed to the kid who had arrived with nothing but a voice trained on poultry.

I wrote that speech the way I have written everything important, by reaching past the impressive version toward the true one. The easy speech would have been the triumphant one, the look-how-far-I-have-come, the inspirational confection that graduations love and that means nothing an hour after the gowns come off. I did not want to give that speech. I wanted to give the speech that the barefoot kid in the back, the one who was me a few years earlier, actually needed to hear, the speech about the road being long and the doubt being normal and the starting point not being the ceiling, the speech I am, in a sense, still giving, because this whole book is that valedictory address expanded to the length of a life.

Standing up there, I thought about the chickens. I genuinely did, in the half-second before I began, the way I had thought about the kerosene lamp before going live at PPP. I thought about the brown hen who listened longest, about the dung floor, about the front of the classroom at Mahemas where I first felt the size of a laugh, about every small ridiculous stage that had been secretly rehearsing me for stages like this one. And I understood, with the clarity that visits you at the great moments, that there had never really been a small stage. There had only ever been one stage, the same stage at every size, and a boy who had treated the chickens like the nation was now, finally, being asked to treat the nation like the chickens, to speak to a room full of important people with the same unguarded fullness he had once given to poultry in the lamplight.

I gave the speech. It landed. People cried, which is the highest compliment a speaker can receive, because tears are involuntary and therefore honest in a way applause is not. And afterward, in the way that the great moments dissolve back into ordinary life, I went back to being a young man with a diploma and a dream and a long road still ahead. But something had been confirmed publicly, witnessed, made real. The bet had paid. The goat had been worth selling. The lineage could rest a little. The valedictorian nobody saw coming had been seen, by everyone, all at once, and the seeing was a kind of permission to believe the rest of the dream might also, against all the odds the world had calculated against me, come true.

*Roylandz Rule #31: When you finally get the big stage, give the speech the kid in the back row needs, not the speech the front row expects. The honored speaker who remembers the back row never runs out of things worth saying.*

## Chapter 32: When COVID Took My Grandparents

On the twenty-fifth of March, 2021, Grandma Gladys died of COVID-19 in Eldoret, and seven days later, on the first of April, Grandpa David followed her, and a structure went out of the world that I have spent every day since trying to rebuild inside myself.

I have been dreading this chapter the way you dread a doorway you know you have to walk through, and I have put it near the end of this section the way the grief came, after the rising, after the valedictory, after the dream had started, so that the loss could land on a man who had something to lose and not just on a boy who had nothing yet. That is the cruelty of grief’s timing. It waits until you are tall enough to feel the full weight, and then it sets the weight down on you, all at once, and walks away.

They went together, almost. Married decades, two halves of one improbable life, the American Quaker and the Kenyan disciplinarian, the library and the law, and the virus took them within a single week of each other, in a hospital in Eldoret, far from the dung-floored simplicity where they had built their whole shared meaning. The world lost two peacemakers, two founders, two people who had given their lives to the radical conviction that enemies can become neighbors and that a child’s mind is full and waiting and worth feeding. The world lost the founder of the African Great Lakes Initiative and the founder of a school and the woman who held a village’s faith together. And I lost my grandparents, which is a smaller and more selfish way to say it, and the only way I can actually say it, because the public scale of their lives does not touch the private size of the hole.

I learned of it by phone, the way the modern world delivers its worst news, a call that turns an ordinary moment into the boundary between the before and the after. And I want to tell you the true thing about that grief, not the inspirational thing, because David and Gladys never traded in inspirational lies and I will not dishonor them by starting now. The true thing is that fame did not help. By then I was rising, I had the dream in motion, I had begun to be somebody, and none of it, not one watt of the attention, made the slightest difference to the fact that the man who handed me books and asked me questions was gone, and the woman who built me with a slipper and a searchlight stare was gone, and I would never again get to be the person who made them proud in person. The being-proud would now have to be done across a distance that has no road, kufa kuzikana, the bond that survives even death, the only thing left to carry.

Achebe wrote that there is no story that is not true, and that the world has no end, and that what is good among one people is an abomination with others. I cling to the second part. The world has no end. David believed that too, in his Quaker way, that the light does not go out, it changes form. So I have tried to believe that the library did not close when David died, it relocated, into me, into every child I will ever hand a book to, into this very chapter you are reading, which is one more room in the library he started and did not finish, because a library, if it is real, is never finished. Grief is the tax you pay on love, the highest tax there is, and I paid it in full in the spring of 2021, and I am still, in small installments, paying it now. But the school he founded in 1968 is still teaching, in its way, and the school he founded in my chest is still in session, and I am the teacher now, and I will not let the lights go off.

*Roylandz Rule #32: Grief is the tax on love, and the bill comes due exactly when you are finally tall enough to pay it. Pay it in full. Then keep the library open. That is the only inheritance that does not die when the people do.*

## Chapter 33: Uncle Tommy and Aunt Joy, the Angels Who Stayed

When heaven took David and Gladys, two people stayed, Uncle Tommy and Aunt Joy, and a child learns the difference between the family that creates you and the family that chooses to remain, and the staying is its own kind of love, quieter than the founding but no less real.

Tommy and Joy are David’s children, raised between America and Kenya, carrying their father’s Quaker conviction and their own American practicality, and they did the thing that holds families together across an ocean and a tragedy, which is that they did not disappear into their own grief. They stayed present. They stayed reachable. They carried forward the relationships that David and Gladys had built, refused to let the deaths sever the threads, became, in the gap the founders left, a kind of continuing presence, the angels who stayed when the others were taken.

I want to honor them precisely, because the world is full of books that praise the dead lavishly and forget the living entirely, and that is an injustice I refuse to commit. It is easy to write moving paragraphs about grandparents in the ground. It is harder, and more important, to say thank you to the people still standing, the ones who picked up the phone after the funeral, who remembered the boy David had poured himself into, who made sure the loud kid from Lugari knew he was still claimed even after his two great claimers were gone. Tommy and Joy did that. They are doing it. They are in this dedication and this chapter not as a courtesy but as a debt, the same kind of debt I owe David’s library and Samson’s borehole, the debt you owe anyone who decided you were worth staying for.

There is a theme that runs under my whole life, and you have surely seen it by now, the theme of being raised by people who did not have to. My mother fled a clan to raise me on her own terms. David, an American with every exit, chose a Kenyan village and a Kenyan grandchild. Samson gave clean help with no lecture. Gladys built me with borrowed discipline she could have spent on her own peace. And now Tommy and Joy, who could have let the connection lapse with the deaths, instead chose to stay. Over and over, my life has been saved and shaped by the chosen love, the love that is not obligated by blood and shows up anyway, which is, I have come to believe, the highest form of love there is, because it is the only kind that is entirely a gift and not at all a duty.

So this chapter is short, on purpose, because Tommy and Joy would be embarrassed by a long one, being the kind of people who do good quietly, the David kind, the Samson kind. I will only say this. When the founders were taken, you stayed. You did not have to. You did anyway. And a boy who has spent his whole life being chosen by people who did not have to choose him knows exactly what that staying is worth, which is everything, which is the whole reason he is still standing tall enough to write a book at all.

*Roylandz Rule #33: The family that creates you is a gift of blood. The family that stays after the funeral is a gift of choice. Honor the dead, but do not forget to thank the living while they can still hear you.*

# PART V: LIGHTS, MICS, AND MISTAKES

*(2024 to the present)*

## Chapter 34: PPP TV, the Day I Went National

The first time the red light at PPP TV caught and I knew that the whole country could see me, the boy who had read to chickens finally stood inside the dream he had been rehearsing for, and the dream, like all arrivals, was both exactly and nothing like what I had imagined.

PPP TV gave me what every aspiring broadcaster spends years chasing, a national platform, a real studio, a co-host in Lucy Ogunde, a chair under the lights, a place at the front of the room scaled up to the size of a nation. I came in as Head of Digital and as a presenter, which means I was doing the on-screen work and the behind-screen architecture at once, the act and the invisible ninety-five percent, the entertainment captain’s double life now playing out on a professional stage with professional stakes. I was building the station’s digital reach and sitting in front of its cameras, and both halves drew on everything I had ever learned, the chickens and the dorm and the campus and the Citizen attachment, all of it converging finally on the thing it had been preparing me for.

I want to describe the actual feeling of going national, because the inspirational books skip the feeling and go straight to the achievement, and the feeling is the truer story. It is terror and homecoming at the same time. Terror, because live national television is the highest-stakes version of the front of the room, no backspace, no second take, hundreds of thousands of judges with no obligation to be kind, and a single bad four seconds can become a clip that follows you. And homecoming, because under the terror is the deepest familiarity, the recognition that you have been doing this your whole life, that the studio is just the largest classroom at Mahemas, that Camera Two is just the brown hen who listened longest, that the nation is just the chickens scaled up and you already know, in your body, how to talk to chickens.

So I did the thing David’s library and Gladys’s discipline and the GSU officer’s one sentence and ten thousand hours of getting-better-in-public had built me to do. I steadied the terror with the homecoming. I let the familiarity win. I opened my mouth and the words came, not only from the prompter but from the floor, from the lamp, from everything, and the nation watched a confident young broadcaster who had absolutely no idea that the polish they were seeing had been sanded smooth by a childhood of poverty and a constellation of people who had refused to let that poverty have the last word.

Going national was the achievement of the dream and, in the way of all dreams achieved, the beginning of the next problem, because the dream is always quieter than you imagined and the cost is always louder, and the chapters after this one are about the cost. But I will not let the cost erase the triumph, because the triumph was real, and the boy deserves to have it stated plainly. He made it. The barefoot kid from Manyonyi who read to poultry by paraffin light made it to a national studio with his name on the screen, against every odd a statistician could have calculated, and if you are a barefoot kid reading this somewhere by candlelight or by the glow of a borrowed phone, I want you to hold onto that, because it is the most important true thing in this whole book. He made it. So can you. The coop just keeps getting bigger.

*Roylandz Rule #34: When you finally arrive at the dream, it will be both exactly and nothing like you imagined, and that is not a betrayal, it is just how arrival works. Steady the terror with the familiarity. You have been rehearsing for this your whole life.*

## Chapter 35: Urban News and the Death of Roylandz

To become a journalist, I had to let Roylandz die, or at least retire the costume, because the persona that built the man could not, in the end, be the man, and the hardest growth is outgrowing the thing that got you there.

Urban News was serious work, journalism aimed at young Kenyans, telling the stories of a generation, and serious journalism asks something of you that a personality brand does not. It asks you to subordinate yourself to the story. The Roylandz persona, the campus king, the confident self-built character, had been a tool for getting attention onto Eugine, a focusing device, a costume worn ahead of the man. But journalism is not about getting attention onto yourself. It is the opposite. It is about getting attention onto the people whose stories deserve telling, the unheard, the overlooked, the communities that are not trending and are therefore invisible to a media industry that measures importance by engagement. The journalist is a window, not a portrait. And a window has to be clean, has to disappear, has to let the light through rather than reflecting itself back.

So Roylandz, the loud self-promoting character, had to quiet down so the journalist could do his actual job, and this was a strange and unexpected grief, smaller than the grief of losing my grandparents but related to it, the grief of outgrowing a self that had served you faithfully. I had built Roylandz when Eugine did not feel big enough to carry the dream. Now the dream had carried me to a place where the costume was in the way, where the self-promotion that had launched me would, if I kept it, limit me, where I had to trust that Eugine was finally big enough to carry the work without the brave loud mask. Letting the costume go was an act of faith in the man underneath it, and the man underneath it had spent years quietly becoming worthy of that faith, in the dark, while the costume got the credit.

This is a pattern worth naming, because every person who builds something will face it. The thing that gets you to the first level is rarely the thing that gets you to the next one, and growth requires the courage to release a winning strategy precisely because it is winning, to outgrow the very approach that made you. The hustle that built the small business cannot run the big one. The self-promotion that launches the personality cannot sustain the journalist. The bravado that gets a poor boy out of the village cannot be the whole of the man he becomes. You have to let the early self die so the later self can be born, kufa kuzikana applied to the versions of your own self, each one burying the last and carrying it forward.

I did not kill Roylandz, exactly. I matured him. The name lives, the brand lives, Roylandz Media is real and building. But the costume came off the man, and the man, Eugine, the actual person with the dung-floor childhood and the chicken audience and the bucket of water and the grief, stepped forward to do the real work, and the work was journalism, the cleaning of the window, the disappearing-into-service that the campus king could never have managed. Urban News was where the persona handed the microphone to the person, and the person, it turned out, had something to say that the persona never could, because the person had actually lived the silence the journalism was trying to break.

*Roylandz Rule #35: The strategy that gets you to the first level will usually block the road to the next one. Have the courage to retire a winning costume. The man underneath has been getting ready the whole time.*

## Chapter 36: Seven Hundred Thousand Subscribers and Twenty Million Views

The numbers got large in a way that should have felt like victory and mostly felt like a magnifying glass, and I learned that scale does not change who you are, it only makes who you are louder.

Seven hundred thousand subscribers. Twenty million monthly views across the shows. These are real numbers, the kind that, told to the boy reading to six chickens, would have sounded like a fairy tale, six chickens multiplied by a number too large for a child to picture. And I want to be honest about what large numbers actually feel like from the inside, because there is a fantasy about this, a belief that crossing some threshold of audience converts you into a different and happier species of person, and the belief is a lie, and the lie hurts people, and I will not pass it on.

Scale is a magnifying glass, not a mirror. It makes everything about you bigger, your reach, your influence, your income, your mistakes, your insecurities, your loneliness, all of it amplified by the size of the audience. The confident parts of me got louder, yes. But so did the wounded parts, the broke-child arithmetic that never fully leaves, the entertainment captain’s loneliness, the boy who learned to perform happiness over a light supper. Twenty million views did not heal any of that. Twenty million views took the unhealed boy and put him on twenty million screens, which is not the same as healing him, which is, if you are not careful, the opposite, a way of getting so much external validation that you never do the internal work, because why fix the foundation when the building is so admired.

There is a particular danger for people who get large audiences young, and I was inside it before I could name it. The danger is that the audience becomes your mirror, that you start to know yourself only through their reaction, that the metrics become your self-worth, that a dip in views feels like a dip in your value as a human being, that you outsource your sense of self to strangers who owe you nothing and will leave the moment you bore them. I felt this pull, the metrics-as-mirror pull, the way a swimmer feels a current. The numbers were a drug and I had the dosage of someone who had been starved of validation his whole childhood and was now being handed it by the truckload, and a starved person handed unlimited food does not become healthy, he becomes a different kind of sick.

The wisdom, when it finally arrived, and it arrived slowly and is still arriving, was the wisdom of the metrics-as-fuel and not metrics-as-verdict, the exact lesson KCSE had tried to teach me at a smaller scale. The numbers are real. Use them. They are a measure of reach, and reach is the whole point if you want to break silences and tell unheard stories. But the day you let the numbers become the judge of your worth instead of the measure of your reach, you have handed seven hundred thousand strangers the gavel over your soul, and a crowd is a terrible judge, fickle and cruel and gone by morning. I am still learning to hold the numbers loosely. Some days I do it well. Some days the magnifying glass wins and I have to remind myself that the boy with the chickens did not need a single view to know his voice mattered, and neither, on the good days, do I.

*Roylandz Rule #36: A large audience is a magnifying glass, not a mirror. It makes everything you are louder, the wounds along with the gifts. Do the internal work the validation lets you avoid, or the bigness will just make you a bigger version of broken.*

## Chapter 37: The Fame That Did Not Fix Me

I am going to say the thing famous people are not supposed to say, the thing that sounds ungrateful and is actually the most useful gift I can hand you. Fame did not fix me. It was never going to. And believing it would was the most expensive mistake I have made.

There is a story almost everyone from a hard beginning tells themselves, a story so common it is practically the water we swim in, the story that says when I make it, when I am known, when I have arrived, then the ache will stop. The ache of the broke childhood, the ache of the silent house, the ache of being the poor loud boy who was laughed at as often as with, the whole accumulated ache of a beginning the world had calculated against you. The story says fame is the cure. Achieve enough, be seen enough, and the wound closes. I believed this story completely, without ever examining it, the way you believe the ground will be there when you step, and I built a great deal of striving on top of it.

And then I achieved a version of the thing, the national screen, the large numbers, the recognition on the street, the dream the boy had rehearsed for, and I waited for the ache to stop. And it did not stop. The wound did not close. The broke child still did his arithmetic. The silent house still echoed. The boy who performed happiness over a light supper was still performing, just on a larger stage, to a larger crowd, with better lighting. Fame had changed my circumstances completely and changed my interior almost not at all, because the interior is not built from circumstances, the interior is built from meaning, and no quantity of external achievement assembles meaning on your behalf. You have to build it yourself, by hand, in the dark, the way Gladys built me, the way David built his schools, slowly, without applause, and fame is loud and fast and applauded and therefore exactly the wrong tool for the job.

This is the great hidden tragedy of the people you envy, and I say it not to complain but to free you, because if you are striving toward some achievement believing it will fix the unfixed thing inside you, I am here, from the inside of the achievement, to tell you it will not, and to save you the years I lost discovering it. The unfixed thing requires fixing, directly, the way a roof requires repair, and the fixing has nothing to do with achievement. It has to do with the unglamorous interior work that no audience ever sees and no metric ever measures, the work I will talk about in the next chapters, the work of actually tending the wound instead of merely covering it with applause.

Will Smith, who achieved about as much external success as a human being can, wrote with painful honesty about discovering that the success had not made him happy, that he had built his whole self around the approval of others and woken up, at the summit, still afraid. I read that and felt the relief of recognition, the relief of finding out you are not uniquely broken, that even the largest success in the world arrives at the same locked door, the door that no achievement can open because the lock is on the inside. Fame did not fix me. It was honest enough, eventually, to show me clearly that it could not, and that clarity, painful as it was, was the beginning of the only fixing that works, the fixing you do yourself, after the lights go off, when no one is watching and the wound is finally telling the truth.

*Roylandz Rule #37: No achievement on the outside will repair a wound on the inside. The lock is on the inside. Fame just turns the lights on bright enough for you to finally see the door you have been ignoring.*

## Chapter 38: Burnout in Designer Sneakers

I burned out wearing shoes that cost more than my first year of school fees, and the contradiction in that sentence is the whole chapter, the exhaustion of a poor boy who finally got the nice things and discovered the nice things do not rest you.

Burnout is a strange illness for someone who grew up broke, because it carries a guilt that the comfortable do not feel. How dare you be tired, the broke-child voice says, you have everything you prayed for, you have the screen and the numbers and the shoes, your grandmother sold metaphorical goats for this exhaustion, sit up straight and be grateful. So you do not rest, because resting feels like ingratitude, like spitting on the goat, like betraying the lineage that bet everything on your head. You push through, because pushing through is the only mode you were ever taught, the borehole-jerrican mode, the get-better-in-public mode, the never-stop mode that got you out and that you do not know how to turn off now that you are out.

And the body keeps the score, as the saying goes, and eventually it sends the invoice. Mine came as the particular flatness that burnout produces, the joy draining out of the work that used to be the dream, the camera that used to thrill becoming just a hot light to perform under, the audience that used to feed me becoming a demand I had to keep meeting. I was laughing on camera and empty off it, the entertainment captain’s loneliness returned at national scale, responsible for everyone’s joy and quietly out of my own. I had the designer sneakers and the dead feeling, the achievement and the ash, and the contradiction was so total that for a while I could not even name it, because the broke-child voice would not allow the sentence I am suffering inside the dream to be spoken.

The thing nobody tells the striver is that the same engine that gets you out can, if you never learn to throttle it, run you into the ground. The relentless drive, the never-enough, the push-through, the conversion of every ache into more striving, all of it is gloriously useful for escaping a hard beginning and quietly lethal once you have escaped, because it does not have an off switch and the situation that required it has ended but the engine does not know that and keeps redlining toward a finish line that no longer exists. I had escaped. The engine kept running as if I had not. And it burned through the fuel that was supposed to last me a life.

Learning to rest was harder than learning to hustle, by an enormous margin, because hustle was native to me and rest was a foreign language I had to learn as an adult with a heavy accent. I am still learning it. I am bad at it. But I have learned enough to tell you the truth I wish someone had told me, which is that rest is not the reward for the work, it is part of the work, it is maintenance, it is the dung-day economics of the soul, the unglamorous regular tending without which the whole structure cracks. My grandmother maintained the floor every Friday whether or not it looked dirty, because she understood that you maintain before the damage, not after. I learned to maintain my floor after the damage, the hard way, in designer sneakers, burned out inside the dream, and I am passing the lesson to you so you can learn it the cheaper way, the Friday way, before the cracks.

*Roylandz Rule #38: The engine that gets you out of a hard beginning has no off switch, and if you never learn to throttle it, it will run you into the ground inside the very dream you built. Rest is not the reward for the work. It is part of the work.*

## Chapter 39: Faith in HD, Prayers Between Takes

I found my way back to God in the strangest cathedral, the gap between takes in a national television studio, and the faith that returned was not the faith I was handed as a child but a quieter, hardier thing I had to grow myself.

I was raised in faith, you know this, the Quaker inner light on one side and the Pentecostal heat on the other and Gladys praying like breathing and the Adventist Sabbaths at Murgusi, the whole rich religious soil of western Kenya. But there is a difference between inherited faith and owned faith, between the religion you are given and the relationship you build, and the inherited faith, like the Roylandz persona, had to be outgrown so the owned faith could be born. Through the rising and the grief and the burnout, the childhood faith had thinned to a habit, the prayers gone automatic, the meaning leaked out, and I had become, without quite deciding to, a man who said the words my grandmother taught me without feeling the thing she meant by them, which is exactly the lie she warned me never to tell.

And then, in the unlikeliest place, it came back. Not in a church. In a studio. In the small dead minutes between segments, when the cameras cut and the crew reset and there is a strange suspended hush in a place built for noise, I started, almost involuntarily, to pray. Real prayers, not the automatic ones. Help me tell this true. Steady my hands. Keep me honest under these lights. Let the window be clean. Thank you that the boy who read to chickens is here at all. Small prayers, between takes, in the gap, and they were the most genuine I had prayed in years, because they were not performed and not inherited and not automatic, they were the actual reaching of an actual soul that had been through enough to know it could not do the next take alone.

This is faith in HD, I came to call it privately, faith in high definition, faith with the resolution turned up by suffering and success and loss, faith you can finally see clearly because life has finally focused it. The childhood faith was beautiful but soft, the faith of a boy who had not yet been tested, inherited whole from people who loved him. The studio faith was harder and clearer, the faith of a man who had buried his grandparents and burned out in the dream and learned the hard way that no achievement opens the inner lock, and who had concluded, from all of it, that he was not the largest thing in his own story, that there was a frequency above the metrics, the God frequency Gladys had tuned the whole house to, and that it had been there the whole time, patient as destiny, waiting in the gap between takes for him to stop performing long enough to hear it.

David, the Quaker, believed the light does not go out, it changes form. Gladys believed God was always the audience and never blinked. Between takes, under the lights that cost more per hour than my childhood, I started to believe them again, not as inheritance but as discovery, the way you rediscover your hometown after you have seen the world, the same place, completely different, because you are completely different. The faith that returned was not a retreat from the world but a way of standing in it, a clean window for a higher light, the journalist’s disappearing-into-service revealed as a kind of prayer, the breaking of silence revealed as a kind of worship. I pray between takes now, on purpose, every time. It is the truest thing I do all day, and the audience never sees it, which is exactly why it works.

*Roylandz Rule #39: Inherited faith has to be outgrown so owned faith can be born. You will find the real thing in the strangest cathedral, probably in the gap between your performances, in the hush you have been too loud to hear. Listen there. It has been waiting.*

# PART VI: FULL CIRCLE

## Chapter 40: Back to Lumakanda, the Dust Still Knew My Name

I went back to Lumakanda on an ordinary Saturday, in a car I owned, wearing the quiet confidence of a man the country now recognized, and the dust did not care even slightly, which was the first gift the place gave me and the one I needed most.

There is a particular fantasy that lives in the chest of everyone who leaves a hard place for a soft success, the fantasy of the return, the triumphant homecoming where the boy who was laughed at drives back transformed and the village gathers and the past kneels. I had rehearsed that fantasy more times than I will admit, in the lean years especially, when the picture of going back vindicated kept me upright on nights the arithmetic did not work. So when I finally went back, genuinely successful, genuinely known, I expected the fantasy to cash itself out, expected the place to perform its part in the script I had written for it across all those hungry years.

The place had not read the script. The red dust rose exactly as it always had, indifferent to my numbers, and settled on my owned car with the same democratic thoroughness it had once settled on my bare feet. The chickens, descendants of the chickens I had read to, regarded me with the identical blank suspicion. The air smelled of woodsmoke and rain coming and growing things, the smell that is underneath every other smell in my whole life, and it did not smell like victory, it smelled like home, which is a different and better thing that I had been too busy striving to remember.

And standing there, recognized by the dust and unimpressed by my résumé, I felt the strangest peace come over me, the peace of being known by something older than my achievements. Nairobi knew me as a presenter. The numbers knew me as a metric. The street knew me as a face from the screen. But the dust of Lumakanda knew me as the boy, the actual boy, before any of it, the one who collected dung and read to chickens and performed happiness over a thin supper, and being known that completely, that far underneath the persona, was a homecoming the fantasy had never imagined, because the fantasy was about being seen as the new thing and the reality was about being remembered as the true thing.

I walked the paths I had walked barefoot. I stood where the home library had been, David’s books, the room that built me. I greeted the old people who remembered Beverlyne’s loud boy and did not particularly care that he was on television, because to them he was simply Kangazi’s grandson, located precisely in the lineage, the Avamaseero thread and the Abatongoi clan meeting in one person standing in the familiar dust. They placed me where I belonged, in the long chain of people who had walked this ground, and the placement was the opposite of the fantasy and so much better than the fantasy, because the fantasy made me the climax of the story and the dust reminded me I was just the talking part of a much longer story, the part that gets the microphone, holding it on behalf of everyone who never did.

The triumphant return I had rehearsed never happened, and I am glad, because what happened instead was truer. I did not conquer the place that built me. I was re-absorbed by it, gently, the way a river that has run all the way to the loud ocean is, by evaporation and weather and time, eventually carried back as quiet rain to the highland where it started. The dust still knew my name. It had been saying it the whole time I was gone, patiently, the way home does, waiting for me to get quiet enough, and successful enough to stop needing the noise, to finally hear it.

*Roylandz Rule #40: The triumphant homecoming you rehearse will not happen, and the real one is better. The place that built you will not be impressed by your résumé. It will do something far kinder. It will remember who you actually are, underneath all of it, and call you by that name.*

## Chapter 41: Dear Younger Me, Sorry I Doubted You

I want to write a letter now, across the years, to the boy collecting dung in the cold Lugari morning, and the first thing I owe him is an apology, because for most of my life I was ashamed of him, and I was wrong.

Dear younger me. Dear loud broke boy with the dust on your feet and the performance ready in your throat. I have to start with sorry, because for years I treated you as the thing I had escaped, the embarrassment I had outrun, the before in a before-and-after story I was desperate to finish. When people asked where I came from, I shaved the edges off you, made you a tidy inspirational anecdote instead of the actual hungry frightened brilliant child you were. I am sorry. You deserved better from the man you became than to be edited into a motivational quote. You deserved to be honored as you were, which is what I am finally, late, trying to do with this whole book.

I owe you some news, because you spent so much of your childhood frightened about things I can now answer. The fear that you would never get out. You got out. The fear that the loudness was a defect. The loudness was the gift, the entire gift, the thing the GSU officer saw and named, the thing that became a career and a calling. The fear that the poverty meant something was wrong with you.
