Nothing was wrong with you. The poverty was a circumstance, never a verdict, and the shame you carried about it was a tax you paid on a debt you never actually owed. You can put that fear down now. I am writing from the other side of all of it to tell you the other side exists.

But I also owe you some harder truth, because a letter that only comforts would be a lie, and you always could smell a lie. Getting out did not fix everything. The wound you think success will close does not close on schedule, and I spent years discovering that, years I could have spent healing if someone had told me sooner, which is why I am telling you now. The fame you are dreaming about, the screen, the recognition, it comes, and it is wonderful, and it does not do the one thing you most need it to do, which is to make you finally feel like enough. That feeling you will have to build yourself, by hand, the way Gladys built the floor, and the building has nothing to do with achievement. Start it early. Do not wait for the summit to discover the summit was never the cure. I waited. Learn from my waiting.

And I owe you gratitude, the deepest, because everything I am was assembled by you in conditions that should have produced a smaller person. You read to chickens so you would have a voice ready when the cameras came, though you could not have known that was why. You performed happiness over thin food and learned the entertainer’s craft in the only studio available. You watched and listened in the silence and became, without a single lesson, a journalist, an observer, a teller. You did the work, all of it, in the cold and the dust and the lack, and you did it without applause, for years, for an audience of poultry and a grandmother and a God you trusted on a frequency you could not see. Every good thing I have, you built. I just got the credit and the lighting.

So this is the letter, younger me. Sorry I doubted you. Sorry I was ashamed of you. Sorry it took me a screen and some numbers and two funerals and a burnout in expensive shoes to understand that you were never the embarrassment, you were the architect, and the man writing this is just the building you imagined, finally standing, still settling onto the foundation you poured in the dark with your small cold hands. Thank you. I see you now. I am sorry it took so long. I will spend the rest of the lights making it up to you, by telling the truth, loudly, the way you always knew it should be told.

*Roylandz Rule #41: The child you were is not the embarrassment you escaped. That child is the architect of everything you became. Go back, in writing if you have to, and apologize for the doubt, and then say thank you. You owe that kid a debt no success can settle, only honor.*

## Chapter 42: The Digital Kemoview

Grandpa Samson Kemoli built a place and named it Kemoview, a piece of ground that announced its own belonging, and it took me until adulthood to understand that I had been trying to build the digital version of it my whole career without knowing the blueprint had a name.

Kemoview was not just a homestead. It was a statement that a family which had been told it owned nothing could plant a flag on a hill and say, this is ours, this is named, this exists, and you cannot tell us otherwise. Samson gave help without lectures, dug boreholes, paid fees, built and named a place, and the whole posture of the man was the posture of someone constructing belonging out of red soil for people the world had tried to keep landless and nameless. I did not understand it as a child. I understood it the day I realized that everything I had built online, the channel, the shows, the seven hundred thousand people who chose to gather, was the same act in a new medium. I had been building a Kemoview made of pixels, a named place on a hill where people who had been told they were nobody could come and belong.

This is the thing I finally understood about my own work, late, after the burnout cleared enough for me to see it. I had thought I was building a career. I was actually building a place. Urban News, Campus Xposure, the whole digital architecture of Roylandz before it and PPP after it, all of it was a hill I was planting a flag on, ground I was clearing and naming so that the loud broke kids from the hard places would have somewhere to gather and see themselves reflected with dignity instead of pity. The boy who read to chickens because he had no other audience had grown into a man building an audience so that no kid would have to read to chickens alone again. I was making the thing I had needed and never had, which is, I have come to believe, the only honest reason anyone builds anything that lasts.

A digital Kemoview has the same rules as the original. You build it on ground others said was worthless, the despised medium, the platforms the serious people sniffed at, the same way Samson built on soil a family was told it could not own. You name it before it is impressive, the way David named his school and Samson named his hill and I named Roylandz when it was a borrowed camera and a dream. You give without lecturing, clean help, the encouragement dropped like seed, the platform handed to the next loud kid without a sermon attached. And you understand, all the way down, that the point of having a place is to make it a place for others, that belonging hoarded is just real estate but belonging shared is a Kemoview, a hill with your family’s name on it where the landless come to remember they were never actually nobody.

So that is what I am building, I can finally say it plainly. Not a brand. Not a following. A digital Kemoview, a named hill in the loud new country of the screen, planted on ground the gatekeepers undervalued, open to every kid doing dung-day economics in some cold morning somewhere who needs to see that the loud broke ones get out, get up, get a microphone, and then, if they remember where they came from, turn around and build a place for the ones still in the dust. Samson dug boreholes so people could drink. I am digging the digital kind, so people can see themselves, and belong, and know that somebody from their exact beginning made it all the way to the hill and left the gate open on purpose.

*Roylandz Rule #42: Do not build a career. Build a place. A named hill on ground others called worthless, where the people who were told they were nobody can gather and remember they were always somebody. The point of having a place is to make it a place for others. That is the only kind of success that does not rot.*

## Chapter 43: Noise With Purpose

I have spent this whole book telling you that loud was my gift, and now I have to tell you the dangerous part, the part that took me decades to learn, which is that loud is only a gift when it is aimed, and aimless loud is just noise that wears a gift’s clothes.

For most of my life loud was simply who I was, an unaimed loud, a loud that filled rooms because filling rooms was the only way the broke kid knew to matter. I performed happiness loudly, debated loudly, hosted loudly, built loudly, and the loudness worked, it got me seen, it got me out, it got me up. But somewhere in the rising I began to sense the difference between the loud that serves something and the loud that only serves itself, between noise with purpose and noise that is just frightened of silence, and the sensing was uncomfortable, because a great deal of my loud, I had to admit, was the second kind, the boy still shouting so the cold morning would not swallow him.

Here is the distinction, hard-won. Noise without purpose is what the frightened do to take up space, to drown the silence they cannot sit in, to be seen because being unseen feels like dying. It is the loudest man in the room from Rule Seven, the one whose volume is fear in costume. I was that man more often than I would like, loud out of the old terror, loud because the broke kid believed that the moment he got quiet the world would forget he existed and the dust would close back over him. That loud is exhausting and hollow and it was a large part of my burnout, because performing volume to keep terror at bay is a job with no end and no pay.

Noise with purpose is a completely different thing, though it can look identical from outside. Noise with purpose is the breaking of a dangerous silence, the giving of voice to the people the silence was crushing, the journalist disappearing into service so a true thing can be heard. It is David’s Peace Teams walking into the post-election fire, loud with reconciliation in a country gone quiet with fear. It is Gladys praying out loud so a frightened boy would know the frequency was always on. It is the GSU officer naming a gift out loud so a teenager would carry the sentence for life. Purposeful noise is not about filling space, it is about clearing it, making room where the silence had taken all the air, and the test of which kind of loud you are running is simply this: does your noise serve you, or does it serve the people who needed someone to finally speak?

I have tried, in the back half of my career and the back half of this book, to convert my loud from the first kind to the second, to take the gift that was real and aim it at something beyond my own survival, because survival is no longer the assignment. I survived. The loud that got me out has to become the loud that reaches back, the noise that breaks silence for the next kid instead of just filling the room for me. That is the whole graduation of a gift, from defense to offering, from the boy shouting so he will not be forgotten to the man speaking so others will not be unheard. The volume can stay the same. The aim is everything. Loud, aimed at your own fear, is noise. Loud, aimed at someone else’s silence, is a calling. I spent half my life confusing the two. I am spending the rest making sure my noise has somewhere worth going.

*Roylandz Rule #43: Loud is only a gift when it is aimed. Noise that fills the room to quiet your own fear is just terror in a costume. Noise that breaks a silence so the unheard can be heard is a calling. The volume can stay the same. The aim is the whole difference between a nuisance and a gift.*

## Chapter 44: Born Broke, Built Loud

So here we are, at the title, at the end, and I owe you the meaning of the four words I have been circling for this entire book, the four words that are my whole life compressed to a size you can carry.

Born broke. That part was assigned to me, written into the first line by a hospital named after a dead country, on a Christmas Eve, in a province that was poor in a country that was struggling, into a family rich in everything that does not spend. I did not choose born broke. None of us choose the first line. It was handed to me complete, the dust and the lack and the cold mornings and the thin suppers and the long arithmetic of need, and for years I believed that first line was also the verdict, the sentence, the whole story compressed into the conditions of its beginning. Born broke felt like a destination I had been shipped to, freight, no return address.

Built loud. That part was the answer, and the answer was not handed to me, the answer I had to construct, out of the only material the beginning provided, which was my voice. Built is the crucial word, the word that took me a lifetime to earn the right to use, because built means it did not happen to me, built means I and the people who loved me made it, deliberately, in the dark, without applause, the way Gladys made the floor and David made the schools and Samson made the hill. And loud is the how, the medium, the gift, the thing the broke beginning could not repossess because it lived in my throat where poverty could not reach. I was born into the silence of lack and I built, out of that very silence, a loud that broke it. Born broke, built loud. The first half is circumstance. The second half is choice. The comma between them is a whole life.

And the deepest thing I know now, the thing this entire book has been trying to say in forty-four different ways, is that the broke and the loud were never opposites, never a before and an after, never a wound and its healing. They were always the same thing seen from two ends. The brokenness was the silence, and the silence is what taught me sound. The lack was the dust, and the dust is what knew my name. The poverty was the pressure, and the pressure is what built the loud. You cannot have my loud without my broke. They are one object. The boy who read to chickens because he had no audience is the man who built an audience of seven hundred thousand, and he is not two people, he is one person, and the chickens were not the embarrassment before the success, they were the first verse of the success, the loud already practicing in the only theater the broke could afford.

So if you are reading this from your own version of broke, your own cold morning and thin supper and first line that feels like a verdict, hear me, because I came all the way from there to tell you this one thing. Your beginning is not your verdict. It is your verse, your raw material, your first line, and the rest of the lines are yours to build. You were maybe born broke. A great many of the people worth knowing were. But broke is the circumstance, never the conclusion, and somewhere in you there is a loud, a gift, a voice, a thing the lack cannot repossess because it does not live in your pockets, it lives in your throat and your hands and your stubborn refusal to accept the first line as the last. Find it. Aim it. Build with it. Build loud, out of the very silence they handed you, until the silence breaks and the dust learns your name and the kid you were finally gets to hear the man you became say the truest sentence either of you will ever speak.

I was born broke. I built loud. And the building, thank God, is not finished, because the lights are still on, and the next take is coming, and somewhere a loud broke kid is reading to chickens in a cold morning, getting a voice ready, not yet knowing that the dust already knows his name.

*Roylandz Rule #44: You were maybe born broke. That was the first line, and you did not write it. But broke is the circumstance and loud is the choice, and the comma between them is your whole life. Find the gift the lack cannot repossess. Aim it. Build with it. Build loud, out of the silence they handed you, until it breaks.*

# Epilogue: When the Lights Go Off

The lights in a television studio do not switch off gently. They cut, all at once, and for a second the eyes keep the ghost of them, bright shapes floating in the sudden dark, the way a sound keeps ringing after the bell is struck. I have stood in that cut a thousand times now, the abrupt dark after the broadcast, and it is in that exact second, every time, that I am returned to the kerosene lamp of my childhood, the small flame in the Lugari dark, the original light before all the manufactured ones.

When the lights go off, the persona goes with them. Roylandz, the presenter, the face, the brand, all of it lives in the light and dies in the cut, and what is left standing in the dark is just the boy, the actual one, Beverlyne’s loud son, David’s library kid, Gladys’s project, Samson’s lineage, the child who read to chickens, breathing in the dark exactly as he breathed in the dark of a home with no electricity twenty years ago. The lights gave me a career. The dark gives me back myself. And I have learned, slowly, to be grateful for both, because a life that is only lights is a performance with no one home, and a life that is only dark is a gift no one ever gets to receive.

I think about David in that dark, who believed the light does not go out but changes form, who is gone now and yet not gone, his form changed into every school he built and every book he handed me and every word of this you have read. I think about Gladys, who believed God was always the audience and never blinked, watching still, I have to believe, from the only seat that was ever full the whole time I performed for emptier ones. I think about the grandparents and the lineage and the long chain of silent people who walked the red dust so that one of them could eventually hold a microphone, and I understand that when my own lights finally cut, for the last time, the form will change again, and something I built in the dark, some school of the spirit, some named hill, some loud broke kid I encouraged without knowing, will keep the light going in a shape I will not be around to see.

That is the whole hope, in the end. Not that the lights stay on, because they do not, for anyone. But that you used them, while you had them, to build something that does not need them. That you converted the bright manufactured fame into something durable and dark-proof, a Kemoview, a library, a gift passed down, a kid who heard you say his beginning was not his verdict and believed you enough to build his own loud. The lights go off. They always go off. But if you built right, in the dark, with your cold small hands, then the going-off is not an ending. It is just the cut between takes, and somewhere the next one is already starting, and the dust, patient as ever, is already learning a new name.

When the lights go off, I am not afraid anymore. I was, for most of my life, terrified of the dark, of being unseen, of the silence closing back over the boy. But I have made my peace with the cut, because I finally understand what David meant. The light does not go out. It changes form. And the form it changed into, this time, is the book in your hands, still glowing a little, I hope, in whatever light you are reading by, carrying a frequency lit in a Lugari dark a long time ago and never, not once, actually extinguished.

Goodnight. Keep your library open. The lights are coming back on in the morning, and the dust still knows your name.

# Acknowledgments

A book about being built by other people cannot honestly pretend it was written alone, so let me name the builders, knowing the list will be incomplete the way all such lists are, and trusting the unnamed to know that the gratitude reaches them anyway.

To my mother, Beverlyne Kangazi Kemoli, who fought a war I only understood as an adult, and won it quietly, in cold mornings, with no medal but this one: every word here is partly yours.

To my father, Joab Philemona Etale Obbayi, and to my brother Danson, who walked the early dust beside me.

To David Zarembka, who handed me books and believed I could read them, who built schools because he loved them and built me because he loved me, and who changed form on the first of April, 2021, into everything he ever made. To Gladys Kamonya, whose love wore work clothes and built my foundation rule by rule, gone seven days before him, both taken by a virus and neither, I insist, actually gone. To Samson Kemoli, who built a hill and named it and gave clean help with no lecture attached, and to Josephine Kemoli, who prayed.

To Uncle Tommy and Aunt Joy, the angels who stayed after the funerals, the family of choice that proved blood is not the only bond that holds.

To Luckyboy Maingi, who filmed The Overview Show on faith with me when we had more belief than equipment. To the team at Royal Media Services and Citizen TV who let a green attaché learn the craft by doing it badly in public. To everyone at TIBS College in Thika, who turned a loud kid into a valedictorian nobody saw coming. To Lucy Ogunde and the whole team at PPP TV, who share the lights with me and make the work a joy.

To the GSU officer whose name I never got, who told a teenage boy he had a gift and never knew he handed him a whole life. Wherever you are, sir, it worked.

To Pacifica and to Millicent, who taught a poor loud boy that his heart worked, each in your own way, with water and with a first kiss and with the two words that still live in me. Thank you for the proof.

To the seven hundred thousand and counting, the loud broke kids and everyone else who chose to gather on the digital hill. You are the Kemoview. You are why.

And to the chickens of Lumakanda, my first and most loyal audience, who showed up, which is more than most. This was always, in the end, for you.

# Roylandz Rules: The Complete Collection

Every chapter in this book ended with a rule, hard-won, paid for in dust and lights and grief and joy. Here they are, gathered in one place, the whole curriculum of a boy who was born broke and built loud, for you to carry, argue with, ignore, or live by.

**Rule #1:** Where you are born does not get a vote in who you become. It only gets to write the first line. Make sure it is a good one.

**Rule #2:** A poor childhood is not a sad story. It is a loud one. Learn the difference and you will never be a victim of your own beginning.

**Rule #3:** Some children inherit money. I inherited timing. One of these things you cannot lose in a bad year.

**Rule #4:** The world will try to shame you for the honest work that kept you alive. Do not accept the loan. The interest on borrowed shame will bankrupt you.

**Rule #5:** Do not wait for a worthy audience to find your voice worth using. Start with chickens. They show up, which is more than most people do.

**Rule #6:** Discipline is love wearing work clothes. You will not recognize it until much later, when it is the only thing still holding your roof up.

**Rule #7:** The loudest man in the room is usually the most frightened. Real power is the quiet one walking toward the fire with both hands open.

**Rule #8:** Silence is not peace. Sometimes silence is just fear that learned good manners. Learn to tell which kind you are sitting in, then break the dangerous kind.

**Rule #9:** Your first stage will be small and unimpressive and probably free. Stand on it like it is national television. The habit is the whole point.

**Rule #10:** You are not the beginning of your story. You are the part that gets to talk. Honor the silent generations who bought you the microphone.

**Rule #11:** One person who hands you a book and believes you can read it is worth a thousand who tell you to be realistic. Find that person. Then become that person for somebody else. That is how the debt gets paid.

**Rule #12:** If your grandmother’s love felt like rules, you were not unloved. You were being built. Go back and read the architecture. It will move you.

**Rule #13:** A system that was not built for you can still be worked by you. Find the crack where your gift fits, and walk through it before anyone tells you it was not a door.

**Rule #14:** Some families pass down land. Mine passed down the conviction that no one can repossess what you know. It is the only inheritance that survives a bad harvest.

**Rule #15:** The best help arrives without a lecture attached. If you are ever in a position to give, give clean. The interest you do not charge is the interest that compounds in someone’s whole life.

**Rule #16:** The first time you see how big the gap is, you get to choose. It is either a sentence or a distance. One you serve. The other you cross. Choose the verb that keeps you moving.

**Rule #17:** When you go back home after seeing the world, do not be ashamed of the smallness. Be assigned by it. The place that built you is not your past. It is your purpose, wearing work clothes again.

**Rule #18:** A hard place will either break you or finish building you, and the difference is mostly whether someone built a foundation in you first. Thank whoever poured your foundation. Then go let the hard place finish the walls.

**Rule #19:** The sacred and the hilarious are not enemies. They are two hands lifting the same heavy room. Learn to use both and you will never leave a room lower than you found it.

**Rule #20:** Leadership is ninety-five percent invisible labor and five percent applause that goes to someone else. If you cannot do the ninety-five for the love of it, do not take the title. The title is the lightest part.

**Rule #21:** The person who changes your life will probably never know they did it. So go drop encouraging sentences everywhere, recklessly, like seeds. You will never see the forest. Plant it anyway.

**Rule #22:** If someone throws water at your face, check what you said before you check who threw it. The most important corrections in your life will arrive cold, in public, and entirely deserved.

**Rule #23:** First love does not promise to last. Its only job is to prove your heart works. Let it do its one job, thank it, and carry the proof into every love that comes after.

**Rule #24:** The people who believe in you are also, accidentally, loading you. Carry their belief as fuel, never as a verdict. The day you let it become the judge, you have lost the case before the bell.

**Rule #25:** The respectable life and the right life are sometimes the same and sometimes opposites, and telling them apart is the first real adult decision you make. Choose the life shaped like your soul, not the one shaped like your résumé.

**Rule #26:** Freedom is not the end of struggle. It is the beginning of choice. The day you can finally point your scarce hours at the right thing, point them hard, because that aim is the whole prize.

**Rule #27:** Give your dream a name and wear it before you have earned it. Not to fool the world. To instruct yourself. The name goes first, the work follows, and one day the man catches up to the costume.

**Rule #28:** Release the rough first thing. Trying is more vulnerable than failing, which is why most people never start. Get better in public. There is no private road to a public gift.

**Rule #29:** Enjoy the small pond, but never confuse it with the ocean. The king of a small pond is still a small fish in training. Drink the applause, then get back in the lab.

**Rule #30:** Be nobody in a great place at least once. It will show you the real size of the ocean and, if you are built right, it will make you more sure of your direction, not less. Smallness is information, not a sentence.

**Rule #31:** When you finally get the big stage, give the speech the kid in the back row needs, not the speech the front row expects. The honored speaker who remembers the back row never runs out of things worth saying.

**Rule #32:** Grief is the tax on love, and the bill comes due exactly when you are finally tall enough to pay it. Pay it in full. Then keep the library open. That is the only inheritance that does not die when the people do.

**Rule #33:** The family that creates you is a gift of blood. The family that stays after the funeral is a gift of choice. Honor the dead, but do not forget to thank the living while they can still hear you.

**Rule #34:** When you finally arrive at the dream, it will be both exactly and nothing like you imagined, and that is not a betrayal, it is just how arrival works. Steady the terror with the familiarity. You have been rehearsing for this your whole life.

**Rule #35:** The strategy that gets you to the first level will usually block the road to the next one. Have the courage to retire a winning costume. The man underneath has been getting ready the whole time.

**Rule #36:** A large audience is a magnifying glass, not a mirror. It makes everything you are louder, the wounds along with the gifts. Do the internal work the validation lets you avoid, or the bigness will just make you a bigger version of broken.

**Rule #37:** No achievement on the outside will repair a wound on the inside. The lock is on the inside. Fame just turns the lights on bright enough for you to finally see the door you have been ignoring.

**Rule #38:** The engine that gets you out of a hard beginning has no off switch, and if you never learn to throttle it, it will run you into the ground inside the very dream you built. Rest is not the reward for the work. It is part of the work.

**Rule #39:** Inherited faith has to be outgrown so owned faith can be born. You will find the real thing in the strangest cathedral, probably in the gap between your performances, in the hush you have been too loud to hear. Listen there. It has been waiting.

**Rule #40:** The triumphant homecoming you rehearse will not happen, and the real one is better. The place that built you will not be impressed by your résumé. It will do something far kinder. It will remember who you actually are, underneath all of it, and call you by that name.

**Rule #41:** The child you were is not the embarrassment you escaped. That child is the architect of everything you became. Go back, in writing if you have to, and apologize for the doubt, and then say thank you. You owe that kid a debt no success can settle, only honor.

**Rule #42:** Do not build a career. Build a place. A named hill on ground others called worthless, where the people who were told they were nobody can gather and remember they were always somebody. The point of having a place is to make it a place for others. That is the only kind of success that does not rot.

**Rule #43:** Loud is only a gift when it is aimed. Noise that fills the room to quiet your own fear is just terror in a costume. Noise that breaks a silence so the unheard can be heard is a calling. The volume can stay the same. The aim is the whole difference between a nuisance and a gift.

**Rule #44:** You were maybe born broke. That was the first line, and you did not write it. But broke is the circumstance and loud is the choice, and the comma between them is your whole life. Find the gift the lack cannot repossess. Aim it. Build with it. Build loud, out of the silence they handed you, until it breaks.

Eugine Micah

**For**

Beverlyne Kangazi Kemoli, my mother, who survived on sugar water and faith and never once let me see her quit.

Joab Philemona Etale Ob’bayi, my father, the lion, who fainted over me before he ever held me.

Danson Gadi, my brother and my first audience, and our sisters Beryl and Belinda, his heartbeats.

David Zarembka and Gladys Kamonya, who built me on purpose, in the dark, without applause.

Samson Kemoli of Kemoview and Josephine Kemoli, who gave my mother away so she could be raised and then raised me anyway.

Rosemary Ongoche Ob’bayi and Philemona Jairo Micah Ob’bayi, and the Reverend Micah Ob’bayi of Bunyore, whose name I carry like a small inheritance of grace.

Tommy and Joy Mutanu Zarembka, the ones who stayed after the funerals.

Aunt Joy Ob’bayi, who paid a hospital bill so that I could exist.

Hassan Omar, who once handed me twenty five shillings and, without knowing it, kept me in school.

And for every barefoot child reading this by the light of a phone screen: your beginning is not your verdict. It is only your first line. Keep reading. So will I.

*Until the lions have their own historians, the history of the hunt will always glorify the hunter.*

A proverb, carried to the world by Chinua Achebe

**Author’s Note**

I need to tell you three things before you give me your time, because I respect it too much to let you spend it under the wrong impression.

The first is that this is not a success story. I know how it looks from the outside, the screen and the talking and the confidence, and I know the shape the world wants this book to take, the rise from nothing to something, the neat arc with the bow on top. But I am writing this still in the middle of it, still climbing, still broke in ways money has not fixed and may never fix. If you came for a man who arrived, you came too early. I have not arrived. I am simply far enough up the road to be able to see where I started, and honest enough to describe both.

The second is that I have changed some names. The women especially. They were young when I knew them and most of them did not choose a public life the way I did, so where I tell you about love and the foolish things I did in its name, the names you read are not the names they answer to. The stories are true. The people are protected. That is the deal I made with my conscience and I am keeping it.

The third is that memory is a liar with good intentions. I have checked what I could, asked my mother the things only she remembers, sat with documents and dates. But a childhood is not a court transcript. Where I have had to choose between the fact and the feeling, I have tried to keep both, and where they disagree, I have told you so. This is the truest version of me I am able to write today. Give me ten more years and I would write a slightly different one, because I will be a slightly different man. That is not dishonesty. That is just being alive.

Now. You do not know me. Let me fix that.

# PROLOGUE: A WORD BEFORE WE START

Let us get one thing straight before you turn another page. You do not know me. Maybe you have seen a face on a screen somewhere, a loud one, talking fast about the news of the day with a confidence that looks like it was always there. It was not always there. That confidence is the receipt. This book is the bill, itemized, and I am going to read you every line.

My name is Eugine Micah, although that is not the name they wrote down when I arrived, and I will get to that, because it is a better story when you have to wait for it. For now here is the only thing you need: I was born with nothing. Not the poetic kind of nothing that comfortable people say at dinners to sound humble while a waiter tops up their wine. The literal kind. My mother delivered me without a single coin to her name, in a hospital she could not pay for, and survived those first days on hot water dressed up as tea and a few arrowroots. That is the sort of detail you do not forget, mostly because your stomach files it under permanent.

People want this to be a motivational story. I understand the appetite. The boy who had nothing now has something, cue the music, sell the mug with the quote on it. But I did not write this to motivate you, and if you came for a self help pamphlet you have grabbed the wrong book off the shelf and I would gently point you three shelves down. This is just a true story. It happens to contain motivation the way a body happens to contain blood, not as the point but as the thing keeping it alive. It also contains a great deal of foolishness, several beatings I richly deserved and a few I did not, one cracked skull belonging to my brother, an entire economy built on cow dung, a girl whose smell I have been chasing in other women for a decade, a school that a white American built out of pure stubborn belief, and two coffins that arrived seven days apart and took the best people I have ever known.

I am going to tell it the way it happened, which means it will be funny in places where you will feel slightly guilty for laughing, and heavy in places where you are still chuckling from the line before. That is not a flaw in the writing. That is just how it was to be me. In my house the same afternoon could hold a hymn, a slap, a joke, and a funeral procession passing on the road, and nobody found that strange. I am only keeping the arrangement intact.

And I am going to tell you something that the shape of my life might tempt you to forget, so let me plant it here on the first page where you cannot miss it. I have not made it. I am still trying to make it. This is the report from a man halfway up a hill he is not sure has a top, written while he is still climbing, sweating, occasionally slipping. If that disappoints you, I am sorry, but I would rather disappoint you with the truth than flatter you with a lie. The lie is what every other version of my story has been, the polished one, the inspirational one, the one with the bow. This one has no bow. This one has me, out of breath, pointing back down the slope at the dust I came from and saying, look, can you believe it, can you believe a person starts there.

So no, you do not know me. Not yet. But by the last page you will know me better than some people who have shared my surname for twenty four years. Let us begin where I began, which, fittingly for a man who talks for a living, is with a crime I was part of before I could speak.

# PART ONE: THE LAND THAT MADE ME

## Chapter One: The First Crime I Ever Took Part In, I Committed From Inside My Mother

The first operation I was ever an accomplice to, I pulled off while still a passenger inside my mother, which I would like noted in my defense before any court, because I could not have consented and I certainly did not drive.

Here is what happened, and I have made my mother tell it enough times that I can now narrate it like I was standing there with a clipboard. My aunt, Joy Ob’bayi, my father’s sister, had a daughter named Velma. Joy and the child’s father had separated, in the way these things separated back then, which is to say messily, with the child becoming territory rather than a person. Velma was living among her father’s people. Joy ached for her daughter the specific way a mother aches when another household is raising the child she carried, a hunger that does not respond to reason. She wanted time with her own girl and could not get it through the front door. So she decided to get her through the side.

And she needed an accomplice. She chose my mother. My mother, Beverlyne, who at this point was not some seasoned operative but a teenager, barely seventeen, and, crucial detail, pregnant. With me. Picture the planning meeting. Two young women, one of them carrying a full term human being out front like a smuggled watermelon, sitting down to organize what was, if we are being strict about the law, a kidnapping. I prefer the word extraction. Extraction sounds like there were professionals involved. There were not. There was my aunt, my very pregnant mother, and unborn me, the silent fourth member of the crew who would later make an entire career out of being loud.

The plan was simple in the way that only desperate plans are. They went to where Velma was staying, near a river, and they were sweet to the relatives, all smiles, just here to see the child, you know how it is. Then one of them said the magic sentence that has launched a thousand schemes since the beginning of human cunning: let me just take her for a little walk, I want to talk to her. And they walked. And they kept walking. And the walk quietly became a different thing entirely, which was an escape, with a small girl in tow and a smaller passenger in front, moving fast toward the main road we called the stage, the tarmac artery that is actually the Kenya to Uganda highway, the road that connected our whole small world to everywhere else that existed.

From a town near our home called Lumakanda, where I would later do most of my growing up, they caught a bus. And not a short bus. They rode that thing south and west, past Kakamega town, the whole way down to Kisumu on the shore of Lake Victoria, a journey of well over a hundred kilometers on roads that in those days punished you for the crime of using them. My aunt worked in Kisumu as a nurse. Kisumu was the safe house. So that is the picture I want you to hold: a getaway by public transport, no money for food along the way, a stolen afternoon turning into a stolen child turning into a brand new life, and my mother feeling the first contractions of her own future somewhere on that long, broke, swaying bus.

Now turn the camera around, because the funniest and the saddest part is happening back home at the very same time. My father, Joab, comes looking for his pregnant wife and finds that she has vanished. Not gone to the market vanished. Gone vanished. And remember the technology of that era, which was none. No phone in a pocket to call and ask where are you. No way on this earth to know whether his wife and his unborn first child were alive, in labor, lost, or lying in a ditch somewhere between here and the lake. My father, who I will spend a whole chapter telling you was a lion, a man the village had literally nicknamed for his ferocity, received this news and did the one thing a lion is never supposed to do.

He fainted.

He hit the ground. The shock folded him in half and dropped him where he stood. My mother tells this part now with a small smile that took her years to earn, because at the time there was nothing funny about a man collapsing from terror over a woman he could not find, but with enough decades stacked between you and a thing, even fear softens into a story you can hold in your hand. My father fainted over me before he ever held me, which is, I have decided after long consideration, the most tender thing he ever did for me without using his hands, both of which he would later use on me with great and memorable generosity.

And then, a few days after that bus pulled into Kisumu, I arrived.

I was born on the twenty fourth of December, 2001. Christmas Eve. The whole world tuning up to celebrate a famous baby born broke in a borrowed room to young parents far from home, and into that exact theme, right on cue, came me. Born broke in a borrowed bed to young parents far from home. The off brand version. No wise men, no star, no gold, just a teenage mother with empty pockets in a hospital that everybody called Russia.

I have to explain the hospital, because the hospital is a joke that God set up and then waited about twenty years to let me get to. Its official name is the Jaramogi Oginga Odinga Teaching and Referral Hospital, a name so long that saying the whole thing is a small cardiovascular event. But nobody said the whole thing. Everybody called it Russia, because the Soviet Union built it back in the nineteen sixties as a gift to a young Kenya during the Cold War, a fat concrete handshake from Moscow to a new nation deciding which way to lean. And here is the part I love so much I want to frame it. The Soviet Union collapsed in 1991. So by the time I was born inside it in 2001, the country the hospital was named after had been dead for a full decade. I made my entrance into the world inside a monument to a nation that no longer existed, named for an empire that had already fallen and been buried. If you sat down with the deliberate intention of designing the perfect birthplace for a boy whose entire life would turn out to be about outliving the things that were supposed to define and limit him, you could not improve on it. I was born in the ruins of one dead dream. It turned out to be excellent training for building my own.

We had nothing. I am going to keep saying it plainly throughout this book, because the temptation is always to soften it, and I refuse the softening. My mother did not have a cent. The hospital in those days did not treat you out of kindness, it treated you out of payment, and there was no payment. It was my aunt Joy, the nurse, the same woman whose mission had dragged my pregnant mother all the way to Kisumu in the first place, who quietly covered the bill. There is a circularity in that which I have come to find almost holy. The extraction that started everything also financed my arrival. My mother survived those first days on what she calls strong tea, which sounds cozy and grandmotherly until you learn that it is simply tea leaves boiled in water with sugar, no milk, because milk was a luxury we could not reach, and a few arrowroots, and some mandazi that Joy carried to her bedside. That was the welcome banquet thrown in my honor. Boiled water with ambition, and fried dough by donation.

They did not name me Eugine Micah. That came later, and in pieces. On the day I was born I was Eugine Ob’bayi, carrying my father’s family name straight off the production line. And before even that, before any certificate, my parents had wanted to call me John. They abandoned the idea quickly, because in our village the name John was attached to one or two characters whose reputations you would not wish on a stray goat, let alone your firstborn son. So John was scrapped on the grounds of bad local branding. I have always found this particular fact delicious, because years and years later a boy named John would walk into my life and walk back out of it holding my first real heartbreak in his hands, and I have wondered in my pettier moments whether my parents dodged a curse at the naming and then had it delivered to me anyway, much later, through the back door. We will get there. Be patient. I am.

The Micah, the part that turned Eugine Ob’bayi into Eugine Micah, came from much higher up the family tree. It came from my great grandfather, the Reverend Micah Ob’bayi of Bunyore, a man of the pulpit from the early days of the Church of God among our people, a patriarch whose name carried real weight in a way that mine would have to earn from absolute scratch. So when my name finally settled into Eugine Micah, it was quietly carrying a reverend inside it. Eugine, I am told, means noble, born of a noble line. Micah is a question, an old one, who is like God. Nobility and divinity, both stitched into a boy who would spend the first decade of his life plastering the floor of his classroom with cow dung. The universe has a sense of humor, and it had apparently been writing me as the punchline for some time before I showed up to hear it.

Out there beyond the maternity ward, my country was sitting at a hinge of its own. Kenya in December of 2001 was in the last full year of Daniel arap Moi, a man who had been president for twenty three years, which is longer than my teenage mother had been alive, a reign so long that to a child it did not feel like a government at all, it felt like weather, like a fixed feature of the sky. The very next year he would hand over power and the country would exhale into something it had not been before. So I was born at the seam of everything. An old century had just ended. An old presidency was about to. An old empire whose name was bolted to the wall above my mother’s bed was already gone. And into all of that ending, squalling and dark and tiny and broke, arrived a beginning. Loud, apparently, from the first breath, because my mother swears that I came out of her announcing myself, and I have decided to believe her, partly because it flatters me and partly because it has simply never once stopped being true.

There is an afterward to this story that nobody tells, because birth stories usually stop at the birth, but a baby has to get home, and getting me home was its own small odyssey. Kisumu sits low and hot on the lakeshore, on the edge of Lake Victoria, the second largest freshwater lake on the whole planet, a body of water so vast that three countries share its shoreline and argue, mostly politely, about the fish. Home was none of that. Home was up and away, more than a hundred and twenty kilometers north and west, into the cool red highlands of Lugari, a different climate, a different altitude, a different world entirely. So my mother, days after giving birth with no money, had to gather her newborn and make the long journey back up the bad roads, from the humid lake to the cold maize country, carrying me into the very situation she had fled, where a father waited who had collapsed at the news of her disappearance and now had to reckon with her return, and with me.

I have tried to imagine that reunion and I cannot quite do it. A teenage mother walks back into a homestead holding the firstborn son of a man who fainted from fear of losing them both. What do you even say. There is no script for it. Whatever was said, it held, because they stayed together, and the staying together is the only reason the rest of this book exists. They were not a calm couple, my parents. I will be honest about that throughout. But in that moment, with a new life between them, they chose forward, and I have spent my life being grateful for a decision two frightened young people made before I could thank them for it.

The country I had been born into was about to change its own life too. Within a year of my arrival, Daniel arap Moi would step down, ending an era so long that ending it felt almost unnatural, like the sun deciding to set in a new direction, and Kenya would lurch into a new political season full of hope that would later curdle, as hope in this country has a habit of doing, into the violence I will tell you about when I am a little older in this story. But in those first months I knew none of it. I knew the inside of a donated jumper, the smell of woodsmoke, the cold of a highland night seeping through mud walls, and the particular safety of being a baby who does not yet understand how thin the line was between his arrival and his never arriving at all.

That is how I got here. Smuggled into the world on the back of somebody else’s rescue mission, born on the busiest birthday of the entire calendar to a mother running on sugar water, in a hospital named after a country that had ceased to exist, briefly almost called John, permanently saved from it, and christened in the end with a dead reverend’s name like a small unearned deposit of grace into an account that otherwise held nothing at all.

Welcome to my life. I will be honest with you, it does not get more sensible from here. It gets, if anything, considerably more difficult, and considerably funnier, and I would not hand you a single page of it if I did not believe with my whole chest that you would walk away better for having read it.

The red dust of Lugari was waiting for me up north. Let me take you home.

## Chapter Two: The Republic of Red Dust

If you want to understand a man, do not start with the man. Start with the dirt he came from, because the dirt was there first and it does not lie about anything.

My dirt is red. I mean that literally. The soil of Lugari, up in the highlands of what was then Western Province and is now Kakamega County, is a deep iron red, the kind that stains the soles of your feet and the hems of your shorts and, if you grow up in it, something a little deeper than that too. It is good soil, fertile, generous when the rain behaves, and the whole region runs on it. Maize taller than a tall man. Beans, sugarcane, sweet potatoes, the green and the gold of a place that feeds itself and a good portion of the country besides. I was raised on that red ground, in a village called Manyonyi, in the Marakusi corner of Lugari, and I want to tell you what that place actually is, because most people who end up on a screen come from somewhere they would prefer you imagine rather than know, and I am the opposite. I want you to know it exactly.

Lugari is not an old ancestral homeland in the way people romanticize. It is, if anything, a more interesting thing than that. It is a made place, a settled place, a republic of borrowed land. After independence the government carved up large stretches of forest into settlement schemes and moved people onto them, and over the decades Lugari filled up with more than ten different Kenyan communities all living shoulder to shoulder on plots that had been measured out by a surveyor rather than handed down by a great great grandfather. We bordered Uasin Gishu, the edge of Kalenjin country, close enough that you could feel two worlds leaning against each other. So I did not grow up in a pure place, a one tribe place, a place sealed in its own traditions. I grew up in a cosmopolitan stew of a village where the man on the next plot might speak a completely different mother tongue, and everybody farmed, and everybody was, in one way or another, a person whose family had arrived from somewhere else and decided to build a home out of trees and patience. I think that did something to me. I think being from a place that was itself assembled out of strangers and effort, rather than inherited whole, is part of why I have never been afraid to assemble myself the same way, out of whatever was lying around.

The house I was born into was made of mud. Not mud as a charming rustic accent. Mud as the entire structure. Walls of mud packed over a frame of sticks, a roof of grass thatch in the early years, the floor a smoothed surface of earth. It was a round hut to start, the traditional kind, and my girlfriend, years later, a city girl, told me she had never in her life seen a thatched mud house and could barely believe people had lived in one within her own lifetime, in her own country. To her it was history. To me it was simply the address. We lived in that grass thatched mud house for years, more years than I can count cleanly, and the things I remember about it are not sad to me even though I understand now that they should probably register as hardship.

We slept, when I was very new, on dried banana leaves. There was no mattress in the house when I was born. My mother tells me that to keep me warm through the cold Lugari nights, which get genuinely cold up in those highlands in a way that surprises people who think all of Africa is a furnace, she would zip me into a big jumper, fold my little arms in, and leave only my face poking out, and that is how I slept, a baby burrito in donated wool, breathing the night air of a house with no electricity and no glass in the windows. The cooking happened on three stones. You arrange three stones in a triangle, you build your fire in the middle of them, you balance your pot on top, and that is your kitchen, the same kitchen humans have used for about as long as there have been humans and pots. On those three stones my mother cooked ugali and boiled arrowroots and fried whatever there was to fry, and the smoke of that fire is one of the first smells I knew, woodsmoke and ugali and the particular green smell of rain on its way.

The floor taught me something about the difference between poor and beaten. Our floor, in the later house, was finished with cow dung, which I will explain in proper and loving detail later because it deserves its own chapter, but the short version is that a dung floor, properly maintained, is smooth and clean and even pleasant. The problem was the nights when the room had not been sealed well, because then the safari ants came. If you have never met a safari ant you have been spared a specific education. They are small and red and they move in armies and they bite, and they bite at night, and waking up at three in the morning to the sensation of a battalion of them feasting on your neck is a memory my body still holds even though my mind would happily release it. You learn to seal a room properly when the alternative is being eaten alive in installments.

We got out of the mud and into iron sheets around 2010. My father worked himself raw to make it happen, and the house, an iron sheet house, the dream house, cost something like fifty thousand shillings. I want to put that number in its true frame, because to a reader in a city or another country fifty thousand shillings is a phone, a weekend, a nothing. In a village like ours, to a family like ours, fifty thousand shillings was a fortune assembled coin by coin over years of refusing to give up. It was the difference between a roof that announced your poverty to every passerby and a roof that bought you a little dignity. My father bled for that iron. I watched him do it. I will tell you how later.

Water was its own long story, and it was the story of the whole region, not just my family. Lugari and the sub locations around it, Manyonyi and Marakusi, struggled for water for decades, an honest, documented, generational struggle that the local authorities are only now, in my adulthood, finally addressing with proper community water projects. In my childhood there was no tap that mattered. Water came from a borehole if you were near one, or from the local stream, where the water collected at a pump the villagers had rigged, a metal pipe driven into the place where the water came up out of the ground, running day and night. We called it the pombo, our own worn down version of the word pump. You went to the pombo with a jerrican and you carried home what your arms could manage, and for the cattle you improvised, throwing a jerrican rigged with a wire handle down into the borehole and hauling it up, again and again, until the troughs were full. I have carried more water in my life than most people will ever see, and I did not think of it as suffering at the time. I thought of it as Tuesday.

And then there were the chickens. Free range chickens, which means chickens that were not pampered in cages but turned loose every morning to fend for themselves and hunted by everything in the sky, hawks and eagles patrolling for an easy lunch. A chicken at our home was not a casual thing. A chicken was honor. A chicken was the centerpiece of any event that mattered, slaughtered for a guest you wished to dignify, for a celebration, for a homecoming. To eat chicken was to be, for one meal, a person of consequence. I mention them now because those chickens become important later, when I tell you about the first audience I ever performed for, an audience of poultry with no interest in me whatsoever, but that too is a later chapter.

I have to tell you how we saw the world, because for a village child in the early two thousands the world arrived through very specific and very narrow windows, and what came through those windows we believed completely, with our whole chests, no questions asked.

The first window was the radio, a small portable thing that ran on batteries and crackled, and through it came news and music and the voices of a country that felt impossibly far from our hill. The second window, the magic one, was the screen, and screens were rare and therefore sacred. There was a television at a neighbor’s place, and a television at my grandfather’s place in Lumakanda, and to watch one you had to either be invited or be clever, and we were usually clever. On that screen, on the national broadcaster, came wrestling, the loud theatrical American kind, and we watched grown men in costumes throw each other around and we took it as gospel truth, every staged blow a real one, every scripted feud an actual war between actual enemies. And then there was DJ Afro.

If you did not grow up in Kenya in those years I cannot fully explain DJ Afro to you, but I will try, because he is part of how I became me. DJ Afro was a narrator, a man who took foreign films, action movies and Indian films and Chinese films and Korean and Vietnamese and Tanzanian and American ones, films in languages no village child could follow, and he narrated over them in our own language, explaining and exaggerating and dramatizing, turning a confusing foreign picture into a thrilling local experience. He made the whole world’s cinema legible to a boy in Lugari for the price of ten shillings at a movie shack. And before even DJ Afro, before the shacks, there were the community screenings, and these I remember like a holy festival. Someone would bring a projector and a generator, or a big battery charged in a distant town, and they would hang a white cloth or a bedsheet between two poles, and as darkness fell the whole village would gather on the ground in front of that glowing sheet, and images would pour out of the machine onto the cloth, powered by a generator coughing in the dark, and we would sit transfixed while the rest of the world flickered to life in a field in Manyonyi.

And here is the thing about all of it, the radio and the wrestling and DJ Afro and the bedsheet cinema. We believed it. Not in the way a city child half believes, knowing somewhere in the back of the mind that it is a show. We believed it the way you believe the ground. If we watched a wrestling match or a movie, we did not simply enjoy it, we absorbed it as fact, and then we did the most important thing, the thing that made me a storyteller before I knew the word. We narrated it. The next day at school, the kids who had seen the thing would retell it to the kids who had not, point by point, blow by blow, and the retelling was never faithful, it was inflated, dramatized, improved, packed with lies and exaggerations that made the story bigger and better than the original, and your standing among your peers depended directly on how well you performed the retelling. I was good at the retelling. I was very good at it. I did not know I was training. I thought I was just showing off. I was doing both.

This is the part of the dust I am most grateful for. A rich child has the whole world handed to him through a screen in his pocket, frictionless, infinite, and he never has to work to possess any of it. I had to fight for every image I ever saw, sneak for it, pay ten shillings for it, sit in a field for it, and then, crucially, I had to give it back out loud to be anybody at all. The scarcity made me a teller. If the world had come to me easily I would have consumed it quietly like everyone with easy access does. Because it came hard, I learned to pass it on, performed and embellished and alive, and that skill, born of having almost nothing, is the exact skill I now sell.

That was the baseline, the floor, the every single day of it. A good day had a little more, a better vegetable, a splash of cooking oil that was actually enough rather than rationed down to a memory of oil. And the bad days, the days there was simply nothing in the pot and nothing in the farm because the season had turned against us, I will tell you about those too, because hunger was a regular guest in my childhood and I will not pretend it knocked politely and waited to be invited. But here is the thing I most want you to take from this chapter, the thing the red dust taught me before I had words for it. Christmas, my own birthday, the day of my arrival, meant something very specific in our house, and it was not gifts and it was not snow and it was not a fat man in a red suit. Christmas meant good food. It meant meat, actual meat, and chapati, and mandazi, and some juice diluted down with water to make it stretch across all of us. That was the whole feast. That was the summit of the year. And we were, on that day, genuinely, uncomplicatedly happy, happier over a plate of meat and a cup of watery juice than many people will ever be over far more, because we knew exactly what it had cost to get there and we knew it would be gone tomorrow.

I have eaten in good restaurants now. I have been handed menus in places where the prices are a quiet act of aggression. And I will tell you, with my hand on my chest, that nothing has ever tasted the way that Christmas meat tasted in the mud house in Manyonyi, on the red dust, by the light of a kerosene lamp. Nothing. The dust gave me a baseline of joy so low to the ground that almost everything since has felt like abundance. That is the gift hidden inside the hardship, and it took me half my life to unwrap it.

## Chapter Three: The Secret in the Family Tree

Every family has a story it tells out loud and a story it keeps in the rafters, and the second story is always the truer one. Mine kept a beautiful, complicated secret in the rafters, and the secret is this: my mother was given away, and the people who gave her away and the people who received her are all, somehow, my grandparents, and I love every one of them, and untangling exactly how I am related to my own relatives is a task that has defeated better mathematicians than me.

Let me try anyway, slowly, because you need this map to follow the rest of the book.

Start with my mother, Beverlyne. She was born to Samson Kemoli and Josephine Kemoli. Samson, my grandfather, is a man who built a place and put his own name on it, a homestead called Kemoview, because his name is Kemoli and the man had the audacity and the humor to look at his patch of earth and christen it like an estate, Kemo’s view, a king naming his small kingdom. Josephine, my grandmother, his wife, was a woman of the church, a pastor, a person who prayed the way other people breathe. These two are my mother’s blood. Her origin. The well she was drawn from.

But Beverlyne was not raised by Samson and Josephine. She was given to Josephine’s sister. That sister was my grandmother Gladys, Gladys Kamonya, and Gladys was married to a man named David Zarembka, and here the story tilts and becomes something almost no other family in Lugari could claim, because David Zarembka was a white American. Let me say that again so it lands. My grandfather, the man who raised my mother, the man who would later become the single most important human being in my own development, was an American. A Quaker. A man from across an ocean who had ended up in our red dust and married into our family and become, in every way that counts except the one written on a birth certificate, my mother’s father and my own grandfather.

So my mother had two sets of parents. The blood set, Samson and Josephine of Kemoview, who brought her into the world and then handed her over. And the raising set, Gladys and David Zarembka, who took her in and made her theirs. There was nothing legal about it, no court, no papers, no judge with a stamp. It was simply a thing that came to be, arranged between two sisters in the way our families arrange enormous things, with conversation and custom and a handshake of the heart, and then it was true forever. I have stopped asking exactly how they decided it. Some agreements are too old and too deep to audit. I just know the result. I know that when I picture my mother as a girl, I picture her at David and Gladys’s place, being shaped by an American Quaker’s library and a Kenyan pastor’s discipline, and I know that David and Gladys had children of their own too, Tommy and Joy Mutanu Zarembka, who are therefore my mother’s siblings and my aunt and uncle, and who will matter enormously near the end of this book, when almost everyone else is gone.

Now cross over to my father’s side, which is its own country.

My father, Joab Philemona Etale Ob’bayi, was born to Rosemary Ongoche Ob’bayi and Philemona Jairo Micah Ob’bayi. My grandmother Rosemary was, like seemingly every powerful woman in my bloodline, a person of the church, a reverend in the Church of God, a woman who would take me to far flung congregations and sit me at the front while she ministered. My grandfather Philemona carried in his own name the name of his father, and his father is the deepest root I can reach and the most important one for understanding me. His father, my great grandfather, was the Reverend Micah Ob’bayi of Bunyore.

Bunyore is in Vihiga, the heartland of the Abanyole people, and it is one of the places where Christianity first took root among the Luhya, where the Church of God planted itself early and grew. The Reverend Micah Ob’bayi was part of that early native clergy, the first generations of our own people to take up the pulpit, to weave the new faith together with the old Luhya world, to become leaders in a time of enormous change. He was a patriarch. He founded a line. And I carry his name, Micah, the question that asks who is like God, handed down across three generations to land on a boy reading by a kerosene lamp who had no idea, for most of his childhood, that his middle name was an inheritance from a reverend rather than just a sound his parents happened to like.

My father, unlike my mother, was never adopted by anyone. He had no Gladys to take him in, no soft landing arranged between sisters. He was, as he likes to put it in his harder moods, adopted by the world. He made himself out of the raw materials of being a firstborn son in a farming family, which in our tradition is less an honor than a sentence to responsibility, and I will tell you all about that in the next chapter, because the man deserves his own.

And then there is the matter of blood and tribe, which in Kenya is never far from the surface and which I have decided to state plainly rather than dance around. On my father’s side I am Abanyole, of the Abatongoi clan, traced back through the generations to an ancestor of the Anyole people, rooted in Bunyore, of the great Bantu stream that flowed into the fertile highlands of western Kenya centuries ago and stayed. On my mother’s side, through the Kemoli line, I carry the Maragoli thread, the Avamaseero, another branch of the Luhya world, a people with a near religious devotion to education that I believe travelled directly down into my mother and out of her into me. So I am a meeting point. Abatongoi and Avamaseero. Bunyore and Maragoli. Two clans, two streams, joined in one dark skinned boy in a mud house, carrying a reverend’s name and a teacher’s hunger for books, raised by an American and a pastor and a king who named his own hill.

I want to pause on David Zarembka, because a white American Quaker landing in a Lugari family tree is the kind of detail that sounds invented, and he is the most real person I know.

He did not come to us as a tourist or a missionary in the pith helmet sense. He came as a man who had given his life to peace work in this part of Africa, who believed, with the deep stubborn conviction of his Quaker faith, that ordinary people could be trained to refuse violence even when violence was all around them, and who put that belief into practice in the most dangerous seasons our region ever knew. He had education most of our village could only dream of, and he carried it lightly, and he married into us and stayed, and he built a life on our red dust that bridged two worlds that were never supposed to touch. To the village he was the Mzungu, the white man, a figure of fascination who drove a car when almost no one had one. To me, eventually, he was simply the grandfather who handed me books and listened to me read them in my mangled grammar without ever once correcting me in the way that stung, the way the teachers corrected, the way that made wrong feel like pain. At his house, being wrong felt like learning. That difference shaped my entire relationship with knowledge, and I owe it to him, and he is gone now, and I am not ready to tell you how, so I will only say that he is the reason I have loved a library my whole life, and leave the grief for its proper chapter.

There is a detail about my two grandfathers’ homes that captures the whole strange spread of my childhood in a single image, and it is the matter of electricity. My grandfather David, the American, had electricity at his place in Lumakanda. My grandfather Samson, the Kenyan king of Kemoview, did not. My grandfather Philemona, my father’s father, did not either. So in my childhood, electricity was not a utility, it was a destination. It lived at exactly one of the three homes I rotated through, and to go from Samson’s place to David’s place was to travel from one century into another, from kerosene lamp to light switch, from the dark that humans have always known to the lit room that is a very recent miracle. I grew up crossing that border back and forth, and I think it gave me an unusual ease with moving between worlds, between the village and the town, the broke and the comfortable, the dark and the lit, because I had been doing it since before I could name it, walking from a grandfather with no power to a grandfather with power and understanding, on some level, that I belonged equally and uneasily to both.

And then there is Kemoview, which I love as a fact about my grandfather Samson more than almost any other. Here was a man of modest means in rural Kenya, a former police officer who had served his country and retired after an injury to his leg, and he looked at his patch of earth and he did not call it the farm or the home or the plot. He named it. Kemoview. He took his own name, Kemoli, and built it into a title for his land, the way the wealthy name their estates, and he planted that name in the red soil like a flag. I have come to see that act as one of the most quietly defiant things in my whole family history. A man who had been given little insisting that what he had was worthy of a name, was a place, was Kemo’s view, a kingdom however small. I did not understand it as a child. I understand it completely now, because I have spent my adult life doing the exact same thing, taking a name and building it into something that announces I was here, this is mine, you cannot tell me it does not count.

I want to go deeper into the two streams that meet in me, because a man should know his rivers.

The Abanyole, my father’s people, are part of the great Bantu movement that flowed into this part of Africa over centuries, peoples who migrated and settled and farmed the fertile western highlands until the land and the people became inseparable. The Luhya nation is not one tribe but a confederation of many subtribes and an enormous web of clans, and within the Abanyole of Bunyore sits my clan, the Abatongoi, traced back to an ancestor in the deep lineage of the Anyole people, our name a thread running back further than any of us can fully follow. To be Abatongoi is to belong to Bunyore, and Bunyore matters in the story of this whole region for one particular reason: it is one of the cradles where Christianity first took deep root among our people. The Church of God established itself there early, at Kima, and out of that early mission grew the first generations of native clergy, our own people stepping into pulpits that had until then belonged to foreigners. And that, exactly that, is where my great grandfather the Reverend Micah Ob’bayi stood. He was part of that turning, that handover, when the faith stopped being something done to us and became something led by us. So the name I carry, Micah, is not a random pretty sound. It is a marker dropped at one of the most important moments in my people’s history, the moment we took up the work of the spirit ourselves, and it travelled three generations down a family tree to land on me. I find that almost unbearably moving when I let myself sit in it. A boy plastering a classroom floor with cow dung was carrying, in his middle name, a piece of the day his people claimed their own pulpit.

The other river is the Maragoli, my mother’s stream, through the Avamaseero clan and the Kemoli line. And here I have to tell you something about the Maragoli that every Kenyan half knows, which is that they are, as a people, almost religiously devoted to education. It is a stereotype because it is a pattern, and it is a pattern because it is largely true, a whole community that decided, generations ago, that the surest road up and out was through the classroom, and that built that conviction into its children so deeply that it became a kind of inheritance. My mother is Maragoli to her marrow, and her unbreakable, sacrificial faith in school is not just her personal virtue, it is the inheritance of her people pouring through her into me. When she gave up her own education and redirected it into mine, she was not acting alone. She was carrying out, in the hardest possible circumstances, the deepest instruction of her bloodline. Educate the child. Whatever it costs. Educate the child.

So look at what meets in me. From Bunyore, a pulpit and a faith claimed by its own people. From Maragoli, a near sacred hunger for the school. The spirit and the book. Faith and learning, the two great engines of my whole family, fused in one dark skinned boy who would grow up to do a job that is, when you strip it down, exactly those two things, the standing up in front of people to tell them true things, and the relentless studying required to know what is true. I did not fall far from either tree. I fell, if anything, directly between them, into the confluence, and the confluence raised me.

When people ask me where I am from, I used to give the short answer, Lugari, Kakamega, and leave it there. I do not do that anymore. I am from a negotiation between two sisters. I am from a pulpit in Bunyore. I am from a hill that a man named after himself. I am from a marriage that crossed an ocean. I am from a clan whose name most Kenyans have never heard and a tribe whose appetite for school built half the professionals in this country. I am not from one place. I am from a confluence, and a confluence, if you think about it, is just a place where two rivers stop being separate and agree to become something larger and louder on their way to the sea.

I think I have been trying to get to the sea my whole life. The next few chapters are about the people who pointed me at it.

## Chapter Four: The Lion

There is a man in my village they call the lion, and the reason they call him the lion is that if you went to war with him you would lose, quickly, and you would not have anything clever to say about it afterward. That man is my father, and loving him has been the most complicated full time job of my life.

Let me give him to you the way he actually was, because the temptation in a memoir is to flatten your father into either a villain or a saint, and my father was the far more interesting and more difficult thing, which is a whole human being operating under enormous pressure with tools he was never given a chance to sharpen.

Joab Philemona Etale Ob’bayi was, before anything else, the hardest working man I have ever encountered, and I have now met some genuinely driven people in the worlds of media and business and I am telling you my father would out work all of them and then ask what else needed doing. He was born into a farming family, a real one, where his own father held something like forty seven or forty eight acres of land that would, in time and according to custom, be divided among the children. As the firstborn son, my father inherited not just a share of that land but the position itself, the heir, the one upon whom the role of the father falls when the father is gone. In our tradition that is not a trophy. It is a yoke. It means you carry the family whether or not you asked to, whether or not you are ready, whether or not anyone ever taught you how.

And so my father carried. From the time I could walk, the farm was the sun his whole life orbited. There was no day off, no concept of a weekend, no notion that a man might simply rest. If we were not harvesting, we were planting. If we were not planting, we were weeding. If we were not weeding, we were tilling, or digging trenches and gabions to hold the soil against the rain, or planting napier grass for the cattle, or tending the cattle, or cleaning the compound, or shelling maize, or hauling water, or doing one of the ten thousand other tasks that a working farm demands in an endless rotation that does not care that you are tired or young or that it is a holiday. We grew everything. Maize and beans and cassava and arrowroots and sukuma wiki and cabbages and carrots and spinach and tomatoes and bananas. Our whole survival lived in the space between the homestead and the farm, and my father policed that space like a general.

He also worked beyond the farm, because the farm alone could not produce the cash a family needs, and this is where his story turns almost heroic and almost tragic at the same time. He worked in the mines. He worked as a driver. He worked as security for important people, a bodyguard of sorts for VIPs. He managed bars. He supervised stone cutters and truck drivers, making sure the men got paid on time and the work got done. Wherever he went, my mother tells me, he rose, because the man’s work ethic was simply not normal, it was a force, and people noticed. But here is the cruelty of it. Despite the rising, despite the relentless competence, his lack of formal education kept the wages low. He gave a giant’s effort and received a small man’s pay, over and over, for years, and a lesser man would have broken on that wheel. My father did not break. He just kept turning it, and turning it, and turning it.

The discipline. I have to tell you about the discipline, because it is part of the truth and I promised you the truth. My father disciplined with a ferocity that matched his nickname.
