The signature move, the one my body has not forgotten, was this: he would catch you by one leg, the right or the left, it did not matter, and he would lift you clean off the ground so that you hung upside down, your head pointed at the earth, and then he would apply the cane to your backside with a thoroughness that left no doubt about the lesson. And as a son of the soil and of his generation, he insulted while he worked, not the worst words, never the truly foul ones, but the standard issue arsenal of the Luhya father, calling you stupid, kuchinga, or kundu kuno, which means roughly this thing, this oversized creature, this monster. To a child these words land hard. To an adult looking back, they are almost quaint, the limited vocabulary of a man expressing a fear he had no other language for, the fear that if he did not press hard enough on us we would slip into the same poverty that had pressed so hard on him.

When he was away working, which was often, we had freedom, and he knew it, and he had a line he delivered before every departure that I can still hear in his exact voice. I have my eyes on you. I have people everywhere. Even if you go anywhere, I will know. It was a bluff and it was not a bluff, because in a village everyone does know everyone, and a father’s reputation is a kind of surveillance that follows his children down every path. We tested it anyway, as children do, but the line worked more often than it had any right to.

I think often about the gap between what my father was and what he was paid, because that gap explains a rage I did not understand as a child and forgive completely as an adult. Here was a man who could walk into a mine, a bar, a security detail, a crew of stone cutters, and rise, every time, because competence radiated off him. And here was a wage structure that looked at his lack of paper, his unfinished schooling, and decided that no matter how hard the lion worked he would be fed like a house cat. He supervised men who made sure truck drivers and stone cutters got their pay on time, and his own pay never matched the size of what he carried. A man can absorb that for a while. Over years it corrodes something. I watched it corrode him, slowly, and I learned a lesson I did not want, which is that effort alone does not set you free, that the world has gates, paper gates, and a man can break his body against them his whole life and never be let through. My father’s unrewarded labor is the reason I became obsessed, later, with the gates themselves, with finding the cracks in them, with refusing to simply work hard inside a system that had already decided how little hard work would buy me. He taught me that by example, painfully, without meaning to. The lion showed me that strength is not enough. You also have to be cunning about which wall you throw it at.

He had a way of leaving for work that I can still perform from memory, because he performed it for us every time. He would stand at the edge of the compound, about to go, and he would deliver the warning, calm and total. I have my eyes on you. I have people everywhere around this place who know me. Even if you go anywhere, I will hear about it, I do not care where you go. And then he would leave, and we would be, technically, free, two boys with the whole red world unsupervised in front of us. But the line worked. It worked because in a village it was halfway true, everyone did know everyone, a father’s name was a net cast over the whole area, and a child who misbehaved two villages away might find the news had beaten him home. We tested the net constantly and got caught in it often, and even when we got away with something, the line sat in the back of the mind, his voice, I have eyes everywhere, a surveillance system made entirely of reputation and dread, and I have come to think it was less about controlling us and more about a frightened man trying to extend his protection over children he had to keep leaving in order to feed.

the thing that no one in any of the earlier tellings of my life ever properly understood, because they were too busy looking at the screen I ended up on to notice the man who, without any idea what he was doing, trained me for it. When my father had us in Nairobi for a season, before I was even circumcised, around 2013 and 2014, he had a rule. Every morning, before he left for work in the mines, he would order my brother and me to watch the news. Not cartoons. The news. The international news, CNN, Al Jazeera, France 24, the big serious channels, the ones with maps and grave voices and the whole turning world laid out in segments. And we were to watch it, and we were to write down everything we had seen, so that when he came home in the evening he could quiz us, and only if we passed would he return our phones, our little button phones, our brick Nokias, so that we could chat with our friends.

Do you understand what that man did? He turned the news into currency. He made the world’s events the price of admission to our own small social lives. And so my brother and I, two village boys, sat in front of CNN and Al Jazeera every morning and absorbed the grammar of news, the rhythm of a report, the way a story is framed and delivered, the names of countries and crises and leaders, the entire architecture of broadcast journalism, years before I had any inkling that it would become my actual profession. We cheated sometimes, I will admit it, sneaking the phones and then scrambling to scroll the channels for an hour before he got home so we would have something to report. But even the cheating was training, because what is a journalist on deadline but a person scrambling to absorb and repackage the world’s events at speed.

I did not stumble into journalism. I did not have some magical innate gift that descended from the sky. My father, a barely educated lion of a man who could not give us money, gave us the news instead, every single morning, as a tax and a test, and in doing so he handed me a nose for what is happening in the world that I have never lost. To this day I can watch a single clip and feel the whole context assemble itself around it. That is not talent. That is a childhood of mandatory CNN before breakfast, enforced by a man who would dangle you by your leg if you got it wrong.

There is a shadow over this chapter, and I will not pretend it away, but I will not detonate it here either, because it deserves its own room later in the book. For many years my father drank, and when he drank he was a different and frightening man, and our home in those seasons was a place of unpredictable weather. It made me hate alcohol with a purity I still carry. And then one day, when I was nearly finished with high school, he quit, simply and finally, and told me about it almost in passing, and I was so happy and so angry at the same time that I did not know what to do with my own face. But that is a story for when you know me better. For now, hold the lion as he was at his best: working, always working, dangling his sons by the leg and feeding them the news, terrified of a poverty he was fighting with everything he had, and fainting, once, long ago, over a pregnant wife he could not find. That last image is the real one. Under the ferocity, a man who loved so hard it once knocked him unconscious.

## Chapter Five: The Praying Storm

If my father was the lion, my mother was the storm, and I mean that as the highest compliment I am capable of, because a storm is the thing that waters the crop and the thing that flattens the fence, often on the same afternoon, and you do not get to choose which one is coming, and you love it anyway because without it nothing grows.

My mother is Beverlyne Kangazi Kemoli. I want to be precise about that name, because there is a name underneath the name. On her national identity card she is Beverlyne Kangazi Kemoli, the daughter of the Kemoli line, and that is the name she was born with and carries in law. The world, after her adoption into Gladys’s household, attached Kamonya to her, but Kamonya is not on the card, Kamonya is the name the raising gave her rather than the name the bearing did, and my mother is a Kemoli woman to her marrow no matter what any document downstream of her birth might say. I tell you this small administrative fact because it is not small at all. My mother spent her life being two things at once, the given away daughter and the taken in daughter, the blood and the adoption, and even her name carries the doubleness. She learned early that a person can belong fully to two places and be perfectly whole, and she passed that lesson down to me without ever sitting me down to teach it.

The first thing to know about my mother is that she is made of faith the way other people are made of bone. She is profoundly, structurally religious, not in a loud performative way but in the way of someone for whom God is simply the medium they move through. She was a Sunday school teacher when she was young and she is a Sunday school teacher still, and she is, I will say it plainly, one of the best I have ever seen, a woman who genuinely understands children, who can hold a roomful of them and make the eternal feel close and kind. People in our world know her for this. She has a gift for the young that she has spent her whole life giving away for free.

She was also, before everything broke and reformed, a teacher in the ordinary sense, in actual schools, even after the family had to scatter and rebuild. Education was her religion’s twin. She believed in school the way she believed in God, as a thing that could save you, and she came by that belief honestly and painfully, which brings me to the wound at the center of my mother that I have spent years learning to see.

My mother was about seventeen when she had me. Seventeen. She had been in her final years of secondary school, Form Four, the threshold of the rest of her life, and then she got pregnant, with me, and the door that school had been holding open swung shut. She never even collected her national exam certificate, her KCSE papers, the document that would have let her walk forward into whatever she might have become. They are sitting somewhere still, uncollected, a frozen moment of who my mother was supposed to be. She tells me about them sometimes, not bitterly, just as a fact she carries, and every time she does I make her the same promise, the promise I intend to keep before I do almost anything else with whatever success I gather. When I make it, truly make it, I am going to go and collect my mother’s papers and put them in her hands, so that the door that closed on a seventeen year old can be reopened for the woman she became, and she can walk through it late but walking, into the further education she gave up so that I could exist. I owe her that. I owe her more than that, but I owe her that specifically and by name.

Because here is what she did with the closed door. She did not lie down in front of it. She turned around and poured everything she had been going to spend on her own becoming into ours instead. She has said it to me in her hardest hours, in the voice she uses when she is asking God for things, Eugine, I stopped everything because of you children, I sacrificed it because of you. And she did. The teacher who never got to finish became the mother who would not let her sons fail to start. That is a transfer of a life, a whole human future redirected, and I am one of the things she spent it on.

The church was not a Sunday thing for my mother, it was the air of the whole week, and because she breathed it, I breathed it, dragged from congregation to congregation across my childhood like a small reluctant disciple. There was the Church of God at Manyonyi, the first sanctuary I ever knew, and then Lugari Church of God, and then the Friends Church at Lumakanda, the Quaker congregation that connected to my grandfather David’s whole way of being in the world. My grandmother on my father’s side, Rosemary, was a reverend, and she would carry me to far away churches to sit at the front while she ministered, sometimes riding there on a motorbike with an uncle driving and the reverend grandmother on the back and me wedged in between, a small sandwich of faith bouncing down a dirt road toward a distant pulpit. And there was a worldly incentive folded into all that holiness that I will confess to, because honesty is the deal, which is that church often meant lunch. A good church lunch, ugali and sukuma wiki and, if God was truly smiling, some fried meat and soup, was an event a hungry child would attend a great many sermons to reach. I learned the gospel and I learned to sit still for it partly out of devotion and partly because devotion was, on the best Sundays, catered.

My mother taught in that church and she taught in schools, and the teaching and the faith were the same instinct in her wearing two uniforms, the instinct to shape the young, to open a door in a child and walk them through it. She could hold a roomful of children the way a good conductor holds an orchestra, and I have sat in the back of rooms she was teaching and watched it and felt a complicated pride, because the woman pouring all this skill into other people’s children for free was the same woman who had given up her own schooling to keep mine alive. She had so much to give that even after sacrificing her own future she had enough left over to teach the whole village’s young. That is not normal. That is a kind of abundance that has nothing to do with money, and my mother, who never had money, was rich in it her entire life.

Now, the storm itself, because I have shown you the calm and the sacrifice and you might be picturing some serene saint floating through our home dispensing gentle wisdom, and that is not who raised me. My mother, when crossed, was a force of nature that you did not want to be standing in front of. She is not a small woman, and when the anger came she could move with a speed that defied her size. The instrument of justice was whatever lay within reach, and since she was usually cooking when we pushed her over the edge, the instrument was usually the mwiko, the wooden cooking stick used to turn ugali, a tool that doubles with terrifying ease into a tool of correction. The mwiko, the slipper, the flat of a half can, the standard arsenal of the African mother, applied with conviction.

There is one memory I return to so often it has worn smooth like a stone in a river. Our house sat on a hill, and my father’s parents’ home, where my paternal grandparents lived, was about a kilometer away, downhill. So when I had done something to summon the storm, and I always knew the instant I had done it, my survival strategy was simple and athletic. I ran. I would launch myself down the slope toward my grandparents’ place, screaming at the top of my considerable lungs the entire way, because that is where safety lived, behind a grandparent or an uncle or an aunt who would shield me from my mother’s righteous pursuit. And my mother, not a small woman, would come down that hill after me regardless, calling out the words that still make me laugh and flinch in equal measure, you come here, you child, come here, come here, come here. I can hear it now. I can feel the wind of that downhill sprint and the specific terror and thrill of being chased by a parent who loves you enough to want to discipline you and is large enough to make the discipline a real possibility.

I laugh at it now. I want to be careful, though, because there is a place where the laughing has to stop, and my mother lived in that place for years, and I saw it, and I will tell you about it properly later. There was violence in our home that was not discipline, that was done to her and not by her, and it shaped everything, and it is the reason she would one day vanish to Nairobi the way she had once helped my aunt vanish to Kisumu. The women in my family have a long history of escaping toward survival, and my mother’s escape is one of the load bearing events of my childhood. But I am not going to spend it here, in the chapter that is supposed to be her praise. Here I want her as the storm at its most generous, chasing a screaming boy down a hill with a cooking stick, praying over him every night that he might one day rise high enough to lift her, and meaning it with the whole of her enormous, sacrificed, unbreakable heart.

She prayed for my success before I had any reason to believe in it. I want you to sit with that. Before there was a single sign, before the screen, before the talking, before any of it, when I was just a poor loud boy in a mud house with a closed door behind his mother and a closed door in front of him, she was on her knees asking God to open something for me. I am, in the most literal sense I know, an answer to a prayer that a seventeen year old started praying with sugar water in her body and never stopped. Whatever I become, I became because a storm decided to water me instead of flattening me. That was a choice. She made it every single day. She is still making it.

## Chapter Six: Dante, My First Audience

Every performer needs a first audience, the one who is there before the lights, before the applause, before anyone is paying, the one who watches you become yourself when becoming yourself is still embarrassing and unfinished. Mine was my brother, and he did not volunteer for the job, and he has the scars to prove the working conditions were poor.

His name is Danson Gadi, though I have called him Dante for so long that Danson sounds almost formal to me now, like his Sunday name. He was born on the eighteenth of April, 2003, which makes him a little over a year younger than me, close enough that we grew up as a single unit, a two boy operation, partners and rivals and each other’s only constant in a childhood where the adults kept disappearing and reappearing. If you want to understand the truest relationship of my early life, it is not me and my father or me and my mother, both of whom were absent for long and crucial stretches that I will explain. It is me and Dante. We raised each other. For years, while our parents were away seeking a future in other towns, we were each other’s family, and that does something to two boys that nothing ever undoes.

Dante was the last born, before our sisters Beryl and Belinda came along much later, in 2020 and after, and like every last born he carried the last born curriculum, which is to say he was stubborn in the specific, unkillable way of a child who has learned that the rules bend a little further for him than they did for the one who came first. He was outgoing, loud in his own right, and the honest truth is that he was a replica of me, or I of him, two versions of the same restless, performing, attention seeking creature, which is exactly why we loved each other ferociously and fought each other the same way.

And we fought. Lord, we fought. Two boys, close in age, sharing everything including a shortage of everything, will find reasons to go to war, and we found them. There is a mark on my right arm that I will carry to my grave, an old deep scratch that never fully healed, and it is Dante’s signature. We were fighting over a ball, the way boys have fought over balls since the invention of the ball, and in the scuffle he shoved me into a fence, and the fence had barbed wire, and the wire opened my arm, and there it stays, a permanent receipt for one childhood disagreement about who the ball belonged to. I look at it sometimes in the middle of doing something very adult and serious, and I think, that is from Dante, and the world I am standing in falls away for a second and I am back on the red dust with my brother and a ball and the sting of wire.

I should explain the kitchen, because the kitchen is where displaced children lived, and we were, for long stretches, displaced children even in our own extended family. When you are the spare boys, the ones whose parents are away, the ones who arrive at a relative’s home to be housed rather than welcomed, you do not get the good room. You get the kitchen, the smoky outbuilding where the cooking fire lives, and you sleep there among the soot and the firewood, and your morning alarm is not a rooster, it is a lungful of woodsmoke when someone lights the fire to cook, smoke that fills the little room and drives you out coughing into the cold whether or not you were finished sleeping. Dante and I slept in kitchens for years. I do not say that for pity. I say it because the kitchen is the unglamorous backstage of my whole childhood, and almost everything I am proud of was rehearsed in rooms that smelled of smoke.

There is a thing I should name about being two boys with no sister for most of our childhood, because it cut against everything our tradition expected. In our world the chores are divided sharply by sex, the women and girls cook and clean and fetch, the men and boys provide and protect, and a circumcised male is not supposed to be found in the kitchen doing women’s work. But there were no girls in our house, only Dante and me and our mother, our sisters not yet born, and so the two of us simply absorbed all of it, the cooking, the cleaning, the fetching, the dishes, the entire domestic load that custom said was not ours to carry. We were mocked for it, called names, taunted as the family that produced only boys and therefore had to make its boys do the work of girls. And I have come to be quietly grateful for the mockery, because it made me a man who knows how to run a home, who is not helpless in a kitchen, who never learned the lie that certain work is beneath him. Poverty did that. Poverty does not respect your traditions about who does what. Poverty hands the nearest pair of hands the nearest task, and the nearest hands were ours, and we cooked and cleaned and grew up more whole for it than the boys who were waited on.

We were living, as we often did, in the kitchen at our grandparents’ place, because the kitchen was where displaced children slept, and the kitchen had a particular torture built into it, which was the smoke. They cooked with firewood, and when someone lit the morning fire the smoke would fill the room and you would wake up coughing, eyes streaming, driven out into the cold dawn whether you were ready or not. So we were already irritable, already raw, the morning that Dante pushed me one notch too far. I do not even remember the offense. I remember the response, which was that I picked up a belt and hit my brother in the head with the buckle end, and it cracked his skin, and blood came, real blood, pouring from my little brother’s head, and the bottom dropped out of my stomach because I was sure I had done something that could not be undone. He survived, obviously. The scalp bleeds dramatically over nothing. But for a few seconds I had stood there watching my brother bleed because of me, and I learned something in those seconds about the weight of my own hands that I have tried, not always successfully, to remember.

That is the violence of it, and I give it to you honestly because our love was not a soft thing, it was forged in genuine combat. But the combat was never the main event. The main event was that we were a team, a crew of two, against a world that kept trying to separate us from the people who were supposed to protect us. When our parents left, it was Dante and me. When an aunt or an uncle decided we were the family’s spare children and treated us accordingly, it was Dante and me who absorbed it together. I had to parent him, often, in the absence of anyone else willing to do it, had to play the father whenever he wandered off the path, had to ridicule and correct and threaten and provide, because that is the job of the firstborn in a family where the first line of adults has thinned out. I did it badly and I did it constantly and I would do it again.

And he was my audience. This is the part that matters for the rest of the book. Long before there was a camera or a stage or a single stranger who cared what I had to say, there was Dante, sitting there, my captive crowd of one, while I narrated movies we had watched, or invented stories, or reenacted a wrestling match we had seen on the neighbor’s television with all the exaggerations and outright lies that made it better than the original. In our world you watched something and then you performed it for everyone who had not, embellished beyond recognition, and the better your performance the higher your standing, and I performed for my brother first. He was the proving ground. If I could hold Dante, restless stubborn Dante who knew all my tricks, I could hold anyone. The skill that would one day pay my rent was rehearsed, for free, on a little boy with a cracked scalp and a barbed wire grudge, in a smoky kitchen, in the years our parents were gone.

I will leave you with the funniest thing we ever pulled, because this chapter has had enough blood in it. When our grandparents brought us things from their travels, real treasure, shoes and clothes from America, we had to divide the spoils between us, and the dividing was a negotiation worthy of a peace summit. There was a pair of shoes Dante wanted that I also wanted, and the boy, even then a born storyteller like his brother, constructed a piece of pure fiction to claim them. He told the younger children, and tried to tell me, that these particular shoes were so powerful, so waterproof, so special, that if you jumped into a puddle while wearing them you could stop thunder. Stop lightning. That the shoes had power over the sky itself. I have thought about that often, my little brother inventing a superpower for a pair of secondhand American shoes in a village in Lugari, and I have realized that we were both, from the very beginning, the same kind of creature. We did not have much. So we made things up. We took the small true thing, a pair of shoes, a watched wrestling match, a hard life, and we built something larger and louder and more wonderful on top of it, because that was the only abundance available to us, the abundance of the story.

I went on to do it on television to seven hundred thousand people. Dante did it first, to a handful of village kids, about a pair of shoes that could stop lightning. We are our father’s sons and our mother’s, yes. But before we were anyone else’s, we were each other’s first audience, and I have never once forgotten where I learned to perform. I learned it for him, in the smoke, on the dust, with a belt in one hand and a story in the other.

*A note before Part Two: This is where my world was still small and almost whole, held together by dust and family and the daily fight to eat. It does not stay whole. In the next part the adults leave, the floor of the house becomes an economy of cow dung, a school in the bushes teaches me to read and to bleed, and a phone call across a great distance breaks something in me that talking for a living has been trying to repair ever since. Stay with me. We are just getting started.*

BORN BROKE, BUILT LOUD

Eugine Micah

Table of Contents

**BORN BROKE, BUILT LOUD**

*Parts Two and Three*

Dust, Dung, and the Gone Parents

The Township Years

by Eugine Micah

# PART TWO: DUST, DUNG, AND THE GONE PARENTS

## Chapter Seven: The School in the Bushes

Before there was a school, there was an interview, and I want you to appreciate the comedy of a barefoot five year old in rural Lugari being interviewed for admission to a mud building, as though there were a waiting list, as though they might say no thank you, we are looking for a different kind of small child.

My education began at Manyonyi P.A.G, the Pentecostal Assemblies of God nursery, where the alphabet and the gospel arrived in the same breath. From there I sat my interview, an exam to a child of that age, and I passed it, and I was admitted to Mahemas Primary School, which sat in the hills of Marakusi surrounded by a great wild bush, and I do mean wild. We walked to that school barefoot, every single day, along paths that cut through a bush that held every animal short of a lion. Snakes, of course, the constant background threat of snakes. Antelopes that broke from the grass and stopped your heart. Flocks of quelea birds that rose like smoke. Skunks and squirrels and weasels and minks that terrorized the village chickens and occasionally terrorized us. To walk to Mahemas was to commute through a small unsupervised safari, and we did it with no shoes, every morning, and thought nothing of it, because nobody had told us that childhood was supposed to be safe.

The school itself was made of mud. Every classroom, mud walls and a roof of cheap rusty iron sheets that sang when it rained so loudly the teacher had to shout. The one exception was the office, a single shared room that held the headmaster and the deputy and all the teachers together, and even that had a floor of rough worn cement that the cheapness had already started to surrender. And the windows. I have to tell you about the windows, because they tell you everything. They were not windows. They were holes. Big holes punched in the mud walls where windows would go in a richer school, no glass, no panes, no frames, just openings to the weather and the bush, and through those holes came the rain and the cold and the light and, on cleaning day, great plumes of red dust rolling out into the air like the building itself was exhaling.

We built our own school, in a sense, which is a thing I did not understand the dignity of until much later. There were community fencing days, when every pupil was sent home with an instruction to return the next morning carrying strong long tree branches, and those branches became droppers in the fence that kept the school’s boundary, and the leftover wood became either firewood for the kitchen or, in a detail that should tell you something about the economy of that place, canes. The same wood that fenced us also flogged us. And if you came to school on a fencing day without your branch, you faced the music, a punishment severe enough that nobody forgot their wood twice.

The cane was the central technology of my early education. I want to be calm and factual about this because it is easy to sensationalize and I would rather you simply understand it. A simple mistake, a wrong answer, a late arrival, a forgotten book, earned you at least three strokes, on a normal day, as a baseline, and the strokes landed on small feeble buttocks that had not done anything to deserve a working life of being beaten. Pupils quit school over it. I watched children disappear from the register because the daily flogging was more than a small body and a small spirit could carry. The practice has since been abolished in Kenya, and I am glad, and I carry no romantic nostalgia for the cane, only the memory of a particular fear that lived in my stomach every morning on the walk through the bush. And yet, here is the strange thing, I loved school. I loved it despite the cane, around the cane, underneath the cane. Because school had a thing in it that the cane could not touch.

It had books, and it had a woman who believed I should read them.

I was, in those early years, the top student. From class one through class four I sat at the front of the performance, the best in my stream, and a child who is good at the one thing the institution rewards gets noticed, and I was noticed by the deputy headmistress, a teacher of English and literature whose name was Madame Zipporah. The library at Mahemas was not a grand room, it was barely a room at all, it was essentially her office, the books shelved in the same small space where she worked. And she would call me in. She would let me into that office, into the books, and she would give me tea and bread, actual tea and actual bread, a luxury I rarely tasted, as though reading were a thing worth feeding a child to encourage. She praised me. She congratulated me. She liked me, openly, and a poor child who is openly liked by a teacher will follow that teacher into anything, and what she led me into was reading, and reading was the first door anyone ever held open for me that did not have a cane on the other side of it.

I have thought a great deal about Madame Zipporah, who probably has no idea that she lives in a book now, that a boy she fed bread to grew up to talk to the country for a living. She did a small thing, repeatedly, with no expectation of return. She let a poor bright boy into a room full of books and gave him tea and told him he was good. That is the entire intervention. And it bent the whole curve of my life, because it taught me, before I had words for it, that I was a reader, that words were mine, that the world held in those 8-4-4 storybooks and dog eared library copies belonged to me as much as to anyone. The cane taught me fear. Madame Zipporah taught me hunger, the good kind, the hunger to know, and between the two of them they made a boy who was terrified and curious in exactly the proportions that would later make a journalist. I was also a scout, from class one, marching and tying knots and learning the small disciplines, but it was the library office and the bread and the woman who believed in me that did the deep work. I have spent my life looking for more rooms like that one, and trying, when I can, to be the person who holds the door.

## Chapter Eight: Dung Day

Every Friday, the entire student body of Mahemas Primary School arrived carrying a polythene bag full of cow dung and a jerrican of water, and if that sentence made you wince, good, sit in the wince, because it was my normal and I want you to feel how normal it was.

Let me walk you through Dung Day properly, because it was an entire economy and an entire ritual and, looking back, an entire education in how the poor make do with what the earth gives them for free. The instruction was simple and non negotiable. Friday, bring dung. Fresh cow dung scooped from your home’s cattle or, if you had no cattle, from a neighbor’s, or from wherever you could find it on the long walk in, bagged in polythene, plus water in a jerrican. You carried this to school like homework, because it was homework, the homework of maintaining the very floor you sat on. At the gate or in the class there was an inspection, because of course there was, this was a school, and a school inspects, and a child who showed up with too little dung or no dung was a child with a problem. The bags and the jerricans were stacked at the back of the classroom, and so the room smelled of dung all week, the dung waiting for its Friday, and I want to be honest, it did not bother us. It did not bother the teachers. We were village children. The smell of cattle was the smell of wealth and survival and home. The city child who reads this and gags is welcome to gag. To us it smelled like Friday.

Then came the work, and the work was beautifully, unconsciously organized along the lines our whole society drew. The girls swept first, raising those great red plumes of dust that rolled out through the hole windows, and the boys moved the massive heavy wooden desks to the walls to clear the floor. Then the dung from all forty or so bags was dumped in the middle of the room and mixed with water into a thick paste, a science we all understood instinctively, too dry and it cracked, too wet and it would not set, just right and it spread like a dream. Water was sprinkled on the bare earth floor to soften it, and then the girls, bending low, used the flat palms of their bare hands to spread the dung paste across the ground in smooth even arcs, sealing the floor, and when it dried, and it dried fast in the highland air, it was a clean, smooth, hard, almost pleasant surface, dust free, ant resistant, the humble high technology of a people with no money for cement.

I have plastered floors with cow dung. I have done it with my own hands. I say that now in rooms where people would not believe a human being still lived that way within their lifetime, and I say it without shame, because there is no shame in it, there is only ingenuity, the ingenuity of turning the one thing your cattle produce in abundance into a building material, a floor sealant, a free renewable resource that a clever poor community figured out long before anyone wrote it in a book. Dung Day was not degradation. Dung Day was a civilization solving a problem with what it had. It only looks like degradation from the outside, to people who have never had to be that clever.

I want to linger on the genius of it for a moment longer, because there is a thread here that runs through my whole philosophy of life and I only found the words for it as an adult. A dung floor is a poor people’s technology, yes, but call it what it actually is: it is a free, renewable, locally sourced, self repairing building material that a community with no money invented out of the one resource its economy produced in endless supply. The cattle ate grass, which cost nothing, and produced dung, which cost nothing, and the dung sealed the floor, which would otherwise have cost cement that nobody had. The whole thing is a closed loop of cleverness, a circle of survival, and the children of that community were drafted every Friday into maintaining it with their own hands. We did not see it as remarkable. We saw it as chores. But I have sat in boardrooms since, listening to people congratulate themselves on sustainability, on circular economies, on doing more with less, and I have thought, quietly, my grandmother was doing that with her grandchildren’s hands every Friday before any of you had a word for it.

And it taught me maintenance, which is an unglamorous virtue almost no one celebrates. The floor did not get plastered once and stay perfect. It wore. It cracked. It needed doing again, every week, whether or not it looked dirty, because you maintain before the damage, not after, and a floor maintained every Friday never reaches the state of a floor neglected for a month. I learned, on my knees with dung on my palms, that the things that last are the things you tend on a schedule whether or not they are screaming for it, and I have applied that to my body, my work, my faith, my relationships, with wildly varying success, but I learned the principle there, in a mud room that smelled of cattle, long before I could have told you it was a principle at all.

one building the room and the other governing it. There was a tariff to misbehavior as fixed as any price list. Lateness, a stroke or several. A wrong answer, strokes. A forgotten book, strokes. Bad handwriting, and I will tell you a thumb shattering story about handwriting later, strokes. The cane was the enforcement mechanism of the whole institution, and a child quickly learned to do the arithmetic of risk, to calculate which infractions were worth which pain. And I learned, in the middle of all that fear, the single most valuable social skill of my life, which is that comedy is a kind of immunity. The class clown, the boy who could make the room laugh, who could make even the teacher’s mouth twitch against its will, held a status the cane could not fully reach. Laughter bought me a protection that good grades alone never could, because grades made the other children resent you a little, but a joke made them need you. I became funny in self defense, in a mud room that smelled of dung, under the constant threat of a stick, and the funniness has paid my rent ever since. I did not develop a sense of humor because life was good. I developed it because life was hard and laughter was the cheapest and most powerful tool available to a small frightened clever boy, and I picked it up the way you pick up any tool you cannot afford to be without.

## Chapter Nine: The Mathematics of Hunger

I learned mathematics late and badly, as you will hear, but there was one branch of it I mastered young, with no teacher and no cane, and that was the mathematics of hunger, the grim arithmetic of a stomach with nothing in it and a day that still has to be lived.

The base equation was simple. On a good day, we ate. On a good day there was ugali and vegetables, and on a very good day the vegetables had enough cooking oil to taste like something rather than just glisten like a promise. But the good days were not guaranteed, and the bad days were a fixed feature of the calendar, not a rare emergency. We went many days on a single meal. We went some days on none. When the season turned against the farm, when the drought came or the previous harvest had been poor and the bananas had not yet matured and the maize was not yet ready, the pot was simply empty, and an empty pot in a poor home is not a crisis that someone swoops in to solve, it is just Tuesday, it is just the way some weeks go.

So you learned to supplement. Mahemas, you will remember, sat in the bushes, and the bushes had fruit, and a hungry child becomes an expert forager faster than any survival course could teach him. We ate fruit from the bush on the way to school and on the way home, wild things whose names I knew then and have half forgotten now, and that wild fruit was, on the worst days, the meal. And there was the other technique, the one that costs a child something he does not know he is spending until years later. You went to a neighbor’s home, around the time you knew they would be eating, and you sat. You did not beg, that would be shameful, you simply sat, present, visible, a child in the room, and you watched them eat, and sometimes, if they were kind or could not bear it, they fed you, and sometimes they did not, and you went home with nothing but the memory of watching. I did that. I sat and watched people eat. I did not understand at the time that it was anything other than normal. It is only as a grown man, with food I can buy whenever I want it, that I have looked back at that small boy sitting in a neighbor’s house watching their ugali disappear into their mouths, and felt the full weight of what that was, and had to sit down for a moment.

Here is the story I tell when I want someone to understand exactly how poor we were, because it is precise and it is unforgettable. There came a day at school when there was an examination, one of the big ones, a serious regional exam, and to sit it you had to pay a fee. The fee was twenty five shillings. Twenty five shillings. I want you to find the smallest amount of money in your own life, the amount you would not bend down to pick up off the street, and I want you to know that there was a day when twenty five shillings stood between me and sitting an exam, and my family did not have it, and could not get it, because there was nothing in the house to sell that would fetch twenty five shillings, nothing, the math simply did not exist. And I would have sat out that exam, would have been turned away, except that a friend of mine, a boy named Hassan Omar, reached into his own pocket and paid my twenty five shillings so that I could sit. Hassan Omar. I will say his name in this book because I owe it to him to say it somewhere permanent. A child gave another child twenty five shillings and kept him in school, and did not know he was doing anything historic, and probably does not remember it, and I have never forgotten it and never will. The biggest debts of your life are often the smallest amounts, paid at the exact moment you could not pay them yourself.

Poverty also taught me about theft, and I will be honest about this because I promised you honesty. In an environment that hungry, the line around other people’s things gets thin. There was stealing, small stealing, the stealing of the desperate, and my brother and I were not saints about it. But here is the detail that breaks my heart and makes me laugh at the same time, the detail that tells you we were thieves only in theory, never in competence. I genuinely would not have known what to do with real money. If I had stolen a thousand shillings, a fortune, I would have been paralyzed, because my imagination did not extend that far, my entire concept of wealth was small. I would have gone to a shop and asked for a thousand shillings of mandazi, because mandazi was the most desirable thing my mind could conceive of, and I would have stood there with a mountain of fried dough and no idea what I had done. My brother had his own version, picking up small money and pretending he had found it on the road, announcing the lucky discovery so we could all eat. We were not criminals. We were children doing the mathematics of hunger with the only variables we had, and coming up, always, just short, and improvising the difference, and surviving, barely, on wild fruit and borrowed shillings and the kindness of a boy named Hassan who will never know he is the reason a sentence in this book exists.

## Chapter Ten: The Lion Runs

There is a particular sound a family makes when its center of gravity suddenly leaves, and I heard it as a boy when my father, the lion, the man who did not run from anything, ran, and took the floor of my whole world with him.

My father, among his thousand jobs, traded in cattle. He bought and sold livestock, which is an honorable hustle in our world and a dangerous one, because the trade runs on trust and the trade attracts thieves, and a man buying and selling cattle is always one bad purchase away from disaster. The disaster, when it came for my father, came in the shape of a single cow. He acquired a cow, and he sold it, in the normal course of his business, and the cow, it turned out, had been stolen, somewhere up the chain, by people he had nothing to do with. But that distinction does not matter to a mob. To the people hunting that cow, my father was the man who had handled it, and in our world, in those years, the punishment for cattle theft was not a courtroom. It was death, delivered by a furious crowd, and then a quiet burial at night, the whole thing erased before the sun came up.

And here is where I have to credit a timing that my father can only call God, because nothing else explains it. On the day the cow’s owners came hunting, murderous, my father had stepped away. He had gone to grab a snack, or to relieve himself at a nearby bush, some small ordinary human errand of a minute or two, and in that exact window the men arrived looking for him, and not finding him, and he, sensing something in the air, did not return to walk into their hands. A minute. His life turned on a minute spent doing the most forgettable thing a body does. He slipped away, came home, and understood very quickly that the danger was not over, that the police were now looking for him too, that the ground had opened under his entire life.

So the lion did the only thing left, which was to run, and he ran with a cunning I have to admire even now. He did not bolt openly. He planted maize, a lot of it, made the whole homestead look like a man planning a long settled season, a man going nowhere, and then, early one morning with everything looking normal, he vanished. To Nairobi. But the night before he went, he came to me, and this is the moment that lives in me, he had my brother and me pack our clothes into bags, every piece we owned, and he sent us to my grandfather’s place, my father’s father, and he told me, looking at me, that he was going away for a while and would be back in a few days, and that I should not tell anyone where he had gone. A few days. I was a child and I was not a fool, and something in me knew, even as he said it, that a few days was not the truth, that the bags and the planted maize and the look on his face added up to something much longer than a few days. It was. It was three years, maybe five. I lost track. He disappeared into Nairobi and out of my childhood, and the boy he left holding the bags became, overnight, the man of a house that had no man and barely had a roof.

That night the police came. They came to the home and, not finding my father, they arrested my aunt, Betty, because she was in possession of his motorbike, and possession was enough, and they took her and held her, and my uncle Willie had to go and retrieve her. And then the rumors began their work, which is its own kind of violence. The story went around the whole area, the village telephone working faster than any wire: Eugine’s father is a thief. And it got worse, because his associates, the other men in the cattle trade, were being hunted too, and most of them did not have my father’s minute of luck. They were caught and killed, one by one, and buried at night according to the grim custom, and my father, when the dust finally settled years later, was nearly the last man standing of his entire circle. One neighbor, the man people said had set everyone up, fingering each trader to the mob, survived a while longer and died during the COVID years, and my father still speaks of him with the particular flatness of a man remembering someone who got people killed.

For me, the boy, the cost was paid in the schoolyard. Children are the cruelest broadcasters of adult catastrophe, and at Mahemas, at break time, the news became a taunt. Eugine’s father is a thief. They will kill him too. Walk carefully, somebody might catch Eugine’s family on the road. I was told not to walk alone, that I might be attacked, beaten, killed for the sins of a father who had handled the wrong cow. We walked in groups, my brother and I, scared down to our bones, and my grades, which had been the one thing I was proud of, the top of the stream, began to fall, because a terrified child cannot concentrate, because the boy who had been first now spent his days bracing for an attack on a dirt road. The lion ran to save his life, and he was right to, and I have never blamed him for it. But when he ran, he left a hole, and the hole filled up with fear and rumor and hunger, and I fell into it, and it took me years to climb back out.

## Chapter Eleven: The Storm Runs, and the Phone Call

My mother ran before my father did, and her running and his running were two different escapes from two different terrors, and the fact that both of my parents had to flee for their survival before I was ten years old is, I think, the central wound of my childhood and the thing I have spent my adult life talking over so I do not have to sit in the silence of it.

I have to name the thing my mother was fleeing, plainly, because softening it would be a betrayal of her. There was violence in our home, and not the disciplinary kind, the other kind, the kind done to a wife by a husband, and in those years and in that place it was so normalized that to hear a man beating his wife was an ordinary evening sound, a thing the community absorbed without alarm. My mother absorbed it for a long time, the way women were expected to, until the day she had absorbed all she could, and then she did what the women in my family do when survival demands it. She ran. She left, the children and the home and all of it, and she went to Nairobi, a city she had never seen, did not know, could not picture. She got onto a bus with just enough fare to reach the city and nothing for food along the way, and the journey from Kakamega to Nairobi in those days, on those roads, took more than a full day, and she rode it hungry and frightened and alone toward a place that was only a name to her, asking at every stop what is this place, where are we now, exposed to every danger a young woman alone on a long road is exposed to, and she made it. She survived the road the way she had survived everything else, and in Nairobi she started again from absolute zero. Her first work was as a house girl. Then she found work with a florist, earning around seven thousand shillings a month, having started even lower, and on that she rebuilt, a tiny rented room, a thin mattress on the floor, a kerosene stove, two cooking pots, two plates. That was the whole of my mother’s life in the city. Two pots, two plates, a mattress on the floor, and a will that would not break.

And so for years, both parents gone, my brother and I were raised by the rotation of grandparents and the mercy and cruelty of the wider family, and I will tell you, the cruelty was real. To be the abandoned children, the spare boys whose parents had vanished, was to occupy the lowest rung of the extended household. Some of the aunts and uncles disciplined us in ways that were not discipline, that were closer to torture, beating us, forcing us to work while they ate, denying us food because we had nothing to contribute, talking about us behind our backs as the abandoned ones, the burdens. We were called names. Taka taka, trash, was one of them, said to our faces. I learned, in those years, exactly how a family can keep a child fed enough to survive and starved enough to never forget that he is a guest, a charge, a problem being managed rather than a child being loved. It marks you. It marked me. Part of the man who works without stopping, who built a name, who needs the whole country to know him, is the boy who was once called trash by his own blood and decided, somewhere deep and wordless, that he would make himself impossible to throw away.

And then there was the phone call. Of all the things in this book, this is the one that still takes the floor out from under me.

We charged our phones at a shop two or three kilometers away, because there was no power at home, and I was the errand boy, the one sent to fetch things, and one day I was sent to collect my grandmother Rosemary’s phone, a Nokia, a brick phone with buttons, from its charging at the shop. And on the long walk back, alone, holding this phone, I did a thing I was not supposed to do. I scrolled through the contacts. And I found one that said, in the simple way contacts were saved, Mum Eugene. My mother. And I called it. And she answered. And I had not heard my mother’s voice in something like three years.

I did not know how to talk to her. That is the part that undoes me. I had been gone from my mother so long that I had lost the word. I had spent those years calling every woman who fed me auntie, calling everyone something other than what they were, and now my actual mother was on the line and I did not know whether to call her mum, because the word had gone strange and far away, the relationship had thinned to the point where the most basic name for it would not come. I just cried. I cried and I asked her the questions a child should never have to ask. Why did you leave us. Why did you leave us to suffer. Did we do something wrong to you. And then I begged her. Mum, come and pick us. They are killing us. They are torturing us. We are suffering. Come and pick us from this place.

She came that December. But before she could, there was a smaller, stupider aftermath that tells you everything about the world I was in. When I brought the phone home, my grandmother saw that some of the credit had been used, and she checked the call list, and she found that I had called my mother, and she became furious, called me ungrateful, said we were using her money, her airtime, that we were wicked children who were not being mistreated at all. The fury over a few shillings of airtime, on top of everything, while a child wept into a phone for a mother he had forgotten how to name. That was the temperature of my childhood. But my mother had heard me. She had heard the boy who could not say mum begging her to come, and a storm, when it hears its child crying, will cross any distance to get to him. She came. And the coming is the next chapter, and it begins, of all places, with a white man in a car in the rain.

## Chapter Twelve: The White Grandfather in the Rain

It was raining the day my life changed direction, raining hard, the heavy highland rain that turns the red dust to red mud and sends everyone huddling under whatever shelter they can find, and my brother and I were at the shops at a place called Lugari Line.

Let me set Lugari Line for you, because it deserves it. It is a small shopping center built where the road makes a sharp turn, an elbow, an L, and the shops cluster at the corner of that L, and to a village child it was a center of commerce, a downtown, a place where things happened. We were there in the rain, two boys without shoes, when a car came along the road, and a car was already an event, but this car, everyone knew this car, because the man driving it was white. My grandfather, David Zarembka, the American, the Mzungu, was passing through Lugari Line in the rain, and he saw us, and he waved, and the car stopped.

We ran to it through the rain, my brother and I, and they rolled down a window, and there was my grandmother Gladys, and she looked at us, and she asked a question that I have never forgotten because it was the question that made me see myself. She asked, why don’t you have shoes. And it was only when she asked it, only when this woman in this car looked at our bare feet and named it, that the realization landed on me with the weight of the rain itself. We did not have shoes. I had not registered it as a lack until someone with shoes pointed at the absence of mine. That is how poverty works on a child. You do not know you are poor until someone whose life is different holds up a mirror, and suddenly you see your own bare feet in the rain and understand, for the first time, that they were supposed to have something on them.

They asked us what we wanted, and we said shoes, and they gave us two hundred shillings each, my brother and me, an enormous sum, a fortune handed through a car window in the rain. And then, being children, being foolish, being trained by a lifetime of having nothing to immediately surrender anything we got to an adult, we handed the money to our aunt, who took it and bought us each a pair of blue plastic sandals for two hundred shillings, the simple molded plastic kind, and oh, I worshipped those sandals. I want you to understand that those two hundred shilling plastic sandals were, to me, the finest footwear ever manufactured. They went everywhere. School, farm, road, church. You could wash them with a single splash of water and they were clean. They were indestructible and they were mine and a white grandfather in the rain had bought them, and I have owned shoes since that cost more than my family earned in a year and not one pair has ever meant what those blue plastic sandals meant.

That encounter cracked a door open. My grandparents told us to come to them in Lumakanda, and not long after, my mother, having heard my weeping on that stolen phone call, finally came for us, and she took us to Nairobi, to the city, for the first time. My parents were not in Nairobi proper but in Juja, in a place called Gachororo, and the journey there blew the doors off my small world. I had never seen a city. I had never, really, seen electricity, except at my grandfather David’s place in Lumakanda, because my grandfather Samson and my grandfather Philemona had none. So electricity, to me, was not a fact of modern life, it was a marvel that lived at exactly one address, and now suddenly here was a whole city blazing with it. I asked my mother a thousand questions on that road. What is that. Look at that building, how is it so tall. Look at that, what is that. I had never seen a skyscraper, never seen a real market where it seemed every single person was selling tomatoes, never watched movies the way the city watched them. My mother, who I expected to snap at our endless naive questions, did not. She was patient with us the whole way, did not insult us, just answered and watched her village boys discover the world, and she has told me since that something in her settled on that journey, a decision to take care of us no matter what, and I believe it, because I felt it.

My father, by then, had found his footing in the city too, through my mother’s help, and his work was as raw and hard as everything else in our story. He cut and carried stone for the construction of the Thika Superhighway, that great road slicing northeast out of Nairobi toward home, hauling rock into trucks for a first wage of around seven hundred shillings, and on that, between the two of them, my parents began to assemble a life. A mattress. Then a bed. Then a television, one of the old heavy ones with the deep back, the stomach TV we called it, a brick of a thing that to us was pure glamour. The culture shock of the city was total and I overcame it fast, because a poor village child is, if nothing else, adaptable, and I had been adapting my whole life. But that season in Juja was brief, a few months, and it ended with a decision that would set the next chapter of my life, because when the time came to return to school, my brother and I refused, flatly, to go back to Mahemas. We had tasted something. We wanted to stay in Lumakanda, with the white grandfather who had books and electricity and had once stopped his car in the rain to ask a barefoot boy a question that changed how he saw himself. And that refusal carried us into the township years, and into the library, and into the people who would build me on purpose. But that, as I keep saying, is the next part.

# PART THREE: THE TOWNSHIP YEARS

## Chapter Thirteen: We Are Not Going Back

There is a particular power in a child’s refusal, and I exercised it exactly once with full force, and it redirected my entire life. When the season in the city ended and the question of school came up, my brother and I planted our small feet and said the thing that children are almost never allowed to say to their parents. We are not going back. Not to Mahemas. Not to the bushes and the cane and the hunger. We want to stay in Lumakanda, with grandmother and grandfather.

It was, looking back, an outrageous demand. We were proposing to leave our home village entirely, to abandon the school we were enrolled in without so much as a clearance form, to relocate ourselves to the township and into the household of David and Gladys. But my mother, perhaps because she had heard me weeping on that phone, perhaps because she understood better than anyone what we were fleeing, did not fight it. She came back and she spoke to my grandmother Gladys, and Gladys agreed to take us, and just like that, with the speed that big decisions sometimes have once the small resistances fall away, we were township children. We did not return to clear our names at Mahemas. We simply did not go back. We enrolled, fresh, no paperwork, no goodbyes, into a new and better school, and a chapter of my life closed without ceremony, the way most chapters actually close, not with a bang but with a child simply not walking down the old road anymore.

The school we walked into was Lumakanda Township Primary, and it was, by the standards of our world, prestigious, one of the good ones, and it carried a weight for my family that made my enrolling there feel like a homecoming to a place I had never been. Because my mother had gone there. My mother, Beverlyne, was an alumna of Lumakanda Township, and so were her siblings, my uncles and aunts on the Kemoli side, the whole clan of them had passed through those classrooms decades before, and when my mother returned to enroll her sons, she found that some of the teachers who had taught her were still there, still teaching, still in the same rooms, an unbroken thread running from her childhood into mine. There is something in that I find almost sacred, the idea of walking your son into the same school that shaped you, into the presence of your own old teachers, a poor woman who had to give up her own further education depositing her children into the institution that had once held her own hopes. I was, without knowing it, being folded into a lineage of learning, the Maragoli devotion to school made literal, generation handing to generation across the same worn desks.

I should describe what Lumakanda actually was to me, because to a Manyonyi boy it was practically a metropolis. Lumakanda was a township, the administrative heart of the old Lugari district, the place that had once been the capital of a whole district before the maps were redrawn, and it had things my home village did not. It had a tarmac feel to it, a busyness, shops and a stage where matatus and buses pulled in, and crucially, in the homes of people like my grandfather David, it had electricity, that marvel that had lived at exactly one address in my childhood and now surrounded me. To move from Manyonyi to Lumakanda was to move up a rung on a ladder I had not even known I was standing on, from deep village to township, from the bushes to the town, and a boy notices that climb in his body, in the way he holds himself, in the new and slightly intoxicating sense that he is now a person from somewhere that matters a little more than where he was from yesterday.

There was a thing about the school that I have come to treasure more with age, which is the continuity of it, the way it stitched me into my mother’s own story. When you enroll in the same school your mother attended, and your aunts and uncles attended, and you sit in rooms where teachers still remember the girl your mother used to be, you stop being a single isolated child and become instead the latest entry in a ledger, a name added to a long list of your own people who passed through these doors hoping the same hope. My mother had sat where I now sat. She had walked these grounds as a girl with her own dreams intact, before the pregnancy and the closed door, and now she was walking her son into the same place, depositing her thwarted ambition into me like a relay baton passed across twenty years. I did not feel the weight of that at the time. I just felt like a new boy at a good school. But I feel it now, and it moves me, the image of my mother enrolling me into the institution that had once held her own future, betting that it would do for her son what circumstance had not let it finish doing for her.

At Mahemas I had been a barefoot boy in a mud building in the bushes, top of the class but flogged for sport, foraging fruit on the walk in. At Lumakanda Township I was a township pupil in a real school, living, at least for stretches, in a home with electricity and books and an American grandfather. I had crossed a border. Not a geographic one, the distance is not large, but a border between two versions of a childhood, and I crossed it because two boys had the audacity to refuse the old road, and a tired mother and a strict grandmother let them. Refusal, used once, at the right moment, with your whole chest, can move your life onto a different track. I did not know that yet as a principle. I only knew that I was not going back to the bushes, and I was right, and the township years were where I began, slowly, to be assembled into someone.

## Chapter Fourteen: The Library That Did Not Judge My Grammar

If you want to know why a poor boy from Lugari fell in love with books and schools and the whole world of the mind, you have to understand my grandfather David’s library, and you have to understand the single most important thing about how he ran it, which is that in that room, being wrong did not hurt.

My grandfather David Zarembka had an office in Lumakanda, and the office had a library, shelves of books, and to a child who had been raised on the cane and the cattle and the cow dung floor, that room was a portal to entire universes. I read everything in it. Historical books, fantasy, anything I could reach. I read Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie, and her novel Half of a Yellow Sun became, and remains, the best book I have ever read, a thing so beautiful and so devastating that it rearranged something in me about what words could do. I read Trevor Noah’s account of his own impossible childhood and laughed and ached in recognition. I read my grandfather’s own books, his accounts of peace work, of a life given to the belief that ordinary people could refuse violence. The room had no bottom. I just kept reading my way down into it.

But the books were only half of it. The other half was the man, and the way the man received a reading boy. At school, wrong meant pain. You read a word incorrectly, you mangled your grammar, you got the answer wrong, and the cane came down, and so reading aloud at school was an act performed under threat, every error a potential bruise. At my grandfather’s house it was the exact opposite, and the opposite changed my life. I would read to him, or talk to him, in my broken mangled child’s grammar, the English of a village boy still finding the language, and he never once corrected me in the way that stung. He listened. He let me be wrong on the way to being right. In his presence, wrong meant learning, wrong meant you were trying, wrong was simply a stage on the road and not a crime to be punished. Do you understand what that does to a child? It removes the fear from the front of the brain, and a brain without fear in front of it can finally do its real work. I learned more in that fearless room, reading badly to a patient old American, than I ever learned under any cane, because the cane teaches you to avoid mistakes and the patience teaches you to make them bravely on your way to mastery, and mastery is made entirely of brave mistakes.

There is a reason a child raised on no screens becomes a reader, and I lived the mechanism of it. We were not children who could melt into a phone or a tablet, because there were no phones or tablets, and the television was rationed to almost nothing by my grandmother. So the hours that a modern child pours into a glowing screen, I poured into pages, because pages were the entertainment that was not forbidden, not rationed, not guarded. Reading was the one indulgence I could binge without anyone twisting my ear, and so I binged it. I read my way through that library the way other children worked through cartoons, compulsively, hungrily, one book bleeding into the next, and somewhere in that compulsion the love stopped being a substitute for the screen and became the real thing, the central pleasure, the appetite that would feed a career.

And I want to be precise about what David’s belief actually felt like, because belief is a word people throw around and it usually means nothing. David Zarembka’s belief in me was not a speech. He never sat me down and told me I was destined for greatness, that is not how the man operated. His belief was structural, practical, daily. It was the door of the library left open. It was the patience when I read badly. It was, later and most decisively, the school fees, which I will tell you about in their place, the money that materialized when there was no other money, against the wishes of people who had given up on my parents, because one old man had decided that this particular poor abandoned boy was worth educating. Belief, from David, was not words. It was a standing offer of resources and patience and an open door, renewed every single day without fuss, and that, I have learned, is the only kind of belief that actually builds a person. The kind made of speeches evaporates. The kind made of open doors and paid fees and unflinching patience builds a man who can stand on national television twenty years later and still feel, behind his own ribs, the warmth of a library where being wrong did not hurt.

but he had the means to act on the belief at a scale she never could, and I would learn, much later and to my astonishment, that his love of schools was not abstract. The man built them. He gave his life to learning and to peace in this region in ways I only fully understood as I grew, and the fact that I have loved a classroom, loved a library, loved the act of learning my entire life, traces directly back to a white American Quaker who handed a poor black boy a book and then refused to flog him for reading it imperfectly. He believed in me when there was very little evidence on which to base a belief. He looked at a frightened, abandoned, hungry child mangling English in his office and he saw something worth feeding with books, and he fed it, and I am, in large part, what that feeding grew. I cannot tell you yet how his story ends, because the ending is a grief I am keeping for its proper place near the close of this book. For now, hold him as I hold him in my happiest memories: in his library, listening to me read badly, never flinching, the one adult in my childhood who made being wrong feel like the beginning of something instead of the end.

## Chapter Fifteen: Love in Work Clothes

My grandmother Gladys loved me, and for years I did not know it, because her love arrived disguised as rules so strict and discipline so firm that I mistook the whole thing for severity, and it took me into adulthood to read the disguise correctly and see the love that had been wearing it the entire time.

The household David and Gladys ran was, by the standards of my chaotic childhood, a place of order, and the order had laws. There was no watching of crime movies, no films full of guns, and we were not even to discuss violence in the house, because my grandfather was a peacemaker to his core and his home was to be a peaceful place, an embassy of nonviolence in a region that knew too much of the other thing. To boys, of course, this was torture, because violence is catnip to boys, the male hormones do their work, and the forbidden films were exactly the films we craved. So we became smugglers of cinema. We snuck off to friends’ houses to watch what we could not watch at home, or we paid ten shillings to watch a whole movie at one of the little movie shacks, gorging on the DJ Afro narrations and the action we were starved of, always with the delicious fear of getting caught.

And caught we got, by a figure who deserves her own small monument in this book, the house help, Nancy. Nancy ran the domestic order of that home like a sergeant, and she had a particular gift for catching us mid sin, materializing exactly when the forbidden movie was at its best, and she would seize us by the ears, literally by the ears, and march us home, lecturing the whole way, I do not want to see you watching this. And here is the part that shames me a little now, because we were arrogant boys who had not yet learned humility. We resented being disciplined by a house help. To our foolish young minds there was a hierarchy, and a house girl was not supposed to be at the top of it, and we would protest, who is she to punish us, as though Nancy were not, in every functional sense, one of the adults raising us, as though her authority were somehow less real than blood. I was wrong about that, completely, and I know it now, and if Nancy ever reads this I want her to know that the boy whose ears she twisted grew up to understand that she was right and he was an idiot, and that pulling a foolish boy out of trouble by the ear is itself a form of love, even when it is also a job.

Let me give you the texture of the rules, because in the abstract they sound merely strict and in the living they were a whole climate. The house ran on time and on order and on a moral seriousness that radiated from my grandfather’s Quaker faith and my grandmother’s iron administration of it. Meals happened when they happened. Prayers happened. Chores were not optional and were inspected. Idleness was suspicious. A boy in that house was always, in some low background way, accountable, and accountability is exhausting to a child who has known the wild ungoverned freedom of a village where the parents are gone and no one is really watching. I had come from a world of too little supervision into a world of a great deal of it, and the adjustment chafed, and I rebelled in the small ways available to me, the sneaking, the forbidden films, the resentment of Nancy, the constant low friction of a wild thing being domesticated.

But here is what I could not see then and see clearly now. A child raised with no rules at all is not free, he is abandoned, and I had tasted that abandonment in the years my parents were gone, and it had nearly drowned me. Gladys’s rules, suffocating as they felt, were the opposite of abandonment. They were attention. They were a grown woman deciding that my behavior mattered enough to govern, that I was worth the enormous daily labor of correction, and labor it was, because raising someone else’s children, year after year, with no help and no end in sight, is one of the heaviest unpaid jobs a human being can take on. She did it until she could not. There came a day, and I will tell it honestly when we reach it, when Gladys reached the end of her strength and put us out, called us names born of pure exhaustion, and sent us down the road to Kemoview. I did not understand it then as anything but rejection. I understand it now as the breaking point of a woman who had carried too much for too long with too little thanks. The love had been real. So had the exhaustion. Both things were true, the way both things are usually true, and the boy who felt only the rejection grew into a man who can finally see the years of unthanked labor underneath it.

She rationed it severely. We were not children who melted into a screen for hours. And because the easy entertainment was withheld, because the screen was rationed, we turned to the thing that was not rationed, the thing that filled the gap, which was reading. I have come to believe that my entire literary appetite, my love of books that fed straight into a career built on words, was a side effect of my grandmother refusing to let me rot in front of a television. She did not hand me a love of reading. She withheld the alternative until reading was what remained, and reading turned out to be the better drug, and I got hooked on it for life, and the hooking was her doing whether she intended it or not. That is what I mean by love in work clothes. The rules looked like control. They were construction. She was building a person, brick by brick, rule by rule, in a household where being a child meant being shaped rather than simply being indulged, and the discipline that felt like coldness at the time reveals itself now, from the distance of years, as the most patient kind of care. She would, in time, grow tired of the burden of raising her absent daughter’s sons and she would reach her limit and there would be a hard rupture, and I will tell you about that, because it is true and I do not hide the true things. But underneath even the rupture was a woman who had spent years building me with rules because rules were the tools she had, and a person built well, even harshly, is still a person who got built. Not every child is so lucky as to be flogged into shape by people who, it turns out, were trying to save him.

## Chapter Sixteen: Kemoview

My grandfather Samson named his home after himself, Kemoview, and the more I have lived, the more I have come to think that naming a poor man’s patch of red earth like an English estate is one of the most quietly magnificent acts of defiance I have ever encountered, and he did it without irony, with total seriousness, because the man understood something about claiming your ground that I would spend my whole career relearning.

Samson Kemoli was, before he was my grandfather, a servant of the country. He had been a police officer, a corporal, who served Kenya for years that I never fully mapped, until an injury to his leg, the result of an accident, ended his service and left him with a limp he carried for the rest of his life and a story he carried with even more care.
