We were mocked for it, called names, taunted as the family that produced only boys and therefore had to make its boys do the work of girls. And I have come to be quietly grateful for the mockery, because it made me a man who knows how to run a home, who is not helpless in a kitchen, who never learned the lie that certain work is beneath him. Poverty did that. Poverty does not respect your traditions about who does what. Poverty hands the nearest pair of hands the nearest task, and the nearest hands were ours, and we cooked and cleaned and grew up more whole for it than the boys who were waited on.

We were living, as we often did, in the kitchen at our grandparents’ place, because the kitchen was where displaced children slept, and the kitchen had a particular torture built into it, which was the smoke. They cooked with firewood, and when someone lit the morning fire the smoke would fill the room and you would wake up coughing, eyes streaming, driven out into the cold dawn whether you were ready or not. So we were already irritable, already raw, the morning that Dante pushed me one notch too far. I do not even remember the offense. I remember the response, which was that I picked up a belt and hit my brother in the head with the buckle end, and it cracked his skin, and blood came, real blood, pouring from my little brother’s head, and the bottom dropped out of my stomach because I was sure I had done something that could not be undone. He survived, obviously. The scalp bleeds dramatically over nothing. But for a few seconds I had stood there watching my brother bleed because of me, and I learned something in those seconds about the weight of my own hands that I have tried, not always successfully, to remember.

That is the violence of it, and I give it to you honestly because our love was not a soft thing, it was forged in genuine combat. But the combat was never the main event. The main event was that we were a team, a crew of two, against a world that kept trying to separate us from the people who were supposed to protect us. When our parents left, it was Dante and me. When an aunt or an uncle decided we were the family’s spare children and treated us accordingly, it was Dante and me who absorbed it together. I had to parent him, often, in the absence of anyone else willing to do it, had to play the father whenever he wandered off the path, had to ridicule and correct and threaten and provide, because that is the job of the firstborn in a family where the first line of adults has thinned out. I did it badly and I did it constantly and I would do it again.

And he was my audience. This is the part that matters for the rest of the book. Long before there was a camera or a stage or a single stranger who cared what I had to say, there was Dante, sitting there, my captive crowd of one, while I narrated movies we had watched, or invented stories, or reenacted a wrestling match we had seen on the neighbor’s television with all the exaggerations and outright lies that made it better than the original. In our world you watched something and then you performed it for everyone who had not, embellished beyond recognition, and the better your performance the higher your standing, and I performed for my brother first. He was the proving ground. If I could hold Dante, restless stubborn Dante who knew all my tricks, I could hold anyone. The skill that would one day pay my rent was rehearsed, for free, on a little boy with a cracked scalp and a barbed wire grudge, in a smoky kitchen, in the years our parents were gone.

I will leave you with the funniest thing we ever pulled, because this chapter has had enough blood in it. When our grandparents brought us things from their travels, real treasure, shoes and clothes from America, we had to divide the spoils between us, and the dividing was a negotiation worthy of a peace summit. There was a pair of shoes Dante wanted that I also wanted, and the boy, even then a born storyteller like his brother, constructed a piece of pure fiction to claim them. He told the younger children, and tried to tell me, that these particular shoes were so powerful, so waterproof, so special, that if you jumped into a puddle while wearing them you could stop thunder. Stop lightning. That the shoes had power over the sky itself. I have thought about that often, my little brother inventing a superpower for a pair of secondhand American shoes in a village in Lugari, and I have realized that we were both, from the very beginning, the same kind of creature. We did not have much. So we made things up. We took the small true thing, a pair of shoes, a watched wrestling match, a hard life, and we built something larger and louder and more wonderful on top of it, because that was the only abundance available to us, the abundance of the story.

I have to dwell on the fact that we raised each other, because it is the truest description of my relationship with Dante and the heaviest. For long stretches of our childhood, the adults who were supposed to raise us were elsewhere, our father vanished into Nairobi and his troubles, our mother fled into the city and her survival, and what was left, in the smoky kitchens of relatives who housed us as charity, was two boys and the gap where the parents should have been. And somebody has to fill that gap, even if the somebody is only a year older and a child himself, and the somebody was me. I parented Dante. I do not say that to claim a halo, I say it as the plain fact of a firstborn in a family where the first line of adults has thinned out. When he wandered, I corrected him. When he needed feeding and there was something to feed him, I fed him. When he needed defending against the larger cruelties of the households we passed through, I defended him, and when he needed disciplining, God help me, I disciplined him, with the same crude tools that had been used on me, which is how a belt buckle once met his scalp. I was a child playing father to a child, badly, constantly, with no training and no relief, and it aged something in me early, installed a sense of responsibility for other people that I have never been able to switch off, that runs through my adult life as both my greatest strength and a low constant exhaustion.

The dividing of the spoils deserves more than the lightning shoes, because the whole economy of being secondhand children was its own education. When my grandparents returned from their travels abroad, from America, they brought goods, real treasure to us, clothing and shoes that had crossed an ocean, and these had to be divided between Dante and me and whatever other children had a claim, and the dividing was a negotiation of high stakes and low resources. There were the famous shoes, of course, the ones Dante claimed could stop lightning if you jumped in a puddle wearing them, a piece of marketing genius from a boy who had nothing to market but a story. But there were also the clothes, and I remember specifically the jorts, the cut off denim shorts, and the T-shirts that said things like I love my grandma and I love my grandpa, slogans printed for American children that ended up on the backs of two boys in Lugari who did, as it happened, love their grandma and grandpa, so the shirts were accidentally true. We wore those things with enormous pride, the foreign clothes that marked us, for a moment, as boys connected to somewhere far away, somewhere with abundance, and I have thought since about how a single donated T-shirt can lift a poor child’s whole sense of himself for a season, how the material of dignity is sometimes that thin and that cheap and that powerful.

And through all of it, the parenting and the fighting and the dividing of foreign shirts, the thing that held us was that we were a unit, a two boy republic, and the republic’s national pastime was the story. We narrated to each other constantly, the movies, the wrestling, the events of our days inflated into legend, and in doing so we kept each other entertained and we kept each other sane and we kept, between us, a flame of imagination alive that the hardness of our circumstances kept trying to put out. Two boys with almost nothing, manufacturing abundance out of words, performing wonder for an audience of one another. That is the workshop where I was made. Not a school, not a stage, a smoky borrowed kitchen with my brother in it, the two of us refusing to be as poor in spirit as we were in fact.

I want to give you one more image of the two of us, because it holds the whole truth of brotherhood under hardship. The smoky kitchen mornings, when the firewood was lit and the room filled with the choking smoke that drove us out coughing into the cold dawn before we were ready, were miserable, and they were also, strangely, ours, a shared misery that bonded us more than any comfort could have. We woke in that smoke together, two boys in the place displaced children sleep, and we faced the long day together, and at night we returned to it together, and there is a closeness forged in shared hardship that children raised in comfort never quite get, the closeness of two people who survived the same thing side by side. We fought, viciously, over balls and shoes and pride, I gave him a scar and he gave me one, but the fighting was never the foundation, it was the weather on top of the foundation, and the foundation was that when everything else fell away, the parents gone, the relatives unkind, the food short, there was still Dante, there was still me, there was still the two boy republic that no adult absence could dissolve. He was my first audience and my first responsibility and my first fellow soldier, and the bond we made in that smoke has outlasted every hardship that tried to break it.

I went on to do it on television to seven hundred thousand people. Dante did it first, to a handful of village kids, about a pair of shoes that could stop lightning. We are our father’s sons and our mother’s, yes. But before we were anyone else’s, we were each other’s first audience, and I have never once forgotten where I learned to perform. I learned it for him, in the smoke, on the dust, with a belt in one hand and a story in the other.

*A note before Part Two: This is where my world was still small and almost whole, held together by dust and family and the daily fight to eat. It does not stay whole. In the next part the adults leave, the floor of the house becomes an economy of cow dung, a school in the bushes teaches me to read and to bleed, and a phone call across a great distance breaks something in me that talking for a living has been trying to repair ever since. Stay with me. We are just getting started.*

BORN BROKE, BUILT LOUD

Eugine Micah

Table of Contents

**BORN BROKE, BUILT LOUD**

*Part Three*

The Township Years

# PART THREE: THE TOWNSHIP YEARS

## Chapter Thirteen: We Are Not Going Back

There is a particular power in a child’s refusal, and I exercised it exactly once with full force, and it redirected my entire life. When the season in the city ended and the question of school came up, my brother and I planted our small feet and said the thing that children are almost never allowed to say to their parents. We are not going back. Not to Mahemas. Not to the bushes and the cane and the hunger. We want to stay in Lumakanda, with grandmother and grandfather.

It was, looking back, an outrageous demand. We were proposing to leave our home village entirely, to abandon the school we were enrolled in without so much as a clearance form, to relocate ourselves to the township and into the household of David and Gladys. But my mother, perhaps because she had heard me weeping on that phone, perhaps because she understood better than anyone what we were fleeing, did not fight it. She came back and she spoke to my grandmother Gladys, and Gladys agreed to take us, and just like that, with the speed that big decisions sometimes have once the small resistances fall away, we were township children. We did not return to clear our names at Mahemas. We simply did not go back. We enrolled, fresh, no paperwork, no goodbyes, into a new and better school, and a chapter of my life closed without ceremony, the way most chapters actually close, not with a bang but with a child simply not walking down the old road anymore.

I have thought a great deal about that refusal, because it was the first time in my life I exercised genuine agency, the first time I was not simply a leaf on the floodwater of adult decisions but an actor who changed his own course, and the fact that it worked taught me something I have never forgotten. Children are usually powerless, and rightly so in many ways, too young to make the large decisions that shape their lives, and so they are carried along by the choices of adults, enrolled and uprooted and shuttled between homes according to forces they cannot influence. I had been carried like that for years, to Mahemas, to the relatives, to the city, back again, never once consulted, never once the author of my own location. And then, at the end of the city season, I refused, and the refusal held, and the adults bent to it, and a boy who had been powerless his whole life discovered that he was not, in fact, entirely powerless, that there were moments when a child’s clear fierce no could redirect the river. That discovery is dangerous and it is precious, and it has run through my whole life since, the conviction that even from a position of near-total powerlessness, a person can sometimes, at the right moment, with enough clarity and enough force, change his own fate. I have built a career out of refusing the place the world assigned me, and the first refusal, the original one, the template for all the rest, was two barefoot boys planting their feet and saying, we are not going back.

But I do not want to overstate my own power in it, because the refusal only worked because of the mercy of others, and that is the other half of the lesson. My no would have meant nothing if my mother had not chosen to honor it, if my grandmother Gladys had not agreed to take us in, if the adults who held the actual power had not, for their own reasons, decided to let the children’s refusal stand. Agency, I learned, is never purely your own. It is your will meeting the mercy or the interest of those with more power than you, and the trick of a life climbed from the bottom is learning to align your fierce refusals with the mercies available to you, to push exactly where the world is willing to give. I refused, and the world, through my mother and my grandmother, happened to be willing to bend at that exact point, and the meeting of my refusal and their mercy changed my life. I have been finding those meeting points ever since, the places where what I want and what the powerful are willing to grant briefly overlap, and pushing there with everything I have.

The school we walked into was Lumakanda Township Primary, and it was, by the standards of our world, prestigious, one of the good ones, and it carried a weight for my family that made my enrolling there feel like a homecoming to a place I had never been. Because my mother had gone there. My mother, Beverlyne, was an alumna of Lumakanda Township, and so were her siblings, my uncles and aunts on the Kemoli side, the whole clan of them had passed through those classrooms decades before, and when my mother returned to enroll her sons, she found that some of the teachers who had taught her were still there, still teaching, still in the same rooms, an unbroken thread running from her childhood into mine. There is something in that I find almost sacred, the idea of walking your son into the same school that shaped you, into the presence of your own old teachers, a poor woman who had to give up her own further education depositing her children into the institution that had once held her own hopes. I was, without knowing it, being folded into a lineage of learning, the Maragoli devotion to school made literal, generation handing to generation across the same worn desks.

I should describe what Lumakanda actually was to me, because to a Manyonyi boy it was practically a metropolis. Lumakanda was a township, the administrative heart of the old Lugari district, the place that had once been the capital of a whole district before the maps were redrawn, and it had things my home village did not. It had a tarmac feel to it, a busyness, shops and a stage where matatus and buses pulled in, and crucially, in the homes of people like my grandfather David, it had electricity, that marvel that had lived at exactly one address in my childhood and now surrounded me. To move from Manyonyi to Lumakanda was to move up a rung on a ladder I had not even known I was standing on, from deep village to township, from the bushes to the town, and a boy notices that climb in his body, in the way he holds himself, in the new and slightly intoxicating sense that he is now a person from somewhere that matters a little more than where he was from yesterday.

There was a daily texture to Lumakanda Township that I want to capture, because it was the backdrop of this whole stretch of my becoming. The school had more of everything than Mahemas, more children, more teachers, more structure, more sense of being a real institution rather than a few mud rooms in the bush. There were proper assemblies and a proper timetable and the whole apparatus of a school that took itself seriously, and being inside that apparatus, being a pupil at a school with a name and a reputation, did something to my sense of my own future, made it seem, for the first time, that a real future might be possible, that the path that ran through good schools toward better things might actually be open to a boy like me. At Mahemas the horizon had been the next meal and the next caning. At Lumakanda Township the horizon extended further, toward exams and high schools and the dim outline of a life beyond the village, and a child whose horizon extends begins to live differently, begins to work toward something rather than merely surviving the day. I started, at the township school, to have ambitions, vague and grand and a little ridiculous, the ambitions of a poor boy who has glimpsed that the world is larger than his hunger, and those ambitions, however unformed, were the engine that would eventually carry me out, and they were first lit in the classrooms of Lumakanda Township, where for the first time the future looked like a place I might actually be allowed to go.

There was a thing about the school that I have come to treasure more with age, which is the continuity of it, the way it stitched me into my mother’s own story. When you enroll in the same school your mother attended, and your aunts and uncles attended, and you sit in rooms where teachers still remember the girl your mother used to be, you stop being a single isolated child and become instead the latest entry in a ledger, a name added to a long list of your own people who passed through these doors hoping the same hope. My mother had sat where I now sat. She had walked these grounds as a girl with her own dreams intact, before the pregnancy and the closed door, and now she was walking her son into the same place, depositing her thwarted ambition into me like a relay baton passed across twenty years. I did not feel the weight of that at the time. I just felt like a new boy at a good school. But I feel it now, and it moves me, the image of my mother enrolling me into the institution that had once held her own future, betting that it would do for her son what circumstance had not let it finish doing for her.

Let me take you inside those first days at Lumakanda Township, because the arrival was its own small drama. I came in as a transfer, a village boy from a bush school, into a township school with a reputation, and I arrived carrying the particular anxiety of the newcomer who does not yet know the unwritten rules, who does not know which children matter and which teachers to fear and where it is safe to sit. A new school is a new country, with its own language and its own borders and its own hierarchies that everyone but you already understands, and I had to learn it fast, the way I had learned to read every new household, scanning for the dangers and the openings. But I had two advantages that the frightened new boy did not at first know he had. The first was that I was not actually behind. I had been top of my stream at Mahemas, and though Mahemas was a poor bush school, the foundation it had given me, the relentless rote and the cane-driven drilling and the hours of reading by lamplight, turned out to be solid, and when the work began I found I could do it, could keep up and then pull ahead, and there is no faster way for a new boy to find his footing than to be quietly, undeniably good at the actual work. The second advantage was the one I had been sharpening my whole life, which was that I was funny, and a funny new boy converts suspicion into welcome faster than any other kind, because the children who might have frozen me out instead found themselves laughing, and a child who makes you laugh becomes, very quickly, a child you want around.

So I made my place, the way I have made my place in every room since, with competence wrapped in comedy, being good enough at the work to earn the teachers’ respect and funny enough in the yard to earn the children’s, threading the narrow path between the resentment that excellence breeds and the dismissal that clowning invites. I have done versions of that walk into a new room my whole life, into Murgusi, into Thika, into the newsroom, onto the national screen, and I learned the choreography of it first at Lumakanda Township, a village transfer turning himself, within weeks, into a fixture, a known boy, a boy with a place. There is a confidence that comes from having done a hard thing before, and every time I have walked nervous into a new and intimidating room as an adult, some part of me has remembered that I was once a barefoot transfer who walked into a township school full of strangers and made it his within a term, and if that boy could do it, this man can do this.

The daily life of a township pupil was richer and stranger than anything Mahemas had offered. There was, for one thing, the town itself, the shops and the stage and the movement of people and vehicles, the sense of being at a small crossroads of the world rather than out in the deep bush, and a boy absorbs his environment, and I absorbed the township’s slightly faster, slightly worldlier rhythm into my own sense of myself. There were more children, from more kinds of families, some of them from homes with money, with proper uniforms and shoes that fit and pocket money for the kiosk, and being among them sharpened both my awareness of my own poverty and my determination to not let the poverty define my standing, because in the yard and in the classroom what counted was not your shoes but your wit and your marks, and those I had in abundance even when the shoes were borrowed. I learned, at Lumakanda Township, that poverty could be outrun, at least within the small society of a school, by the things that poverty could not touch, the quickness of your mind and the sharpness of your tongue, and that lesson became the whole strategy of my life, the conviction that a poor boy could win on the fields where money did not play, the field of intelligence, the field of performance, the field of sheer relentless effort.

And there was a recovery happening in me, quiet and crucial, that I only understood later. At Mahemas, in the years of my parents’ flight, the boy who had been first had cratered, his grades falling under the weight of fear and hunger and the schoolyard taunts about his fugitive father. That boy had learned to think of himself as someone whose promise had been broken, whose early excellence had proven fragile. Lumakanda Township gave that boy a chance to be first again, to rebuild the identity that catastrophe had knocked down, to remember that he was, underneath the abandonment and the shame, a genuinely capable mind. The township years were where I climbed back up from the academic hole my father’s flight had dropped me into, where I stopped being the thief’s son with the falling grades and became, again, the bright boy at the front of the class, and that recovery of a broken self-image is one of the most important things that ever happened to me, because a child who has been first, lost it, and clawed his way back to first knows something a child who was simply always first can never know, which is that you can fall and rise again, that a low season is not a life sentence, that the boy you were knocked down from being is still in there waiting to be rebuilt. I learned resilience not as a word but as a lived experience, the actual felt sequence of falling and climbing back, and I have drawn on that memory in every low season since, the knowledge, earned at Lumakanda Township, that I have come back from worse than this before.

And I want to mark the size of the change, because it was total. At Mahemas I had been a barefoot boy in a mud building in the bushes, top of the class but flogged for sport, foraging fruit on the walk in. At Lumakanda Township I was a township pupil in a real school, living, at least for stretches, in a home with electricity and books and an American grandfather. I had crossed a border. Not a geographic one, the distance is not large, but a border between two versions of a childhood, and I crossed it because two boys had the audacity to refuse the old road, and a tired mother and a strict grandmother let them. Refusal, used once, at the right moment, with your whole chest, can move your life onto a different track. I did not know that yet as a principle. I only knew that I was not going back to the bushes, and I was right, and the township years were where I began, slowly, to be assembled into someone.

## Chapter Fourteen: The Library That Did Not Judge My Grammar

If you want to know why a poor boy from Lugari fell in love with books and schools and the whole world of the mind, you have to understand my grandfather David’s library, and you have to understand the single most important thing about how he ran it, which is that in that room, being wrong did not hurt.

My grandfather David Zarembka had an office in Lumakanda, and the office had a library, shelves of books, and to a child who had been raised on the cane and the cattle and the cow dung floor, that room was a portal to entire universes. I read everything in it. Historical books, fantasy, anything I could reach. I read Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie, and her novel Half of a Yellow Sun became, and remains, the best book I have ever read, a thing so beautiful and so devastating that it rearranged something in me about what words could do. I read Trevor Noah’s account of his own impossible childhood and laughed and ached in recognition. I read my grandfather’s own books, his accounts of peace work, of a life given to the belief that ordinary people could refuse violence. The room had no bottom. I just kept reading my way down into it.

But the books were only half of it. The other half was the man, and the way the man received a reading boy. At school, wrong meant pain. You read a word incorrectly, you mangled your grammar, you got the answer wrong, and the cane came down, and so reading aloud at school was an act performed under threat, every error a potential bruise. At my grandfather’s house it was the exact opposite, and the opposite changed my life. I would read to him, or talk to him, in my broken mangled child’s grammar, the English of a village boy still finding the language, and he never once corrected me in the way that stung. He listened. He let me be wrong on the way to being right. In his presence, wrong meant learning, wrong meant you were trying, wrong was simply a stage on the road and not a crime to be punished. Do you understand what that does to a child? It removes the fear from the front of the brain, and a brain without fear in front of it can finally do its real work. I learned more in that fearless room, reading badly to a patient old American, than I ever learned under any cane, because the cane teaches you to avoid mistakes and the patience teaches you to make them bravely on your way to mastery, and mastery is made entirely of brave mistakes.

I want to give you the scene of it, the boy and the old American in the library, because it is one of the warmest images of my whole childhood and I return to it often. I would sit with my grandfather, and I would read, or we would talk, and the talk ranged everywhere, because David Zarembka was a man who had lived across the world and thought about large things, and he took a poor Kenyan boy’s questions seriously, answered them fully, treated my curiosity as worthy of real engagement rather than the half-attention adults usually give children. I would ask him about America, about the wider world, about the books I was reading, about the peace work he did, about anything, and he would answer, and in the answering he did the thing that changed me, which was to treat my mind as real, as worth addressing, as capable of understanding serious things. Nobody had ever done that before. The adults of my childhood had mostly issued instructions and corrections, the cane and the chore and the rule. David conversed with me, as though I were a person whose thoughts mattered, and being conversed with like that, being taken seriously as a thinking being, did more to build my actual intelligence than any lesson, because a mind grows toward the expectations placed on it, and David expected mine to be capable, so it became capable.

The reading aloud was its own ritual, and I have to return to the gift of it, the not-correcting, because I cannot overstate what it did. I read badly. My English was the English of a village boy still acquiring the language, full of errors, mispronunciations, mangled constructions, and at school every one of those errors was a potential bruise, reading aloud a minefield of opportunities to be flogged. With David, I read into a space of total safety, and the safety changed the reading itself, because a boy who is not afraid of being wrong reads more bravely, attempts harder words, takes more risks, and through the risk-taking improves faster than any fearful reader ever could. David’s patience was not passive, it was active, it was a teaching method more effective than any cane, because it removed the fear that the cane installs and freed the mind to do its natural work of trying and failing and trying again toward mastery. I learned to read well by being allowed to read badly without consequence, and there is a whole philosophy of education in that single fact, a philosophy I wish every teacher with a cane could understand, that you cannot beat competence into a child, you can only make space for it to grow, and the space is made of patience and safety, not fear and pain.

And the books gave me something beyond literacy, beyond even the love of reading, they gave me a self that was larger than my circumstances. A poor child’s life is small, bounded by the village and the want and the daily struggle, and without something to enlarge it, that smallness can become the whole of a person, can shrink the soul to the size of the deprivation. Books enlarged me. Every book I read in that library added a room to the house of my inner life, a place I could go that was not the red dust and the hunger, a wider world I carried inside me everywhere, so that even when my outer life was at its smallest and hardest, my inner life was vast, populated by everything I had read, every place and person and idea the books had given me. That inner vastness is, I think, the single greatest gift of a reading childhood, and it is the gift that David’s library gave me, the construction of an interior world large enough that the poverty of my exterior world could never fully define me. I was a poor boy with a rich mind, and the richness of the mind, built book by book in a Lumakanda library, was the wealth that no circumstance could repossess, the capital I would eventually convert into a life.

There is a reason a child raised on no screens becomes a reader, and I lived the mechanism of it. We were not children who could melt into a phone or a tablet, because there were no phones or tablets, and the television was rationed to almost nothing by my grandmother. So the hours that a modern child pours into a glowing screen, I poured into pages, because pages were the entertainment that was not forbidden, not rationed, not guarded. Reading was the one indulgence I could binge without anyone twisting my ear, and so I binged it. I read my way through that library the way other children worked through cartoons, compulsively, hungrily, one book bleeding into the next, and somewhere in that compulsion the love stopped being a substitute for the screen and became the real thing, the central pleasure, the appetite that would feed a career.

And I have to say a word about what it meant that the man who did all this was white, an American, a foreigner, because in the context of my country and its history it was not a simple thing. I grew up in a place with its own complicated memory of white people, of colonizers and missionaries and aid workers, of a relationship between black Africans and white outsiders that history had loaded with power and pain. And into that loaded context came my grandfather David, a white man who had not come to rule or to convert or to study us, but to marry into us, to live among us, to give his life to peace in our region and to the education of our children, to love a Kenyan woman and raise Kenyan grandchildren and be buried, eventually, in Kenyan soil. He complicated, by his simple existence, every easy story about white people and black people, because here was a white man who had made himself genuinely one of us, who had earned, through decades of real work and real love, a place in a Kenyan family that no colonial relationship could ever produce. I did not think about any of this as a child. He was simply grandfather, the man with the library and the patience. But I think about it now, about the strangeness and the grace of being built, in part, by a white American Quaker in rural Kenya, and about how his life was itself an argument against every ideology that says people are defined and divided by the color of their skin, an argument made not in words but in a life given to people who looked nothing like him, and I carry that argument, his living refutation of tribalism and racism both, as one more thing he handed me in that library.

And I want to be precise about what David’s belief actually felt like, because belief is a word people throw around and it usually means nothing. David Zarembka’s belief in me was not a speech. He never sat me down and told me I was destined for greatness, that is not how the man operated. His belief was structural, practical, daily. It was the door of the library left open. It was the patience when I read badly. It was, later and most decisively, the school fees, which I will tell you about in their place, the money that materialized when there was no other money, against the wishes of people who had given up on my parents, because one old man had decided that this particular poor abandoned boy was worth educating. Belief, from David, was not words. It was a standing offer of resources and patience and an open door, renewed every single day without fuss, and that, I have learned, is the only kind of belief that actually builds a person. The kind made of speeches evaporates. The kind made of open doors and paid fees and unflinching patience builds a man who can stand on national television twenty years later and still feel, behind his own ribs, the warmth of a library where being wrong did not hurt.

Let me tell you about the actual books, because they were not interchangeable, they were specific, and certain ones rearranged me. Half of a Yellow Sun, by Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie, I have already named as the best book I have ever read, and I need to tell you why a poor boy in Lugari was so undone by a novel about the Biafran war in Nigeria decades before he was born. It was the first time I saw African people rendered in literature as fully, completely, beautifully human, as complicated and intelligent and tragic and real as any characters in any book, not as objects of pity or anthropology but as subjects of a great and devastating story, in love and at war and grieving and surviving, on our own continent, in our own kind of skin. I had read books before, but they had mostly been about other worlds, other kinds of people, and somewhere underneath my reading had run the quiet assumption that serious literature, important stories, happened to others, elsewhere, to people unlike me. Half of a Yellow Sun detonated that assumption. Here was a story as important and as beautifully made as anything in the world, and it was ours, African, and that meant, by extension, that my story, the story of a boy in the red dust, might also be worth telling, might also be literature, might also matter. I think this book, more than any other single thing, planted the seed that has become the pages you are holding, the conviction that a life like mine is worth writing down, because Adichie showed me that lives like ours are worth the most serious kind of telling.

And Trevor Noah’s account of his own impossible childhood, the mixed boy born a crime under apartheid, did something different but just as deep, because it taught me that you could tell the story of a brutal childhood while laughing, that comedy and pain were not opposites but partners, that you could make a reader ache and laugh on the same page, sometimes in the same sentence. I had been using comedy my whole life as a survival tool, the class clown deflecting the cane, and here was a man who had turned that exact survival comedy into literature, into a way of telling hard truths that went down easier for the laughter wrapped around them. I recognized myself in him, the funny boy from the hard place using jokes as both shield and bridge, and I learned from him the register I would one day write and broadcast in, the funny-serious, the laughter that does not deny the pain but carries it, and if you have laughed and ached in the same breath anywhere in this book, you have Trevor Noah and a boy reading him in a Lumakanda library to thank for the technique. Beyond those two I read whatever the shelves held, historical accounts and fantasies and my grandfather’s own writings, and each book widened the world a little more, and the cumulative effect of all that reading was the slow construction of an inner life large enough to survive the smallness of my outer circumstances, a private universe of stories and ideas that no poverty could repossess.

I have to tell you more fully who my grandfather was, because the library was an expression of the man, and the man was extraordinary in a way I did not grasp as a child. David Zarembka was an American Quaker who had given his life to peace, and not peace as a sentiment but peace as hard, practical, dangerous work, the work of helping ordinary people in this region refuse the violence that periodically tore it apart. He had founded and run an organization devoted to peace-building across the African Great Lakes region, the area scarred by some of the most terrible violence the continent has known, and he had spent decades training people in the techniques of nonviolence, in the slow patient work of reconciliation, in the radical Quaker conviction that there is something of God in every person and that to do violence to a person is therefore a kind of blasphemy. When my country tore itself apart in the terrible season of post-election violence, when neighbors turned on neighbors along the very tribal lines I have described, my grandfather was among those doing the unglamorous, perilous work of helping communities step back from the slaughter, of training peacemakers, of insisting that the cycle could be broken. The library where I read badly without fear was the personal corner of a life given entirely to the proposition that learning and peace were the same project, that an educated mind and a nonviolent heart grew from the same soil, and that the way to heal a wounded region was, in part, to put books into the hands of its children and refuse to flog them for reading imperfectly.

And his love of schools, I would learn, was not metaphorical. The man built them, founded them, gave his energy and his resources to the establishing of places where children who would otherwise have had nothing could learn. To grow up loved by a man who built schools is to absorb, without being taught it in words, the conviction that a school is a sacred thing, that education is the holiest form of charity, that to open a door of learning for a child is among the best things a human being can do with a life. I have loved schools my whole life, loved classrooms and libraries and the entire apparatus of learning, and for years I thought this was simply my own temperament, until I learned that my grandfather had founded a school, had given that exact gift over and over, and I understood that my love of schools was inherited, was his love living on in me, the grandson he educated carrying forward the school-builder’s faith that learning is the way out and the way up and the way home. I did not just read in his library. I was apprenticed, without either of us naming it, to his entire philosophy, that the mind is worth feeding, that the wrong answer is the beginning of the right one, that peace and learning are one work, and that the highest use of whatever you have been given is to open doors for those who have been given less.

I want to set the two worlds side by side one more time, because the contrast is the whole point. By day I attended a school where being wrong meant the cane, where a mistake was a crime to be punished, where I learned to fear exposure and hide my errors and shrink from the front of the room. And in the evenings, in my grandfather’s library, I entered a world where being wrong meant learning, where a mistake was a stage on the road to mastery, where an old man listened to my mangled grammar without a flicker of judgment and let me find my way to the right word in my own time. I was being educated by both worlds simultaneously, the world of fear and the world of patience, and I want to tell you which one actually built me, because it matters, because there are still people who believe the cane is what makes a child learn. It was the patience. It was always the patience. Everything durable in me, the love of words, the courage to be wrong in public on the way to being right, the willingness to read badly until I read well, the whole foundation of a career built on language, came from the patient world of the library and not the fearful world of the cane. The cane taught me to hide. The library taught me to reach. And a life is built out of reaching, not hiding, which is why I credit a patient old American with my career far more than I credit any teacher who ever beat an answer into me.

David Zarembka believed in education the way my mother believed in it, but he had the means to act on the belief at a scale she never could, and I would learn, much later and to my astonishment, that his love of schools was not abstract. The man built them. He gave his life to learning and to peace in this region in ways I only fully understood as I grew, and the fact that I have loved a classroom, loved a library, loved the act of learning my entire life, traces directly back to a white American Quaker who handed a poor black boy a book and then refused to flog him for reading it imperfectly. He believed in me when there was very little evidence on which to base a belief. He looked at a frightened, abandoned, hungry child mangling English in his office and he saw something worth feeding with books, and he fed it, and I am, in large part, what that feeding grew. I cannot tell you yet how his story ends, because the ending is a grief I am keeping for its proper place near the close of this book. For now, hold him as I hold him in my happiest memories: in his library, listening to me read badly, never flinching, the one adult in my childhood who made being wrong feel like the beginning of something instead of the end.

## Chapter Fifteen: Love in Work Clothes

My grandmother Gladys loved me, and for years I did not know it, because her love arrived disguised as rules so strict and discipline so firm that I mistook the whole thing for severity, and it took me into adulthood to read the disguise correctly and see the love that had been wearing it the entire time.

The household David and Gladys ran was, by the standards of my chaotic childhood, a place of order, and the order had laws. There was no watching of crime movies, no films full of guns, and we were not even to discuss violence in the house, because my grandfather was a peacemaker to his core and his home was to be a peaceful place, an embassy of nonviolence in a region that knew too much of the other thing. To boys, of course, this was torture, because violence is catnip to boys, the male hormones do their work, and the forbidden films were exactly the films we craved. So we became smugglers of cinema. We snuck off to friends’ houses to watch what we could not watch at home, or we paid ten shillings to watch a whole movie at one of the little movie shacks, gorging on the DJ Afro narrations and the action we were starved of, always with the delicious fear of getting caught.

And caught we got, by a figure who deserves her own small monument in this book, the house help, Nancy. Nancy ran the domestic order of that home like a sergeant, and she had a particular gift for catching us mid sin, materializing exactly when the forbidden movie was at its best, and she would seize us by the ears, literally by the ears, and march us home, lecturing the whole way, I do not want to see you watching this. And here is the part that shames me a little now, because we were arrogant boys who had not yet learned humility. We resented being disciplined by a house help. To our foolish young minds there was a hierarchy, and a house girl was not supposed to be at the top of it, and we would protest, who is she to punish us, as though Nancy were not, in every functional sense, one of the adults raising us, as though her authority were somehow less real than blood. I was wrong about that, completely, and I know it now, and if Nancy ever reads this I want her to know that the boy whose ears she twisted grew up to understand that she was right and he was an idiot, and that pulling a foolish boy out of trouble by the ear is itself a form of love, even when it is also a job.

I want to balance the rules with the care, because a household is not only its laws, and Gladys’s home, for all its strictness, was also a place of provision and order that I had never known before and desperately needed. There was food, regularly, at set times, which to a boy who had lived the mathematics of hunger was itself a kind of miracle, the simple reliability of meals appearing on a schedule. There was cleanliness, a kept house, washed clothes, the basic dignities that poverty had so often denied us. There was the electricity, the light, the books. There was, underneath the iron administration, a woman making sure that the children in her care were fed and clothed and schooled and held to a standard, and a child reads all of that, even when he is chafing against the rules, as a form of being valued, of mattering enough to be maintained. I had been, in the years of abandonment, a child who was barely maintained, fed last and least, his upkeep a grudging afterthought. In Gladys’s house I was maintained properly, deliberately, to a standard, and even as I rebelled against the discipline that came with the maintenance, some deeper part of me was drinking in the experience of being properly cared for, of belonging to a household that took its responsibility for me seriously.

I remember the texture of the evenings especially, because they were so different from anything before. In the village, evening had meant the kerosene lamp and the dark and the early sleep of people with no light to stay up by. At Gladys and David’s, evening meant electric light, and reading, and the structured calm of a household winding down in order, prayers and the settling of the day, and there was a security in that nightly order that I had never felt, the security of a child in a home that runs on rails, where tomorrow will arrive on schedule and breakfast will be there and the day will unfold according to a known pattern. For a boy whose life had been pure chaos and uncertainty, the boredom of that order was secretly a balm, the rails were secretly a comfort, and though I would never have admitted it then, I think some wounded part of me relaxed for the first time in years inside the predictability of that disciplined home, finally able to be a child because the adults had taken charge of the world.

And I have one memory I hold above the others, a small one, because it shows the love underneath the work clothes. Gladys was not a demonstrative woman, not one for soft words or easy affection, her love came out as provision and correction rather than tenderness, and so the rare moments when the tenderness broke through the administration are precious to me out of all proportion to their size. There was a time I was sick, genuinely sick, and the iron administrator became, briefly, simply a grandmother tending a fevered boy, and the care in it, the attention, the way the strictness dissolved into something gentler when I actually needed gentleness, told me what had been true underneath all along, that the rules had always been a form of love, that the woman enforcing the standards was the same woman who would sit with a sick child, that the severity and the tenderness came from the same source. I clung to that memory in the years afterward, especially after the rupture, when I needed evidence that the love had been real, and that memory was the evidence, the grandmother who tended me when I was sick, proof that underneath the work clothes of her discipline there was a heart that did, in its own constrained and undemonstrative way, love the difficult boy she had taken in.

Let me give you the texture of the rules, because in the abstract they sound merely strict and in the living they were a whole climate. The house ran on time and on order and on a moral seriousness that radiated from my grandfather’s Quaker faith and my grandmother’s iron administration of it. Meals happened when they happened. Prayers happened. Chores were not optional and were inspected. Idleness was suspicious. A boy in that house was always, in some low background way, accountable, and accountability is exhausting to a child who has known the wild ungoverned freedom of a village where the parents are gone and no one is really watching. I had come from a world of too little supervision into a world of a great deal of it, and the adjustment chafed, and I rebelled in the small ways available to me, the sneaking, the forbidden films, the resentment of Nancy, the constant low friction of a wild thing being domesticated.

But here is what I could not see then and see clearly now. A child raised with no rules at all is not free, he is abandoned, and I had tasted that abandonment in the years my parents were gone, and it had nearly drowned me.

I want to put those two conditions side by side, because living through both taught me a thing about freedom that most people get backward. In the years of my parents’ absence I had something that looked like total freedom, no rules, no supervision, no one governing my days, and it was not freedom at all, it was neglect, it was a child left to raise himself, and it nearly destroyed me, the grades cratering, the fear, the hunger, the wildness of a boy with no one steering him. Then I came into Gladys’s house, into rules and structure and constant accountability, into what felt like the opposite of freedom, and it was there, paradoxically, inside the structure, that I actually began to flourish, to read and to learn and to recover and to grow into someone. The lesson is one our whole culture of self-expression and do-what-you-feel gets exactly wrong: structure is not the enemy of a child’s flourishing, it is the precondition of it, the rails that let the train actually go somewhere instead of derailing in a field. I needed Gladys’s rules far more than I needed the false freedom of my abandonment, and the proof is in what each produced, the neglect producing a drowning boy and the structure producing a boy who could finally swim. I have never since confused freedom with the absence of structure, because I have lived the absence of structure and it is not freedom, it is drowning, and the rules that felt like a cage were in fact the banks of the river that let the water actually flow somewhere. Gladys’s rules, suffocating as they felt, were the opposite of abandonment. They were attention. They were a grown woman deciding that my behavior mattered enough to govern, that I was worth the enormous daily labor of correction, and labor it was, because raising someone else’s children, year after year, with no help and no end in sight, is one of the heaviest unpaid jobs a human being can take on. She did it until she could not. There came a day, and I will tell it honestly when we reach it, when Gladys reached the end of her strength and put us out, called us names born of pure exhaustion, and sent us down the road to Kemoview. I did not understand it then as anything but rejection. I understand it now as the breaking point of a woman who had carried too much for too long with too little thanks. The love had been real. So had the exhaustion. Both things were true, the way both things are usually true, and the boy who felt only the rejection grew into a man who can finally see the years of unthanked labor underneath it.

I want to give you Gladys more fully, because she is one of the most important and most complicated people in this entire book, and a few rules and a house help twisting our ears do not capture her. She was a woman of enormous capability and enormous will, the kind of woman who runs a household and a marriage and a web of obligations with an iron competence that makes everything look effortless and is in fact the product of relentless invisible labor. She had married an American, a white Quaker, and built with him a life that bridged two worlds, the rural Kenyan and the international, and she moved between those worlds with a poise I did not appreciate as a child but marvel at now. She kept a home that was, by the standards of everything around it, orderly and clean and disciplined and provisioned, an island of structure in a region of chaos, and she did it through sheer force of administration, through rules and routines and the constant vigilant management of every person and resource under her authority. To a wild village boy fresh from the ungoverned freedom of abandonment, she was a force of nature, a woman whose attention missed nothing and whose standards bent for no one, and I both chafed against her and, I see now, desperately needed her, because a boy who has been let run wild by absence is a boy starving for exactly the structure that feels, from the inside, like oppression.

The rules were many and they were enforced, and the cinema smuggling was only the most dramatic of our rebellions against them. There was a whole code of conduct, about how you spoke and how you behaved and what you were permitted to consume, and underneath it all was my grandfather’s pacifism, the conviction that the home must be an embassy of peace, which meant that the violent films and the war games and the aggressive talk that boys naturally gravitate toward were all forbidden, all pushed outside the walls of the house. So we smuggled them in, or rather we went out to them, to the movie shacks with their DJ Afro narrations, those gloriously translated and editorialized action films where the local DJ talked over the movie in Kiswahili, explaining and embellishing and cracking jokes, ten shillings for a whole afternoon of the violence and excitement our home denied us, and we came home from those forbidden screenings vibrating with guilty pleasure, and sometimes Nancy caught us, the ears, the lecture, the march home, and sometimes we got away with it and felt like masters of the universe. The rebellion was small and the stakes were low, but it was real, the eternal push of the young against the structure of the old, and I look back on it now with a tenderness for both sides, the boys hungry for the forbidden excitement and the household trying to raise them into something gentler than the violence all around.

But I have to tell you the hard thing about Gladys too, the thing that complicates every warm memory, because I promised you I would not hide the true things and this is among the truest and most painful. The same Gladys who built me with her rules, who fed me and structured me and shaped me into someone, was, to my own mother, a different and harder figure. My mother had been given to Gladys as a child, adopted into her care, and the adoption had not been the warm folding-in that the word should mean. For long years, my mother was not treated as a true daughter, was withheld the support and the standing that a daughter should receive, while others in the family were favored, lifted, given the things my mother could only watch others receive. I did not understand this as a child, did not see the injustice running underneath the household, because to me Gladys was the grandmother who took me in and built me, and a child sees the version of a person that is turned toward him, not the version turned toward someone else. It was only as I grew, only as I came to understand my mother’s life and her quiet lifelong ache, that I had to reckon with the fact that the woman who was a builder to me had been, to my mother, something closer to a withholder, and that both of these things were true at once, the same woman, the same heart, generous in one direction and unjust in another. People are not single. The grandmother who saved me and the adoptive mother who failed my mother were the same person, and I have had to learn to hold that contradiction without letting either truth cancel the other, to be grateful for what she gave me while grieving what she denied my mother, and that holding of contradiction is one of the harder adult skills this family forced me to learn.

There came a day, too, and I will tell it in full in its proper place near the painful center of this book, when Gladys reached the end of her enormous strength and put us out, sent us down the road to Kemoview with words born of pure exhaustion, and the boy I was experienced it as a pure rejection, a casting-out, a confirmation that even here, even in the house that had taken me in, I was finally a burden to be discarded. I carried that wound for years. It was only much later that I could see it for what it was, not rejection but exhaustion, the breaking point of a woman who had carried other people’s children for too long with too little help and too little thanks, and who simply ran out, the way a person runs out, the way even the strong and the dutiful eventually run out. The love had been real, and so had the limit of it, and a child who is loved up to the limit of a tired woman’s strength and then set down when that strength fails has been both genuinely loved and genuinely failed, and learning to hold those two truths together, again, is the work this family handed me. Gladys built me and Gladys set me down, Gladys saved me and Gladys failed my mother, and she was one woman, and I loved her, and I have spent years learning to love her honestly, which means loving her whole, the gift and the wound together.

The masterstroke of Gladys’s rule, the one I curse and bless in equal measure, was the limit on television. She rationed it severely. We were not children who melted into a screen for hours. And because the easy entertainment was withheld, because the screen was rationed, we turned to the thing that was not rationed, the thing that filled the gap, which was reading. I have come to believe that my entire literary appetite, my love of books that fed straight into a career built on words, was a side effect of my grandmother refusing to let me rot in front of a television. She did not hand me a love of reading. She withheld the alternative until reading was what remained, and reading turned out to be the better drug, and I got hooked on it for life, and the hooking was her doing whether she intended it or not. That is what I mean by love in work clothes. The rules looked like control. They were construction. She was building a person, brick by brick, rule by rule, in a household where being a child meant being shaped rather than simply being indulged, and the discipline that felt like coldness at the time reveals itself now, from the distance of years, as the most patient kind of care. She would, in time, grow tired of the burden of raising her absent daughter’s sons and she would reach her limit and there would be a hard rupture, and I will tell you about that, because it is true and I do not hide the true things. But underneath even the rupture was a woman who had spent years building me with rules because rules were the tools she had, and a person built well, even harshly, is still a person who got built. Not every child is so lucky as to be flogged into shape by people who, it turns out, were trying to save him.

## Chapter Sixteen: Kemoview

My grandfather Samson named his home after himself, Kemoview, and the more I have lived, the more I have come to think that naming a poor man’s patch of red earth like an English estate is one of the most quietly magnificent acts of defiance I have ever encountered, and he did it without irony, with total seriousness, because the man understood something about claiming your ground that I would spend my whole career relearning.

Samson Kemoli was, before he was my grandfather, a servant of the country. He had been a police officer, a corporal, who served Kenya for years that I never fully mapped, until an injury to his leg, the result of an accident, ended his service and left him with a limp he carried for the rest of his life and a story he carried with even more care. I was always curious about that leg, the way children are curious about the visible mysteries of adults, and getting the story out of him was like mining, because he was a man of few words about his own pain. He had given his body to the service of a country that gave him a small pension and a permanent limp in return, and he came home to Kemoview, to the hill he had named, and he became the keeper of the family’s older world, the traditions, the cattle, the way things had been done before the township and the white grandfather and the books.

Life at Kemoview was the most rural and, in some ways, the most purely joyful stretch of my whole childhood, the part that felt least like surviving and most like simply being a boy. There were cattle to herd, four of them, and herding cattle is hours of glorious boredom, a boy and some cows and a whole day, and into that boredom my friends and I poured invention. There were boys named Indeche and Andaji and Mike and others, and we built things, elaborate things, houses out of maize stalks, structures and stadiums woven from corn stalks, whole architectures of imagination raised in the bush while the cattle grazed. We swam in the streams and the watering troughs, and swimming came with its own dangers and its own comedy, because sometimes a snake would drop from a branch into the very water you were in and send you exploding out of it, and sometimes the area’s guard would come to chase off the boys who were not supposed to be swimming there.

And it is that last one that gives me the funniest story of my childhood, so let me tell it properly. We had gone swimming, a whole crew of us, naked the way boys swim, our clothes piled on the bank, and I, even then, did not like to show my nakedness, so I had kept on a short. We had even done our preparation, clearing the branches that hid the spot so the sun could reach the water. I was up on the rock we jumped from, surveying my domain, when out of the corner of my eye I saw him. The guard. Coming. With a cane. And I tried to warn the others, he is coming, he is coming, but they were too deep in their splashing to hear me over their own noise. So I made a decision that has defined my approach to crisis ever since. I did not wait. I grabbed the nearest clothing, which happened to be a friend’s t-shirt, threw it on, and ran, and I drove the cattle home and was gone before the guard reached the water. The others, naked, with no clothes within reach and nowhere to run, simply had to stop, climb out, lie down on the grass, and accept a thorough caning, one by one, while I was already halfway home with the cows. And when they came back that evening, sore, I braced for them to be furious at me for escaping. Instead they were amazed. How did you escape? You are so clever. I had turned my own survival into legend, and I learned something I have used a hundred times since, which is that the world forgives the one who gets away clean and even admires him, and that a quick decision in a moment of chaos is worth more than all the loyalty and hesitation in the world. Cruel little lesson. Useful little lesson. I was a child, naked but for a short, and I had already understood the politics of escape.

My grandmother Josephine was the other half of Kemoview, Samson’s wife, and, in the deepest layer of my family’s secret, my mother’s blood mother, the woman who had given my mother to her sister Gladys.

But before I come to Josephine, let me dwell at Kemoview a while longer, because it was the freest stretch of my childhood and it deserves its full measure. Grandfather Samson, the retired corporal with the limp, was a different kind of authority than Gladys, a rural patriarch rather than a household administrator, and his domain was not rules and routines but land and cattle and the old ways of doing things. He had served the country in uniform and come home wounded, and he carried himself with the particular dignity of a man who had seen more of the world than the hill he now kept, a man whose few words held the weight of things he did not say. I was endlessly curious about his policing years, about the leg, about what he had seen and done in the service of a country that had sent him home with a limp and a small pension, and prying stories out of him was slow patient work, because he did not perform his past the way some men do, did not turn his service into a constant tale. The stories came rarely, in fragments, when the mood took him, and I treasured each one, the boy at the feet of the old corporal, gathering pieces of a life that had touched parts of the country and the world I could barely imagine. He taught me, mostly without words, a certain rural masculinity that I have carried, a quietness, a self-sufficiency, a way of meeting hardship without complaint, the bearing of a man who has been hurt in the service of something and does not make a production of it.

The work of Kemoview was the herding, and the herding was the texture of my days there. We had cattle, a handful of them, and the cattle had to be taken out to graze, and the taking-out fell to the boys, to me and my brother and the friends who herded alongside us, Indeche and Andaji and Mike and the others, a small republic of herd-boys loose on the land with our animals and our whole long days. Herding cattle is, I have to tell you, mostly boredom, hours and hours of following slow animals across the grass while they eat, with nothing required of you but to keep them from wandering into someone’s crops or down to a stream where a snake might be waiting, and a boy faced with that much unstructured time either goes mad or becomes an inventor, and we became inventors. We built. We took the maize stalks that littered the harvested fields and we wove and stacked and engineered them into structures, houses and stadiums and whole imagined cities of cornstalk, elaborate architectures that we raised across an afternoon and that the wind or the next day’s grazing would knock down, and we built them again, the pure creative play of children with time and raw material and nothing else, and I have come to think those cornstalk cities were my first real making, the first time I built something out of the materials at hand for no reason but the joy of building, and there is a straight line from those structures to every show and every brand and every thing I have built since out of whatever was lying around.

We hunted in our small way, and we swam, and we ranged across the land with the freedom that rural children had and that is vanishing from the world, the freedom to be gone all day, unsupervised, miles from any adult, navigating the streams and the bush and the fields on our own judgment, learning the land in our bodies. The streams were our swimming pools and our danger both, because a stream in that country might have a snake drop into it from an overhanging branch, sending a whole crew of naked boys exploding out of the water in shrieking panic, and the watering troughs and the deeper pools were where we cooled off in the heat, and the swimming was forbidden often enough to make it delicious, which brings me back to the t-shirt escape I have already told you, the day I grabbed a friend’s shirt and ran while the others took the caning, the day I learned the politics of the clean getaway. That whole stretch, the herding and the building and the swimming and the ranging, was the closest I came in my entire childhood to an ordinary boyhood, to simple uncomplicated joy, and it is precious to me precisely because it was so rare, a window of freedom and play between the abandonment that came before and the long disciplined walks to school that came after. At Kemoview, for a season, I was just a boy with cattle and friends and cornstalks and streams, and I did not know to be grateful for the simplicity of it then, but I am grateful now, because every child deserves at least a season of that, and that season at Kemoview was mine.

Let me give you a single full day at Kemoview, because the rhythm of it holds the whole feeling of that season. We woke early, though not as brutally early as the school days would later demand, and there were chores before anything else, because a rural homestead runs on chores and a boy is free labor, water to fetch and animals to see to and the small endless tasks of keeping a farm alive. Then the cattle had to go out, and that was our work, mine and the other boys’, and we would gather the animals and drive them out to wherever the grazing was good that day, and the day would open up before us, long and unstructured and ours. We walked the cattle to the grass and then we settled in to the strange work of watching them eat, which is no work at all and all day long at once, and into that vast emptiness we poured everything a boy’s imagination can generate. We talked, endlessly, the way boys talk, about everything and nothing, about the girls we were too young to do anything about and the futures we could barely picture and the films we had seen and the fights we had witnessed and the wild rumors of the village. We competed, at everything, who could throw furthest and run fastest and climb highest and hold his breath longest, the endless ranking tournaments of boyhood. And we built, the cornstalk cities, the elaborate structures, the engineering projects that consumed whole afternoons. The cattle ate, and we lived entire epics in the grass beside them, and at the end of the day we drove them home again, and that was a day at Kemoview, repeated across a season, the freest and simplest days I have ever known.

The friends were the heart of it, Indeche and Andaji and Mike and the others, a band of herd-boys bound together by the shared long days, and there is a particular kind of friendship that forms among children who spend unstructured time together in the wild, a friendship of pure companionship unmediated by any institution, not classmates assigned to each other but boys who chose each other across the grass. We were a small society with our own codes and our own hierarchy and our own justice, our own inside jokes and ongoing sagas and remembered triumphs, like the day I escaped the swimming guard in a stolen t-shirt while they took the caning, a story that entered our shared lore and was told and retold. I learned among those boys the politics of the group, how to hold a place in a band of equals, how to lead sometimes and follow sometimes and clown always, the social arts that I would carry into every group I ever joined. And I learned loyalty and its limits, the bonds of a crew and the moments those bonds break, the whole emotional education of boyhood friendship, conducted in the grass beside the grazing cattle, far from any adult eye.

The seasons shaped everything, because at Kemoview, more than anywhere I had lived, I lived close to the turning of the agricultural year. There was the planting and the rains and the growing and the harvest and the dry stretch after, and the cattle and the children and the whole homestead moved with that cycle, and a boy learned to read the year in the fields and the sky, to know what work each season demanded and what freedoms each season allowed. The harvested fields, stripped of their maize, became our building sites, the cornstalks our raw material. The rains brought the swimming and the danger of the swollen streams. The dry season brought the dust and the long thirsty herding to wherever water remained. I learned the year in my body at Kemoview, the deep rhythm that governs all farming people, and it gave me a sense of time that the city has never quite erased, the knowledge that life moves in cycles, that every season passes into the next, that nothing, neither the plenty nor the want, is permanent, because the year is always turning. That cyclical sense of time, learned among the cattle, has steadied me through every boom and every bust of my adult life, the deep conviction that this season too will turn, because every season I ever knew as a boy did.

And there was a freedom at Kemoview that I want to name precisely, because it is nearly extinct in the modern world and it shaped me profoundly. We were unsupervised. For long hours, miles from any adult, we made our own decisions, ran our own risks, settled our own disputes, navigated real dangers with no one watching, and that unsupervised freedom, which would horrify a modern parent, was the making of a certain self-reliance and judgment that supervised children never develop. When you are eight years old and a snake drops into the water you are swimming in and there is no adult to run to, you learn, fast, to handle your own crises. When you are responsible for keeping cattle out of a neighbor’s crops with no one to ask what to do, you learn to make decisions and live with them. The unsupervised freedom of Kemoview was a kind of trust, an assumption that children could handle the world, and rising to that trust built in me a confidence in my own judgment, a willingness to act without permission, that has served me my whole life, that is in fact the very trait that let me build a show on a bedsheet without waiting for anyone’s blessing. I was trusted to handle the world as a boy at Kemoview, and a child who is trusted to handle the world grows into an adult who believes he can.

And Samson salted the cattle’s water, I remember, added salt to make them drink and thrive, the small inherited science of animal husbandry that I have mentioned before, and watching him tend the animals, watching the care and the knowledge in his rough hands, I learned a thing about the dignity of practical competence, about how a man who knows how to keep animals alive and land productive carries a real and unglamorous wisdom that no certificate confers. My grandfather could not have lectured on much, but he could keep cattle and read weather and manage land and survive, and there is a kind of intelligence in that, the intelligence of the hands and the seasons, that our certificate-worshipping world undervalues, and I absorbed respect for it at Kemoview, respect for the people who know how to actually do the physical work of survival, a respect that has kept me, I hope, from ever fully becoming the kind of educated person who sneers at the unschooled competence of those who feed him.

Josephine carried, in her quiet way, the deepest secret of my whole family, the secret I have already told you, that she was my mother’s blood mother, the woman who had given my mother away to her own sister Gladys, and so the grandmother frying mandazi at Kemoview was, in the hidden architecture of my family, the origin of the great displacement that had shaped my mother’s whole life and therefore mine. I did not know this as a child stealing her mandazi, did not understand that the warm grandmother in the kitchen had once made the wrenching decision to give a daughter away, did not grasp the layers of love and loss folded into her ordinary presence. I knew her simply as grandmother Josephine, the pastor, the mandazi-fryer, the kind one. It was only later that I learned to see her whole, to understand that this gentle woman had carried for a lifetime the particular grief of a mother who gave her child to another, and that her kindness to me, her grandson by the daughter she had given away, might have held inside it some of that old grief and that old love finding their way back to her through me. I have come to think that the family secrets do not stay buried, that they live on quietly in the relationships they shaped, that Josephine frying mandazi for the son of the daughter she gave away was the secret still working itself out, love trying to complete a circuit that an old decision had broken. But all of that I understood only with time. As a boy, I just knew she was kind, and that she let me steal her mandazi, and that Kemoview, with its cattle and its streams and its gentle pastor grandmother, was the warmest place I knew.

Josephine was, like every powerful woman in my line, a pastor, ministering in the Friends Church, and she is the source of my single most charming small crime. There were always celebrations being planned, weddings and church events, and the planning involved committees and Harambees and the cooking of vast quantities of mandazi, and whenever mandazi was being fried, I was first in line to volunteer for any task that put me near them, because I would steal them. I would take five at a time, fresh hot mandazi spirited away while my grandmother’s back was turned, and I thought I was a master thief. Years later Josephine told me, smiling, that she had always known.
