We were reminded, in words and in a thousand small gestures, that we were there on sufferance, that our presence was a burden grudgingly borne, that the food we ate was food taken from someone more deserving. I learned to read a household’s resentment in the set of a shoulder, the tone of a summons, the size of a portion, and I learned to make myself useful past the point of exhaustion, to work harder than the household’s own children, to earn my place daily because it was never simply granted, and that frantic earning of a place that should have been free is a habit I carried straight into adulthood, into every job and every relationship, the deep belief that I must constantly justify my own existence through usefulness or be thrown out as trash. The lion’s flight did not only leave a hole. It enrolled me, young, in a hard school of conditional belonging, and I was a diligent student, and I have spent years trying to graduate from it, trying to believe that I might be worth keeping even when I am not being useful, which is a lesson the abandoned child finds almost impossible to learn.

I understand him now in a way I could not then. I understand that the planting of the maize and the lie about a few days and the packed bags in the night were not abandonment but strategy, the desperate cunning of a man trying to save his life without destroying his family’s cover, that he ran because running was survival and survival was the only gift he could still give us, his continued existence somewhere in the world, a father alive in Nairobi being infinitely better than a father beaten into a night grave in Lugari. I understand that he sent money when he could, that he was working himself half to death cutting stone in the city even as we went hungry in the village, that the absence which felt to a child like rejection was in fact a kind of love conducted at a distance under threat of death. But understanding came later, with adulthood, with my own acquaintance with fear and impossible choices. At the time there was only the hole, and the fear, and the taunts, and a boy who had been first in his class learning what it feels like to have your own father’s name turned into a weapon against you on a dusty playground. The lion ran. I do not blame the lion. But I was the cub left in the open, and I learned, in the years of his running, exactly how cold the world gets when the thing that was protecting you stands up and disappears into the dark to save its own life.

## Chapter Eleven: The Storm Runs, and the Phone Call

My mother ran before my father did, and her running and his running were two entirely different escapes from two entirely different terrors, and the fact that both of my parents had to flee for their very survival before I was ten years old is, I have come to believe, the central wound of my childhood and the thing I have spent my whole loud adult life talking over so that I would never have to sit in the silence of it.

I have to name the thing my mother was fleeing, plainly and without softening, because to soften it would be to betray her, and she has been betrayed enough. There was violence in our home, and not the disciplinary kind that the whole society endorsed, the other kind, the kind a man does to his wife, and in those years and in that place it was so normalized, so woven into the ordinary fabric of marriage, that to hear a man beating his wife was an ordinary evening sound, a thing the neighbors absorbed without alarm, a private matter, a husband’s prerogative, a wife’s lot. My mother absorbed it for a long time, the way the women of her generation were taught to absorb it, swallowing it season after season because that was what a wife did, because where would she go, because the children, because the shame, because all the reasons that have kept battered women in their homes since the beginning of homes. She absorbed it until the day she had absorbed all that a human being can absorb and remain a human being, and then she did the thing that the women in my family do when survival finally demands it, the thing she had perhaps watched other women fail to do and die for failing. She ran.

She left. She left the home and the violence and, hardest of all, the children, because she could not carry us into the uncertainty she was fleeing into, and she went to Nairobi, a city she had never seen, did not know, could not even properly picture in her mind. Think about the courage and the desperation tangled together in that. A young woman, with little money and less certainty, getting onto a bus bound for a city that was only a name to her, with just enough fare to reach it and nothing left over for food along the way, riding for more than a full day on the bad roads of that time, hungry and frightened and entirely alone, asking at every stop what place is this, where are we now, how much further, exposed to every danger that a young woman alone on a long road is exposed to, traveling toward a place where she knew no one and had nothing waiting, because the place she was leaving had become more dangerous than the unknown. And she made it. She survived the road the way she had survived everything else, by simply refusing to be destroyed by it, and she arrived in Nairobi with nothing and began, from absolute zero, to build a life.

Her first work in the city was as a house girl, a domestic servant in someone else’s home, the lowest and most common entry point for a woman arriving in Nairobi with no connections and no capital but her own labor. From there she found work with a florist, arranging and selling flowers, earning around seven thousand shillings in a month, and she had started even below that, her very first wages something closer to a thousand, and on that thin and uncertain income she rebuilt a life one object at a time. A single rented room. A thin mattress on the floor. A kerosene stove for cooking. Two cooking pots. Two plates. That was the entire material world my mother assembled in the city, two pots and two plates and a mattress on the floor of a rented room, and it represented an almost unimaginable triumph, because every single item of it had been earned by a woman who had arrived with nothing, fleeing a man’s fists, in a city that owed her nothing and gave her nothing but the chance to work. I have furnished homes since with things that cost more than my mother earned in years, and not one of those purchases has ever held the dignity of her two pots and two plates, bought with the wages of survival, the foundation stones of a life rebuilt from the ground up by a woman who would not be beaten, in either sense of the word.

And so for years, with my father vanished into his own flight and his own troubles and my mother rebuilding in the distant city, my brother and I were raised by the rotation of grandparents and the mixed mercy and cruelty of the wider family, and I want to give you the texture of that rotation, because it was its own peculiar education. We were not in one home. We moved, passed between households as circumstances and the patience of relatives shifted, a season here and a season there, never quite settled, never quite at home, always the visiting burden rather than the resident child. And each household had its own weather, its own rules, its own ratio of kindness to cruelty, and a child who is passed between homes becomes a quick and anxious reader of new environments, learning fast upon each arrival who could be trusted and who could not, where the food was and whether he would be given any, which adult might show a flash of tenderness and which would reach first for the cane. I became fluent in the reading of households the way I had become fluent in the reading of the bush, scanning each new home for its dangers and its few mercies, adapting myself to its particular weather, making myself as useful and as small and as unobjectionable as I could, because the visiting burden survives on sufferance and sufferance is withdrawn from children who make trouble.

There were homes where it was bearable, where an aunt or an uncle treated us with something close to ordinary family kindness, and I remember those homes with a gratitude out of all proportion to what they actually offered, because a little kindness to a child who expects none lands like a feast. And there were homes where it was not bearable, where we were worked and beaten and starved and reminded daily of our nothingness, where taka taka was the word for us, and I remember those homes too, with a clarity that has not faded, because cruelty to a child engraves itself deeper than kindness, cuts a groove in the soul that the years do not fully smooth. I learned in those harder homes a thing that no child should have to learn, which is that family is not automatically safe, that blood does not guarantee love, that the people who are supposed to protect you can be the very people you most need protecting from, and that lesson made me, for many years, a person who found it difficult to trust the very relationships that are supposed to be the most trustworthy, who held even love at a wary distance, braced for the moment it would turn into its opposite.

And underneath all the moving and the reading of households and the bracing, there was the simple bottomless longing for my mother, the ache that no relative’s home could fill because it was specifically her shape, her voice, her particular love that I was missing, and that no substitute could supply. I would see other children with their mothers, ordinary scenes, a mother calling a child in for food, scolding a child, embracing a child, the casual daily currency of belonging to your own mother, and the wanting that those scenes opened in me was almost unbearable, the wanting of a thing that everyone around me seemed to have for free and that had been taken from me by a violence I only half understood. That longing is the engine underneath the phone call I am about to tell you about again, the years of accumulated wanting that finally found a phone and a number and broke open into a flood of crying, and you cannot understand why a boy would weep so hard into a brick phone unless you understand the years of silent longing that the phone call finally released.

That longing, and the cruelty that surrounded it, taught me the lowest lesson of my childhood, the one stamped into me by a single word. To be the abandoned children, the spare boys whose parents had both, for their different reasons, disappeared, was to occupy the very lowest rung of the extended household, beneath the children whose parents were present and providing, beneath even the animals in some reckonings, because the animals at least produced milk or eggs or labor and we produced only need. Some of the aunts and uncles who housed us disciplined us in ways that were not discipline, that crossed over into something closer to cruelty, beating us beyond any correction, working us hard while their own children rested, feeding us last and least, talking about us within our hearing as the abandoned ones, the burdens, the problems that had been dumped on them. We were called names. Taka taka, trash, garbage, was one of them, said to our faces, by adults, about children. I learned, in those years, exactly how a family can keep a child just fed enough to survive and just starved enough never to forget that he is a guest, a charge, a problem being managed rather than a child being loved, and that lesson marked me as deeply as any other in this book. Part of the man who works without ceasing, who built a loud name, who needs an entire country to know him and approve of him, is the small boy who was once called trash by his own blood and decided, somewhere deep below words, in a place he could not even see, that he would make himself so valuable, so impossible to ignore, so undeniably worth something, that no one would ever again be able to call him garbage and have it stick.

I have told you my side of those years, the abandoned boy’s side, but I have come to understand my mother’s side too, and it is its own quiet agony that I owe her the telling of. Picture her in the city, in the rented room with the two pots and the two plates, working her long days as a house girl and then among the flowers, and picture what she carried inside her the whole time, which was us. A mother does not flee her children because she has stopped loving them. She flees because staying has become a danger she can no longer survive, and she leaves them behind not out of indifference but out of an impossible calculation, the brutal arithmetic that says she cannot protect them if she is dead or destroyed, that she must save herself first if she is ever to be able to save them, and that the relatives, whatever their cruelties, will at least keep them alive while she builds a place to bring them to. My mother carried the guilt of that calculation every single day in the city, the knowledge that her sons were suffering somewhere in the west while she arranged flowers for strangers, and she sent back what she could, small sums wired home, never enough, always shadowed by the awareness that money was not what we most needed, that what we needed was her, and that her was the one thing she could not yet safely give. She has told me since how she would lie on that thin mattress at night and wonder what we were eating, whether we were being treated well, whether we still remembered her, and the not-knowing was its own torture, a mother in exile from her own children, building a future for them out of flowers and domestic labor while her heart stayed four hundred kilometers to the west, in a village she had fled to survive.

And so the separation was not, as it felt to me as a child, a simple abandonment, a mother choosing to leave. It was two people, mother and son, suffering the same wound from opposite ends, both of us longing across the same impossible distance, both of us doing the hard daily work of survival in our separate hard places, both of us waiting for the day the distance could finally be closed. I did not know, as I sat hungry in the relatives’ homes, that my mother was lying awake in the city aching for me. She did not know, as she wired home her small sums, that I had forgotten how to say the word mum. We were each carrying the same grief in ignorance of the other’s carrying, and that is perhaps the cruelest thing about a separation of poverty, that it isolates even the love, keeps the mother and the child from even comforting each other with the knowledge that the longing runs both ways. When I finally called her and cried into that brick phone, part of what broke open was exactly this, the sudden collision of two separate griefs that had been running in parallel for three years, hers in the city and mine in the village, finally meeting on a crackling phone line and recognizing each other.

And then there was the phone call. Of everything in this book, of all the hungers and the canes and the floggings and the fears, this is the one that still, to this day, takes the floor out from under me when I let myself stand on it.

We charged our phones at a shop two or three kilometers away from home, because there was no power where we lived, no electricity, and so the phones of the household made regular pilgrimages to the shop with electricity to drink their fill and come back, and I was the errand boy of the family, the one sent to fetch and carry, and one day I was sent to collect my grandmother Rosemary’s phone, a Nokia, one of the old indestructible brick phones with the small screen and the physical buttons, from its charging at that distant shop. And on the long walk home, alone, holding this phone, this small box of possibility, I did a thing I had not been given permission to do. I scrolled through the contacts. I do not fully know why, except that a lonely child holding a phone full of names is going to look at the names, is going to search them for something, for someone. And I found a contact saved, in the simple unadorned way contacts were saved then, as Mum Eugene. My mother. Her number, sitting there in my grandmother’s phone, a thread to the woman who had vanished into the city. And I called it. And she answered. And I had not heard my mother’s voice in something close to three years.

And I did not know how to talk to her. That is the part that still undoes me completely, the part I can barely write even now. I had been separated from my mother for so long, so completely, that I had lost the word for her. I had spent those years calling every woman who fed me or housed me auntie, calling everyone something other than what they were, surviving inside a vocabulary of borrowed and distant relations, and now my actual mother, my own mother, was on the line, her real voice in my ear after three years of silence, and I did not know whether I could call her mum, because the word had gone strange and far away in my mouth, because the relationship had been stretched so thin by absence that the most basic and natural name for it would not come. So I did not really call her anything. I just cried. I cried, and through the crying I asked her the questions that no child should ever have to ask his mother, the questions that had been building in me for three years. Why did you leave us. Why did you leave us here to suffer. Did we do something wrong, was it our fault, did you leave because of something we did. And then, when the questions ran out, I begged. Mum, come and pick us. Please come and pick us. They are mistreating us. They are torturing us. They are calling us trash and beating us and we are hungry and we are suffering, please, please come and pick us from this place.

She came that December. But before she could come, before her hearing of my crying could become her arriving, there was a smaller, stupider, more revealing aftermath that tells you everything about the temperature of the world I was living in. When I brought my grandmother Rosemary’s phone home, she checked it, as people checked their phones then, and she saw that some of the credit, some of the airtime, had been used, and she investigated, and she found in the call log that I had called my mother, and she became furious. Not concerned. Not moved. Furious. She called me ungrateful. She said we were wicked, scheming children who were stealing her airtime, that we were not being mistreated at all, that we were liars and burdens who repaid her charity by using her credit to call the mother who had dumped us on her. The fury over a few shillings of airtime, layered on top of everything else, while a child wept into a phone for a mother he had forgotten how to name. That was the exact temperature of my childhood, and that single scene holds it perfectly, the weeping boy and the counted airtime, the desperate reaching and the petty accounting, the child’s three years of grief weighed against a few shillings of credit and found wanting.

In the days after that phone call, something shifted in me, a small terrible hope that I almost did not dare to hold. I had reached her. I had heard her voice say my name. She had heard me beg. And now there was a possibility, fragile and enormous, that the begging might actually work, that the mother who had been a rumor and a distant voice might become, again, a real woman who would arrive and take us away from the homes where we were trash. I held that hope carefully, the way you hold a flame against the wind, because hope is dangerous to a child who has learned that wanting things is the fastest way to be disappointed in them, and I had wanted my mother for three years and she had not come, and now I had a reason to want her arrival concretely, with a date attached, December, and the wanting was almost unbearable, a physical ache of anticipation laid over the older ache of absence. I counted toward that December the way I had counted the maize in the granary, watchful, rationing my hope, refusing to fully believe in the rescue until I could see it with my own eyes, because to fully believe and then be disappointed would have been more than I could carry.

And I have come to understand that the phone call did something permanent to me, something that shaped the entire architecture of the man I became, and I want to name it precisely because it is the hinge between the wounded boy and the loud man. When I lost the word mum, when I sat on that road unable to name the most important relationship of my life because absence had stretched it past the reach of language, I learned, in the deepest part of myself, that words can be lost, that the things you most need to say can simply go missing when you need them most, that silence can swallow even your mother’s name. And I think the man spent the rest of his life making sure that would never happen again. I became a person who would never again be caught without words, who fills every silence, who has built an entire career on the refusal to ever again sit speechless before the thing that matters most. I talk for a living. I talk constantly, fluently, professionally, to hundreds of thousands of people, and underneath all of it is a boy on a dirt road who once could not find the single word his heart was breaking to say, and who swore, without knowing he was swearing it, that he would never be wordless again. My fluency is built on the foundation of my one great speechlessness. My loudness is the scar tissue over the silence. The man you see talking so easily on the screen is the boy who once could not say mum, healed over and overcompensating, turning the wound of that lost word into a lifelong torrent of found ones.

I have thought a great deal about my grandmother Rosemary’s fury over the airtime, because it would be easy to make her a villain and the truth is more complicated and more sad. She was not a wealthy woman. She was an old woman of the church, stretched thin, raising grandchildren who were not her responsibility, counting every shilling because every shilling had to be counted, and a few units of airtime spent without permission was, in the brutal economy of her life, a real loss, a theft from a budget that had no slack in it anywhere. I can see her side now. But a child cannot see an old woman’s budget, a child can only feel the temperature of the response to his deepest grief, and the temperature of the response to my three years of longing finally breaking open into a phone call was anger about airtime, and that is what lodged in me, the lesson that my grief was, to the people around me, mostly an inconvenience and an expense, a thing to be managed and resented rather than comforted. The poor do not have the luxury of soft responses to a child’s pain, because they are too busy surviving their own, and so a poor child’s grief often meets not tenderness but irritation, not comfort but the counting of costs, and he learns to stop expecting comfort, learns to carry his griefs quietly and alone, learns that the world does not have time for his tears. I learned that lesson thoroughly, and it took me into adulthood, this conviction that my pain was an imposition, that I should handle it myself and not trouble anyone with it, and it is a hard conviction to unlearn, the belief, planted by an airtime fury, that even your deepest grief is mostly a bill someone else will resent paying.

But my mother had heard me. Across all that distance, through all that crying, she had heard the boy who could not say mum begging her to come, and a storm, when it finally hears its own child crying out from inside the danger it had to flee, will cross any distance and break any obstacle to reach him. She came. That December, she came, and the coming is the next chapter, and it begins, of all the unlikely places, with a white man in a car, in the rain, asking a barefoot boy a question that would change how he saw himself forever. But before we get to the rain, sit for one more moment with the phone call, because I need you to feel it the way I felt it. A boy, on a dirt road, holding a borrowed phone, discovering that he had been separated from his mother for so long that he had lost the word mum, and crying into a brick phone for a rescue he was no longer sure was coming. That boy grew up to talk for a living, to never run out of words, to fill every silence with sound, and I have come to understand that the man’s endless words are built directly on top of the moment the boy ran out of the only word that mattered. I talk so much now, I think, because once, when it counted most, I could not find the single word my heart was breaking to say.

## Chapter Twelve: The White Grandfather in the Rain

It was raining the day my life changed direction, raining hard, the heavy unhurried highland rain that turns the red dust to red mud in minutes and sends everyone scrambling for whatever shelter they can find, and my brother and I were caught in it, at the shops, at a place called Lugari Line, two barefoot boys with nowhere dry to be.

Let me set Lugari Line for you, because it deserves to be seen properly. It is a small shopping center built at the place where the road makes a sharp turn, an elbow, an L, and the shops cluster at the corner of that L, a row of dukas and kiosks selling the small necessities of rural life, and to a village child it was a center of commerce, a downtown, a place where things happened and people gathered and the wider world occasionally passed through on its way to somewhere larger. We were there in the rain, sheltering as best we could, two boys without shoes and without much else, when a car came along the road, and a car was already an event in itself, but this car, everyone in the area knew this car, because the man driving it was white. My grandfather, David Zarembka, the American, the Mzungu, the man married into our family from across an ocean, was passing through Lugari Line in the rain, and he saw us, two of his grandsons huddled at the shops, and he waved, and the car slowed and stopped.

We ran to it through the rain, my brother and I, splashing through the red mud, and a window rolled down, and there inside the dry warm car was my grandmother Gladys, and she looked out at us, at the two soaked barefoot boys who had run to the car, and she asked a question that I have never forgotten, that I will carry to my grave, because it was the question that made me see myself for the first time. She asked, why don’t you have shoes. Why don’t you have shoes. And it was only when she asked it, only when this woman in this dry car with her particular life looked at our bare feet and named the absence out loud, that the realization landed on me with all the weight of the rain coming down. We did not have shoes. I had not, until that exact moment, registered shoelessness as a lack, as a thing missing, as a deprivation, because everyone I knew was more or less in the same condition and a thing that everyone lacks does not feel like a lack, it feels like the way the world simply is. But the question, asked from inside a car, by a person whose feet were shod, held up a mirror, and in that mirror I saw, suddenly and permanently, my own bare feet in the mud, and understood, for the first time in my life, that they were supposed to have something on them, that there existed a world in which children’s feet were covered, and that I was standing, barefoot, in the rain, outside that world.

That is how poverty works on a child. You do not know you are poor until someone whose life is arranged differently holds up the mirror, until a question asked in innocence or in kindness reveals to you the shape of your own deprivation, and then, having seen it, you can never unsee it. Before my grandmother’s question, I was simply a boy in the rain. After it, I was a poor boy in the rain, a boy who lacked something other boys had, and that small shift in self-perception, from boy to poor boy, is one of the most consequential things that ever happened to me, because you cannot climb out of a hole you do not know you are in, and Gladys’s question, gentle and devastating, showed me the hole. I have thought since that this is the double edge of every encounter between the poor and the better-off, that the mirror which reveals your poverty is also the thing that first makes you want to escape it, that the wound of being seen as lacking is also the seed of the ambition to lack no longer, and that my whole driven loud climbing life began, in a real sense, with a question asked through a car window in the rain, why don’t you have shoes.

They asked us what we wanted, and we said shoes, of course we said shoes, and they gave us money, two hundred shillings each, my brother and me, an enormous sum, a fortune handed through a car window in the rain, more money than I had ever held. And then, being children, being foolish, being trained by a lifetime of having nothing to immediately surrender anything we received to the nearest adult, we took that money home and handed it to our aunt, who took it and bought us each a pair of shoes, blue plastic sandals, the simple molded plastic kind, two hundred shillings the pair, and oh, I want you to understand that I worshipped those sandals. I have owned shoes since that cost more than my whole family earned in a year, fine leather things, designer things, shoes that announce a man’s arrival, and not one pair of them has ever meant a fraction of what those blue plastic sandals meant to me. They were indestructible. You could wash them clean with a single splash of water. They went everywhere with me, to school and to the farm and to church and along the bush paths, and they were mine, truly mine, and a white grandfather had stopped his car in the rain and a grandmother had asked about my feet and the result was that for the first time in my life my feet were covered, and I have never since felt richer than I felt in those blue plastic sandals, because wealth, I learned that day, is not the price of the thing but the size of the lack it fills, and those two-hundred-shilling sandals filled a lack I had not even known I carried until a question revealed it.

That encounter in the rain cracked a door open, and through that door came the rescue I had begged for on the phone. My grandparents told us to come to them, to Lumakanda, and not long after, my mother, having heard my weeping across all that distance, finally arrived, that December, as she had promised, and she took us to the city, to Nairobi, for the first time in our lives. My parents were not in Nairobi proper but in Juja, in a place called Gachororo, on the city’s edge, and the journey there and the arrival blew the doors clean off my small world. I had never seen a city. I had never, really, seen electricity in abundance, because the only place electricity had ever lived in my experience was my grandfather David’s home in Lumakanda, my grandfather Samson and my grandfather Philemona having none, and so electric light was not, to me, a fact of modern life, it was a marvel that existed at exactly one address in the entire world, and now suddenly here was a whole city of it, blazing through the night, light everywhere, on every street and in every building, an ocean of the marvel I had thought existed only at my grandfather’s house.

I asked my mother a thousand questions on that journey and in those first city days, and I want you to picture the scene, because it is one of the few unambiguously joyful ones in this whole stretch of my story. What is that, I asked. Look at that building, how is it so tall, how does it not fall. Look at that, what is that, what is it for. I had never seen a skyscraper and could not understand how a building could climb so far into the sky. I had never seen a real city market, where it seemed that every single person was selling something and a great many of them were selling tomatoes, mountains of tomatoes, more tomatoes than I had imagined existed. I had never watched movies the way the city watched them, never seen the lit shopfronts, the matatus in their painted glory, the sheer overwhelming density of people and things and light. And my mother, who I half expected to snap at our endless naive village questions, did not snap. She was patient with us the entire way, did not insult us, did not shame us for our wonder, just answered our questions one after another and watched her village boys discover the world, and she has told me since that something settled in her on that journey, a decision, a vow, to take care of us no matter what it cost, and I believe her, because I felt it, felt the warmth of a mother who had crossed back over the distance to gather her children and was not going to lose them again.

I want to dwell in that city wonder a little longer, because the joy of it is rare in this story and deserves its space. Everything in Nairobi was a question my village had not prepared me to answer. The matatus astonished me, the public minibuses painted in wild colors and blasting music and covered in images and slogans, careening through traffic with a confidence that seemed to defy death, and I could not understand how so many vehicles could move so fast so close together without constant catastrophe, could not understand the choreography of a city’s traffic, which to a village boy looked like pure chaos and was in fact an intricate order I had no key to. The shops stunned me, shops with glass fronts and lit interiors and more goods inside a single one than existed in all of Lugari Line, shelves of things I had no names for, abundance stacked to the ceiling, and the casual way city people walked past all of it without wonder, when each shop window was to me a small museum of the impossible. The food stunned me, the variety of it, the street vendors and the smells, the idea that you could simply buy cooked food from a stranger and eat it, that hunger here was a thing you solved with money rather than a season you endured. And the people, the sheer number of them, more people on a single street than I had seen in my entire life gathered in one place, a river of strangers, and the strange city fact that you could be surrounded by thousands of people and known by none of them, a kind of crowded loneliness I had never experienced in a village where everyone knew everyone and your business was the whole community’s business.

The first time I watched a movie properly, in the city way, I was transfixed. We had narrated movies to each other in the village, my brother and I, performed them from memory and imagination, but to sit and watch one delivered whole, the pictures and the sound and the story all arriving together without my having to manufacture any of it, was a different order of experience entirely, and I drank it in, the boy who would grow up to make screen content sitting slack-jawed before a screen, not yet knowing that this was his future flickering in front of him, only knowing that it was wonderful. And the electricity, I keep returning to the electricity because it was the deepest marvel, the simple fact that you could push a switch and banish the dark, that the night did not have to be navigated by kerosene flame, that light was abundant and casual and everywhere, taken for granted by people who had never read by a koroboi or done their evening chores by the orange light of a single wick. I would push switches just to watch the light obey me, the village boy commanding the marvel that had lived at one address his whole life and now lived everywhere, and I think some part of my hunger for the bright lights of a studio, the literal bright lights I would one day stand under for a living, was born in those first switch-flipping days in Juja, the boy who had grown up in the dark falling permanently in love with the light.

My father, by then, had found his footing in the city too, through my mother’s help, and his work there was as raw and hard as everything else in our story had been. He cut and carried stone for the construction of the Thika Superhighway, that great road being driven northeast out of Nairobi toward our home region, hauling broken rock into trucks under the sun for a first wage of around seven hundred shillings, and on that wage, between the two of them, my parents began to assemble an actual life, item by item, the way my mother had assembled her room of two pots and two plates. First a mattress. Then a bed to lift the mattress off the floor. Then, the great triumph, a television, one of the old heavy ones with the deep bulging back, the stomach TV we called it for its protruding belly, a brick of a thing that to us was pure unimaginable glamour, a screen of our own, light and pictures and the wider world delivered into our own rented room. The culture shock of the city was total, and I overcame it fast, because a poor village child is, if nothing else, supremely adaptable, and I had been adapting to hard new conditions my entire short life, and a city, for all its overwhelming strangeness, was just one more set of conditions to adapt to, and the adapting, for once, was upward, toward light and abundance and the marvel of electricity, rather than downward into hunger and fear.

There was something profound happening underneath the wonder of those city months, something I could feel even as a child without having words for it, which was that my family was, for the first time in years, beginning to reassemble itself. For so long we had been scattered, my father in his flight, my mother in her exile, my brother and I in the rotating custody of relatives, the family blown apart by violence and rumor and poverty into separate pieces in separate places. And now, in a rented room in Juja, the pieces were coming back together, my mother and my father and my brother and me under one roof again, eating from the same pot, sleeping in the same room, watching the same stomach TV, and after the long scattering there was a sweetness to that simple togetherness that I cannot fully convey, the sweetness of a family that has been broken and is, tentatively, against the odds, mending. It did not last, and the mending was never complete, and there were storms still to come, but for those few months in the city I had something I had nearly forgotten the feeling of, which was a family in one place, parents within reach, the basic ordinary miracle of belonging to a household that actually contained the people you belonged to.

I have come to see those Juja months as a kind of hinge in my childhood, the turning point between the years of being scattered and abandoned and the years of being, however imperfectly, gathered and built. Before Juja I was a child the world was happening to, blown about by forces far larger than myself, the flight of my father and the exile of my mother and the cruelty of relatives, a leaf on the floodwater. In Juja, and in what came after it, I began to be a child with a direction, a child being deliberately gathered up and pointed somewhere by people who had decided to invest in him, and the difference between those two conditions, between being blown about and being aimed, is the difference between drowning and swimming. The city did not just show me electricity and skyscrapers and tomatoes. It showed me my own family choosing, against everything, to come back together around me, and that choosing was the first solid ground I had stood on in years.

But that season in Juja was brief, a matter of months, and it ended with a decision that set the entire next chapter of my life, because when the time came to return to school, my brother and I refused, flatly, with the full force of a child’s rare absolute refusal, to go back to Mahemas. We had tasted something else. We had seen the city and the electricity and the shoes, and more than that we had felt, in my grandfather’s library and my grandmother’s car in the rain, the pull of a different kind of life, a life with books and light and people who asked about your feet, and we did not want to return to the mud school in the bushes and the cane and the hunger. We wanted to stay in Lumakanda, with the white grandfather who had stopped his car in the rain, the grandfather with the library and the electricity and the patience for a boy’s bad grammar. And that refusal, that small fierce planting of two pairs of feet, the same feet that had been bare in the rain at Lugari Line, now shod in blue plastic and unwilling to walk the old road again, carried us out of one life and into another. It carried us into the township years, into the library that did not judge my grammar, into the people who would build me on purpose. But that, as I keep promising you, is the next part, and it begins where this one ends, with two boys in blue sandals deciding that they were not going back, and a family, against all the odds of its poverty and its scattering, slowly, painfully, beginning to gather itself back together around them.

BORN BROKE, BUILT LOUD

Eugine Micah

*Until the lions have their own historians, the history of the hunt will always glorify the hunter.*

A proverb, carried to the world by Chinua Achebe

**Author’s Note**

I need to tell you three things before you give me your time, because I respect it too much to let you spend it under the wrong impression.

The first is that this is not a success story. I know how it looks from the outside, the screen and the talking and the confidence, and I know the shape the world wants this book to take, the rise from nothing to something, the neat arc with the bow on top. But I am writing this still in the middle of it, still climbing, still broke in ways money has not fixed and may never fix. If you came for a man who arrived, you came too early. I have not arrived. I am simply far enough up the road to be able to see where I started, and honest enough to describe both.

The second is that I have changed some names. The women especially. They were young when I knew them and most of them did not choose a public life the way I did, so where I tell you about love and the foolish things I did in its name, the names you read are not the names they answer to. The stories are true. The people are protected. That is the deal I made with my conscience and I am keeping it.

The third is that memory is a liar with good intentions. I have checked what I could, asked my mother the things only she remembers, sat with documents and dates. But a childhood is not a court transcript. Where I have had to choose between the fact and the feeling, I have tried to keep both, and where they disagree, I have told you so. This is the truest version of me I am able to write today. Give me ten more years and I would write a slightly different one, because I will be a slightly different man. That is not dishonesty. That is just being alive.

Now. You do not know me. Let me fix that.

# PROLOGUE: A WORD BEFORE WE START

Let us get one thing straight before you turn another page. You do not know me. Maybe you have seen a face on a screen somewhere, a loud one, talking fast about the news of the day with a confidence that looks like it was always there. It was not always there. That confidence is the receipt. This book is the bill, itemized, and I am going to read you every line.

My name is Eugine Micah, although that is not the name they wrote down when I arrived, and I will get to that, because it is a better story when you have to wait for it. For now here is the only thing you need: I was born with nothing. Not the poetic kind of nothing that comfortable people say at dinners to sound humble while a waiter tops up their wine. The literal kind. My mother delivered me without a single coin to her name, in a hospital she could not pay for, and survived those first days on hot water dressed up as tea and a few arrowroots. That is the sort of detail you do not forget, mostly because your stomach files it under permanent.

People want this to be a motivational story. I understand the appetite. The boy who had nothing now has something, cue the music, sell the mug with the quote on it. But I did not write this to motivate you, and if you came for a self help pamphlet you have grabbed the wrong book off the shelf and I would gently point you three shelves down. This is just a true story. It happens to contain motivation the way a body happens to contain blood, not as the point but as the thing keeping it alive. It also contains a great deal of foolishness, several beatings I richly deserved and a few I did not, one cracked skull belonging to my brother, an entire economy built on cow dung, a girl whose smell I have been chasing in other women for a decade, a school that a white American built out of pure stubborn belief, and two coffins that arrived seven days apart and took the best people I have ever known.

I am going to tell it the way it happened, which means it will be funny in places where you will feel slightly guilty for laughing, and heavy in places where you are still chuckling from the line before. That is not a flaw in the writing. That is just how it was to be me. In my house the same afternoon could hold a hymn, a slap, a joke, and a funeral procession passing on the road, and nobody found that strange. I am only keeping the arrangement intact.

And I am going to tell you something that the shape of my life might tempt you to forget, so let me plant it here on the first page where you cannot miss it. I have not made it. I am still trying to make it. This is the report from a man halfway up a hill he is not sure has a top, written while he is still climbing, sweating, occasionally slipping. If that disappoints you, I am sorry, but I would rather disappoint you with the truth than flatter you with a lie. The lie is what every other version of my story has been, the polished one, the inspirational one, the one with the bow. This one has no bow. This one has me, out of breath, pointing back down the slope at the dust I came from and saying, look, can you believe it, can you believe a person starts there.

So no, you do not know me. Not yet. But by the last page you will know me better than some people who have shared my surname for twenty four years. Let us begin where I began, which, fittingly for a man who talks for a living, is with a crime I was part of before I could speak.

# PART ONE: THE LAND THAT MADE ME

## Chapter One: The First Crime I Ever Took Part In, I Committed From Inside My Mother

The first operation I was ever an accomplice to, I pulled off while still a passenger inside my mother, which I would like noted in my defense before any court, because I could not have consented and I certainly did not drive.

Here is what happened, and I have made my mother tell it enough times that I can now narrate it like I was standing there with a clipboard. My aunt, Joy Ob’bayi, my father’s sister, had a daughter named Velma. Joy and the child’s father had separated, in the way these things separated back then, which is to say messily, with the child becoming territory rather than a person. Velma was living among her father’s people. Joy ached for her daughter the specific way a mother aches when another household is raising the child she carried, a hunger that does not respond to reason. She wanted time with her own girl and could not get it through the front door. So she decided to get her through the side.

And she needed an accomplice. She chose my mother. My mother, Beverlyne, who at this point was not some seasoned operative but a teenager, barely seventeen, and, crucial detail, pregnant. With me. Picture the planning meeting. Two young women, one of them carrying a full term human being out front like a smuggled watermelon, sitting down to organize what was, if we are being strict about the law, a kidnapping. I prefer the word extraction. Extraction sounds like there were professionals involved. There were not. There was my aunt, my very pregnant mother, and unborn me, the silent fourth member of the crew who would later make an entire career out of being loud.

The plan was simple in the way that only desperate plans are. They went to where Velma was staying, near a river, and they were sweet to the relatives, all smiles, just here to see the child, you know how it is. Then one of them said the magic sentence that has launched a thousand schemes since the beginning of human cunning: let me just take her for a little walk, I want to talk to her. And they walked. And they kept walking. And the walk quietly became a different thing entirely, which was an escape, with a small girl in tow and a smaller passenger in front, moving fast toward the main road we called the stage, the tarmac artery that is actually the Kenya to Uganda highway, the road that connected our whole small world to everywhere else that existed.

From a town near our home called Lumakanda, where I would later do most of my growing up, they caught a bus. And not a short bus. They rode that thing south and west, past Kakamega town, the whole way down to Kisumu on the shore of Lake Victoria, a journey of well over a hundred kilometers on roads that in those days punished you for the crime of using them. My aunt worked in Kisumu as a nurse. Kisumu was the safe house. So that is the picture I want you to hold: a getaway by public transport, no money for food along the way, a stolen afternoon turning into a stolen child turning into a brand new life, and my mother feeling the first contractions of her own future somewhere on that long, broke, swaying bus.

Now turn the camera around, because the funniest and the saddest part is happening back home at the very same time. My father, Joab, comes looking for his pregnant wife and finds that she has vanished. Not gone to the market vanished. Gone vanished. And remember the technology of that era, which was none. No phone in a pocket to call and ask where are you. No way on this earth to know whether his wife and his unborn first child were alive, in labor, lost, or lying in a ditch somewhere between here and the lake. My father, who I will spend a whole chapter telling you was a lion, a man the village had literally nicknamed for his ferocity, received this news and did the one thing a lion is never supposed to do.

He fainted.

He hit the ground. The shock folded him in half and dropped him where he stood. My mother tells this part now with a small smile that took her years to earn, because at the time there was nothing funny about a man collapsing from terror over a woman he could not find, but with enough decades stacked between you and a thing, even fear softens into a story you can hold in your hand. My father fainted over me before he ever held me, which is, I have decided after long consideration, the most tender thing he ever did for me without using his hands, both of which he would later use on me with great and memorable generosity.

And then, a few days after that bus pulled into Kisumu, I arrived.

I was born on the twenty fourth of December, 2001. Christmas Eve. The whole world tuning up to celebrate a famous baby born broke in a borrowed room to young parents far from home, and into that exact theme, right on cue, came me. Born broke in a borrowed bed to young parents far from home. The off brand version. No wise men, no star, no gold, just a teenage mother with empty pockets in a hospital that everybody called Russia.

I have to explain the hospital, because the hospital is a joke that God set up and then waited about twenty years to let me get to. Its official name is the Jaramogi Oginga Odinga Teaching and Referral Hospital, a name so long that saying the whole thing is a small cardiovascular event. But nobody said the whole thing. Everybody called it Russia, because the Soviet Union built it back in the nineteen sixties as a gift to a young Kenya during the Cold War, a fat concrete handshake from Moscow to a new nation deciding which way to lean. And here is the part I love so much I want to frame it. The Soviet Union collapsed in 1991. So by the time I was born inside it in 2001, the country the hospital was named after had been dead for a full decade. I made my entrance into the world inside a monument to a nation that no longer existed, named for an empire that had already fallen and been buried. If you sat down with the deliberate intention of designing the perfect birthplace for a boy whose entire life would turn out to be about outliving the things that were supposed to define and limit him, you could not improve on it. I was born in the ruins of one dead dream. It turned out to be excellent training for building my own.

We had nothing. I am going to keep saying it plainly throughout this book, because the temptation is always to soften it, and I refuse the softening. My mother did not have a cent. The hospital in those days did not treat you out of kindness, it treated you out of payment, and there was no payment. It was my aunt Joy, the nurse, the same woman whose mission had dragged my pregnant mother all the way to Kisumu in the first place, who quietly covered the bill. There is a circularity in that which I have come to find almost holy. The extraction that started everything also financed my arrival. My mother survived those first days on what she calls strong tea, which sounds cozy and grandmotherly until you learn that it is simply tea leaves boiled in water with sugar, no milk, because milk was a luxury we could not reach, and a few arrowroots, and some mandazi that Joy carried to her bedside. That was the welcome banquet thrown in my honor. Boiled water with ambition, and fried dough by donation.

They did not name me Eugine Micah. That came later, and in pieces. On the day I was born I was Eugine Ob’bayi, carrying my father’s family name straight off the production line. And before even that, before any certificate, my parents had wanted to call me John. They abandoned the idea quickly, because in our village the name John was attached to one or two characters whose reputations you would not wish on a stray goat, let alone your firstborn son. So John was scrapped on the grounds of bad local branding. I have always found this particular fact delicious, because years and years later a boy named John would walk into my life and walk back out of it holding my first real heartbreak in his hands, and I have wondered in my pettier moments whether my parents dodged a curse at the naming and then had it delivered to me anyway, much later, through the back door. We will get there. Be patient. I am.

The Micah, the part that turned Eugine Ob’bayi into Eugine Micah, came from much higher up the family tree. It came from my great grandfather, the Reverend Micah Ob’bayi of Bunyore, a man of the pulpit from the early days of the Church of God among our people, a patriarch whose name carried real weight in a way that mine would have to earn from absolute scratch. So when my name finally settled into Eugine Micah, it was quietly carrying a reverend inside it. Eugine, I am told, means noble, born of a noble line. Micah is a question, an old one, who is like God. Nobility and divinity, both stitched into a boy who would spend the first decade of his life plastering the floor of his classroom with cow dung. The universe has a sense of humor, and it had apparently been writing me as the punchline for some time before I showed up to hear it.

Out there beyond the maternity ward, my country was sitting at a hinge of its own. Kenya in December of 2001 was in the last full year of Daniel arap Moi, a man who had been president for twenty three years, which is longer than my teenage mother had been alive, a reign so long that to a child it did not feel like a government at all, it felt like weather, like a fixed feature of the sky. The very next year he would hand over power and the country would exhale into something it had not been before. So I was born at the seam of everything. An old century had just ended. An old presidency was about to. An old empire whose name was bolted to the wall above my mother’s bed was already gone. And into all of that ending, squalling and dark and tiny and broke, arrived a beginning. Loud, apparently, from the first breath, because my mother swears that I came out of her announcing myself, and I have decided to believe her, partly because it flatters me and partly because it has simply never once stopped being true.

There is an afterward to this story that nobody tells, because birth stories usually stop at the birth, but a baby has to get home, and getting me home was its own small odyssey. Kisumu sits low and hot on the lakeshore, on the edge of Lake Victoria, the second largest freshwater lake on the whole planet, a body of water so vast that three countries share its shoreline and argue, mostly politely, about the fish. Home was none of that. Home was up and away, more than a hundred and twenty kilometers north and west, into the cool red highlands of Lugari, a different climate, a different altitude, a different world entirely. So my mother, days after giving birth with no money, had to gather her newborn and make the long journey back up the bad roads, from the humid lake to the cold maize country, carrying me into the very situation she had fled, where a father waited who had collapsed at the news of her disappearance and now had to reckon with her return, and with me.

I have tried to imagine that reunion and I cannot quite do it. A teenage mother walks back into a homestead holding the firstborn son of a man who fainted from fear of losing them both. What do you even say. There is no script for it. Whatever was said, it held, because they stayed together, and the staying together is the only reason the rest of this book exists. They were not a calm couple, my parents. I will be honest about that throughout. But in that moment, with a new life between them, they chose forward, and I have spent my life being grateful for a decision two frightened young people made before I could thank them for it.

The country I had been born into was about to change its own life too. Within a year of my arrival, Daniel arap Moi would step down, ending an era so long that ending it felt almost unnatural, like the sun deciding to set in a new direction, and Kenya would lurch into a new political season full of hope that would later curdle, as hope in this country has a habit of doing, into the violence I will tell you about when I am a little older in this story. But in those first months I knew none of it. I knew the inside of a donated jumper, the smell of woodsmoke, the cold of a highland night seeping through mud walls, and the particular safety of being a baby who does not yet understand how thin the line was between his arrival and his never arriving at all.

That is how I got here. Smuggled into the world on the back of somebody else’s rescue mission, born on the busiest birthday of the entire calendar to a mother running on sugar water, in a hospital named after a country that had ceased to exist, briefly almost called John, permanently saved from it, and christened in the end with a dead reverend’s name like a small unearned deposit of grace into an account that otherwise held nothing at all.

Welcome to my life. I will be honest with you, it does not get more sensible from here. It gets, if anything, considerably more difficult, and considerably funnier, and I would not hand you a single page of it if I did not believe with my whole chest that you would walk away better for having read it.

The red dust of Lugari was waiting for me up north. Let me take you home.

## Chapter Two: The Republic of Red Dust

If you want to understand a man, do not start with the man. Start with the dirt he came from, because the dirt was there first and it does not lie about anything.

My dirt is red. I mean that literally. The soil of Lugari, up in the highlands of what was then Western Province and is now Kakamega County, is a deep iron red, the kind that stains the soles of your feet and the hems of your shorts and, if you grow up in it, something a little deeper than that too. It is good soil, fertile, generous when the rain behaves, and the whole region runs on it. Maize taller than a tall man. Beans, sugarcane, sweet potatoes, the green and the gold of a place that feeds itself and a good portion of the country besides. I was raised on that red ground, in a village called Manyonyi, in the Marakusi corner of Lugari, and I want to tell you what that place actually is, because most people who end up on a screen come from somewhere they would prefer you imagine rather than know, and I am the opposite. I want you to know it exactly.

Lugari is not an old ancestral homeland in the way people romanticize. It is, if anything, a more interesting thing than that. It is a made place, a settled place, a republic of borrowed land. After independence the government carved up large stretches of forest into settlement schemes and moved people onto them, and over the decades Lugari filled up with more than ten different Kenyan communities all living shoulder to shoulder on plots that had been measured out by a surveyor rather than handed down by a great great grandfather. We bordered Uasin Gishu, the edge of Kalenjin country, close enough that you could feel two worlds leaning against each other. So I did not grow up in a pure place, a one tribe place, a place sealed in its own traditions. I grew up in a cosmopolitan stew of a village where the man on the next plot might speak a completely different mother tongue, and everybody farmed, and everybody was, in one way or another, a person whose family had arrived from somewhere else and decided to build a home out of trees and patience. I think that did something to me. I think being from a place that was itself assembled out of strangers and effort, rather than inherited whole, is part of why I have never been afraid to assemble myself the same way, out of whatever was lying around.

The house I was born into was made of mud. Not mud as a charming rustic accent. Mud as the entire structure. Walls of mud packed over a frame of sticks, a roof of grass thatch in the early years, the floor a smoothed surface of earth. It was a round hut to start, the traditional kind, and my girlfriend, years later, a city girl, told me she had never in her life seen a thatched mud house and could barely believe people had lived in one within her own lifetime, in her own country. To her it was history. To me it was simply the address. We lived in that grass thatched mud house for years, more years than I can count cleanly, and the things I remember about it are not sad to me even though I understand now that they should probably register as hardship.

We slept, when I was very new, on dried banana leaves. There was no mattress in the house when I was born. My mother tells me that to keep me warm through the cold Lugari nights, which get genuinely cold up in those highlands in a way that surprises people who think all of Africa is a furnace, she would zip me into a big jumper, fold my little arms in, and leave only my face poking out, and that is how I slept, a baby burrito in donated wool, breathing the night air of a house with no electricity and no glass in the windows. The cooking happened on three stones. You arrange three stones in a triangle, you build your fire in the middle of them, you balance your pot on top, and that is your kitchen, the same kitchen humans have used for about as long as there have been humans and pots. On those three stones my mother cooked ugali and boiled arrowroots and fried whatever there was to fry, and the smoke of that fire is one of the first smells I knew, woodsmoke and ugali and the particular green smell of rain on its way.

The floor taught me something about the difference between poor and beaten. Our floor, in the later house, was finished with cow dung, which I will explain in proper and loving detail later because it deserves its own chapter, but the short version is that a dung floor, properly maintained, is smooth and clean and even pleasant. The problem was the nights when the room had not been sealed well, because then the safari ants came. If you have never met a safari ant you have been spared a specific education. They are small and red and they move in armies and they bite, and they bite at night, and waking up at three in the morning to the sensation of a battalion of them feasting on your neck is a memory my body still holds even though my mind would happily release it. You learn to seal a room properly when the alternative is being eaten alive in installments.

We got out of the mud and into iron sheets around 2010. My father worked himself raw to make it happen, and the house, an iron sheet house, the dream house, cost something like fifty thousand shillings. I want to put that number in its true frame, because to a reader in a city or another country fifty thousand shillings is a phone, a weekend, a nothing. In a village like ours, to a family like ours, fifty thousand shillings was a fortune assembled coin by coin over years of refusing to give up. It was the difference between a roof that announced your poverty to every passerby and a roof that bought you a little dignity. My father bled for that iron. I watched him do it. I will tell you how later.

Water was its own long story, and it was the story of the whole region, not just my family. Lugari and the sub locations around it, Manyonyi and Marakusi, struggled for water for decades, an honest, documented, generational struggle that the local authorities are only now, in my adulthood, finally addressing with proper community water projects. In my childhood there was no tap that mattered. Water came from a borehole if you were near one, or from the local stream, where the water collected at a pump the villagers had rigged, a metal pipe driven into the place where the water came up out of the ground, running day and night. We called it the pombo, our own worn down version of the word pump. You went to the pombo with a jerrican and you carried home what your arms could manage, and for the cattle you improvised, throwing a jerrican rigged with a wire handle down into the borehole and hauling it up, again and again, until the troughs were full. I have carried more water in my life than most people will ever see, and I did not think of it as suffering at the time. I thought of it as Tuesday.

And then there were the chickens. Free range chickens, which means chickens that were not pampered in cages but turned loose every morning to fend for themselves and hunted by everything in the sky, hawks and eagles patrolling for an easy lunch. A chicken at our home was not a casual thing. A chicken was honor. A chicken was the centerpiece of any event that mattered, slaughtered for a guest you wished to dignify, for a celebration, for a homecoming. To eat chicken was to be, for one meal, a person of consequence. I mention them now because those chickens become important later, when I tell you about the first audience I ever performed for, an audience of poultry with no interest in me whatsoever, but that too is a later chapter.

I have to tell you how we saw the world, because for a village child in the early two thousands the world arrived through very specific and very narrow windows, and what came through those windows we believed completely, with our whole chests, no questions asked.

The first window was the radio, a small portable thing that ran on batteries and crackled, and through it came news and music and the voices of a country that felt impossibly far from our hill. The second window, the magic one, was the screen, and screens were rare and therefore sacred. There was a television at a neighbor’s place, and a television at my grandfather’s place in Lumakanda, and to watch one you had to either be invited or be clever, and we were usually clever. On that screen, on the national broadcaster, came wrestling, the loud theatrical American kind, and we watched grown men in costumes throw each other around and we took it as gospel truth, every staged blow a real one, every scripted feud an actual war between actual enemies. And then there was DJ Afro.

If you did not grow up in Kenya in those years I cannot fully explain DJ Afro to you, but I will try, because he is part of how I became me. DJ Afro was a narrator, a man who took foreign films, action movies and Indian films and Chinese films and Korean and Vietnamese and Tanzanian and American ones, films in languages no village child could follow, and he narrated over them in our own language, explaining and exaggerating and dramatizing, turning a confusing foreign picture into a thrilling local experience. He made the whole world’s cinema legible to a boy in Lugari for the price of ten shillings at a movie shack. And before even DJ Afro, before the shacks, there were the community screenings, and these I remember like a holy festival. Someone would bring a projector and a generator, or a big battery charged in a distant town, and they would hang a white cloth or a bedsheet between two poles, and as darkness fell the whole village would gather on the ground in front of that glowing sheet, and images would pour out of the machine onto the cloth, powered by a generator coughing in the dark, and we would sit transfixed while the rest of the world flickered to life in a field in Manyonyi.

And here is the thing about all of it, the radio and the wrestling and DJ Afro and the bedsheet cinema. We believed it. Not in the way a city child half believes, knowing somewhere in the back of the mind that it is a show. We believed it the way you believe the ground. If we watched a wrestling match or a movie, we did not simply enjoy it, we absorbed it as fact, and then we did the most important thing, the thing that made me a storyteller before I knew the word. We narrated it. The next day at school, the kids who had seen the thing would retell it to the kids who had not, point by point, blow by blow, and the retelling was never faithful, it was inflated, dramatized, improved, packed with lies and exaggerations that made the story bigger and better than the original, and your standing among your peers depended directly on how well you performed the retelling. I was good at the retelling. I was very good at it. I did not know I was training. I thought I was just showing off. I was doing both.

This is the part of the dust I am most grateful for. A rich child has the whole world handed to him through a screen in his pocket, frictionless, infinite, and he never has to work to possess any of it. I had to fight for every image I ever saw, sneak for it, pay ten shillings for it, sit in a field for it, and then, crucially, I had to give it back out loud to be anybody at all. The scarcity made me a teller. If the world had come to me easily I would have consumed it quietly like everyone with easy access does. Because it came hard, I learned to pass it on, performed and embellished and alive, and that skill, born of having almost nothing, is the exact skill I now sell.

That was the baseline, the floor, the every single day of it. A good day had a little more, a better vegetable, a splash of cooking oil that was actually enough rather than rationed down to a memory of oil. And the bad days, the days there was simply nothing in the pot and nothing in the farm because the season had turned against us, I will tell you about those too, because hunger was a regular guest in my childhood and I will not pretend it knocked politely and waited to be invited. But here is the thing I most want you to take from this chapter, the thing the red dust taught me before I had words for it. Christmas, my own birthday, the day of my arrival, meant something very specific in our house, and it was not gifts and it was not snow and it was not a fat man in a red suit. Christmas meant good food. It meant meat, actual meat, and chapati, and mandazi, and some juice diluted down with water to make it stretch across all of us. That was the whole feast. That was the summit of the year. And we were, on that day, genuinely, uncomplicatedly happy, happier over a plate of meat and a cup of watery juice than many people will ever be over far more, because we knew exactly what it had cost to get there and we knew it would be gone tomorrow.

I have eaten in good restaurants now. I have been handed menus in places where the prices are a quiet act of aggression. And I will tell you, with my hand on my chest, that nothing has ever tasted the way that Christmas meat tasted in the mud house in Manyonyi, on the red dust, by the light of a kerosene lamp. Nothing. The dust gave me a baseline of joy so low to the ground that almost everything since has felt like abundance. That is the gift hidden inside the hardship, and it took me half my life to unwrap it.

## Chapter Three: The Secret in the Family Tree

Every family has a story it tells out loud and a story it keeps in the rafters, and the second story is always the truer one. Mine kept a beautiful, complicated secret in the rafters, and the secret is this: my mother was given away, and the people who gave her away and the people who received her are all, somehow, my grandparents, and I love every one of them, and untangling exactly how I am related to my own relatives is a task that has defeated better mathematicians than me.

Let me try anyway, slowly, because you need this map to follow the rest of the book.

Start with my mother, Beverlyne. She was born to Samson Kemoli and Josephine Kemoli. Samson, my grandfather, is a man who built a place and put his own name on it, a homestead called Kemoview, because his name is Kemoli and the man had the audacity and the humor to look at his patch of earth and christen it like an estate, Kemo’s view, a king naming his small kingdom. Josephine, my grandmother, his wife, was a woman of the church, a pastor, a person who prayed the way other people breathe. These two are my mother’s blood. Her origin. The well she was drawn from.

But Beverlyne was not raised by Samson and Josephine. She was given to Josephine’s sister. That sister was my grandmother Gladys, Gladys Kamonya, and Gladys was married to a man named David Zarembka, and here the story tilts and becomes something almost no other family in Lugari could claim, because David Zarembka was a white American. Let me say that again so it lands. My grandfather, the man who raised my mother, the man who would later become the single most important human being in my own development, was an American. A Quaker. A man from across an ocean who had ended up in our red dust and married into our family and become, in every way that counts except the one written on a birth certificate, my mother’s father and my own grandfather.

So my mother had two sets of parents. The blood set, Samson and Josephine of Kemoview, who brought her into the world and then handed her over. And the raising set, Gladys and David Zarembka, who took her in and made her theirs. There was nothing legal about it, no court, no papers, no judge with a stamp. It was simply a thing that came to be, arranged between two sisters in the way our families arrange enormous things, with conversation and custom and a handshake of the heart, and then it was true forever. I have stopped asking exactly how they decided it. Some agreements are too old and too deep to audit. I just know the result. I know that when I picture my mother as a girl, I picture her at David and Gladys’s place, being shaped by an American Quaker’s library and a Kenyan pastor’s discipline, and I know that David and Gladys had children of their own too, Tommy and Joy Mutanu Zarembka, who are therefore my mother’s siblings and my aunt and uncle, and who will matter enormously near the end of this book, when almost everyone else is gone.

Now cross over to my father’s side, which is its own country.

My father, Joab Philemona Etale Ob’bayi, was born to Rosemary Ongoche Ob’bayi and Philemona Jairo Micah Ob’bayi. My grandmother Rosemary was, like seemingly every powerful woman in my bloodline, a person of the church, a reverend in the Church of God, a woman who would take me to far flung congregations and sit me at the front while she ministered. My grandfather Philemona carried in his own name the name of his father, and his father is the deepest root I can reach and the most important one for understanding me. His father, my great grandfather, was the Reverend Micah Ob’bayi of Bunyore.

Bunyore is in Vihiga, the heartland of the Abanyole people, and it is one of the places where Christianity first took root among the Luhya, where the Church of God planted itself early and grew. The Reverend Micah Ob’bayi was part of that early native clergy, the first generations of our own people to take up the pulpit, to weave the new faith together with the old Luhya world, to become leaders in a time of enormous change. He was a patriarch. He founded a line. And I carry his name, Micah, the question that asks who is like God, handed down across three generations to land on a boy reading by a kerosene lamp who had no idea, for most of his childhood, that his middle name was an inheritance from a reverend rather than just a sound his parents happened to like.

My father, unlike my mother, was never adopted by anyone. He had no Gladys to take him in, no soft landing arranged between sisters. He was, as he likes to put it in his harder moods, adopted by the world. He made himself out of the raw materials of being a firstborn son in a farming family, which in our tradition is less an honor than a sentence to responsibility, and I will tell you all about that in the next chapter, because the man deserves his own.

And then there is the matter of blood and tribe, which in Kenya is never far from the surface and which I have decided to state plainly rather than dance around. On my father’s side I am Abanyole, of the Abatongoi clan, traced back through the generations to an ancestor of the Anyole people, rooted in Bunyore, of the great Bantu stream that flowed into the fertile highlands of western Kenya centuries ago and stayed. On my mother’s side, through the Kemoli line, I carry the Maragoli thread, the Avamaseero, another branch of the Luhya world, a people with a near religious devotion to education that I believe travelled directly down into my mother and out of her into me. So I am a meeting point. Abatongoi and Avamaseero. Bunyore and Maragoli. Two clans, two streams, joined in one dark skinned boy in a mud house, carrying a reverend’s name and a teacher’s hunger for books, raised by an American and a pastor and a king who named his own hill.

I want to pause on David Zarembka, because a white American Quaker landing in a Lugari family tree is the kind of detail that sounds invented, and he is the most real person I know.

He did not come to us as a tourist or a missionary in the pith helmet sense. He came as a man who had given his life to peace work in this part of Africa, who believed, with the deep stubborn conviction of his Quaker faith, that ordinary people could be trained to refuse violence even when violence was all around them, and who put that belief into practice in the most dangerous seasons our region ever knew. He had education most of our village could only dream of, and he carried it lightly, and he married into us and stayed, and he built a life on our red dust that bridged two worlds that were never supposed to touch. To the village he was the Mzungu, the white man, a figure of fascination who drove a car when almost no one had one.
