We were not children who could melt into a phone or a tablet, because there were no phones or tablets, and the television was rationed to almost nothing by my grandmother. So the hours that a modern child pours into a glowing screen, I poured into pages, because pages were the entertainment that was not forbidden, not rationed, not guarded. Reading was the one indulgence I could binge without anyone twisting my ear, and so I binged it. I read my way through that library the way other children worked through cartoons, compulsively, hungrily, one book bleeding into the next, and somewhere in that compulsion the love stopped being a substitute for the screen and became the real thing, the central pleasure, the appetite that would feed a career.

And I want to be precise about what David’s belief actually felt like, because belief is a word people throw around and it usually means nothing. David Zarembka’s belief in me was not a speech. He never sat me down and told me I was destined for greatness, that is not how the man operated. His belief was structural, practical, daily. It was the door of the library left open. It was the patience when I read badly. It was, later and most decisively, the school fees, which I will tell you about in their place, the money that materialized when there was no other money, against the wishes of people who had given up on my parents, because one old man had decided that this particular poor abandoned boy was worth educating. Belief, from David, was not words. It was a standing offer of resources and patience and an open door, renewed every single day without fuss, and that, I have learned, is the only kind of belief that actually builds a person. The kind made of speeches evaporates. The kind made of open doors and paid fees and unflinching patience builds a man who can stand on national television twenty years later and still feel, behind his own ribs, the warmth of a library where being wrong did not hurt.

but he had the means to act on the belief at a scale she never could, and I would learn, much later and to my astonishment, that his love of schools was not abstract. The man built them. He gave his life to learning and to peace in this region in ways I only fully understood as I grew, and the fact that I have loved a classroom, loved a library, loved the act of learning my entire life, traces directly back to a white American Quaker who handed a poor black boy a book and then refused to flog him for reading it imperfectly. He believed in me when there was very little evidence on which to base a belief. He looked at a frightened, abandoned, hungry child mangling English in his office and he saw something worth feeding with books, and he fed it, and I am, in large part, what that feeding grew. I cannot tell you yet how his story ends, because the ending is a grief I am keeping for its proper place near the close of this book. For now, hold him as I hold him in my happiest memories: in his library, listening to me read badly, never flinching, the one adult in my childhood who made being wrong feel like the beginning of something instead of the end.

## Chapter Fifteen: Love in Work Clothes

My grandmother Gladys loved me, and for years I did not know it, because her love arrived disguised as rules so strict and discipline so firm that I mistook the whole thing for severity, and it took me into adulthood to read the disguise correctly and see the love that had been wearing it the entire time.

The household David and Gladys ran was, by the standards of my chaotic childhood, a place of order, and the order had laws. There was no watching of crime movies, no films full of guns, and we were not even to discuss violence in the house, because my grandfather was a peacemaker to his core and his home was to be a peaceful place, an embassy of nonviolence in a region that knew too much of the other thing. To boys, of course, this was torture, because violence is catnip to boys, the male hormones do their work, and the forbidden films were exactly the films we craved. So we became smugglers of cinema. We snuck off to friends’ houses to watch what we could not watch at home, or we paid ten shillings to watch a whole movie at one of the little movie shacks, gorging on the DJ Afro narrations and the action we were starved of, always with the delicious fear of getting caught.

And caught we got, by a figure who deserves her own small monument in this book, the house help, Nancy. Nancy ran the domestic order of that home like a sergeant, and she had a particular gift for catching us mid sin, materializing exactly when the forbidden movie was at its best, and she would seize us by the ears, literally by the ears, and march us home, lecturing the whole way, I do not want to see you watching this. And here is the part that shames me a little now, because we were arrogant boys who had not yet learned humility. We resented being disciplined by a house help. To our foolish young minds there was a hierarchy, and a house girl was not supposed to be at the top of it, and we would protest, who is she to punish us, as though Nancy were not, in every functional sense, one of the adults raising us, as though her authority were somehow less real than blood. I was wrong about that, completely, and I know it now, and if Nancy ever reads this I want her to know that the boy whose ears she twisted grew up to understand that she was right and he was an idiot, and that pulling a foolish boy out of trouble by the ear is itself a form of love, even when it is also a job.

Let me give you the texture of the rules, because in the abstract they sound merely strict and in the living they were a whole climate. The house ran on time and on order and on a moral seriousness that radiated from my grandfather’s Quaker faith and my grandmother’s iron administration of it. Meals happened when they happened. Prayers happened. Chores were not optional and were inspected. Idleness was suspicious. A boy in that house was always, in some low background way, accountable, and accountability is exhausting to a child who has known the wild ungoverned freedom of a village where the parents are gone and no one is really watching. I had come from a world of too little supervision into a world of a great deal of it, and the adjustment chafed, and I rebelled in the small ways available to me, the sneaking, the forbidden films, the resentment of Nancy, the constant low friction of a wild thing being domesticated.

But here is what I could not see then and see clearly now. A child raised with no rules at all is not free, he is abandoned, and I had tasted that abandonment in the years my parents were gone, and it had nearly drowned me. Gladys’s rules, suffocating as they felt, were the opposite of abandonment. They were attention. They were a grown woman deciding that my behavior mattered enough to govern, that I was worth the enormous daily labor of correction, and labor it was, because raising someone else’s children, year after year, with no help and no end in sight, is one of the heaviest unpaid jobs a human being can take on. She did it until she could not. There came a day, and I will tell it honestly when we reach it, when Gladys reached the end of her strength and put us out, called us names born of pure exhaustion, and sent us down the road to Kemoview. I did not understand it then as anything but rejection. I understand it now as the breaking point of a woman who had carried too much for too long with too little thanks. The love had been real. So had the exhaustion. Both things were true, the way both things are usually true, and the boy who felt only the rejection grew into a man who can finally see the years of unthanked labor underneath it.

She rationed it severely. We were not children who melted into a screen for hours. And because the easy entertainment was withheld, because the screen was rationed, we turned to the thing that was not rationed, the thing that filled the gap, which was reading. I have come to believe that my entire literary appetite, my love of books that fed straight into a career built on words, was a side effect of my grandmother refusing to let me rot in front of a television. She did not hand me a love of reading. She withheld the alternative until reading was what remained, and reading turned out to be the better drug, and I got hooked on it for life, and the hooking was her doing whether she intended it or not. That is what I mean by love in work clothes. The rules looked like control. They were construction. She was building a person, brick by brick, rule by rule, in a household where being a child meant being shaped rather than simply being indulged, and the discipline that felt like coldness at the time reveals itself now, from the distance of years, as the most patient kind of care. She would, in time, grow tired of the burden of raising her absent daughter’s sons and she would reach her limit and there would be a hard rupture, and I will tell you about that, because it is true and I do not hide the true things. But underneath even the rupture was a woman who had spent years building me with rules because rules were the tools she had, and a person built well, even harshly, is still a person who got built. Not every child is so lucky as to be flogged into shape by people who, it turns out, were trying to save him.

## Chapter Sixteen: Kemoview

My grandfather Samson named his home after himself, Kemoview, and the more I have lived, the more I have come to think that naming a poor man’s patch of red earth like an English estate is one of the most quietly magnificent acts of defiance I have ever encountered, and he did it without irony, with total seriousness, because the man understood something about claiming your ground that I would spend my whole career relearning.

Samson Kemoli was, before he was my grandfather, a servant of the country. He had been a police officer, a corporal, who served Kenya for years that I never fully mapped, until an injury to his leg, the result of an accident, ended his service and left him with a limp he carried for the rest of his life and a story he carried with even more care. I was always curious about that leg, the way children are curious about the visible mysteries of adults, and getting the story out of him was like mining, because he was a man of few words about his own pain. He had given his body to the service of a country that gave him a small pension and a permanent limp in return, and he came home to Kemoview, to the hill he had named, and he became the keeper of the family’s older world, the traditions, the cattle, the way things had been done before the township and the white grandfather and the books.

Life at Kemoview was the most rural and, in some ways, the most purely joyful stretch of my whole childhood, the part that felt least like surviving and most like simply being a boy. There were cattle to herd, four of them, and herding cattle is hours of glorious boredom, a boy and some cows and a whole day, and into that boredom my friends and I poured invention. There were boys named Indeche and Andaji and Mike and others, and we built things, elaborate things, houses out of maize stalks, structures and stadiums woven from corn stalks, whole architectures of imagination raised in the bush while the cattle grazed. We swam in the streams and the watering troughs, and swimming came with its own dangers and its own comedy, because sometimes a snake would drop from a branch into the very water you were in and send you exploding out of it, and sometimes the area’s guard would come to chase off the boys who were not supposed to be swimming there.

And it is that last one that gives me the funniest story of my childhood, so let me tell it properly. We had gone swimming, a whole crew of us, naked the way boys swim, our clothes piled on the bank, and I, even then, did not like to show my nakedness, so I had kept on a short. We had even done our preparation, clearing the branches that hid the spot so the sun could reach the water. I was up on the rock we jumped from, surveying my domain, when out of the corner of my eye I saw him. The guard. Coming. With a cane. And I tried to warn the others, he is coming, he is coming, but they were too deep in their splashing to hear me over their own noise. So I made a decision that has defined my approach to crisis ever since. I did not wait. I grabbed the nearest clothing, which happened to be a friend’s t-shirt, threw it on, and ran, and I drove the cattle home and was gone before the guard reached the water. The others, naked, with no clothes within reach and nowhere to run, simply had to stop, climb out, lie down on the grass, and accept a thorough caning, one by one, while I was already halfway home with the cows. And when they came back that evening, sore, I braced for them to be furious at me for escaping. Instead they were amazed. How did you escape? You are so clever. I had turned my own survival into legend, and I learned something I have used a hundred times since, which is that the world forgives the one who gets away clean and even admires him, and that a quick decision in a moment of chaos is worth more than all the loyalty and hesitation in the world. Cruel little lesson. Useful little lesson. I was a child, naked but for a short, and I had already understood the politics of escape.

My grandmother Josephine was the other half of Kemoview, Samson’s wife, and, in the deepest layer of my family’s secret, my mother’s blood mother, the woman who had given my mother to her sister Gladys. Josephine was, like every powerful woman in my line, a pastor, ministering in the Friends Church, and she is the source of my single most charming small crime. There were always celebrations being planned, weddings and church events, and the planning involved committees and Harambees and the cooking of vast quantities of mandazi, and whenever mandazi was being fried, I was first in line to volunteer for any task that put me near them, because I would steal them. I would take five at a time, fresh hot mandazi spirited away while my grandmother’s back was turned, and I thought I was a master thief. Years later Josephine told me, smiling, that she had always known. She had always seen me stealing the mandazi, and she had let me, every time, because I helped her in the kitchen and because, I think, she understood that a hungry boy stealing fried dough from his grandmother is not theft at all, it is just a child loving the only sweetness available to him, and a grandmother letting him is just love, fried golden and looking the other way.

## Chapter Seventeen: Fifteen Kilometers and a Cold Shower

There came a season when my grandmother Gladys, worn down at last by the long years of raising her absent daughter’s sons with no support from our parents, reached the end of her enormous patience and put us out, and we went to live at Kemoview with Samson and Josephine, and from Kemoview the walk to school was fifteen kilometers, and I want you to walk it with me, because that walk made my legs and my will.

The day began in the dark. Four, sometimes five in the morning, we rose, and the first act of the day was a cold shower, taken outside, in the highland cold before the sun, water that had not been warmed by anything sluicing over a small body in the dark. It was a ritual older than us, the same ritual my aunts had performed in their day, the aunts Emily and Rebecca and Jillian and Carol and Gloria and Charity, and my mother, and my uncles, the whole Kemoli generation before us had risen in this same dark and shocked themselves awake with this same cold water and set out on this same long road to Lumakanda Township. We were walking in their literal footsteps. And then we walked, in the dark turning to dawn, and the road was hilly, genuinely hilly, up from Kemoview to the main stage and then up again toward Lumakanda, climb after climb, about an hour and a half on small legs, every single day, there and back. By the time we reached school we were already spent, our energy left somewhere on the hills, and the school did not care. If we were late, and the long walk made us late, we were caned at the gate as we arrived, and then caned again in class for whatever we had failed to do, the punishment of poverty stacked on the punishment of distance, beaten for being far away from a school we had walked three hours to reach.

I have done that walk in every weather. In the dry season the red dust rose around our feet and coated us so that we arrived at school already filmed in the color of home. In the rains the path turned to a sucking red mud that pulled at the cheap shoes and the bare feet alike, and the hills that were merely hard in dry weather became genuinely treacherous, and still we walked them, because not walking them was not an option, because school was on the far side and there was no other way to reach it but to put one foot in front of the other for an hour and a half through whatever the sky was doing. I built my legs on those hills. I built something else too, something harder to name, a baseline assumption that getting where you need to go simply requires walking there no matter how far it is, that distance is not a reason, that the destination does not care how tired you are or how early it is or how cold the water was, and that the only response to a fifteen kilometer gap between you and your future is to start walking and not stop. I have outworked more talented people my whole life on the strength of a stubbornness that was poured into me, literally, step by step, on that road.

And I think often about the others who walked it before me, the long line of Kemoli aunts and uncles rising in the same dark, shocking themselves with the same cold water, climbing the same hills toward the same school, generation after generation, because that township school was the family’s chosen instrument of escape and the long walk was simply the price of admission. We were not the first to pay it. We would not be the last. There is a strange comfort in suffering a thing that your whole bloodline has suffered before you, a sense that the hardship is not a personal punishment but an inheritance, a rite, the toll our family had always paid at the gate of education and would keep paying until one of us walked far enough to make sure the next generation could ride. I have tried to be that one. Every time I see a child walking a long road to school in the dark, I see myself, and I see my mother before me, and I want to put the whole line of us in a warm bus and drive us all to the future at once.

There was Mr Otipa, Francis Otipa Monze, my English teacher and eventually my class teacher, a man I was close to, who would call me and ask me for small money and treat me as something like a favorite, and who also, in one unforgettable instance, taught me the limits of a teacher’s mercy. My thumb was broken, an actual fracture from a metal rod, and with a broken thumb my handwriting, never elegant, became a disaster, and Mr Otipa, looking at my ruined handwriting, did not ask why it had gotten worse. He caned me for it. And when I tried to explain, holding up the broken thumb, that I could not write properly because the thumb was broken, he delivered a line I have never forgotten, did I send you to break your own thumb, and he beat me anyway. I laugh about it now, the absurd cruelty of being flogged for the bad handwriting caused by an injury you were already suffering from, but the boy did not laugh, the boy just absorbed another lesson in a world that punished you for things outside your control.

And then there was mathematics, and the man who made me hate it, Mr Khatela, who turned numbers into terror. He would stand us on the field and fire multiplication at us like artillery, six times this, three million times that, impossible sums hurled at small children, and anyone without an answer had to stand, and then he would cane the standing, and his tariff was diabolically personal: he caned you according to your age, so many strokes as you had years. The older you were, the more it hurt to be wrong. I stood on that field many times, mind blank with fear, the numbers refusing to assemble, and took my strokes, and I came out of Lumakanda Township with a hatred of mathematics so pure and so deep that it would follow me all the way through high school and leave its mark, literally, on my final certificate. Some teachers light a fire in you. Mr Khatela put one out, and stamped on the ashes, with a cane, on a cold field, in the name of long division. I do not hate the man. But I hated the subject he beat into me, and I have made an entire successful life out of words precisely because numbers were turned, early and violently, into a thing I fled. We become, so often, the shape of what we were running from.

## Chapter Eighteen: Vuteni Kamba

I found my voice, the actual instrument, the thing I would one day make my living with, in a choir at Lumakanda Township Primary, and it began with a song I could not stop hearing, a song about pulling a rope.

I was in the upper classes when I first really heard the school choir rehearse, and they were good, properly good, melodious in the way that stops you where you stand, and they were singing a piece whose refrain called out about pulling the rope, vuteni kamba, pull the rope, and the sound of those voices braided together did something to my chest that I had no name for. I was already a performer in my bones, already the boy who narrated movies and made the class laugh, but this was different, this was music, this was many voices building one beautiful thing, and I wanted in with a hunger that surprised me. In class eight I went to Mr Otipa, who ran the music among his thousand other duties, and asked to join, and he auditioned me on the spot, gave me a note to find and hold, and I found it and held it, and he placed me in the tenors, and just like that I was a chorister, and the choir became my first real stage.

And the stage travelled. The Kenyan schools music festival is a vast machine, a national tradition nearly a century old, that funnels millions of children up through levels, from the zone to the sub county to the county to the region and finally, for the best, to the nationals, and through that choir I began to move up and out of Lugari for the first time in my life on the strength of my voice. We went to Kisumu. We went to Bungoma. I stood on stages in towns I had only heard of, a boy who had foraged fruit in the bushes now performing in front of adjudicators and crowds, and I have called it ever since my first emotion stage, because it was the first time I felt the particular electricity of performing for people who were really watching, the first time I tasted the thing that would become my whole life. The choir was where I learned that my voice was not just for talking my way out of trouble or narrating wrestling matches, that it could be trained, placed, braided with others, carried onto a real stage and made to move strangers. I did not know I was in training for television. I thought I was just singing tenor about pulling a rope. I was doing both.

There is something I learned in that choir that I have used in every studio I have ever stood in, and it is this: a single voice is fragile, but a voice that knows how to find its place inside many voices is powerful, and the discipline of choral singing is the discipline of listening as much as sounding. As a tenor I could not simply sing what I felt like singing at the volume I felt like singing it. I had to find my note inside the chord, hold my line against the pull of the other lines, blend without disappearing, stand out only when the music asked me to and otherwise serve the whole. That is, I have come to realize, the entire art of working with other people, in a choir or a newsroom or a marriage, the art of holding your own line while serving something larger than your line, and I learned it as a boy braiding my tenor into a song about pulling a rope. Vuteni kamba. Pull the rope. We were many hands on one rope, many voices in one sound, and the song was, without any of us knowing it, a parable about exactly the kind of collective effort that the song itself required.

And I tasted, on those festival stages, the specific drug that would organize my whole adult life, the drug of the watching crowd. There is a moment, when you walk onto a stage and feel the attention of a roomful of strangers turn toward you, when something in the chest both tightens and opens at once, a terror and a thrill so tangled you cannot separate them, and the first time I felt it, as a boy in a school choir in a strange town, I knew, in a wordless animal way, that this was a feeling I would chase for the rest of my life. Some people are terrified of the watching crowd and spend their lives avoiding it. I was terrified of it and ran toward it anyway, because underneath the terror was a hunger to be seen that my whole abandoned, called trash, top of the class but flogged for it childhood had planted in me deep, and the stage was the place where being seen felt like a gift instead of a danger. I did not have language for any of this at the time. I just knew that standing up there singing tenor, I felt more like myself than I felt anywhere else on earth, and a boy who finds the one place he feels most himself will find his way back to it, again and again, until it becomes his life.

I was a scout, had been since class one, and scouting took me to camps in distant schools, camps that cost two hundred and fifty or three hundred shillings that I had to extract from a reluctant grandmother, and at those camps I learned the small useful disciplines, how to tie the reef knot and the others, how to do first aid, trained for a week by the St John Ambulance to be a first aider, a child being taught to save lives. And in the holidays there was money to be made being a march boy at weddings, the boys who march in the wedding procession, and my brother and I were good at it, in demand, hired out around the villages for weddings, performing even there, even in someone else’s celebration, because performing was simply what I did, the way water runs downhill. I sang, I marched, I camped, I tied knots, I stole mandazi, I narrated movies, I made the class laugh, and through all of it, without my knowing it, a single instrument was being tuned, the instrument of a boy who could hold a crowd, and it would be years before anyone, including me, understood what it was being tuned for.

## Chapter Nineteen: The Curse of 287

Every Kenyan child of my generation knows that a single number can become a verdict, can follow you, can be spoken about you by adults in a tone usually reserved for the dead, and for me that number was 287, the marks I scored in my national primary exam, the KCPE, in 2015, and I have carried it like a small stone in my shoe ever since.

I sat the KCPE as a class eight pupil, the great gate at the end of primary school, the exam that sorts Kenyan children toward their futures, and by the time the results came I was in Nairobi, and I remember the moment my father checked the number. Two hundred and eighty seven. To understand the weight of that, you have to understand the threshold, because in our world there was a magic number, three hundred, and three hundred marks and above was the gate to the good schools, the recognized ones, the institutions that could carry a poor bright child somewhere. I had scored 287. I had missed the gate by thirteen marks. Thirteen. After everything, after being top of my class at Mahemas before my world fell apart, after the township, after the choir and the scouts and the long walks, I came to the great sorting exam and missed the threshold by a margin so thin it was almost crueler than missing it by a hundred. So close you could see through the gate to the other side, and not close enough to walk through it.

My father was furious. I remember the night, the specific temperature of his anger, the words, the most stupid person, hurled at a boy who had been carrying a falling family on his back for years, whose grades had cratered not from laziness but from terror and abandonment and hunger. He did not see all that, in the heat of the night and the number. He saw a son who had missed three hundred, who had missed the schools that mattered, and he was disappointed in the way that fathers are disappointed when their own thwarted hopes come due in their children. I absorbed it. I had become very good at absorbing.

I have made my peace with 287, but it took years, because a number like that does something strange to a young mind, it becomes a verdict on your worth rather than a measurement of one exam on one set of days. For a long time I carried 287 as proof of something, proof that I was almost but not quite, proof that I fell short at the gate, and I let it whisper to me in the low moments. But I have come to see it differently, and I want to give you the reframe because some child reading this is carrying their own version of 287. The number was not a verdict on me. It was a snapshot of a boy taking the most important exam of his young life while his father was a hunted fugitive, his mother was a domestic worker in a distant city, he had been called trash by his own relatives, he had been walking fifteen kilometers to school on an empty stomach, and he had watched his early academic promise crater under the weight of an abandonment no child should carry. That boy scored 287. Honestly, looking at the conditions, 287 is not a failure. 287 is a small miracle. 287 is the sound of a drowning child still somehow swimming.

And here is the lesson the calling letters taught me, the lesson that would shape my whole adult war against the world’s gates. I learned that being wanted is not the same as being able to walk through the door, that the world is full of gates that open to your talent and then close again at the sight of your wallet, and that for a poor child the cruelest experiences are not the doors that stay shut but the doors that open just wide enough to show you the room before slamming on your empty pockets. School after school called me. School after school priced me out. And somewhere in that catalogue of opened and closed doors, a conviction formed in me that I have never lost, that I would spend my life learning to get through gates by means other than money, by talent, by noise, by cunning, by sheer refusal to accept that a price tag is a final answer. The poor bright child either breaks against that wall or learns to climb it, tunnel under it, talk his way past the man guarding it. I decided, somewhere in the season of the calling letters, that I was going to be a climber. I just did not yet know that the gate I would finally get through was a billboard by a roadside stage, advertising a school in Kalenjin country that my father could actually afford.

You wait, December into the new year, and then schools send letters, calling you to enroll, and the letters came for me, letter after letter, this school and that school, because 287 was not a bad score, it was a good score that had merely missed a great one, and good schools wanted me. I would come home with the letters, called to this institution and that institution, and for a moment there was the dizzy feeling of being wanted, of having options. And then the options collapsed against the one wall my family could never get over, which was money. School after school that called me, my father looked at the fee structure and shook his head, because the good schools cost fifty thousand shillings and more, sums my father could not assemble no matter how he worked, and so the calling letters became, one by one, a catalogue of doors that had opened only to be closed again by a price tag. I was wanted and I was unaffordable, the precise and particular agony of the poor bright child, and my father, the lion, set out to solve it the only way he knew, by hunting for a school he could actually pay for, going from gate to gate looking for the place where his son’s future was on sale at a price a stone cutter could meet. Where that hunt ended, at a billboard by a stage, across a tarmac road on the border of two counties, is where the next part of my life begins, in a high school in Kalenjin country that I imagined as a desert and that turned out to be the making of me. But that, as ever, is the next part.

# PART FOUR: MURGUSI

## Chapter Twenty: The Billboard by the Stage

My entire secondary education, and therefore a great deal of who I became, hung on a billboard my father happened to glance at from the back of a motorbike on a road he was only on because he had just been told no somewhere else. That is how thin the thread was. That is how close I came to not being whatever I am.

After the curse of 287, my father went hunting for a school, and the hunt was brutal, because every school good enough to want him was too expensive for him to afford, the same wall the calling letters had run me into. He went to Kivaywa Boys, a solid school, and the fees were hectic, his word, hectic, an impossible mountain. He went to others. Hectic, hectic, hectic. Door after door priced beyond a stone cutter’s reach. And then, on his way back from Kivaywa, defeated, sitting on a matatu or standing at the stage near Lumakanda town, my father looked across the tarmac road, the road that runs along the border between Kakamega and Uasin Gishu counties, the seam between Luhya country and Kalenjin country, and he saw a billboard. A billboard for a school. Murgusi High School. Right there, across the road, at the edge of two worlds.

And my father, a man with nothing left to lose and one more son to place, took a motorbike the fifteen kilometers to that school on the strength of a billboard, walked in, and asked. And they admitted me. On the spot. Without my father paying a single shilling that day, they took me in and told him he could pay later, words that to a poor parent are either a miracle or a trap and in our case were a miracle. The fee structure was something he could almost reach, around thirty to thirty five thousand a year against the fifty thousand and more of the schools that had called me, and my father, looking at a number he could fight rather than a number that simply ended the conversation, decided. This is the school.

To make it real, he sold land. I want that to land with its full weight, because in our world land is not an asset, land is the family’s body, land is what your ancestors fought and farmed and were buried in, and a man does not sell a piece of his land lightly, he sells it the way you would sell a finger. My father sold a piece of our land in Lugari to send me to Murgusi, and even after selling it he could not finish the fees, he managed my Form One fees and then spent the rest of my time there struggling, behind, scrambling, against the endless secondary school money machine that demands not just tuition but remedial fees and ream papers and a games kit and a uniform and a hundred small extractions that a poor family feels like cuts. He sold part of the family body to buy me a chance, and the chance was real, and I have never once forgotten the price of it.

I was admitted, the records show, on the eleventh of February, 2016, and I arrived with nothing. Not nearly nothing. Nothing. No pocket money, no provisions, none of the small things that make a boarding school student a person rather than a charity case, and I suffered for over a month, watching other boys receive what they received while I received the particular education of having nothing in a place where everyone could see. And then, one random Sunday, with no warning, my mother appeared. She had relocated fully from Nairobi back to Kakamega by then, the family slowly regathering itself in the west, and she came to see me, and before she left she pressed into my hand my first ever pocket money. Two hundred and fifty shillings. My mother was not rich. I was not rich. Two hundred and fifty shillings is not a fortune by any measure the world uses. But it was the first money that was ever simply mine, given out of love and not for a chore, and I held it like a door had opened, because in a way it had. That two hundred and fifty shillings was the first coin on a road that I was determined would one day lead somewhere money came from. I did not know where yet. I just knew, holding my mother’s small impossible gift on a random Sunday, that I was going to find out.

## Chapter Twenty One: The Boy in the Blue Shorts

I arrived at Murgusi carrying a head full of nonsense about the place I was going to, and I want to confess all of it, because the gap between what I imagined and what I found is one of the funniest and most instructive things that ever happened to me.

Murgusi sat in Kalenjin country, across that border road, and to a Luhya boy from the maize highlands, Kalenjin country was an idea more than a place, an idea built entirely out of ignorance. In my mind I was being sent somewhere half desert, a dry harsh land, and I had constructed a whole picture of the students I would meet there, that they would be clumsy, that they would shuffle around in brown shoes and brown trousers looking somehow lesser, a backward people in a backward place. I am ashamed of that picture now, and I share it precisely because I am ashamed of it, because it is exactly the kind of small ignorant prejudice that one group carries about another in this country, and the cure for it is what happened to me next, which is reality.

I walked into Murgusi and found young men in sharp grey trousers, crisp white shirts, maroon sweaters, and clean black shoes, looking, frankly, better than I did, looking modern and put together and entirely unlike the clumsy phantoms of my imagination. And I, the boy who had come to judge them, was the one who looked ridiculous, because I had arrived in my primary school clothes, blue shorts and a blue and white checked short sleeved shirt, the uniform of a child, and I was not even a slim child, I was a fat boy in short trousers among tall sharp young men in long ones, and I had to wear that primary school outfit for about a month while the money for the real uniform was found, a whole month as the visibly poor new boy in children’s clothes. There is a specific humility that comes from being the one who showed up to judge and discovered he was the one who looked foolish, and Murgusi served it to me on the first day, and I have tried to remember the taste of it every time since that I have caught myself deciding I know what another kind of person is like before I have met a single one of them.

Murgusi was a Seventh Day Adventist school, and that detail shaped the rhythm of my whole secondary life, because it meant that the holy day was not Sunday but Saturday, the Sabbath, and the Sabbath in an Adventist school is a serious, total thing. From Friday evening the school changed temperature, the worldly fell away, the Sabbath descended, and Saturday belonged entirely to God, to worship and rest and the particular hush of a community observing its holy day with discipline. I had come from a rich religious soil already, the Church of God and the Friends Church and my grandmother’s Quaker household and my mother’s relentless faith, and now I was folded into yet another tradition, the Adventist one, with its own rules and its own Sabbath and its own seriousness, and I took to it, I genuinely did, I leaned into the faith of that place, learned the Bible deeply there, eventually was baptized in the Adventist church even though I had already been baptized in the Church of God, collecting baptisms the way I collected leadership roles, because I was a boy who joined things all the way in. I became a leader in the Christian Union, a Bible study leader, one of the spiritually serious students, and I want you to hold that alongside everything I am about to tell you in the coming chapters about the funkies and the numbers and the girls, because both were true at once, the devout Bible study leader and the playboy in training were the same boy in the same body, and the tension between them is not a contradiction I am going to resolve for you, it is simply the truth of being seventeen and hungry for God and hungry for girls and not yet wise enough to know what either hunger really was.

## Chapter Twenty Two: Lemindet

There is a Kalenjin word, lemindet, and it means a Luhya person, and at Murgusi it was not said as a description, it was said as a wall, and I spent four years on the wrong side of that wall learning the precise weight of a word that decides you do not fully belong.

Let me be careful and fair here, because I am writing about tribe in Kenya and that is a place where careless words start fires. Murgusi sat in Kalenjin land, and its student body was heavily Kalenjin, and into it came a minority of us, Luhya boys mostly, outsiders in their territory, and we were called lemindet, the Kalenjin term for our people, and the calling of it marked the line. It was not always violent. Often it was just there, a background fact, a sorting of the world into us and them that ran underneath the surface of school life. But it had teeth. There was segregation in the small daily ways that matter to a teenager, the Kalenjin students keeping to themselves, holding their own meetings, sometimes at night, organizing around an identity that did not include us, and when the politics of the school came around, the elections for the positions that matter so much in a boarding school’s small society, tribe came out into the open, and it became a contest of us against them, the lemindet against the locals.

And here is the thing I am proud of and that I think tells you something about me before I ever held a microphone. We won anyway. Despite being the minority, despite being the outsiders, despite the word and the wall and the night meetings, those of us who refused to accept that being lemindet meant being lesser organized ourselves, campaigned, persuaded, and won the positions, and then, having won, we were opposed, resisted, made to fight for every inch of the authority we had legitimately earned, because a minority that wins is more threatening than a minority that stays quiet. I learned tribal politics in its rawest, smallest, most honest form in that school, the way a whole system can be arranged to keep certain people out and the way certain people can refuse to stay out anyway, and I learned that winning while marked as an outsider does not end the fight, it begins a new one, the fight to be allowed to actually hold what you won. I have thought about Murgusi a great deal as I have watched the same patterns play out on the national stage, the same tribal arithmetic, the same walls, the same words, and I have thought, I have been here before, in a smaller room, in a maroon sweater, being called lemindet and winning anyway.

There was another front in this tribal war, and it was the front of girls, and it is the one I find both funny and revealing. The Kalenjin boys resented us, the outsiders, for a specific and wounding reason, which is that their own girls, the local Kalenjin girls, did not always want to date them, and sometimes wanted to date us instead, the lemindet, the outsiders, and there is no insult to a young man’s pride quite like watching a girl from your own community choose a boy your community has labeled lesser. We took their girlfriends, in the brutal accounting of teenage romance, or rather the girls chose, and the choosing humiliated the boys who had been passed over, and the resentment of it fed the wall higher. And here is the part history loves, the part that turns the whole thing into a comedy of human stubbornness, the boys those girls eventually married, in real life, years later, were often exactly the kind of outsiders they had been warned against, because attraction does not read tribal memos, and the heart, given a choice, chooses the person and not the label. I watched, even as a boy, the gap between what the tribe demanded and what the human being actually wanted, and I filed it away, because it is the whole story of this country in miniature, a people endlessly instructed to sort themselves by tribe and endlessly, in the actual living of their lives, in love and friendship and choosing, refusing to. The wall is real. But it leaks, everywhere, all the time, because human beings are finally more interested in each other than in their categories, and that leak is, I have come to believe, the quiet hope of the whole nation.

## Chapter Twenty Three: The Rapper and the Crown

I arrived at Murgusi a fat boy in blue shorts who had been called lesser, and I decided, with the only strategy I had ever known, that I would make these people unable to ignore me, and the tool I reached for was the one I had been sharpening since I narrated wrestling matches to my brother in a smoky kitchen. I would perform. I would become, by sheer force of talent and noise, somebody.

It started with rap. Before the choir, before the official roles, I was a rapper, and I had a trio, a crew, the three of us, myself and a boy named Steve and a boy named Bayan Kimutai who went by Bian, and we made music together, wrote verses, performed, built a little reputation out of rhythm and words. There is something about a poor loud boy and a microphone, real or imagined, that is almost inevitable, because rap is the art form of exactly that boy, the one with nothing but words and the nerve to say them with style, and I had words and I had nerve, and so I rapped, and people listened, and the fat lemindet in blue shorts started to become, in the small economy of school fame, a name.

Then I joined the music club, in Form One, and the music club became my kingdom, and I do mean kingdom, because I rose through it until I was running it, the head, the leader, and through it the stage expanded again the way it had at Lumakanda Township, except now it carried me further. I held the tenor line, as I always had, and the school choir and music teams travelled, to Nakuru County, to Nairobi County, to Masinde Muliro University in Kakamega for the festival, the same MMUST, named after a politician and founding father, where the nation’s young performers gather. I stood on those stages representing Murgusi, a school in Kalenjin country, a boy who had been an outsider in it, now its voice, and the irony was not lost on me even then, the lemindet carrying the school’s name to the nationals.

But I have to tell you the thing that limited us, because it is one of the most important lessons of my whole life and it had nothing to do with talent. We never won beyond a certain level. We would rise to the regionals and there we would stall, and the reason we stalled was not in our voices, it was in our heads. We believed, deep down, that we could not win, because we were from a poor school, a school that was not nationally recognized, a school without a name, and that belief did our losing for us before any adjudicator ever scored a note. I have come to understand that we were not defeated by the better schools. We were defeated by our own conviction that the better schools were better, by a poverty of belief that matched our poverty of money, and it cost us, year after year, prizes that our actual ability might have won. That lesson has never left me. I have watched it operate everywhere since, in myself and in everyone who comes from where I come from, the way the poor and the marginal carry inside them a ceiling that no one else installed, a learned smallness that does the work of every gatekeeper for free. The most expensive thing about being from a poor school was not the lack of resources. It was the belief that the lack of resources meant we could not win. We could have. We did not, because we thought we could not. I have spent my whole career since trying to murder that belief, in myself first and then in everyone I can reach.

I became, in time, the entertainment captain, the entertainment prefect, the boy in charge of the whole school’s joy, head of the music club, head of the scouts, head of the first aid, head of the drama, leader in the Christian Union, head of nearly everything I touched, because I could not help it, because joining all the way in and rising to the top was simply my metabolism. And I learned, in those roles, the heavy unglamorous truth about leadership that the title hides, which is that being responsible for everyone’s entertainment is a lonely and thankless labor, ninety five percent invisible work and five percent applause, and that I once got demoted from the entertainment headship, my first taste of being stripped of a position I had earned, a humbling that taught me that the crown is loaned, never owned, and can be taken back, and that the only thing they cannot demote you from is the talent itself. They can take the title. They cannot take the voice. I held onto the voice.

## Chapter Twenty Four: The Most Expensive Grandson

I want to stop the noise of the funkies and the stages for one chapter and tell you the quiet thing underneath all of it, the thing that made all of it possible, which is that an old white American man paid for my entire education, against the wishes of his own wife, because two children sat him down in 2017 and told him the truth, and he decided we were worth saving.

My father had managed my Form One fees by selling land, and then he had run out, the way the poor always run out, and the question of how I would continue at Murgusi became, by Form Two, a genuine crisis, because there was no more land to sell that he was willing to sell, and the alcohol I have not yet fully told you about was eating what little there was, and my mother was rebuilding her own life with two pots and a will, and the machine of secondary school fees does not pause for any family’s hardship. We were drowning, quietly, in the specific way that school fees drown a poor Kenyan family, the letters home, the sending away from school for fees, the shame of it. And so, in 2017, my aunt Laureen, who was then herself in university, and I, sat down with my grandfather, David Zarembka, and we told him everything, plainly, without performance. We told him about our father and the drink. We told him about the money that was not there. We told him that we did not know how we were going to survive, let alone continue our education, that the road simply seemed to end.

And my grandfather, who had spent his whole life believing that ordinary people could choose to act rather than let the worst happen, acted. He decided to pay our school fees. And here is the part that I hold with complicated reverence, because it was not a simple act of generosity in a happy family, it was a decision made against opposition, because my grandmother Gladys did not want to do it. Gladys had reached her own limit, worn down by years of carrying her absent daughter’s children while the parents failed to step up, and she had concluded, not without reason, that the family could not keep rescuing us, that at some point the responsibility had to fall back on our own parents. She and David argued. They quarreled over us, over whether to educate us, an old married couple fighting about whether two poor grandsons were worth the cost, and my grandfather went against his wife’s wishes and paid anyway. He paid my fees from Form Two through Form Four. He would go on to pay for my university, all the way through to completion, and my rent, and my life. He gave us money to survive the impossible seasons, and pocket money, and a hope that I cannot fully describe to you except to say that because of him, even in my lowest moments, even finishing my final exams with everything around me falling apart, I was strangely confident that I would get to college, because I had a grandfather who had decided I would, and his deciding was more solid than my doubting.

He used to complain that I was his most expensive grandson, and he was right, and the complaint is one of my most treasured possessions, because of how it was delivered. I wrote him emails, in those years and especially later in my first year of university, asking for things, money, a laptop, the tools I needed, and I asked for a lot, because I needed a lot and because I had finally found someone who said yes, and he would write back, this American grandfather corresponding with his Kenyan grandson by email, and grumble that I was costing him more than all the others, his most expensive grandson, and pay anyway. His son, my uncle Tommy, has carried me in one way or another too, and the whole Zarembka line has, and I will tell you near the end of this book what it cost me when they were gone. But here, in the high school years, I want it on the record, plainly, that I did not lift myself by my own bootstraps, that the bootstrap story is a lie the comfortable tell, that I was lifted, deliberately and expensively, by a man who chose me when even his own wife thought the choosing had to stop. Everything loud I have ever done was paid for, quietly, by that yes. I have spent my life trying to be worth it.

## Chapter Twenty Five: The Sisters and the Boy Named John

I have to talk about girls now, and specifically about two sisters, and I have to do it honestly, which means I have to show you a version of myself that I am not proud of, the playboy version, the boy who learned to collect hearts the way he collected leadership roles and treated some of them just as casually. The names here are not their real names, because they did not ask to be in a book, and they deserve their privacy more than this story deserves their truth. I will call the older one Mara and the younger one Sasha.

The school had a whole institution for romance, and it was called the funky, the funkies, the inter school functions where the boys’ schools and the girls’ schools came together for a day of music and performance and, for boys like me, the real business, which was girls. We went for these functions, sometimes a few days a term, and the unofficial competition among the boys was numbers, the phone numbers of girls collected, traded, bragged about, a scoreboard of teenage conquest where your standing rose with your count. And I was good at it, dangerously good, because I had the tools, the confidence built on stages, the baby face, the talk, the humor, the small fame of being the entertainment captain who performed at the very functions where the girls were watching. I would come back from a single funky with the numbers of ten, fifteen, twenty girls, and I want to be clear about what this was and was not, because it matters, it was collection, it was conquest as sport, it was almost never anything physical, no kissing, no sex, just the accumulation of girls as a kind of score, the performance of desirability rather than desire itself. I was building a reputation, not relationships. I was, in the honest old word for it, a playboy, and I wore the title with a pride I now find a little sad, because a boy who needs to be wanted by everyone is usually a boy who once felt wanted by no one, and I had been called trash by my own family, and the funkies were where I went to be told, by stranger after stranger, that I was not.

Mara was different from the numbers, because Mara was real, a presence in my actual daily life. She was my deskmate, from Form Two into Form Three, and my best friend, and I had a quiet helpless crush on her that ran underneath our friendship the whole time. And Mara talked, constantly, about her little sister, Sasha, who was still coming up, still in primary, and the way Mara described her sister built a picture in my mind, an idea of Sasha, and because I was already half in love with Mara and could not have her in the simple way I wanted, I began to want the sister instead, the way a hungry man will want the meal he has only been told about. I wanted Sasha because I expected her to be like Mara, a copy of the girl I actually wanted, a version of my crush I might be allowed to have. And then, when I was in Form Three, Mara told me her sister was joining our school, as a Form One. Sasha was coming. And I wanted her before she arrived.

She came, and we started dating, the Form Three entertainment captain and the Form One new girl, and I am not going to dress up what that was. I was, in the cruel honest sum of it, treating people badly. I had wanted Sasha as a substitute for her sister, I dated her while my heart was really chasing Mara, and at some point I did the thing that I still wince at, I turned toward the elder sister, the one I had always wanted, and in doing so I did Sasha wrong, because a girl deserves to be wanted for herself and not as the understudy for her own sibling. I was a playboy operating on a family, and that is about as ugly as the playboy thing gets, and I am putting it in the book without softening it because I told you at the start that I would not fear condemning myself.

And it was Mara, in the end, who gave me my first kiss, and the memory of it is so tender that I almost cannot reconcile it with the foolishness around it. It was during the season of the final exams, the KCSE, the air thick with study and ending, and we were hanging around a corner of the school where no one could see, the two of us, teasing, smiling like fools, and I told her to close her eyes. She asked why. We were grinning. I asked if I could kiss her, and she started to shake, nervous, and I leaned in and kissed her, one deep first kiss, and then she pulled back and looked at me and ran, ran laughing toward the girls’ dormitories, and that was it, the only time we ever kissed, one perfect frightened tender kiss in the corner of an Adventist school during exam season. She smelled like something I have spent years failing to find again. She married someone, a couple of years later, about a year after we finished school, and the girl who gave me my first kiss became someone’s wife while I was still a boy figuring out what a kiss even meant, and I let her go, because first love does not promise to last, its only job is to prove your heart works, and Mara proved mine worked, once, around a corner, with my eyes closed.

The heartbreak, the real one, the one that broke something, came through Sasha and a boy named John. There is a comedy in the name that I cannot resist pointing out, because my parents had once wanted to name me John and decided against it on the grounds that the name was attached to bad characters, and here, years later, a boy named John walked into my life and took the girl and broke my heart, as if the name I dodged had come back to collect anyway. John took Sasha. I do not need to detail the mechanics of teenage romance, the shifting, the choosing, the way a girl moves from one boy to another in the small hot world of a school. What matters is that it broke me, my first true heartbreak, and that my fury, crucially, went toward John and not toward her. I fought John. Not Sasha. The rage of a wounded boy needs a target and I aimed mine at the other boy, the rival, the thief, as boys do, as though she were a thing that had been stolen rather than a person who had chosen, and I have had to grow up a great deal to understand that she was never mine to be stolen, that John did not steal anything, that a girl is allowed to choose and that my heartbreak, real as it was, did not make me the wronged party in a crime. But I did not know that then. Then I just knew the specific new pain of a heart that had finally, after all the numbers and all the collecting, actually attached to someone and been put down, and I did the worst possible thing with that pain, which is the subject of a later chapter, because the heartbreak did not make me gentler, it made me a wrecker, a boy who went on to break many hearts on purpose just to prove that his own still worked. But that comes later, in a town called Thika, in a self I had not yet become. For now, hold the boy in the corner with his eyes closed, getting his first kiss from a girl he should have just loved honestly, in a school where he was an outsider, in the last season of a childhood that was almost over.

## Chapter Twenty Six: The Year of the C

My final year at Murgusi was the year everything important happened at once, the way life seems to save up its biggest blows and gifts and spend them all in a single season, and I sat my final exams in the middle of a personal storm, and the result was a single letter that has sat on my certificate, and on my chest, ever since.

The storm at home reached its climax in my Form Four year. My parents, who had quarreled and separated and reunited across my whole childhood, separated again, hard, just before I went back for my final stretch of school. My mother left and went to Nairobi once more, repeating the old escape, and I went back to sit the most important examination of my life carrying the fresh knowledge that my family had broken again behind me. It is difficult to describe to someone who has not lived it the particular impossibility of trying to concentrate on chemistry equations and literature essays while the foundation of your home is cracking, of being expected to perform academically at the highest level while emotionally you are a child watching his parents come apart for what feels like the final time. As the firstborn, I felt every fracture in real time, in a way my brother, cushioned a little by being younger, did not. Every separation was mine to absorb first.

And then, into that same season, came a strange and complicated gift. My father, the lion, the man whose drinking had terrorized our home and shaped my entire hatred of alcohol, came to me during that final year and told me, almost in passing, that he had quit. After everything, after years of the violence and the unpredictability and the shame of it, he had stopped drinking, simply and finally. And I did not know what to do with my own face when he told me, because I felt two enormous things at once, a flood of joy that the father I loved underneath the drink had returned, and a hot flash of anger at the timing, because I wanted to grab him and say, why now, why did you not tell me sooner, why give me this in the middle of my exams when I cannot even properly feel it, when I needed you sober for the seventeen years before this one. Both feelings were true. I was grateful and I was furious and I was seventeen and sitting my finals, and my father had chosen that exact moment to become the man I had always needed, too late to undo the damage and just in time to confuse me completely.

I sat the KCSE, the great national secondary exam, in the class of 2019, and when the results came I had earned a C. A plain C, a mean grade C, the average mark of an average student, and stamped within it, like a signature, the proof of a war I had lost years earlier on a cold field at Lumakanda Township, a poor grade in mathematics, the subject Mr Khatela had beaten the love out of and I had never recovered. I had even tried, in my own desperate funny way, to fight the math, making posters that said a math a day keeps failure away and hanging them up in the classrooms like spells against my own weakness, and the spells did not work, and the failure stayed, and it is there on my certificate to this day, a permanent record of the one wall I never did learn to climb. A C is not a triumph. A C does not open the doors that an A opens. But a C earned in the middle of a family breaking apart, by a boy who had been abandoned and called trash and walked fifteen kilometers to school and been flogged for his handwriting and his arithmetic, a C is, like the 287 before it, a small stubborn miracle of a drowning boy still swimming.

When it was all over, when I had sat my last paper and the school was emptying out, the person who came to collect me was my grandfather David. He drove to Murgusi and took me and my belongings home, the white grandfather collecting his most expensive grandson from the end of his secondary education, and my brother still has the photographs of that day, a record of the old American and the Luhya boy he had paid to educate, leaving the school together. My father had wanted me to go to university, and a part of me had assumed I would, but something in me had already turned, already decided that the standard road, the university road, was not the one I was going to walk. I had a C and a hatred of mathematics and a love of stages and a voice that had been tuned my whole life, and I was about to make a choice that disappointed my father and saved my life, the choice to chase the thing I was actually good at instead of the thing I was supposed to want. That choice carried me south, out of the west entirely, to an industrial town in Kiambu whose name, in the language of the people who live there, means both to bury and to plant. And that is where I stopped being only the boy I had been, and started, slowly, painfully, foolishly, to build the man. That is the next part.

# PART FIVE: THIKA AND THE WILDCAT

## Chapter Twenty Seven: The Town Whose Name Means to Bury or to Plant

I left the west for the first time as a man making his own decision, and I went to Thika, an industrial town about forty two kilometers northeast of Nairobi, on the great road my father had once cut stone for, and I did not know yet that the name of the town carried my whole future inside it like a seed.

Thika comes from a Kikuyu word, guthika, and guthika means to bury, and it also means to plant, the same act, the same word, the same putting of a thing into the ground, and whether it is a burial or a planting depends entirely on whether you expect the thing to come back up. I did not know this when I arrived. I learned it later and felt the floor tilt, because it is the truest possible name for the town where I went to either bury the boy I had been or plant him, and the difference, it turned out, was up to me. Everything I am circles around that one word. The same dust that buries you is the dust you plant yourself in. The same hard ground that takes the dead also takes the seed. I came to Thika broke and heartbroken and uncertain, carrying the boy I had been, and I put him in the ground there, and I have spent every year since finding out whether it was a funeral or a farm.

I had refused university. My father had wanted it, the standard prestigious road, but I had a C and a wounded relationship with the whole machinery of formal academics, and more than that I had a conviction, barely formed but stubborn, that the thing I was actually good at, the stages and the voice and the talking and the performing, was not going to be reached by the university road. So I chose differently. I enrolled to study journalism and mass media at a college in Thika, a path my father considered a step down from the university dream and that I considered, correctly as it turned out, a step toward the only future that had ever made sense to me. I would not be a lawyer, the respectable thing. I would be a journalist, a media person, a maker of the kind of noise I had been making my whole life, and I would learn to do it properly, in a town whose name meant to bury or to plant, in a county I had never set foot in, far from everyone who knew me.

I arrived with almost nothing, the way I always arrived everywhere, a metal box of belongings and a small amount of money and an enormous appetite. And I felt, for the first time in my life, a thing I had no preparation for, which was freedom. Real freedom. No grandmother’s rules, no father’s eyes everywhere, no village telephone reporting my movements, no boarding school bells, no one who had known me as the abandoned boy or the lemindet or the entertainment captain, just me, anonymous, unsupervised, an adult in a city for the first time with the whole shape of my days in my own hands. And I want to tell you the truth about freedom that nobody tells the poor child who dreams of it, which is that it is terrifying. Freedom is not the absence of pressure, it is the transfer of pressure onto yourself, the sudden total responsibility for a life that no one is going to direct for you anymore. Some boys, handed that freedom, build something with it. Some boys, handed that freedom, destroy themselves with it. I did, over the next few years, a great deal of both, simultaneously, and the two halves of what I built and destroyed in Thika are the rest of this part.

## Chapter Twenty Eight: The Naming of Roylandz

Before I had built anything worth a name, I gave myself one, and people will tell you that is backward, that you earn the name first and wear it after, and those people have never been poor enough to understand what a name is for.

I became Roylandz in Thika, and I want to be precise about what Roylandz was and was not, because I told you on the very first page of this book that I am Eugine Micah and that Roylandz is a thing I built and not a person I am, and I meant it, and now you get to see the thing being built. Roylandz was a name I made for myself, a brand, an identity I designed and put on like armor, before I had done anything to deserve it. And I did it for the most understandable reason in the world, which is that I had spent my whole life being nobody, being the abandoned boy, the spare child, the trash, the outsider, the poor student who showed up in blue shorts, and I was tired, in my bones, of being erased, and a name, a chosen name, a name that was mine and not handed to me by the circumstances of my birth, was a way of refusing the erasure. Eugine Micah was the name the world had assigned me, freighted with my whole history. Roylandz was the name I assigned myself, freighted with nothing but my ambition, a clean banner I could march under into a future I had not yet built.

I have come to understand the naming as the same act my grandfather Samson performed when he looked at his poor patch of red earth and called it Kemoview. It is the act of a person who has been told he amounts to little insisting, against the evidence, that he amounts to something, planting a flag with his own name on it and daring the world to argue. Samson named a hill. I named myself. Both of us were doing the same defiant thing, taking the one thing we could control, the name, and using it to declare a worth that the world had not yet granted us. You give the dream a name and you wear it before you have earned it, not to fool anyone else, but to instruct yourself, to give yourself something to grow into, to put the costume on first so the man has a shape to fill. The name goes first. The work follows. And one day, if you are stubborn and lucky, the man catches up to the costume, and the borrowed name becomes a true one.

But I want to be careful here, because there is a shadow in the naming that took me years to see. A chosen identity can be armor, and armor protects you, but armor is also a thing you hide inside, and Roylandz became, for a long time, a place to hide Eugine. The brand was bolder than the boy, louder, more confident, less wounded, and it was easier to be Roylandz than to be Eugine, easier to perform the invented self than to feel the real one, and so the armor that I built to survive slowly became a cage I lived in. That is the trap of the chosen name, the brand, the persona, and I fell all the way into it, and it would take me years and a great deal of pain to climb back out and reclaim the original name, the true one, Eugine Micah, the boy under the armor. But that reckoning is for the final part of this book. Here, in Thika, hold the triumph of it, a poor heartbroken boy with a C and a metal box giving himself a glorious name and daring the world to make him grow into it. It was the bravest stupid thing, and the stupidest brave thing, and it worked, which is the only review history ever writes.

## Chapter Twenty Nine: The Overview Show

Everyone who builds something from nothing has a creation myth, a moment that sounds absurd in the retelling, and mine involves a cheap phone, a bedsheet, and a refusal to be embarrassed by how small the beginning was, and I treasure it precisely because it was so small.

In Thika, studying journalism by day, I decided I was not going to wait for permission to be a broadcaster, that the entire problem with the media industry from the point of view of a poor boy was that it was a locked room and the key was held by people who would never hand it to someone like me, and that therefore I was simply going to start broadcasting without the key, without the room, without anyone’s blessing. I started a show. I called it The Overview Show. And I built it out of nothing, with a co creator, a friend named Luckyboy Maingi, the two of us deciding to make television without a television station, content without a studio, a show on faith and stubbornness and a cheap Tecno phone for a camera and a bedsheet hung up for a backdrop, because that was what we had, and what we had was going to have to be enough because it was all there was.

We filmed on faith. That is the only honest way to describe it. We had no audience, no equipment worth the name, no reason on paper to believe that two broke college students filming themselves against a bedsheet with a phone were doing anything other than embarrassing themselves. The early views were humiliating, a handful, three, the kind of numbers that are statistically indistinguishable from your own mother and your co creator’s mother watching out of pity. And we kept going. That is the whole thing, that is the entire secret, we kept going, we released the rough early terrible stuff, we got better in public where everyone could see how not good we currently were, because there is no private road to a public gift, you cannot practice broadcasting in secret until you are excellent and then emerge polished, you have to be bad in front of people first, on purpose, repeatedly, and survive the embarrassment, and most people will not survive the embarrassment, and that is the real filter, not talent, the willingness to be visibly bad on the way to being good.

I learned more about who I am from The Overview Show than from any classroom, because it revealed the thing about me that the funkies and the choir had only hinted at, which is that I am not, finally, a performer who needs a stage someone else built, I am a builder who makes his own stage and then performs on it. I did not wait for a platform. I made one out of a bedsheet. And that instinct, the refusal to wait for permission, the willingness to build the room rather than beg for the key, is the single most important professional trait I have, and it was forged with a Tecno phone in a rented space in Thika by a heartbroken boy who had decided that being ignored by the industry was not going to stop him from joining it. The show was small. The show was rough. The show, by any external measure at the time, was nothing. But it was mine, I had built it, and building it taught me that I could build, and a person who knows he can build is a dangerous and unstoppable thing, no matter how broke, no matter how small the beginning, no matter how few are watching at the start.

## Chapter Thirty: Pendo

I have to go back now, to pick up a thread I left hanging years earlier, because there was a love that ran underneath my whole adolescence and into my early manhood like a river under a city, and it does not fit neatly into any single year of this story, and it ended, finally, in Thika, so this is where I will tell it. Her name is not Pendo. Pendo means love, and I am calling her that because she was my first real one, and because she deserves the privacy that her real name would not give her.

Pendo was my first real relationship, and by real I mean the thing the funkies and the numbers never were, an actual sustained attachment between two people over years, with all the tenderness and toxicity that real things carry. I met her at the very end of primary school, before my KCPE, when she was in class seven and I was in class eight, two children at the threshold of leaving childhood, and somehow that connection survived the leaving. We were on and off for six years. Six years. From the red dust of primary school all the way through high school and into my first year of university, this thing between us persisted, dying and resurrecting, sweet and bitter, the longest continuous human relationship of my young life apart from my own family.

It was a long distance love for most of its span, the cruelest kind, because she ended up in Nairobi and I was in the west, hundreds of kilometers apart, the distance from Kakamega to Nairobi being the better part of a full day’s hard travel, and we tried to carry a relationship across that gap with the technology of the poor, the button phones, the brick phones, texting and calling across an impossible distance, trying to keep a thing alive on airtime we could barely afford. And I will confess to you the small absurd details, because they are the truth of it. She made me kneel, once, to apologize to her, an actual kneeling, a girl in Nairobi having reduced a boy to his knees in penance over some lovers’ quarrel, and I laughed about it later but I knelt, because that is what love at that age does, it brings you to your knees over things that will not matter in a month. She had me send her photographs through some application that worked even on those primitive button phones, a small miracle of technology in service of young longing, a boy figuring out how to send his face across the country to a girl on a phone designed for nothing more than calls. We were children operating heavy emotional machinery with no instructions.

I will tell you my real confession about Pendo, the one that costs me something. I believe she is part of why my KCPE grades fell. I was in love, and a boy in love would rather text the girl than study the book, would rather spend his scarce attention and his scarcer airtime on her voice than on his revision, and I poured into Pendo the focus that should have gone into the exam that would sort my future, and the exam returned the favor with 287, the near miss, the curse. I do not say this to blame her. I was the one who chose her voice over my books. But it is true that my first great love and my first great academic failure are tangled together, that I traded marks for longing, and that I would, knowing everything, probably do it again, because a seventeen year old in love is not capable of the arithmetic that says this girl will be gone in two years and these marks will follow you for life. The heart does not do that math. The heart only knows it wants to hear her voice.

It ended in Thika, in my first year of university, after COVID had rearranged the world, six years of on and off finally running fully off, the long distance at last too long, the thing that had survived everything not surviving adulthood. And I want to be honest about what her ending did to me, because it connects to the man I became, the wrecker I am about to confess. Pendo was the real one, and when the real one finally ended, something in me decided, in a place below conscious thought, that I would never again let a love put me on my knees, that I would be the one who left, the one who could not be left, and I went out into my campus years armored against ever feeling again what the end of Pendo made me feel.
