That decision made me, for a season, a genuinely harmful person to the women who loved me. But that is the next chapters. Here, let me just lay Pendo down gently, my first real love, the class seven girl who became the long distance that finally ran out, the one I knelt for, the one I chose over my own future, the proof, before all the damage, that my heart had once been capable of the real thing. It would take me years to let it be that capable again.

## Chapter Thirty One: The First Time and the Confession

I am going to tell you about sex now, and about skin, and I am going to do it the way I have tried to do everything in this book, honestly but with dignity, because these are the parts of a life that are easiest to either brag about or hide, and I intend to do neither.

I broke my virginity in my first year of university, after COVID, with a young woman I am not going to name, and I am not going to give you the scene, because the scene belongs to the two of us and to no reader, and because a memoir that respects its own author does not turn its tender private moments into entertainment. What I will tell you is what it meant, which is that it opened a door in me that I then proceeded to walk through far too many times. After that first time, I became, in the slang of my world, a wildcat, a young man who pursued and was pursued, who used the considerable tools I had been given, the confidence, the baby face, the talk, the humor, the small growing fame, to move through the romantic landscape of campus and the city in a way I am not going to pretend was admirable. There were single rooms and rented places, the geography of young adult appetite, and I will leave the specifics in the privacy where they belong, but I will not pretend it did not happen, because the whole promise of this book is that I do not pretend.

And I have to make a confession that sits underneath all of it, a confession about skin, because it is true and because it reveals something painful about what colonialism and colorism have done to the minds of people like me. Almost every woman I have ever been with, the overwhelming majority, has been light skinned. I am dark skinned, have always been dark skinned, and somewhere in me, planted early and deep by a whole world that taught me lightness was beauty and darkness was ordinary, I developed a pull toward light skinned women that I did not choose and have only recently begun to honestly examine. It is not a preference I am proud of. It is, when I look at it clearly, a wound, the internalized message that a dark skinned boy improves himself, elevates himself, by being seen with a light skinned woman, that her lightness reflects onto him and lifts him out of the ordinariness his own darkness was taught to feel. I am ashamed of how long I lived inside that without questioning it. I am putting it in the book because somewhere a dark skinned boy is living inside the same unexamined pull, mistaking a colonial wound for a personal taste, and I want him to at least know it is worth examining, that the preference might not be his at all but something installed in him by a world that ranked skin, and that a man who does not examine it is not choosing freely, he is obeying a programming he never agreed to. I am still untangling mine. I do not have the clean resolution to hand you. I only have the honesty of naming it, the dark skinned man admitting the strange sad arithmetic of his own desire, and the hope that naming it is the first step to one day desiring as a free man instead of a haunted one.

I am not the wildcat anymore. I want that on the record too, because confession without change is just bragging in a minor key. A man has grown. I do not chase the way I chased, do not collect the way I collected, do not move through people the way I once moved through them, and the growing was not a sudden conversion but a slow exhausting maturation, the gradual discovery that the wildcat was not free, he was running, that all that pursuit was a man fleeing from something rather than moving toward anything, and that the something he was fleeing was the boy who had been called trash and abandoned and left, the boy who needed every new conquest to tell him again that he was wanted, because the original wound had never healed and no quantity of wanting from strangers was ever going to heal it. The wildcat was not a stud. The wildcat was a wound with good cheekbones. It took me years to understand that, and the understanding is the beginning of the only freedom that matters, the freedom from needing to be wanted, and I am not all the way there, but I am no longer the boy in the rented rooms, and that is its own small quiet victory that no one applauds and that matters more than any of the conquests ever did.

## Chapter Thirty Two: The Laws of Power and the Gospel of Kibe

There was a season in my young manhood when I deliberately studied how to move people like furniture, and I got good at it, and I want to tell you about it honestly, because it is the darkest skill I ever acquired and the one I am most relieved to have put down.

I read a book called The 48 Laws of Power, by Robert Greene, and I did not just read it, I read it and reread it, I studied it, I metabolized it, because it spoke directly to the part of me that had decided, after Pendo and the heartbreaks, that I would never again be the one who got hurt, that the world was a game of power and I had been losing it my whole life and it was time to learn the rules. And the book taught me the rules, the cold ones, the laws of how influence and manipulation and leverage actually operate beneath the polite surface of human relations, and alongside it I absorbed what people call dark psychology, the study of how to read people’s weaknesses and move them, how to use charm and timing and the careful management of other people’s emotions to get what you want. And I became good at it. Frighteningly good. I could read a room, read a person, find the lever, and pull it, and people would move, and I told myself it was just understanding human nature, just being strategic, just refusing to be naive in a world full of predators.

I am not going to teach you any of it, and I want to be clear about why, because the why is the whole lesson. I am not going to lay out the techniques because they work, and because a tool that works is a weapon in the wrong hands, and I have decided that this book will not be the place where some lonely wounded young person learns to manipulate the people who love them. I will only tell you what the skill cost me, which is this: a person who learns to move others like furniture slowly forgets how to sit with them like family. Manipulation is a solvent, and it dissolves the very thing that makes human connection worth having, which is the not knowing, the vulnerability, the genuine uncertain reaching toward another person without a strategy. When you always have a strategy, you are never actually present, you are always running the angle, and the people around you can feel it even when they cannot name it, and they withdraw the realest parts of themselves from a man who is always playing, and so the master manipulator wins every interaction and ends up profoundly, structurally alone, surrounded by people he has successfully moved and not one he has actually touched. I did the worst of this in the romantic arena, and it connects directly to the heartbreak I am about to confess, the spree of broken hearts, because the laws of power in the hands of a wounded young man become, mostly, a manual for hurting people who made the mistake of loving him.

The other voice in my head in those years was Andrew Kibe, the controversial Kenyan content creator, and I will be honest that I was a true follower of his for a time, and that one lesson of his genuinely served me even as much of the rest of his philosophy was poison I had to later spit out. The lesson that served me was this: do not let other people’s ideologies define you, do not simply follow the herd of human beings dictating how you should live, make your own rules and live by your own lights and your own conscience. That much was good and true and I needed to hear it, because I had spent my life being defined by others, by the family that called me trash, by the school that called me lemindet, by everyone who had decided what a boy like me could be. The permission to make my own rules was a real gift. But a philosophy of pure self rule, untempered, in the mind of a young man already armed with the laws of power and the techniques of dark psychology, is a dangerous cocktail, because make your own rules slides very easily into the rules do not apply to me, and the refusal to be defined by others slides very easily into the refusal to be accountable to anyone, and I drank that cocktail and it made me, for a season, exactly the kind of self justifying, manipulative, accountable to no one young man that the cocktail produces. I have kept the one good drop, make your own rules, define yourself. I have poured out the rest, the contempt, the manipulation, the self as the only law, because I learned, the hard way, on the bodies of people who loved me, where that road actually goes.

## Chapter Thirty Three: Work as Armor

I want to end this part with the truest thing about me, the engine underneath everything, the thing that is both my greatest strength and the wall I have used to hide from my own life, and that thing is work. I am a workaholic, and I became one on purpose, and I want to tell you exactly why, because the why is not the inspirational reason you are expecting.

The respectable story, the one I could sell you, is that I work relentlessly because I am driven, because I have a vision, because greatness demands sacrifice, all the things successful people say into microphones. And there is truth in it. But the deeper truth, the one underneath, is that I learned, in the years of abandonment and hunger and being called trash, that work was the only way out of poverty available to a boy like me, that nobody was coming to rescue me except, eventually, one old American, and that my hands and my voice and my relentless effort were the only assets I would ever reliably own, and so I gripped work like a drowning man grips wood, and I have never let go. I work because I am terrified of going back to the dust, terrified in a way that no amount of present comfort can soothe, because the dust is in my body, the hunger is in my memory, and the only thing that quiets the terror, even temporarily, is the motion of working, building, earning, making myself more impossible to throw away.

And here is the confession, the thing the inspirational version hides. I use work as armor. I use it as an excuse, a hiding place, a way to avoid the things I am afraid of, and the things I am afraid of are intimacy and stillness and the feelings that come up when I am not busy. When a relationship gets too close, too real, too likely to put me on my knees the way Pendo once did, I have work to retreat into, a legitimate sounding reason to be unavailable, and no one can argue with a man who is busy building his future. When the sadness rises, the old sadness, the abandoned boy’s grief, I do not sit with it, I out run it, I bury it in tasks, because a moving target is hard for depression to hit, and I have kept myself moving for years partly so that the darkness could never quite draw a bead on me. Work is the most socially acceptable addiction in the world, the only one people congratulate you for, and I am addicted, and I have hidden inside my own productivity from women and from grief and from depression and from the simple frightening stillness in which a person actually has to feel what their life feels like. The country sees a hard worker. The truth is a frightened man who learned that if he ever stops moving, the dust will catch up with him.

I am telling you this because I promised you, at the very start, that this is not a success story, and here is the proof. A success story would let you believe that I worked my way out and now I am free and happy and whole. The truth is that I worked my way out and I am still running, still armored, still using the very discipline that saved me as a place to hide from the parts of myself that the discipline cannot fix. I am still trying to make it, and making it, I have started to suspect, is not actually about the next achievement or the bigger platform or the larger numbers, it is about one day being able to put the armor down, to sit still, to be Eugine and not Roylandz, to let someone close without running the angle, to feel the grief without burying it in work, to be wanted without needing it. I have not made it, by that definition. I have built a great deal and healed very little, and the building is visible and the healing is not, and the gap between them is the unfinished work of my actual life. The town where I planted myself was called to bury or to plant. I planted a worker. I am still finding out whether I also buried the boy who needed, more than he needed to work, simply to rest. That reckoning, and the deaths that forced it, and the strange grace that is slowly teaching me to put the armor down, is the final part of this book. I will see you there.

# PART SIX: THE SCREEN

## Chapter Thirty Four: Baptism by Broadcast

There is a particular terror in finally being handed the thing you have wanted your whole life, because want is safe and having is not, and the day I walked into a real television station, a proper one, with proper cameras and proper professionals, I discovered that the boy who had narrated wrestling matches to chickens and filmed himself against a bedsheet was about to be tested by the actual industry he had only ever dreamed at from a great distance.

It came through an attachment, an industrial attachment, the practical placement that a journalism student does to convert theory into the real thing, and mine took me to Royal Media Services, the home of Citizen TV, the largest media house in the country, the very top of the mountain I had been climbing toward since my father made me watch CNN before breakfast. Picture it. A boy from a mud house in Manyonyi, who had plastered floors with cow dung and walked fifteen kilometers to school and been turned away from an exam for want of twenty five shillings, walking through the doors of the biggest broadcaster in Kenya, badge on, terrified, trying to look like he belonged in a building that represented everything his whole life had been pointed at and nothing his whole life had prepared him for.

I will not pretend I arrived ready. I arrived green, raw, a college attaché among seasoned professionals, and the station did to me what a real workplace does to a confident young person, which is to gently and repeatedly demonstrate how much you do not yet know. But I had one advantage that no classroom and no privilege can give you, and it was the advantage of a person who had been getting better in public his whole life and was not afraid of being visibly bad on the way to being good. I had built The Overview Show on a bedsheet for an audience of three. Humiliation held no terror for me, because I had already metabolized it, I had already learned that the only people who never embarrass themselves are the people who never try anything, and so while other attachés froze at the fear of looking foolish, I simply did the work, badly at first and then less badly, soaking up everything, watching how the professionals moved, how a real broadcast is actually assembled out of a thousand unglamorous decisions, learning the difference between the dream of television and the disciplined daily labor of it.

And that is the lesson I carried out of Royal Media that has served me more than any other, the lesson that consistency beats brilliance, that the industry does not reward the most talented person, it rewards the person who shows up, prepared, every single day, who can be relied upon to deliver an acceptable thing on time rather than an extraordinary thing occasionally. I had thought, as a young performer, that the goal was to be dazzling. The station taught me that the goal was to be dependable, that dazzle is a bonus on top of dependability and worth nothing without it, that the camera does not care how gifted you are if you cannot be trusted to be in the chair, ready, when the light goes red. I was a gifted, undependable boy when I walked in. I left understanding that my gift was the cheap part and my reliability would be the expensive part, the part I would have to build, and I set about building it, because I had finally seen, up close, the actual machine I wanted to spend my life inside.

## Chapter Thirty Five: The Valedictorian Nobody Saw Coming

When they told me I was to be the valedictorian, the one chosen to speak for the entire graduating class at my college, I laughed, not out of false modesty but out of genuine disbelief, because the boy who scored 287, the boy who got a plain C, the boy whose mathematics had been beaten out of him on a cold field, was not supposed to be the one standing at the podium speaking for everyone. And yet there I was, and the gap between the 287 boy and the valedictorian is, I think, the entire argument of this book compressed into a single ceremony.

I had gone to TIBS, the college in Thika, to study journalism and mass media, the path I had chosen over the university my father wanted, and I had done a thing there that I had never quite managed in all my years of formal schooling before, which is that I had worked, with the dependability I had learned at Royal Media, and the work had compounded. Diligence, I discovered, is an exponential force, not a linear one. A little more effort than the next person, sustained over a long enough time, does not add up, it multiplies, it pulls away, it opens a gap that looks from the outside like talent or luck and is actually just the quiet accumulation of a boy who decided to out work the room every single day. I had finally found, in a subject I loved, taught in a way that did not flog me, a place where my hunger could convert directly into excellence, and it did, and at the end of it I was the top, the valedictorian, the boy chosen to speak.

And I want to tell you what I did with that speech, because it reveals what I had already started to understand about who I was for. I did not give the speech the front row expected, the polished triumphant speech about our bright collective future. I gave the speech the kid in the back row needed, the broke kid, the abandoned kid, the kid who had been told he could not, because I had been that kid in every room my whole life and I knew he was in this one too, and I knew that the front row did not need anything from me, the front row was going to be fine, but the back row needed to hear, from someone who looked like them and had come from where they came from, that the back row gets to win sometimes, that the boy who scored 287 can end up at the podium, that diligence is available to the poor in a way that almost nothing else is, that it costs no money, only will, and that it is the one lever a person with nothing can still pull. I spoke for everyone who had ever been told they could not. I spoke as living proof that the telling is sometimes wrong. And I watched it land on the faces in the back of the hall, and I understood, in that moment, that this, exactly this, standing up in front of people and saying a true thing that the overlooked ones needed to hear, was not a departure from my purpose, it was my purpose, the whole of it, and I had found it.

My father had wanted a lawyer, or at least a university graduate, the respectable thing. I had given him a valedictorian in journalism instead, and I hope that on that day, watching his most difficult son speak for an entire graduating class, the lion understood that the boy had not thrown his life away by refusing the respectable road, that the boy had simply chosen the road shaped like his actual soul instead of the road shaped like a résumé, and that the soul road, it turned out, also led to a podium, just a different one than the father had imagined.

## Chapter Thirty Six: Seven Days

I have carried this chapter unwritten for years, because some griefs do not want to be sentences, they want to stay shapeless inside you where they cannot be looked at directly, and the death of my grandmother is the thing in my whole life I have been least able to say out loud. I am going to say it now. It is going to take a while. Sit with me.

It was 2021, the second pandemic year, and I was in Thika, in the Ngoingwa area, staying through the university holidays because going home to Kakamega, the better part of four hundred kilometers away, was a luxury I could not afford and did not want, because what was there for me at home but expense and difficulty. There was a Christian camp I was meant to attend, and I was a comrade, the word we use for a Kenyan university student, broke in the specific proud way of the comrade, always one phone call away from the relative who keeps you alive. My relative who kept me alive was my grandfather David. So I called him, to ask for three thousand shillings, just three thousand, to facilitate the camp and to survive the week, the ordinary small ask of a student to the grandfather who had decided long ago that I was worth funding.

He was coughing. That is the first thing I noticed, before any words, the cough underneath his voice. And he said it plainly, the way he said most things. It is COVID. I suspect it is COVID, I do not know what it is, but I hope everything will be okay. He was not panicked. He did not sound desperate. He sounded, I understand now though I did not let myself understand it then, like a man speaking from very far away, calm in the way the truly tired are calm. I asked him, have you gone to the hospital. He said, not yet, but I will. And I told him everything would be okay, told him to please go to the hospital, and we prayed, and I held onto his okayness because the alternative was unthinkable, because vaccines had not yet reached Kenya, because both my grandparents carried type two diabetes in their bodies like a door left unlocked, and because if I let myself know what that cough was I would not have been able to stand up. So I did not know it. I chose not to know it. I went back to my camp.

We prayed for them at that camp, my grandfather and my grandmother both, every day, the way young believers pray, with total confidence that the praying would work, that God could not possibly take two people so good and so needed. I would call home for updates, and the news was always the same unimproving hum, he is in and out of the office, he goes back to his room to sleep, the situation is not getting better but it is not getting worse. The house was full, as our houses are, full of the family and the favoured son's children, three of them staying there with my grandparents, a boy whose mother was out of the country and two girls whose mother, one of the favoured son's former wives, was also in the home, and a couple of others, all of us scattered through the rooms in the careless way of a big African household, not yet understanding that the house had a virus moving through it like a slow tide.

Then my grandfather was moved. I got the call that he had been transferred to Eldoret, to the Moi Teaching and Referral Hospital, taken into the intensive care unit in a critical condition, and yet, and this is the cruelty of what came next, his situation in the ICU was reported as not deteriorating, even improving, the doctors managing him, the family allowing itself to breathe. He was the one we were afraid for. He was the one in the ICU. And so when the blow came, it came from the side we were not watching.

My grandmother Gladys, at home, felt her pressure rising, and she collapsed. An ambulance came and took her to a hospital at Kipkaren River, where they gave her medication, but she did not improve, and the doctors said she had to be taken to Eldoret too, and on that road, on the way to Eldoret, my grandmother died. They confirmed it at the hospital. She was gone. The one who was not even in the ICU, the one we had not been holding our breath for, went first.

I learned it on Facebook. I want you to feel the obscenity of that, because it is its own small wound inside the larger one. I was in my house in Thika, and I saw a post, a rest in peace, and the name in it was my grandmother's, posted I believe by my brother Dan, and I had spoken to her so recently, and now her name was inside the phrase people use for the dead. I could not sit. My whole body started shaking and I went cold, a strange physical cold in the warm Thika afternoon, and I got up and I walked. I walked for ten, fifteen kilometers, out of the houses and into a field, and I sat down in that field alone and I cried in a way I had never cried, and I prayed, and the prayer was not a sophisticated thing, it was a child bargaining with the sky, God, please, not him too, you have taken her, please do not take him too, please leave me grandpa. I sat in a field and begged God to spare the one who was still in the ICU. I wrote my grandfather a long email that same season, telling him how sorry I was about the death of his wife, how much it hurt to even write the words, and that email is still sitting somewhere in an account I have never been able to open since, because some letters you write are not meant to be reread, they are meant to be survived.

Now I have to tell you about the documents, because they are the part of this story that turns grief into a fight, and they are the reason my grief and my family's survival got tangled together in a way that did not let me mourn cleanly.

When I was a small boy, I spent a great deal of time in my grandfather's office, the library office, the room that built me, and I knew that room better than almost anyone, knew where things lived, knew its drawers and its shelves and its locks. And my grandfather, who was a careful and far seeing man, had given me an instruction, knowing what his illness might mean, knowing the shape of what could come after he was gone. He wanted the documents secured. The title deeds. The certificates. The logbook of the car. The papers that prove who owns what, the papers that, in the wrong hands, can be used to take a family's land out from under it. He had foreseen that there would be a grab, and he had quietly entrusted the securing of those papers to the grandson who knew the office, and so when my grandmother died and the household began to fill with mourners and the favoured son began to circle, I did what my grandfather had asked. I orchestrated the hiding of those documents. Carefully. Deliberately. A grieving university student executing the last administrative wish of the man who had funded his whole life, securing the family's future against a theft we all knew was coming.

Getting home to do it was its own ordeal, because the country was in lockdown, and lockdown did to transport what disasters always do, it made it scarce and made it expensive. The fare from Nairobi to Kakamega, normally around twelve hundred shillings, had spiked to five and six thousand, an impossible sum for a comrade, and I was alone in Nairobi with only the money my grandfather had given me before he fell ill, the last money from his hand, which I was now spending to get to his wife's grave. I called everyone. My prayer partners, the friends I had been at the camp with, they sent what they could, and two of them especially, two young women who were then students and worship leaders and are now both married, one of them, Neema, now a lady pastor, sent me money so that I could travel. With their help and my grandfather's last shillings, I rode a bus through the hiked-up lockdown country and arrived in Kakamega in the evening, and I did not go to rest, I went straight to the office, and I got my mother, and together we packed the documents into a backpack, and I carried them home, secured, exactly as my grandfather had asked. The very night I arrived to bury my grandmother, I was also quietly completing the last task he had given me. Grief and duty in the same backpack.

Gladys was buried the next day. I had arrived on a Sunday and on Monday we put her in the ground, because the customs of that time, the COVID burial laws, demanded it, demanded immediacy, demanded that the dead go down fast to stop the spread. Her casket was brought and it was sealed, wrapped in a transparent covering, some kind of foil or plastic sheeting that meant no one could open it and no one could come near it, and a grave had been dug in front of the property, and she was lowered into it almost as soon as she arrived, while people stood at a careful frightened distance, many of them seeing for the very first time what it looked like to bury someone who had died of this new disease, a sealed package lowered fast into red earth with no one allowed to touch it, no last look, no hand on the wood, nothing. We buried my grandmother like a parcel the world was afraid of. That is how the woman who built my spine went into the ground.

And then, two days later, the second blow we had been praying against landed. We had gone to my grandfather Kemoli's place at Kemoview, a few of us, to rest and regroup, and my uncle Mickey had cooked for us, good chicken, well cooked, the kind of meal that is supposed to be a small mercy in a hard week, and we were eating it, and I went onto Facebook, and there it was. You know how those posts begin, the African way, we tried our best, we loved him but God loved him more, rest in peace. My grandfather David had died. The one in the ICU, the one whose situation had not been deteriorating, the one I had begged the sky to spare in a field in Thika. COVID does a particular thing, it strips your immunity down to almost nothing, and even a man who seems to be improving can be hollowed out and taken in a moment, and it took him, seven days after it took his wife. Fifty years of marriage, of building schools and raising other people's children and arguing about whether to pay my fees and choosing, in the end, to pay them, and the disease could not even bear to let them be apart for more than a week. I put down the chicken. I have never really picked my life back up in the same way since.

He was buried two days later, the same way, immediate, sealed, no one near, the COVID law applied to the American Quaker exactly as it had been applied to his Kenyan wife, both of them lowered fast into the red dust of a country he had crossed an ocean to serve. And at his burial there was a man, a prominent local man, a neighbor whose house sat at the corner just before you reached my grandparents' place, a newly built beautiful house, and during the procession this man stood up and objected, loudly, that a person should not be buried like this, sealed and rushed and untouched, that this was being buried like a dog. A few weeks later that same man collapsed in Lumakanda town and died. I am not telling you that to claim a curse, I am telling you because it happened, because in the strange dark accounting of that season several of the people who stood and criticized those frightened hurried burials met their own sudden ends not long after, and the village noticed, and I noticed, and I leave the meaning of it to you, because I do not have one, I only have the facts and the chill they still give me.

I wish the story ended at the second grave, with grief, clean grief, the honest sorrow of a grandson who lost the two people who made him. But it does not, because almost before the soil had settled there was a fight over the land, and the fight is the part that taught me what I am willing to do for the people I love.

You have to understand the long wound underneath it. My mother was adopted into this family, given by her own mother Josephine to her aunt Gladys, and she has told me that it began gently enough, that before I was even born or thought of she was called to a meeting and first sat with Gladys in a local restaurant and shared a single bottle of soda, and she was entirely okay with the arrangement. But somewhere along the years the warmth went out of it. Gladys, my mother says, ceased to support her, turned against her, and for ages my mother was never treated like a true daughter at all, while the favoured son was pampered, taken to places my mother could only dream of, given the support and the standing she was denied, allowed to bring home wife after wife who bore children in that home. I have seen some of those children myself. My mother lived a life of quiet suffering inside a family that could have lifted her and chose, repeatedly, not to, and that is the painful contradiction I have to hold about my grandmother, that the same Gladys who built me with her rules and her discipline was, to my own mother, a withholding and unjust parent, and both of those things are true, and I have stopped trying to flatten them into a single verdict, because people are not single verdicts, they are contradictions wearing one face.

So when the elders were gone, the favoured son moved on the land, and he came for the documents, and he came, in his rage, for my mother. He had searched the office the evening before my grandfather was buried, gone through the locks and the shelves and found nothing, because the papers were already in a backpack in a safe place, hidden by the boy who knew the office. And at the family meeting after the burial, the gathering held in a tent in the compound that was supposed to be for thanks and remembrance, he was drunk, had been drunk through the night, disturbing everyone, and he turned the meeting into an interrogation of my mother, pointing his finger at her, accusing her of taking the documents, demanding the title deeds and the certificates and the car logbook, and my mother denied it and denied it and finally simply took the fall, went quiet, absorbed the accusation rather than reveal what her son had done. And he raged at her, and the rage carried threats in it, threats frightening enough that I felt the temperature of the whole thing change, and my father, the lion, watched from the edge, too furious to even enter the meeting, holding himself back with a visible effort. And I stood up. I was a grieving university student who had just buried both his grandparents in a week, and I stood up and I told that man, plainly, that if he dared to touch my mother he would see something he had never seen. I meant it with my whole body. I had hidden the documents, I had secured the land, and I was not going to also let him lay a hand on the woman who had survived everything to raise me. The favoured son had the family's old favoritism on his side. I had the truth and a backpack and a son's absolute refusal, and that day, the refusal was enough.

And then it was over, and the silence after it was the worst sound I have ever heard. Both builders in the ground seven days apart. The land defended. My mother protected. And me, twenty something years old, standing in the wreckage of the workshop where I had been made, with nothing left to do, no document to hide, no mother to defend, no grandparent to call for three thousand shillings. The task was complete and the people were gone and there was nothing to hold the grief back anymore, and so it came, and it took me down, and I fell into a depression that lasted the better part of ten months, a flat lightless season in which I stopped believing in most things, including, for a while, God, because I had prayed, I had prayed in a field on my knees, and the praying had not worked, and a young man who has been told his whole life that God hears the faithful, and who then watches the two most faithful people he knows die seven days apart despite all his praying, does not always keep his faith intact. I lost mine, for a time. I became, in my own private wreckage, something close to an atheist, a believer's son sitting in the dark asking what the point of any of it had been.

But that ten month darkness, I have come to see, was not only a grave. It was also, strangely, a seed, planted in the same red dust that takes the dead, and the town where I was living was named, you will remember, for exactly that doubleness, to bury or to plant. From 2021 onward, out of the very bottom of that depression, my real life began. I will tell you how I climbed out, and how my faith came back in the unlikeliest way, in the chapters ahead. But this chapter, the hardest one, the one I have never been able to say, ends here, in the dark, honestly, with a grieving boy who has lost his builders and his belief and is sitting in a field that is either a grave or a garden, not yet knowing which.

## Chapter Thirty Seven: The Ones Who Stayed

When the people who built you are lowered into the ground, you discover very quickly who remains standing at the edge of the grave looking back at you, willing to keep doing the unglamorous work of loving you now that the great builders are gone, and in my case the ones who stayed were David and Gladys’s own children, my uncle Tommy and my aunt Joy, and I want to honor them, because the family that stays after the funeral is a different and rarer gift than the family that creates you.

Tommy and Joy Mutanu Zarembka are the biological children of David and Gladys, which makes them, in the tangled beautiful map of my family, my mother’s siblings by her adoption and therefore my uncle and aunt, and they did not have to keep me. That is the thing about the ones who stay, they are under no obligation, the great builders who bound us together are gone, the contract such as it ever was has expired, and a person in that position can quietly let the connection lapse, can drift, can decide that the poor loud nephew was their parents’ project and not theirs. Tommy and Joy did the opposite. They stayed. My uncle Tommy especially has carried me, in one way or another, through season after season, the way his father carried me, picking up a thread of care that could so easily have been dropped when the hands that first held it went still. We have a special relationship, Tommy and I, the kind that is built not on grand gestures but on the accumulated weight of someone simply continuing to show up, continuing to answer, continuing to treat you as family long after the family member who made you family has died.

I have come to believe that this kind of love, the staying kind, the unglamorous continuing kind, is actually the highest form, higher than the dramatic rescue, higher than the grand sacrifice, because the rescue is a moment and the staying is a life. Anyone can be moved to a single act of generosity. It takes something deeper, something I do not fully have the words for, to keep choosing a person, year after year, in the ordinary unwitnessed Tuesdays of life, when there is no audience and no occasion and no dying parent’s wish to honor, just the quiet decision, renewed again and again, that this person is yours and you will not let them fall. The givers of the future were David and Gladys. The keepers of it, after the givers were gone, are Tommy and Joy, and I am alive and standing and able to write this in part because when the great builders died, their children looked at the unfinished construction project that was me and decided, without being asked, to keep building.

There is a lesson in this that I press on everyone who will listen, especially the young people from hard places who write to me as though I have arrived. Honor the dead, yes, always, carry them, let them change form inside you. But do not be so busy mourning the builders that you forget to thank the keepers while they can still hear you. I lost David and Gladys before I had said a fraction of what I owed them, and the regret of that has made me almost aggressive about gratitude toward the living, about telling Tommy and Joy and my mother and everyone still here exactly what they are to me, out loud, now, repeatedly, because I have learned in the hardest way that the chance to say it is not permanent, that the people who stay are not guaranteed to stay forever, and that a thank you delivered to a grave is just a sob with good manners. Say it now. To the ones who stayed. While they can still hear you. I am saying it now, in this book, to Tommy and Joy, in case I have not said it enough in person, which I almost certainly have not, because gratitude, like grief, is never quite finished.

## Chapter Thirty Eight: The Day I Went National

There is a symmetry in my life so neat that if I put it in a novel an editor would cut it for being too convenient, and yet it happened, exactly, in reality, and it is this: the national television station where I became a national name is the very same station I used to watch as a poor student, dreaming, never believing the dream could include me.

When I was a boy doing my final school exams, watching television in the small stolen moments allowed to me, there was a channel I followed, PPP TV, and on it were presenters I admired from the unbridgeable distance of a viewer who assumes the people on the screen are a different species, made of different material, born on the other side of a wall he will never climb. There was a presenter named Prince Newton, and one called Ala C, and they hosted a show that everyone was watching, a trendy thing, and I watched it the way a hungry person watches someone else eat, with longing and the quiet conviction that this was a meal for other people. The gengetone sound was rising then, a whole new energy in Kenyan youth culture, and PPP TV was riding it, and I was a poor boy in the west absorbing it all and thinking, in the back of my mind, the impossible thought, one day, maybe, if I could make it on the screen, and then dismissing the thought as the kind of fantasy that gets poor boys hurt.

And then, years later, I walked into PPP TV not as a viewer but as one of them. As a presenter. As a national name on the same channel I had watched from the far side of the wall. I became the Head of Digital and a presenter, I host shows, Urban News, Campus Xposure, I sit beside a co host, Lucy Ogunde, under the lights, and the country watches me now the way I once watched the people I thought were a different species. I want you to feel the vertigo of that, because I still feel it. The wall I assumed I would never climb did not just let me over, it dissolved, and I found myself standing on the exact spot I used to stare at, looking back out at all the poor boys in all the western villages watching me now and assuming I am made of different material, and knowing, with absolute certainty, that I am not, that I am made of mud house and cow dung and fifteen kilometer walks and a borrowed twenty five shillings, that the wall is not real, that it never was, that it is only ever made of the belief that it is there.

The day I went national was supposed to be the day the dream was completed, the summit, the arrival, and I will be honest with you the way I have promised to be honest, it was glorious and it was hollow in a proportion I did not expect. Glorious because the boy who read to chickens was now read by a country, because the symmetry was so perfect, because some part of me that had been waiting since those stolen viewing sessions finally got to exhale. And hollow because I had believed, without ever examining the belief, that arriving on that screen would fix the thing inside me that the abandonment and the hunger and the calling me trash had broken, and it did not, and the not fixing was a quiet devastation that I was not prepared for and that became the subject of the entire final part of this book. I had climbed the exact wall I dreamed of climbing. I had become the thing I watched. And the wound was still there, waiting for me on the other side, having made the climb right alongside me, because wounds are excellent climbers, and no summit has ever healed one. But that is the next part. Here, let me give the boy his moment. He watched PPP TV from the far side of a wall, and then he became PPP TV, and for one shining vertiginous moment the symmetry was enough.

## Chapter Thirty Nine: Seven Hundred Thousand People I Will Never Meet

The numbers, when they finally came, came faster than my ability to understand them, and I want to talk about the numbers honestly, because numbers are the new measure of a life like mine and they are both more and less meaningful than they appear.

Around seven hundred thousand people subscribe to what I make. Around twenty million views move through it in a month. I write those figures down and they do not feel like they refer to anything real, because seven hundred thousand is not a number a human being from a village of a few thousand can actually conceive of, it is an abstraction, a population, a crowd larger than most towns I have ever stood in, and they have all, in some small way, chosen me, chosen to follow the boy from the mud house, and I cannot picture even a fraction of their faces. Seven hundred thousand people I will never meet. Twenty million encounters a month with strangers I will never shake hands with. It is the strangest form of intimacy ever invented, this mass anonymous closeness, where vast numbers of people feel they know you and you know none of them, where you are simultaneously the least lonely and the most lonely person, surrounded by a crowd of hundreds of thousands and unable to actually touch a single one.

Recognition came with the numbers, the being known on the street, the turning heads, the people who approach with the specific warmth of someone greeting a person they feel they already know, and I will not pretend it is not gratifying, because it is, the boy who was called trash by his own family is now greeted with affection by strangers in the city, and there is a justice in that, a healing of the original wound that I would be lying to deny. But I learned quickly that fame is not freedom, it is responsibility, it is a magnifying glass and not a mirror, it makes everything you are larger and louder, the wounds along with the gifts, and it hands you the power to influence hundreds of thousands of young minds whether or not you have done the work to be worthy of that power. A poor boy dreams of being seen. He does not dream of the weight of being seen, the way every word now travels, the way the platform he built to escape invisibility becomes a pulpit he is morally responsible for, the way seven hundred thousand people watching means seven hundred thousand people who can be lifted or misled by what the formerly invisible boy decides to say.

And here is the quiet truth underneath the numbers, the one that connects directly to the reckoning of the final part. The metrics arrived, and I waited for them to fill the hole, and they did not, because a hole shaped like a grandmother’s discipline and a grandfather’s belief and a mother’s sacrifice and an abandoned boy’s need cannot be filled by a number, no matter how large, because the number is external and the hole is internal, and you cannot pour the outside into the inside, the geometry simply does not work. I have seven hundred thousand subscribers and I have spent nights as lonely as any I spent in the smoky kitchen of my childhood. I have twenty million monthly views and I have sat in the back of a car after a triumphant broadcast and felt nothing, the specific nothing of a man who got exactly what he wanted and discovered it was not what he needed. The numbers are real and they are wonderful and they are, in the way that matters most, completely beside the point, and understanding that, fully, painfully, is the beginning of the only growing up that counts, the kind that happens after the dream comes true and turns out not to be the cure. The boy chased the numbers his whole life. The man is learning that the numbers were never going to save him, and that learning, that disappointment, that clear eyed grief over the emptiness of the achieved dream, is, strange to say, the most hopeful thing that has ever happened to me, because a man who knows the outside cannot fix the inside has finally, at last, started looking in the right place. Inside. Which is where the final part of this book, and the rest of my actual life, takes place.

# PART SEVEN: STILL TRYING TO MAKE IT

## Chapter Forty: The Fame That Did Not Fix Me

I am going to say the thing that famous people are not supposed to say, the thing that sounds ungrateful and is actually the most useful sentence I can hand you, and I am going to say it plainly, because I have earned the right to say it and you have earned the right to hear it. Fame did not fix me. It was never going to. And believing that it would was the most expensive mistake I have made, more expensive than any school fee, because I paid for it with years.

There is a story that almost everyone from a hard beginning tells themselves, a story so common it is practically the water we swim in, and the story says that when I make it, when I am known, when I have arrived, then the ache will stop. The ache of the broke childhood, the ache of the abandoned years, the ache of being called trash, the ache of the silent house and the hungry days, the whole accumulated ache of a beginning that the world had calculated against you. The story promises that fame is the cure, that recognition is the medicine, that enough strangers loving you will finally heal the wound that the people who were supposed to love you first put there. I believed this story completely, without ever once examining it, the way you believe the ground will hold when you step on it, and I built an enormous amount of striving on top of that unexamined belief.

And then I achieved a version of the thing. The national screen. The seven hundred thousand. The recognition on the street. The dream the boy had rehearsed against a bedsheet with a Tecno phone. And I waited for the ache to stop. And it did not stop. The wound did not close. The abandoned boy still did his anxious arithmetic. The kitchen still echoed. The boy who had been called trash still, somewhere deep, believed it, and no quantity of strangers’ affection reached down far enough to tell him otherwise, because the affection was external and the wound was internal, and I have said it before in this book and I will say it again because it is the single most important thing I know: you cannot pour the outside into the inside. Fame changed my circumstances completely and changed my interior almost not at all, because the interior is not built from circumstances, the interior is built from meaning, and no amount of external achievement assembles meaning on your behalf. You have to build it yourself, by hand, in the dark, the way Gladys built a floor and David built a school, slowly, without applause, and fame is the opposite of that, fame is loud and fast and applauded, and therefore exactly the wrong tool for the deepest job.

I started, eventually, to do the actual work, the interior work, the unglamorous work that no audience ever sees and no metric ever measures, and part of that work, I will tell you because the stigma is a killer and I refuse to feed it, has involved sitting down with someone whose job is to help you look honestly at your own mind. And in that looking I have begun to understand things about myself that the fame had let me avoid for years, that my loudness was not simply a gift, it was also a trauma response, a survival strategy from a boy who learned that being quiet meant being forgotten and being forgotten meant being left. That my relentless drive was not simply ambition, it was a frightened child running from the dust. That my need for the validation of strangers was the abandoned boy still standing at the edge of the family compound, still hoping that if he was good enough, wanted enough, impressive enough, someone would finally not leave. The fame did not fix any of that. The fame, if anything, fed it, gave the wounded strategies more fuel, made the loud boy louder and the runner faster and the validation seeker’s hunger vaster. The fixing has had to happen somewhere the cameras never go, in a quiet room, with hard questions, looking at the boy I was instead of the brand I built. And it is the hardest thing I have ever done, harder than the fifteen kilometer walk, harder than the bedsheet show, harder than going national, because it cannot be out worked, and out working things is the only solution I ever learned.

## Chapter Forty One: Burnout in Expensive Shoes

I burned out wearing shoes that cost more than my first year of secondary school fees, and the contradiction inside that sentence is the entire chapter, the specific exhaustion of a poor boy who finally got the nice things and discovered that the nice things do not rest you.

Burnout is a strange affliction for someone who grew up the way I grew up, because it arrives wrapped in guilt that the comfortable never have to feel. How dare you be tired, says the voice of the broke child inside me, you have everything you prayed for, you have the screen and the numbers and the shoes, your father sold land for this exhaustion, your grandfather paid fees against his wife’s wishes for this exhaustion, your mother gave you her first two hundred and fifty shillings for this exhaustion, sit up straight and be grateful. And so you do not rest, because resting feels like ingratitude, like spitting on the sold land, like betraying the whole lineage that bet everything on your head, and you push through, because pushing through is the only mode you were ever taught, the borehole and jerrican mode, the walk fifteen kilometers and do not complain mode, the get better in public mode, the never stop mode that got you out and that you now do not know how to switch off because the situation that required it has ended and the engine does not know that and keeps redlining toward a finish line that no longer exists.

And the body, as the wise ones say, keeps the score, and eventually it presents the invoice. Mine came as the particular grey flatness that burnout produces, the joy quietly draining out of the work that had been the whole dream, the camera that used to thrill becoming just a hot light to perform under, the audience that used to feed me becoming a demand I had to keep meeting, the relentless seven day weeks and the four hour nights and the meals eaten standing up between takes adding up, finally, to a man laughing on camera and empty off it, the entertainment captain’s old loneliness returned at national scale, responsible for everyone’s joy and quietly bankrupt of his own. There was a stretch where I simply disappeared for a while, stepped back, because the alternative was to break in a way that could not be hidden, and stepping back, for a man whose entire identity is built on never stepping back, felt like a small death.

What I have learned, and I am still a poor student of it, is that the very engine that gets you out of a hard beginning becomes, if you never learn to throttle it, the thing that runs you into the ground inside the dream you built. The drive that saved my life was trying, slowly, to take it, and the cruelty of that is almost funny, that a boy could survive abandonment and hunger and fifteen kilometer walks only to be nearly undone, decades later, by the same survival instinct operating in an environment that no longer required it. Rest, I am learning, the hardest lesson of my adult life, is not the reward you receive after the work, it is part of the work, it is maintenance, it is the dung floor that has to be plastered every Friday whether or not it looks dirty, because you maintain before the damage and not after. My grandmother understood that about a floor. I am learning it, late and badly and in expensive shoes, about a soul. And I am putting it in this book, the burnout, the disappearing, the grey flatness, precisely because the inspirational version would hide it, and I told you this is not the inspirational version, I told you this is the true one, and the true one is that the boy got everything he wanted and it nearly emptied him, and the getting and the emptying happened at the same time, in the same body, in the same very expensive shoes.

## Chapter Forty Two: Faith in HD

I found my way back to God in the strangest cathedral, the few dead seconds between takes in a television studio, and the faith that came back to me there was not the faith I was handed as a child but a quieter, harder, more genuinely mine thing that I had to grow myself out of the wreckage of the inherited one.

You know my religious soil by now, the richest in the country, the Church of God and the Friends Church and my grandmother Gladys praying like breathing and my grandmother Josephine pastoring and my grandmother Rosemary in the pulpit and my great grandfather the Reverend Micah whose name I carry and the Adventist Sabbaths of Murgusi, faith from every direction, poured into me from before I could speak. But there is a difference between inherited faith and owned faith, between the religion you are given and the relationship you build, and through the rising and the grief and the burnout, the inherited faith had thinned in me to a habit, the prayers gone automatic, the meaning leaked out, and I had become, without quite deciding to, a man who said the words my grandmother taught me without feeling the thing she meant by them, which is exactly the emptiness that all that praying was supposed to protect me from.

And then the grief took even the habit. When my grandparents died seven days apart in 2021, despite all my praying, despite the field in Thika where I had begged God on my knees to spare at least the second of them, something in me simply broke off and went dark. I had been raised to believe that God hears the faithful, and I had watched the two most faithful people I knew die anyway while I prayed, and a young man cannot always survive that with his belief intact. I did not. For a season, in the depths of those ten months of depression, I stopped believing altogether. I became, quietly and bitterly, something close to an atheist, a preacher019s descendant sitting in the dark concluding that the praying had never worked, that the frequency my grandmother had tuned the whole house to was static, that there was no one on the other end. I want to be honest about that, because believers are not supposed to admit they lost it, and I lost it, completely, for the better part of a year.

What brought it back was not an argument and not a quiet personal epiphany. It was a crusade. After about a year in the dark, I drifted back toward church in 2022, not believing much, going through the motions, and then in 2023 I found myself at a Healing Jesus Campaign led by the evangelist Dag Heward-Mills, a great week-long open-air crusade in Nakuru, the kind of gathering where thousands of people fill a field night after night. And I went, half empty, half daring God to show me something, and over that week I saw things I could not explain away, things people call miracles, the kind of healings and turnings that my cynicism had no shelf to put on. I am not going to argue you into believing they were what they appeared to be, because faith is not won by argument and I am done pretending it is. I will only tell you what happened inside me, which is that my heart, which had been a closed cold fist since the field in Thika, opened. Something turned over in my chest in that crowd in Nakuru, and the static became a voice again, and the boy who had lost God in a field in Thika found Him again in a field in Nakuru, two fields, two years apart, the losing and the finding both happening in open ground under an open sky, which is exactly the kind of symmetry I have stopped being surprised by, because my whole life is built out of it.

And then it came home for good in the unlikeliest place. Not in a church. In a studio. In the small dead minutes between segments, when the cameras cut and the crew resets and there is a strange suspended hush in a place built entirely for noise, I started, almost involuntarily, to pray. Real prayers, not the automatic inherited ones. Help me tell this true. Steady my hands. Keep me honest under these lights. Let me be a clean window for whatever truth is supposed to pass through me to all those people I will never meet. Thank you that the boy who read to chickens is here at all. Small, genuine, unrehearsed prayers, in the gap between takes, and they were the most authentic words I had said to God in years, because they were not performed and not inherited and not automatic, they were the actual reaching of an actual soul that had been through enough by then to know it could not do the next take, or the next day, alone.

I have come to call it, privately, faith in high definition, faith with the resolution turned all the way up by suffering and success and loss, faith you can finally see clearly because life has finally focused the lens. The childhood faith was beautiful but soft, the faith of a boy who had not yet been truly tested, inherited whole from people who loved him. The studio faith is harder and clearer, the faith of a man who has buried the two people who built him seven days apart, who has burned out inside the achieved dream, who has learned in his body that no summit heals the wound and no number fills the hole, and who has concluded, from all of it, that he is not the largest thing in his own story, that there is a frequency above the metrics, the God frequency my grandmother tuned the whole house to when I was small, and that it has been there the entire time, patient, waiting in the hush between my performances for me to get quiet enough, and broken enough, and honest enough, to finally hear it again. David believed the light does not go out, it changes form. I am starting to believe him, not as an inheritance now but as a discovery, the way you rediscover your own hometown after you have seen the world, the same place, completely transformed, because you are completely transformed. I pray between takes now, on purpose, every time. It is the truest thing I do all day, and the audience never sees it, which is precisely why it works.

## Chapter Forty Three: The Boy Who Wants to Lead Lugari

I am going to tell you my largest ambition now, the one I have been circling the whole book, the one that scares me to write down because writing it down makes it a promise, and the ambition is this: one day I intend to lead my people. The people of Lugari. The red dust republic that made me. I want to go home, eventually, not as a visitor but as a servant with power, and I want to fight for the place that fought to raise me.

Let me tell you why, and let me be honest that the why is tangled, part noble and part personal, the way all real ambition is. The noble part is straightforward. I come from a place that has been neglected for as long as I have been alive, a cosmopolitan settlement-scheme country of more than ten communities living shoulder to shoulder, farming the same red soil, that struggled for water for decades, that watched its bright children walk fifteen kilometers to school on empty stomachs, that produced talent it could not afford to develop, including, very nearly, me. I know that place from the inside, from the bare feet up, and I know that its problems are not mysteries, they are simply the problems of a place that has never had a leader who actually came from the dust and never forgot the taste of it. I have a voice now, a national one, and a face people trust, and a hunger for the public stage, and I have started to understand that those tools, which I built to escape Lugari, might be exactly the tools required to return to it and serve it. The boy who got out has an obligation to reach back, and reaching back, for someone with my particular gifts, might mean reaching all the way to leadership.

The personal part I will also confess, because the deal of this book is honesty. I have a hunger for significance that the abandoned boy installed in me and that no amount of media success has fully satisfied, and politics, leadership, the chance to actually shape the lives of the people I come from, calls to that hunger in a way that subscriber counts never will. I have to be careful with that hunger, I know, because a man who seeks power to fill a personal hole is exactly the kind of man who does damage with it, and I have read enough, the 48 Laws and all the rest, to know precisely how power corrupts the wounded who reach for it. So I am doing the interior work first, on purpose, trying to heal the boy before I hand him a mandate, because Lugari does not need another leader using the public office to soothe a private wound, it has had enough of those, the whole country has. It needs someone who came from the dust, who remembers it exactly, who has done enough of his own healing to serve from a place of fullness rather than hunger, and I am trying, slowly, to become that someone, so that when the time comes, and I believe it will come, I can offer my people a servant and not just another ambitious man with a microphone.

I do not know the shape it will take or the year it will arrive. I only know that the trajectory of my whole life bends toward it, that everything, the choir and the stages and the journalism and the national platform and the studying of power and the painful learning of its dangers, has been, whether I knew it or not, a preparation for service to the place that made me. I was born in the ruins of one dead country’s dream, in a hospital named after a fallen empire, and I have built a name in the loud new country of the screen, and the arc of it, I am increasingly certain, is meant to bring me home, to the red dust, to lead. Born broke in Lugari. Built loud in the world. Returning, one day, to build Lugari itself. If God grants the years and the healing, that is the whole point of the noise.

## Chapter Forty Four: Roylandz Is What I Built, Eugine Micah Is Who I Am

I told you on the very first page of this book that you did not know me, and that by the end you would, and we are nearly at the end now, so let me make good on the promise by settling, finally, the question of my own name, because the answer to that question is the answer to who I actually am.

For years I hid inside Roylandz. I have told you how the name was born, in Thika, as armor, a chosen identity for a boy tired of being nobody, a clean banner with nothing on it but ambition, and I have told you how the armor became a cage, how it was easier to be the bold invented Roylandz than the wounded real Eugine, how I performed the persona so I would not have to feel the person. And for a long stretch of my rising, Roylandz won, Roylandz was the public self, the brand, the loud confident untouchable thing, and Eugine Micah, the boy from the mud house, the abandoned one, the one who had been called trash and forgot how to say mum and got a C and knelt for a girl in Nairobi, that boy got smaller and quieter and more hidden behind the magnificent armor of the name I had made.

And then the grief and the burnout and the quiet rooms did their work, and I came to understand the thing that this whole book has been built to say. Roylandz is not me. Roylandz is something I made, like the choir I sang in or the show I filmed on a bedsheet or the platform I built, a thing, a creation, a costume, real and valuable and mine, but a thing nonetheless, and a man who confuses the thing he built with the person he is has lost himself inside his own creation. Eugine Micah is the person. Eugine Micah is the constant, the one who was there before Roylandz and will be there after, the boy under the armor, the human being who has to be healed, who has to rest, who has to pray between takes, who has to put the costume down at the end of the day and be loved, if he is going to be loved at all, as himself and not as his brand. The name my great grandfather the reverend handed down to me, the name that means noble and asks who is like God, that is the true one, the one that carries my whole history and my whole soul, and Roylandz is the one that carries only my ambition.

I am not abandoning Roylandz. Let me be clear about that, because it is part of the honesty. Roylandz is what I built and it remains something I will build again, a venture, a brand, a vehicle for the work, and the future will have Roylandz in it, in new forms I have not yet made. But Roylandz will be a thing I do, never again a place I hide, never again confused with the man doing it. Eugine Micah is who I am, the progressive constant, the through line, the one the whole book has been about, and reclaiming him from inside the armor, learning to be him out loud rather than hiding him behind the brand, is the central victory of my adult life, more important than the screen or the numbers or any of it. The boy spent his whole childhood being told who he was by other people, by the family that called him trash and the school that called him lemindet and the world that ranked his dark skin and his empty pockets. Then he spent his young manhood telling the world who he was through a name he invented to escape all that. And now, finally, he is learning the hardest and best thing, which is simply to be who he actually is, Eugine Micah, no armor, no performance, the real one, and to discover that the real one, the boy under everything, was always worth more than the brand he hid him behind.

## Chapter Forty Five: Born Broke, Built Loud

So here we are, at the end, at the title, and I owe you the meaning of the four words I have been living inside for this entire book, the four words that are my whole life pressed down to a size you can carry out of here in your pocket.

Born broke. That part was assigned to me, written into the first line by a hospital named after a country that no longer existed, on a Christmas Eve, to a teenage mother running on sugar water, in a province that was poor in a country that was struggling, into a family rich in everything that does not spend. I did not choose born broke. Nobody chooses their first line. It was handed to me complete, the dust and the lack and the cold mornings and the thin suppers and the long arithmetic of need, and for years I believed that first line was also the verdict, the sentence, the whole story compressed into the conditions of its beginning.

Built loud. That part was the answer, and the answer was not handed to me, the answer I had to construct, out of the only material the beginning had provided, which was my voice. Built is the word I had to earn the right to use, because built means it did not merely happen to me, built means that I and the people who loved me made it, deliberately, in the dark, without applause, the way Gladys made a floor and David made a school and Samson named a hill. And loud is the how, the medium, the gift, the one thing the broke beginning could not repossess because it did not live in my pockets, it lived in my throat, where poverty could not reach. I was born into the silence of lack, and I built, out of that very silence, a loud that broke it. Born broke, built loud. The first half is circumstance. The second half is choice. The comma between them is a whole life.

And the deepest thing I know now, the thing forty four chapters have been trying to say, is that the broke and the loud were never opposites, never a before and an after, never a wound and its cure. They were always the same thing seen from two ends. The brokenness was the silence, and the silence is exactly what taught me sound. The lack was the dust, and the dust is exactly what knew my name. The poverty was the pressure, and the pressure is exactly what built the loud. You cannot have my loud without my broke. They are one object. The boy who read to chickens because he had no other audience is the same man a country now listens to, and he is not two people, he is one, and the chickens were not the embarrassment before the success, they were the first verse of it, the loud already practicing in the only theater the broke could afford.

But I am going to keep my promise, the one I made on the first page, and refuse you the bow. Because the honest ending is not that I built loud and now I am whole. The honest ending is that I was born broke, and I built loud, and I am still, today, as you read this, trying to make it, still climbing, still healing, still learning to rest and to pray and to be Eugine instead of Roylandz, still reaching back toward the red dust that made me, still unfinished in every way that matters. This is not a success story. It is a true one. And the truest thing in it is that the building is not done, that the loud is still being built, out of a broke that never fully goes away, by a man who has decided that the unfinished climbing is not a failure but the entire point, the proof that he is still alive, still going, still here. I was born broke. I built loud. And I am still building, which means, thank God, that the best of it is still in front of me, and in front of you too, whoever you are, reading this by whatever light you have, with whatever first line you were handed. Your beginning is not your verdict. Mine was not. Now go build something loud out of your own silence. I will be doing the same. We are not finished. We are just getting started.

# Epilogue: When the Lights Go Off

The lights in a television studio do not fade out gently. They cut, all at once, and for a second the eyes keep the ghost of them, bright shapes swimming in the sudden dark, and it is in that exact second, every single time, that I am carried back to the kerosene lamp of my childhood, the small flame in the Lugari dark, the original light before all the manufactured ones.

When the lights go off, the persona goes with them. Roylandz lives in the light and dies in the cut, and what is left standing in the dark is just the boy, the actual one, Beverlyne’s son, David’s library kid, Gladys’s project, Samson’s lineage, the child who read to chickens, breathing in the dark exactly as he breathed in the dark of a home with no electricity twenty years ago. The lights gave me a career. The dark gives me back myself. And I have learned, slowly, to be grateful for both, because a life that is only lights is a performance with nobody home, and a life that is only dark is a gift no one ever gets to open.

I think about David in that dark, who believed the light does not go out but only changes form, who is gone now and not gone, his form changed into every school he built and every book he handed me and every word of this you have read. I think about Gladys, who believed God was always the audience and never once blinked, watching still, I have to believe, from the only seat that was full the whole time I performed for emptier ones. And I understand that when my own lights finally cut, for the last time, the form will change again, and something I built in the dark, some library of the spirit, some encouraged kid, some served corner of Lugari, will keep the light going in a shape I will not be around to see.

That is the whole hope, in the end. Not that the lights stay on, because they do not, for anyone. But that you used them, while you had them, to build something that does not need them. When the lights go off, I am not as afraid as I used to be. I was terrified of the dark for most of my life, of being unseen, of the silence closing back over the boy. But I have made a kind of peace with the cut, because I finally understand what David meant. The light does not go out. It changes form. And the form it changed into, this time, is the book in your hands, still glowing a little, I hope, in whatever light you are reading by, carrying a flame that was lit in a Lugari dark a long time ago and has never, not once, actually gone out.

Goodnight. Keep your own library open. The lights come back on in the morning, and the dust still knows your name.

# Acknowledgments

A book about being built by other people cannot pretend it was written alone, so let me name the builders, knowing the list will be incomplete the way all such lists are.

To my mother, Beverlyne Kangazi Kemoli, who survived the road and the violence and the two pots and the closed door, and who prayed for my success before there was a single reason to. Every word here is partly yours, and I have not forgotten my promise to collect your papers.

To my father, Joab Philemona Etale Ob’bayi, the lion, who fed me the news before dawn and sold his land for my schooling and quit the drink at last, too late and just in time. I understand you better now than I did, and I love you more for the understanding.

To my brother, Danson Gadi, my first audience, my crew of one, who has the scar and gave me mine, and to our sisters Beryl and Belinda.

To David Zarembka and Gladys Kamonya, who built me on purpose and left seven days apart, and whose form has only changed. I am the gift. I am trying to be worthy of the giving.

To Samson Kemoli of Kemoview and Josephine Kemoli, who gave my mother away and raised me anyway, and to Rosemary Ongoche Ob’bayi and Philemona Jairo Micah Ob’bayi, and to the Reverend Micah Ob’bayi of Bunyore, whose name I carry like grace I did not earn.

To Tommy and Joy Mutanu Zarembka, the ones who stayed after the funerals. To Aunt Joy Ob’bayi, who paid the bill that let me exist. To my aunt Laureen, who sat with me in 2017 and helped tell the truth that changed everything.

To Hassan Omar, who paid twenty five shillings and kept a boy in school and will never know it. To Madame Zipporah, who fed me bread for reading. To Luckyboy Maingi, who filmed a show on faith with me against a bedsheet.

To the team at Royal Media Services and Citizen TV who let a green attaché learn by doing it badly in public. To everyone at TIBS College in Thika who turned a C student into a valedictorian. To Lucy Ogunde and the whole team at PPP TV, who share the lights with me, on the very channel I once watched from the far side of a wall.

To the women I have loved and the women I have failed, who taught me that my heart works and that working it carelessly does damage, and whose privacy I have kept because they kept faith with me when I deserved less.

To the seven hundred thousand and counting, the loud broke kids and everyone else who chose to gather around the noise. You are the reason. And to the chickens of Manyonyi, my first and most loyal audience, who showed up, which is more than most. This was always, in the end, for you.
